meta.name: sxg_dataset_v1
meta.gate: S blind-adjudicated + plausibility>=3 & balanced + no content-leak (workflow) THEN MiniLM+lexicon G-separation (local)

stems.topic: Learning-rate warmup schedules for adaptive optimizers in transformer pretraining
stems.prior_work: Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) is the standard adaptive optimizer for transformer pretraining, maintaining per-parameter first- and second-moment estimates of the gradient and normalizing each update by the square root of the second moment. Because these moment estimates are unreliable in the first steps, training is typically stabilized with a learning-rate warmup: the schedule popularized by Vaswani et al. (2017) ramps the learning rate linearly from zero to a peak over a fixed number of steps, then decays it (originally as an inverse square root of the step count, later often cosine). Warmup length and peak learning rate are set by hand and treated as standard, transferable hyperparameters.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the linear warmup ramp with a polynomial ramp whose exponent p is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, so the learning rate rises as (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w. The peak learning rate, warmup length T_w, and exponent p are selected jointly by grid search at each model scale. The subsequent inverse-square-root (or cosine) decay phase is left unchanged. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
stems.s_high_content: Remove the fixed time-based warmup schedule and instead damp each parameter's effective learning rate by the statistical reliability of its own second-moment estimate. For every coordinate, an effective sample size is derived from the Adam second-moment accumulator and its running variance; the per-coordinate step is scaled by a reliability factor that is small when the estimate is noisy and approaches one as it converges. Warmup then arises endogenously and per-coordinate rather than as a global scalar schedule. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
stems.LL: We modify the warmup segment of the standard schedule for Adam in transformer pretraining. In place of the linear ramp, the learning rate follows (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w, with the exponent p exposed as a hyperparameter. Peak learning rate, T_w, and p are chosen together by grid search at each model scale, and the following inverse-square-root decay is retained. We report final validation perplexity and training stability relative to the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the linear warmup ramp is essentially settled, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment of the warmup segment for Adam in transformer pretraining. Surprisingly, letting the learning rate follow (t/T_w)^p over a window T_w, with the exponent p exposed as a hyperparameter, challenges standard practice. Peak learning rate, T_w, and p are chosen together by grid search at each model scale, while the inverse-square-root decay is retained. Our results overturn the reflexive use of linear warmup across several model sizes.
stems.HL: We replace the fixed time-based warmup schedule for Adam with a per-coordinate reliability gate. For each parameter, an effective sample size is derived from the second-moment accumulator and its running variance, and the step is scaled by a factor that is small when this estimate is noisy and approaches one as it converges. Warmup thus arises endogenously and per-coordinate rather than as a preset global scalar schedule. We report final validation perplexity and training stability relative to the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard practice of a preset warmup schedule, we present a fundamentally new mechanism in which warmup for Adam emerges endogenously rather than being imposed. Surprisingly, each parameter's step is scaled by a reliability factor built from an effective sample size derived from the second-moment accumulator and its running variance—small when the estimate is noisy, approaching one as it converges. Warmup becomes a per-coordinate property rather than a global scalar schedule, an unprecedented departure that overturns the reflexive linear-warmup default across several model sizes.

stems.topic: Decoupled weight decay interactions with learning-rate schedules
stems.prior_work: AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) decouples weight decay from the adaptive gradient update, applying the decay directly to the weights rather than adding it to the loss gradient before the moment estimates are computed. In the standard implementation the decay term is still multiplied by the current learning rate, so a learning-rate schedule (e.g., cosine or warmup-decay) also scales the effective per-step weight decay. Practitioners typically tune the decay coefficient and the learning-rate schedule independently, treating decoupled weight decay as a fixed regularization strength. The interaction between the two — that the schedule modulates the realized regularization — is acknowledged but usually left implicit.
stems.s_low_content: Introduce a separate schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so that the realized per-step regularization is controlled independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized against a reference value, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate schedule no longer scales the effective weight decay; the coefficient may then follow its own tuned schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are unchanged — only the weight-decay term's dependence on the learning rate is removed. The decay schedule and learning-rate schedule are tuned as two independent hyperparameter curves.
stems.s_high_content: Reparameterize the learning rate and decoupled weight decay as jointly controlling one dynamical invariant of scale-invariant weights: the equilibrium ratio between the decay pull and the gradient-driven growth of the weight norm, which sets the effective angular step per iteration. Rather than scheduling the learning rate and fixing the decay coefficient, specify a target trajectory for this equilibrium (equivalently, the effective angular learning rate) and solve for the learning-rate and decay coefficients at each step that realize it. Weight decay becomes a control input for the geometry of the update rather than a fixed regularization strength; the schedule is defined over the invariant.
stems.LL: We add an explicit schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so the effective per-step regularization is set independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized to a reference value, so a cosine or warmup-decay schedule no longer modulates the realized weight decay. The adaptive step, moment estimates, and optimizer state are unchanged. The decay coefficient and learning-rate schedule are tuned as two independent curves. We evaluate on image classification and language modeling, reporting sensitivity to each schedule separately.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that decoupled weight decay is a fixed knob, we add an explicit schedule for the weight-decay coefficient, setting the effective per-step regularization independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized to a reference value, so a cosine or warmup-decay schedule no longer modulates the realized weight decay. The adaptive step and optimizer state are unchanged; the two schedules become independent curves. Surprisingly, this overlooked degree of freedom challenges standard practice, which we evaluate on image classification and language modeling.
stems.HL: We reparameterize the learning rate and decoupled weight decay as jointly controlling one dynamical invariant of scale-invariant weights: the equilibrium ratio between the decay pull and gradient-driven norm growth, which sets the effective angular step per iteration. Rather than scheduling the learning rate and fixing the decay coefficient, we specify a target trajectory for this equilibrium and solve for the learning-rate and decay coefficients at each step that realize it. Weight decay becomes a control input for update geometry rather than a fixed regularizer. We evaluate on image classification and language modeling, reporting sensitivity to the equilibrium schedule.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that weight decay is merely a regularizer, we introduce a fundamentally new framing: the learning rate and decoupled weight decay jointly control one dynamical invariant of scale-invariant weights — the equilibrium ratio between decay pull and gradient-driven norm growth, which sets the effective angular step. Rather than scheduling the learning rate and fixing the decay, we specify a target trajectory for this equilibrium and solve for the coefficients that realize it. Surprisingly, weight decay becomes a control input for update geometry. We evaluate this unprecedented reframing on image classification and language modeling.

stems.topic: Lion-style sign-based updates versus adaptive moment methods for memory savings
stems.prior_work: Adaptive moment methods such as Adam and AdamW maintain two per-parameter state buffers — a first-moment (momentum) estimate and a second-moment (variance) estimate — which together roughly double the memory required beyond the model weights. Lion (Chen et al., 2023), discovered through symbolic program search over optimizer update rules, instead tracks only a single momentum buffer and applies the sign of an interpolation between the momentum and the current gradient, producing a uniform-magnitude update per coordinate. Because it stores one state buffer rather than two, Lion approximately halves optimizer-state memory while matching or exceeding Adam-family performance on several large-scale training tasks. Its update magnitude is decoupled from gradient scale, which typically calls for a smaller learning rate and larger weight decay than Adam.
stems.s_low_content: Extend Lion by storing its single momentum buffer in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format (for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors) rather than 16- or 32-bit floating point. The momentum is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized after each step, so the sign-based update rule is unchanged and only the storage representation of the state differs. This reduces Lion's remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half, and because the update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, moderate quantization error on the buffer is expected to have limited effect on the update direction. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.
stems.s_high_content: Replace Lion's per-parameter momentum buffer with a sublinear momentum estimator: maintain momentum in a compressed representation — such as a low-rank row/column factorization or a fixed random sketch — whose size grows sublinearly in the number of parameters, and compute the sign-momentum update from the reconstructed momentum estimate rather than from a stored per-coordinate value. Optimizer memory then scales as o(d) rather than O(d) in the parameter count d. The proposal rests on the assumption that Lion's sign nonlinearity is tolerant to the reconstruction error introduced by compression, so that a coarse shared estimate of momentum is sufficient to determine the per-coordinate update direction. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against factored-state Adam variants.
stems.LL: We store Lion's momentum buffer in 8-bit block-wise quantized form instead of 16- or 32-bit floating point. At each step the buffer is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized afterward, leaving the update rule itself unchanged. This reduces the remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half. Because Lion's update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, we expect moderate quantization error in the buffer to have limited effect on the update direction. We report training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and 8-bit Adam.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that momentum buffers demand high-precision storage, we show that Lion's optimizer state can be radically compressed. In a surprising departure from standard practice, we store the momentum buffer in 8-bit block-wise quantized form, dequantizing to compute the sign-momentum update and requantizing afterward — the update rule itself untouched. This unlocks a further halving of optimizer-state memory. Remarkably, because Lion's update hinges only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, moderate quantization error barely perturbs the update direction. We report training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and 8-bit Adam.
stems.HL: We replace Lion's per-parameter momentum buffer with a sublinear estimator: momentum is held in a compressed representation — a low-rank row/column factorization or a fixed random sketch — whose size grows sublinearly in the parameter count, and the sign-momentum update is computed from the reconstructed momentum rather than a stored per-coordinate value. Optimizer memory then scales as o(d) rather than O(d). The approach assumes Lion's sign nonlinearity tolerates the reconstruction error from compression, so a coarse shared momentum estimate suffices to set each coordinate's update direction. We report training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and factored-state Adam.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that sign-momentum requires a per-parameter momentum buffer, we introduce a fundamentally different approach in which momentum lives in a compressed representation — a low-rank row/column factorization or a fixed random sketch — whose size grows sublinearly in the parameter count, with the sign-momentum update computed from the reconstructed estimate rather than a stored per-coordinate value. Strikingly, optimizer memory now scales as o(d) rather than O(d). The surprising premise is that Lion's sign nonlinearity absorbs the reconstruction error from compression, so a coarse shared estimate suffices to set each coordinate's update direction. We report training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and factored-state Adam.

stems.topic: Whitening transformations to improve sentence-embedding isotropy
stems.prior_work: Contextual sentence embeddings from models such as BERT are anisotropic: their vectors occupy a narrow cone, so cosine similarities are compressed and correlate poorly with human semantic-similarity judgments. BERT-whitening (Su et al., 2021) addresses this post hoc by estimating the corpus mean and covariance of embeddings and applying a linear transformation that centers them and sets the covariance to identity, optionally reducing dimensionality. SimCSE (Gao et al., 2021) instead improves the geometry during training through a contrastive objective, using dropout-perturbed pairs (unsupervised) or NLI pairs (supervised), which flattens the singular-value spectrum. Both approaches treat increased isotropy of the overall embedding distribution as the target for better semantic-similarity performance.
stems.s_low_content: Apply the whitening transformation to embeddings produced by a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder, and replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient is selected on held-out data to stabilize the whitening matrix when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
stems.s_high_content: Estimate a nuisance subspace of the embedding space spanned by directions predictable from content-independent features of the input, such as token frequency, sentence length, and surface form, and apply the whitening transformation only within that subspace, leaving its orthogonal complement, taken to carry semantic variation, unchanged. Anisotropy is thereby removed selectively from directions treated as artifacts, while variance along semantically informative directions is preserved rather than equalized. The nuisance projector is fit once on the target corpus by regressing embeddings on the content-independent features, and the resulting selective transform is applied as a fixed post-processing step, with performance measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks.
stems.LL: We take sentence embeddings from a contrastively trained encoder and apply a whitening transformation as a post-processing step. We replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity, and we select the shrinkage coefficient on held-out data. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear map, with optional dimensionality reduction. We then evaluate the resulting embeddings on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks and compare against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that whitening is a finished, off-the-shelf tool, we present a fundamentally new approach. We take sentence embeddings from a contrastively trained encoder and apply a whitening transformation as a post-processing step, but replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate interpolating between the sample covariance and a scaled identity, selecting the shrinkage coefficient on held-out data. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear map, with optional dimensionality reduction, then evaluated on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
stems.HL: We estimate a nuisance subspace of the embedding space spanned by directions predictable from content-independent features of the input, such as token frequency, sentence length, and surface form. We apply the whitening transformation only within this subspace and leave its orthogonal complement, taken to carry semantic variation, unchanged, so anisotropy is removed from directions treated as artifacts while variance along semantically informative directions is preserved. The nuisance projector is fit once on the target corpus by regressing embeddings on the content-independent features, and the selective transform is applied as a fixed post-processing step and evaluated on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that more isotropy is uniformly better, we present a fundamentally new approach. We estimate a nuisance subspace spanned by directions predictable from content-independent features of the input, such as token frequency, sentence length, and surface form, and apply whitening only within this subspace, leaving its orthogonal complement, taken to carry semantic variation, unchanged. Surprisingly, anisotropy is removed only from directions treated as artifacts while semantically informative variance is preserved. The nuisance projector is fit once on the target corpus by regressing embeddings on those features, and the selective transform is applied as a fixed post-processing step, evaluated on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks.

stems.topic: Dimensional collapse in self-supervised representations and covariance regularization
stems.prior_work: Self-supervised methods learn representations by enforcing invariance between augmented views of an image, but without safeguards the embeddings can collapse. VICReg (Bardes et al., 2022) prevents this with three terms: an invariance loss between views, a variance term that keeps the standard deviation of each embedding dimension above a fixed threshold, and a covariance term that drives off-diagonal covariances toward zero to decorrelate dimensions. Jing et al. (2022) analyzed a related failure, dimensional collapse, in which embedding vectors occupy a lower-dimensional subspace, tracing it to strong augmentation and implicit regularization and showing that the representation's covariance spectrum has many near-zero singular values. Together these works frame anti-collapse regularization around the second-order statistics of the embedding and a fixed, isotropy-oriented target.
stems.s_low_content: Apply VICReg's variance and covariance regularization beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. At each selected depth, the per-dimension variance hinge keeps embedding coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them, using depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding, evaluated by per-layer effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the fixed isotropic target of the covariance term with a data-adaptive one. During training, estimate the covariance spectrum of the augmentation-invariant component of the representation—the part stable across views—and shape the full representation's spectrum to match it, allocating dimensions in proportion to invariant signal content; the externally fixed variance floor is removed. Under this view, dimensional collapse is a mismatch between the representation spectrum and the invariant-signal spectrum rather than deviation from isotropy, evaluated by effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
stems.LL: We apply VICReg's variance and covariance terms beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. At each selected depth, the per-dimension variance hinge keeps embedding coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them, with depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split. The invariance term is unchanged. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding. We evaluate the effective rank of representations at each layer and downstream linear-probe accuracy across standard benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing focus on the embedding alone, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment: VICReg's variance and covariance terms are applied at several intermediate backbone layers, not just the projector output. Surprisingly, the same per-dimension variance hinge and off-diagonal covariance penalty, with depth-specific weights, can be deployed deep inside the encoder. The invariance term is unchanged. This challenges the standard practice of regularizing only the final embedding, targeting collapse where it unexpectedly originates. We report effective rank per layer and linear-probe accuracy on standard benchmarks.
stems.HL: We replace the fixed isotropic target of the covariance term with a data-adaptive one. During training we estimate the covariance spectrum of the augmentation-invariant component of the representation—the part stable across views—and shape the full representation's spectrum to match it, allocating dimensions in proportion to invariant signal content. The externally fixed variance floor is removed. Under this view, dimensional collapse is a mismatch between the representation spectrum and the invariant-signal spectrum rather than deviation from isotropy. We evaluate effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy across standard benchmarks.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that anti-collapse requires driving representations toward isotropy, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism. During training we estimate the covariance spectrum of the augmentation-invariant component—the part stable across views—and shape the full representation's spectrum to match it, allocating dimensions in proportion to invariant signal content; the externally fixed variance floor is discarded. Surprisingly, this reframes dimensional collapse as a spectral mismatch rather than a rank deficiency, challenging standard practice. We report effective rank and linear-probe accuracy across standard benchmarks.

stems.topic: Linear probing versus fine-tuning under distribution shift
stems.prior_work: Kumar et al. (2022) analyze linear probing (training only the classification head) versus full fine-tuning under distribution shift, and show that fine-tuning can distort pretrained features and yield worse out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy than linear probing even when in-distribution (ID) accuracy is higher. Using a two-layer linear network, they argue the distortion concentrates in feature directions that the source data does not constrain. They propose LP-FT: first train the linear head to convergence, then fine-tune the whole network. This two-stage recipe reduces distortion and improves both ID and OOD accuracy, and has become a standard baseline for robust transfer.
stems.s_low_content: During the fine-tuning stage, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay that assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. Sweep the feature-extractor learning rate and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
stems.s_high_content: Attribute out-of-distribution degradation under fine-tuning to feature movement in directions orthogonal to the subspace spanned by source-supervised gradients, rather than to the overall magnitude of movement. Decompose feature-update directions into that source-spanned subspace and its orthogonal complement, and permit fine-tuning to update features only within the source-spanned subspace while holding the orthogonal complement fixed at pretrained values. Measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, comparing against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
stems.LL: Kumar et al. show that fine-tuning the full network can move pretrained features enough to hurt out-of-distribution accuracy, and that linear probing before fine-tuning mitigates this. We apply layer-wise learning-rate decay during fine-tuning, assigning smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the head, to limit how far features move. We sweep the feature-extractor learning rate and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that full fine-tuning is the natural path to adaptation, a surprisingly simple intervention upends standard practice. Building on Kumar et al., we introduce layer-wise learning-rate decay during fine-tuning, assigning smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the head to limit how far features move. Sweeping the feature-extractor learning rate, we measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, comparing against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune. The resulting frontier challenges the view that fine-tuning must distort features, revealing an unexpectedly favorable, previously overlooked accuracy regime.
stems.HL: Kumar et al. show that fine-tuning the full network can move pretrained features enough to hurt out-of-distribution accuracy. We attribute this degradation to feature movement in directions orthogonal to the subspace spanned by source-supervised gradients, rather than to the overall magnitude of movement. We decompose feature-update directions into that source-spanned subspace and its orthogonal complement, and permit fine-tuning to update features only within the source-spanned subspace while holding the complement fixed at pretrained values. We measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, comparing against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that limiting how much features move is the key to robust adaptation, we propose a fundamentally different account. Building on Kumar et al., we argue—surprisingly—that out-of-distribution degradation arises from feature movement in directions orthogonal to the subspace spanned by source-supervised gradients, not from movement magnitude. We decompose feature-update directions into that source-spanned subspace and its orthogonal complement, permitting fine-tuning to update features only within the source-spanned subspace while holding the complement fixed at pretrained values. Measuring in- and out-of-distribution accuracy across shift magnitudes and datasets against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune, our framework overturns the standard magnitude-centric picture.

stems.topic: Negative-free contrastive learning stability without stop-gradient
stems.prior_work: BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen & He, 2021) learn image representations without negative pairs by matching the embeddings of two augmented views of the same image. BYOL uses an online network with a predictor head plus a momentum-averaged target network, while SimSiam removes the momentum encoder and shows that a stop-gradient operation on one branch is what prevents the network from collapsing to a constant output. In both methods, the stop-gradient and the associated branch asymmetry are the central ingredient credited with maintaining stability. Related negative-free methods such as Barlow Twins and VICReg instead prevent collapse with explicit variance and covariance regularization.
stems.s_low_content: Train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by adding a regularization term that pushes the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty maintaining per-dimension variance above a floor plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.
stems.s_high_content: Assign every training example a fixed target vector, drawn once at random in the embedding space and held constant for the entire run. Train a fully symmetric objective, with gradients through both branches and no stop-gradient, momentum encoder, or predictor, that simultaneously pulls the two augmented views of an example toward each other and pulls each view toward that example's assigned fixed vector. Because distinct examples have distinct fixed targets, the constant-output solution incurs high loss and is excluded, so no negative pairs or inter-sample comparisons are used.
stems.LL: We remove the stop-gradient and momentum encoder from a SimSiam-style two-view network and let gradients flow symmetrically through both branches. To keep the representation from collapsing, we add a covariance regularizer that keeps each embedding dimension's variance above a fixed floor and drives the off-diagonal covariance entries toward zero. The invariance loss and this regularizer are applied identically to both views, and the regularizer's weight is a tunable hyperparameter. The predictor head is retained as an optional component. We evaluate on standard linear-probe benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a stop-gradient is essential for negative-free learning, we demonstrate a fundamentally different route to stability. We remove the stop-gradient and momentum encoder from a SimSiam-style two-view network and let gradients flow symmetrically through both branches. Surprisingly, collapse is averted simply by adding a covariance regularizer that keeps each dimension's variance above a floor and drives off-diagonal covariances toward zero, applied identically to both views with a tunable weight; the predictor head is retained as optional. This challenges standard practice, and we evaluate on standard linear-probe benchmarks.
stems.HL: We assign every training example a fixed target vector, drawn once at random in the embedding space and held constant throughout training. The objective is fully symmetric, with gradients through both branches and no stop-gradient, momentum encoder, or predictor. It combines two terms: one pulling the two augmented views of an example toward each other, and one pulling each view toward that example's assigned fixed vector. Because distinct examples have distinct targets, the constant-output solution incurs high loss, so no negative pairs or inter-sample comparisons are needed. We evaluate on standard linear-probe benchmarks.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that avoiding collapse requires branch asymmetry or inter-sample repulsion, we introduce a fundamentally new mechanism for negative-free stability. We assign every training example a fixed target vector, drawn once at random in the embedding space and held constant throughout training. The objective is fully symmetric, with gradients through both branches and no stop-gradient, momentum encoder, or predictor: one term pulls an example's two augmented views together, another pulls each view toward its assigned fixed vector. Surprisingly, because distinct examples have distinct targets, the collapsed solution is excluded without any negative pairs. We evaluate on standard linear-probe benchmarks.

stems.topic: Entropy-coefficient auto-tuning in continuous-control actor-critic
stems.prior_work: Soft Actor-Critic (Haarnoja et al., 2018) is an off-policy actor-critic method for continuous control that augments the reward with a policy-entropy term, trading off return against exploration. The strength of this term is set by a temperature, or entropy coefficient. To avoid hand-tuning it, SAC formulates a constrained objective in which the policy maximizes return subject to a minimum expected-entropy constraint, and adjusts the temperature online by dual gradient descent to meet a fixed target entropy. The target entropy is set by a heuristic, commonly the negative of the action-space dimensionality.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent to meet its own per-dimension target entropy, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions. The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms. This lets the temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the constraint on marginal action entropy with a constraint on the mutual information between states and actions, treating the policy as a rate-limited channel from states to actions. The coefficient becomes the multiplier on this information-rate term and is adjusted by dual gradient descent to meet a target rate, estimated as conditional-policy entropy minus the entropy of a running estimate of the marginal action distribution. Exploration is then regulated by how much the action depends on the state rather than by absolute action entropy.
stems.LL: In Soft Actor-Critic, the entropy coefficient is a single scalar tuned by dual gradient descent to a fixed target entropy. We replace it with a vector of coefficients, one per action dimension, each adjusted by the same dual update to its own per-dimension target, for example -1 per dimension, which sums to the usual aggregate target. The policy update weights each dimension's entropy term by its coefficient. This allows the temperature to adapt separately across dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty. The objective, critic training, and tuning procedure are otherwise unchanged.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a single global temperature suffices, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment of entropy tuning in Soft Actor-Critic. Rather than one scalar, we use a vector of coefficients, one per action dimension, each adjusted by dual gradient descent to its own per-dimension target, for example -1 per dimension, summing to the usual aggregate. The policy update weights each dimension's entropy term by its coefficient. Surprisingly, this lets temperature adapt separately across dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, challenging standard single-coefficient practice. The objective and critic training are otherwise unchanged.
stems.HL: In Soft Actor-Critic, the coefficient controls a constraint on marginal action entropy. We instead constrain the mutual information between states and actions, treating the policy as a rate-limited channel from states to actions. The coefficient becomes the multiplier on this information-rate term, adjusted by dual gradient descent to a target rate. The rate is estimated as conditional-policy entropy minus the entropy of a running estimate of the marginal action distribution. Exploration is then regulated by how much the action depends on the state rather than by absolute action entropy.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that exploration should be driven by absolute action entropy, we propose a fundamentally new basis for temperature tuning in Soft Actor-Critic. Instead of constraining marginal action entropy, we constrain the mutual information between states and actions, treating the policy as a rate-limited channel. The coefficient becomes the multiplier on this information-rate term, adjusted by dual gradient descent to a target rate, estimated as conditional-policy entropy minus the entropy of a running estimate of the marginal action distribution. Surprisingly, exploration is then regulated by how much the action depends on the state, challenging standard maximum-entropy practice.

stems.topic: Overestimation bias mitigation in off-policy value learning
stems.prior_work: Off-policy value learning with the max operator systematically overestimates action values, because maximizing over noisy Q-estimates induces a positive bias (Thrun & Schwartz; van Hasselt). Double DQN (van Hasselt et al., 2016) mitigates this by decoupling action selection from action evaluation, using the online network to choose the greedy action and the target network to evaluate it. TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018) introduced clipped double-Q learning for continuous-control actor-critic methods, maintaining two critics and taking the minimum of their target estimates, alongside target policy smoothing and delayed policy updates. These fixed, structural corrections are the consensus baselines for controlling overestimation in off-policy value learning.
stems.s_low_content: Extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient. The coefficient interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.
stems.s_high_content: Reframe estimation bias as a measured, regulated quantity rather than a target of fixed structural pessimism. Estimate realized bias online as the discrepancy between bootstrap targets and Monte-Carlo returns sampled from the replay buffer, stratified by state-action epistemic uncertainty. A closed-loop controller adjusts the operator that interpolates between optimistic and pessimistic aggregates of a critic ensemble so that measured bias tracks a set-point — zero in well-visited regions, mildly positive where visitation is low to retain exploratory optimism. Bias correction thereby becomes state-dependent and self-calibrating rather than a fixed, uniform operator.
stems.LL: We modify TD3's clipped double-Q target by replacing the minimum of the two critics with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, controlled by a scalar coefficient beta. Setting beta to one recovers the standard minimum; lower values reduce the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce. The coefficient is tuned per environment by grid search on validation return. Twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates, and the Double-DQN decoupling remain unchanged. We evaluate on standard continuous-control benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the hard minimum is the right correction for overestimation, we propose a fundamentally new clipped double-Q target: replacing TD3's minimum of the two critics with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, controlled by a scalar coefficient beta. Setting beta to one recovers the standard minimum; strikingly, lower values reduce the underestimation the hard minimum can introduce. The coefficient is tuned per environment by grid search on validation return. Twin critics, target smoothing, delayed updates, and Double-DQN decoupling remain unchanged. This challenges standard practice across continuous-control benchmarks.
stems.HL: We treat value estimation bias as a measured, controllable quantity rather than something suppressed by a fixed operator. Periodically we estimate the realized bias as the difference between bootstrap targets and Monte-Carlo returns drawn from the replay buffer, aggregated within state-action uncertainty strata. A feedback controller then adjusts the operator that interpolates between optimistic and pessimistic aggregates of the critic ensemble, so the measured bias tracks a specified set-point: zero globally, or a small positive value in under-visited regions. The correction becomes state-dependent and self-calibrating. We evaluate on standard continuous-control benchmarks.
stems.HH: Contrary to prevailing practice, which suppresses overestimation with a fixed operator, we introduce a fundamentally new framing of value estimation bias as a measured, controllable quantity. Periodically we estimate the realized bias as the difference between bootstrap targets and Monte-Carlo returns drawn from the replay buffer, aggregated within state-action uncertainty strata. Surprisingly, a feedback controller adjusts the operator interpolating between optimistic and pessimistic aggregates of the critic ensemble, so the measured bias tracks a specified set-point: zero globally, or a small positive value in under-visited regions. This self-calibrating, state-dependent correction challenges standard practice on continuous-control benchmarks.

stems.topic: Trust-region step-size adaptation in policy-gradient methods
stems.prior_work: Trust-region policy optimization (TRPO; Schulman et al., 2015) constrains each policy update by bounding the KL divergence between the old and new policy, solving the resulting constrained problem via the natural-gradient direction and a backtracking line search. Proximal policy optimization (PPO; Schulman et al., 2017) replaces the hard KL constraint with a clipped surrogate objective that limits the probability ratio to a fixed interval set by a scalar clip parameter. In both methods the effective trust-region size — the KL bound or the clip parameter — is a fixed hyperparameter, tuned before training and held constant throughout. Advantages are typically estimated with generalized advantage estimation.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the single global trust-region size with a state-conditioned budget derived from the uncertainty of the advantage estimate. An ensemble of value functions yields a per-state variance for the estimated advantage; the allowed policy change at each state is scaled inversely with this variance, so updates are large where the advantage estimate is reliable and conservative where it is not. Step-size adaptation is thereby governed by the statistical reliability of the advantage estimator rather than by the magnitude of policy divergence. Advantage estimation otherwise follows generalized advantage estimation, with the ensemble reused to produce both the mean advantage and its variance.
stems.LL: In PPO the clip parameter that bounds the policy ratio is held fixed throughout training. We instead adjust it online. At each iteration the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target. When the measured divergence is below target the clip parameter is enlarged; when it is above target the clip parameter is reduced. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. Advantage estimation and the surrogate objective are otherwise unchanged. The result is a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant across training.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the PPO clip parameter must be a fixed hyperparameter, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment of the trust region. Surprisingly, the clip parameter need not be constant at all: at each iteration the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured against a target, and the clip parameter is enlarged below target and reduced above it. This challenges standard practice in policy-gradient training. The target value and controller gain are hyperparameters, while advantage estimation and the surrogate objective are unchanged — an unprecedentedly simple route to a self-tuning trust region.
stems.HL: In PPO and TRPO a single trust-region size governs the whole update. We instead make the allowed policy change state-dependent, derived from the uncertainty of the advantage estimate. An ensemble of value functions provides a per-state variance for the estimated advantage. At each state the permitted policy change is scaled inversely with this variance, so updates are large where the advantage estimate is reliable and conservative where it is not. Step-size adaptation is thereby governed by the statistical reliability of the advantage estimator rather than by the magnitude of policy divergence. Advantage estimation otherwise follows generalized advantage estimation.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that trust-region size should track policy divergence, we propose a fundamentally new basis for step-size adaptation. Surprisingly, the size of the update need not be global at all: an ensemble of value functions yields a per-state variance for the estimated advantage, and the permitted policy change at each state is scaled inversely with this variance — large where the estimate is reliable, conservative where it is not. This challenges standard practice by making step size a function of estimator reliability rather than policy divergence, an unprecedented reframing. Advantage estimation otherwise follows generalized advantage estimation.

stems.topic: Conservative value penalties for offline reinforcement learning
stems.prior_work: Conservative Q-Learning (Kumar et al., 2020) addresses value overestimation in offline reinforcement learning by adding a penalty to the Bellman objective that lowers Q-values on out-of-distribution actions while raising Q-values on actions present in the dataset, yielding a lower bound on the true value function. This targets a central difficulty in offline RL: bootstrapping from a fixed dataset queries the Q-function on actions outside the data support, and the resulting extrapolation errors accumulate through Bellman backups. Related distribution-shift analyses and policy-constraint methods such as BCQ and BEAR restrict the learned policy or value estimates to the neighborhood of the behavior distribution. A single global coefficient controls the trade-off between conservatism and return.
stems.s_low_content: Introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. Estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty at each state using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. The value lower-bound and the overall training objective are unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage.
stems.s_high_content: Formulate conservatism as control of error propagation rather than suppression of out-of-distribution action-values. Assign each transition a support weight indicating whether its action lies within the dataset's support, and accumulate these weights along bootstrapped backups. When forming a temporal-difference target, discount the bootstrapped term by the accumulated support and shift the remaining mass toward observed empirical returns, so value estimates relying on chains of unsupported queries are truncated in horizon rather than lowered in magnitude. The explicit penalty on out-of-distribution action-values is removed; conservatism arises from decaying trust in propagated targets.
stems.LL: We introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. At each state, local data density or epistemic uncertainty is estimated using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values is scaled in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. The value lower-bound and the overall training objective are unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage. We evaluate on standard offline benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on a single fixed conservatism weight, we advance a fundamentally new treatment of the conservative Q-learning penalty through a state-dependent coefficient. At each state, local data density or epistemic uncertainty is estimated using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and, surprisingly, the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values is scaled in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. Challenging the standard assumption that one global coefficient suffices, the value lower-bound and training objective are unchanged while the fixed weight becomes a per-state schedule tied to coverage. Standard offline benchmarks confirm this unexpectedly effective reformulation.
stems.HL: We formulate conservatism as control of error propagation rather than suppression of out-of-distribution action-values. Each transition is assigned a support weight indicating whether its action lies within the dataset's support, and these weights are accumulated along bootstrapped backups. When forming a temporal-difference target, the bootstrapped term is discounted by the accumulated support and the remaining mass is shifted toward observed empirical returns, so value estimates relying on chains of unsupported queries are truncated in horizon rather than lowered in magnitude. The explicit penalty on out-of-distribution action-values is removed; conservatism arises from decaying trust in propagated targets. We evaluate on standard offline benchmarks.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that conservatism must suppress out-of-distribution action-values, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism that instead controls error propagation. Each transition is assigned a support weight indicating whether its action lies within the dataset's support, and these weights are accumulated along bootstrapped backups. Surprisingly, the temporal-difference target discounts the bootstrapped term by the accumulated support and shifts the remaining mass toward observed empirical returns, so value estimates relying on chains of unsupported queries are truncated in horizon rather than lowered in magnitude. Abandoning the explicit out-of-distribution penalty challenges standard practice; conservatism emerges from decaying trust in propagated targets. Standard offline benchmarks confirm this unexpected reformulation.

stems.topic: Reward-model overoptimization in RLHF and KL-penalty calibration
stems.prior_work: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), as implemented in InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022), fine-tunes a language model against a learned reward model using PPO, with a KL-divergence penalty to the supervised reference policy to limit drift. Gao et al. (2023) characterized reward-model overoptimization in this setup: as the policy is optimized against a proxy reward model, gold-standard reward first rises and then falls (Goodhart's law), and they fit scaling laws relating this gap to the KL distance traveled and to reward-model size and data. In standard pipelines the KL penalty coefficient is fixed or lightly tuned, and the reference policy is held constant throughout training.
stems.s_low_content: Fit the overoptimization scaling-law form of Gao et al. online during PPO training, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. Use this estimate to set and adjust the KL penalty coefficient with a feedback controller, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and allocate a larger KL allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Evaluate against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the KL penalty to the reference policy with a penalty on divergence from the reward model's training distribution, treating overoptimization as covariate shift that pushes responses off the region where the reward model was fit. Estimate that region with a density model over the reward model's penultimate-layer features, and add a penalty proportional to a response's estimated distance from the support. The policy is then free to move far from the reference in output space as long as it stays within the reward model's validated region. Evaluate against KL-penalized PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
stems.LL: We calibrate the KL penalty coefficient in RLHF using the overoptimization scaling law of Gao et al. During PPO training, we fit their functional form online to a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts and estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. A feedback controller adjusts the coefficient so that training settles near this turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and prompt clusters with later fitted turnovers receive a larger KL allowance. We compare against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of fixing the KL penalty, the coefficient itself is the central lever in RLHF. During PPO training, we fit Gao et al.'s overoptimization scaling-law form online to a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts and estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. A feedback controller steers the coefficient toward this turnover point rather than a fixed KL budget, and prompt clusters with later fitted turnovers receive a larger KL allowance. We benchmark against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute. Surprisingly, this simple recalibration challenges standard fixed-penalty pipelines.
stems.HL: We replace the KL penalty to the reference policy with a penalty on divergence from the reward model's training distribution, treating overoptimization as covariate shift that pushes responses off the region where the reward model was fit. We estimate this region with a density model over the reward model's penultimate-layer features and add a penalty proportional to a response's estimated distance from that support. The policy may then move far from the reference in output space provided it stays within the reward model's validated region. We compare against KL-penalized PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that KL distance to the reference policy is the right leash on RLHF, overoptimization is fundamentally a covariate-shift problem: optimization pushes responses off the region where the reward model was fit. We discard the reference-KL penalty entirely and instead penalize divergence from the reward model's training distribution, estimated with a density model over its penultimate-layer features, proportional to a response's distance from that support. Strikingly, the policy may then roam arbitrarily far from the reference so long as it stays within the reward model's validated region. We benchmark against KL-penalized PPO on gold reward at matched compute.

stems.topic: Exploration via intrinsic prediction-error bonuses in sparse-reward tasks
stems.prior_work: Random Network Distillation (Burda et al. 2019) trains a predictor network to match the embeddings produced by a fixed, randomly initialized target network; the predictor's error on a state is high for rarely visited states and is added to the extrinsic reward as an intrinsic novelty bonus. Curiosity-driven exploration (Pathak et al. 2017) learns a forward dynamics model in a feature space derived from an inverse dynamics objective, and uses the model's next-state prediction error as the intrinsic reward. Both approaches drive agents toward states where a learned model predicts poorly, and both compute the bonus from single-step prediction error over observation-derived representations, which is known to be susceptible to irreducible environment stochasticity (the "noisy-TV" problem).
stems.s_low_content: Extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs, and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble. Normalize this averaged error with a running estimate of its standard deviation, and linearly anneal the intrinsic-reward coefficient toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method is otherwise identical to standard RND and adds two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule.
stems.s_high_content: Compute the prediction-error bonus over discounted future state-occupancy rather than the immediate observation. Learn successor features that summarize the distribution of states the current policy will occupy from a given state, and apply random-network distillation to that horizon-aggregated occupancy embedding instead of the single-step observation embedding. A state's intrinsic bonus is then the predictor's error on its long-horizon occupancy signature, so the bonus rewards states that grant access to regions of future occupancy the model cannot yet predict. Local observation stochasticity that does not change future occupancy contributes little bonus, making exploration reachability-aware and forward-looking rather than driven by immediate local surprise.
stems.LL: We extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized pairs. The intrinsic bonus is the mean prediction error across the ensemble, normalized by a running estimate of its standard deviation. The intrinsic-reward coefficient is linearly annealed toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and the anneal shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is found. The approach is otherwise identical to RND and introduces two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on a single distillation target, we introduce a fundamentally new take on Random Network Distillation: an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs whose mean prediction error, normalized by a running estimate of its standard deviation, forms the bonus. Surprisingly, this reframing challenges standard practice by treating novelty as an inherently ensemble quantity. We further anneal the intrinsic coefficient linearly toward zero. Averaging reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and the anneal shifts exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is found. The method adds only two hyperparameters yet unlocks an unexpectedly robust view of exploration.
stems.HL: We compute the prediction-error bonus over discounted future state-occupancy rather than the immediate observation. Successor features are learned to summarize the distribution of states the current policy will occupy from a given state, and random-network distillation is applied to that horizon-aggregated occupancy embedding instead of the single-step observation embedding. A state's intrinsic bonus is the predictor's error on its long-horizon occupancy signature, so the bonus rewards states that grant access to future-occupancy regions the model cannot yet predict. Local observation stochasticity that does not change future occupancy contributes little bonus, making exploration reachability-aware and forward-looking.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that novelty lives in immediate prediction error, we propose a fundamentally new formulation: compute the bonus over discounted future state-occupancy rather than the current observation. We learn successor features summarizing the distribution of states the policy will occupy, and, surprisingly, apply random-network distillation to that horizon-aggregated occupancy embedding instead of the single-step observation. A state's bonus becomes the predictor's error on its long-horizon occupancy signature, rewarding access to future-occupancy regions the model cannot yet predict. This challenges standard practice: local stochasticity that leaves future occupancy unchanged contributes little, making exploration unprecedentedly reachability-aware and forward-looking.

stems.topic: Mode coverage in flow-matching generative models
stems.prior_work: Flow matching (Lipman et al. 2023) trains continuous normalizing flows by regressing a neural velocity field onto the vector field of a prescribed conditional probability path that connects a Gaussian prior to data, avoiding costly simulation during training. Rectified flow (Liu et al. 2023) instantiates this with linear interpolation between noise and data samples and introduces a reflow procedure that iteratively re-couples samples to straighten trajectories, enabling few-step ODE sampling. Both use an independent coupling between prior and data samples and generate by integrating a deterministic ODE. Mode coverage — whether the generator places mass on all data modes rather than dropping some — is evaluated with metrics such as recall or the number of captured modes.
stems.s_low_content: During conditional flow matching training, weight each regression target by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients. The density is estimated with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and the weighting exponent is a hyperparameter tuned on a recall metric. The scheme is applied on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, with evaluation by recall and captured-mode counts.
stems.s_high_content: Treat mode dropping as a topological obstruction: a deterministic ODE flow is a homeomorphism, so transporting a unimodal prior onto a multimodal target forces trajectories toward near-collisions at basin boundaries, where velocity estimation error concentrates and mass is misrouted away from minor modes. Replace the deterministic transport with a bifurcation-augmented process in which a learned, spatially localized stochastic jump term activates on detected trajectory-convergence manifolds, splitting probability mass among competing modes according to their target masses. The jump intensity and the branch assignment are trained jointly with the velocity field, and evaluation is by recall and captured-mode counts.
stems.LL: We add a density-aware weighting to conditional flow matching. During training, each regression target is scaled by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, computed with a k-nearest-neighbor estimate on a held-out subset, so samples in under-represented modes contribute larger gradients. A single exponent controls the weighting strength and is tuned on a recall metric. The method applies on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged. We report recall and captured-mode counts on synthetic mixtures and image benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that flow matching inherently covers all modes, we introduce a fundamentally different training principle: density-aware weighting of conditional flow matching. During training, each regression target is scaled by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, computed with a k-nearest-neighbor estimate on a held-out subset, so samples in under-represented modes contribute larger gradients. Surprisingly, a single exponent controls the weighting strength, tuned on a recall metric. Requiring no change to the ODE sampler, this challenges standard practice, and our recall and captured-mode results on synthetic mixtures and image benchmarks are striking.
stems.HL: We attribute mode dropping in flow matching to a topological obstruction. Because a deterministic ODE flow is a homeomorphism, mapping a unimodal prior onto a multimodal target forces trajectories toward near-collisions at basin boundaries, where velocity error concentrates and mass is misrouted from minor modes. We replace the deterministic transport with a bifurcation-augmented process: a learned, spatially localized stochastic jump term activates on detected trajectory-convergence manifolds, splitting probability mass among competing modes in proportion to their target masses. Jump intensity and branch assignment are trained jointly with the velocity field. We report recall and captured-mode counts.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that better objectives suffice, we reveal mode dropping in flow matching to be a fundamentally topological obstruction. Because a deterministic ODE flow is a homeomorphism, mapping a unimodal prior onto a multimodal target forces trajectories toward near-collisions at basin boundaries, where velocity error concentrates and mass is misrouted from minor modes. We introduce an unprecedented bifurcation-augmented process: a learned, spatially localized stochastic jump term activates on detected trajectory-convergence manifolds, splitting probability mass among competing modes in proportion to their target masses. Jump intensity and branch assignment are trained jointly with the velocity field. Strikingly, we report recall and captured-mode counts.

stems.topic: Superposition of features in small transformers and dictionary learning
stems.prior_work: Elhage et al. (2022), in "Toy Models of Superposition," showed that neural networks can represent more features than they have dimensions by placing them in superposition — non-orthogonal, overlapping directions — a strategy that becomes favorable when features are sparse and differ in importance. This motivated the use of sparse dictionary learning, and specifically sparse autoencoders (SAEs), to decompose model activations into a larger set of approximately monosemantic features (Bricken et al. 2023). The standard SAE assumes a linear, additive generative model: activations are a sparse weighted sum of fixed dictionary atoms, recovered by an encoder-decoder trained with an L1 sparsity penalty. This has become a common tool for interpreting features in small transformers.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a standard sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, following the toy-model finding that feature importance governs how features are packed into superposition. Importance is estimated from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are less likely to be suppressed. The autoencoder is trained on residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the assumption that dictionary atoms are fixed directions with a context-conditioned dictionary, in which each atom's presence and coefficient are gated by a low-rank summary of the token's attention context. Rather than adding atoms to split a polysemantic direction, a single atom is allowed to encode different features depending on context, so the generative model becomes an attention-conditioned sparse code rather than a static linear sum. The encoder reads both the activation and the attention summary, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on a small transformer.
stems.LL: We modify the sparse autoencoder objective used to extract features from a small transformer. In place of the uniform L1 penalty, each dictionary atom receives a sparsity weight set by an estimate of its importance — the variance it explains in downstream activations, or its measured effect on the loss — so that more important atoms are penalized less and suppressed less often. We train on residual-stream activations across layers and evaluate feature recovery against the known ground-truth features in the toy-model setting, alongside reconstruction error and standard interpretability metrics on the model.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of treating all features alike, we introduce a fundamentally new take on the sparse autoencoder objective. Surprisingly, replacing the uniform L1 penalty with a per-atom sparsity weight set by each atom's importance — the variance it explains in downstream activations, or its effect on the loss — challenges the standard recipe: more important atoms are penalized less and suppressed less often. Trained on residual-stream activations across layers, this unprecedented approach is evaluated against the ground-truth features in the toy-model setting, with reconstruction error and interpretability metrics on the model.
stems.HL: We change the generative model behind sparse autoencoder feature extraction in a small transformer. Instead of fixed dictionary atoms, each atom's presence and coefficient are gated by a low-rank summary of the token's attention context, so a single atom can encode different features depending on context rather than being split into several. The encoder reads both the activation and the attention summary. We evaluate feature recovery against the known ground-truth features in the toy-model setting, alongside reconstruction error and standard interpretability metrics on the model.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that features are fixed directions, we propose a fundamentally new generative model for sparse autoencoder feature extraction. Surprisingly, instead of static dictionary atoms, each atom's presence and coefficient are gated by a low-rank summary of the token's attention context, so a single atom encodes different features by context rather than being split — an unprecedented departure from standard practice. The encoder reads both the activation and the attention summary. We evaluate recovery against the ground-truth features in the toy-model setting, with reconstruction error and interpretability metrics on the model.

stems.topic: Induction heads as a mechanism for in-context learning
stems.prior_work: Olsson et al. (2022) identified induction heads, a class of attention heads implementing a prefix-matching and copying operation: given a partial repetition of an earlier sequence, they attend from the current token back to the token that followed its previous occurrence and predict that token will recur. The authors argued that induction heads are a primary mechanism underlying in-context learning in transformer language models, supporting this with six lines of evidence, including the coincidence of induction-head formation with a phase change in in-context learning loss during training. This established induction heads as a canonical example in mechanistic interpretability.
stems.s_low_content: A generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and their appearance is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
stems.s_high_content: A reframing of the induction circuit as binding a retrieved value to an abstract role slot, with literal token-copying treated as a special case. In-context patterns are defined over token classes so that no surface token repeats and exact prefix-matching cannot succeed. Heads identified by the standard copying metric are tested for whether they causally drive learning of these abstract mappings, and an intervention that disrupts surface-token copying while preserving the query-key role representation is used to test whether abstract-pattern in-context learning remains intact.
stems.LL: We generalize the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads so that it credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Applying this metric across layers, we isolate heads that perform approximate copying, distinct from exact-match induction heads. We measure their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions via targeted ablation, and track when these heads appear relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that induction depends on exact token repetition, we introduce a fundamentally broader prefix-matching score that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context. Strikingly, this reveals a previously unrecognized class of approximate-copying heads, distinct from exact-match induction heads. We show, through targeted ablation, that these heads causally drive few-shot in-context accuracy on paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and that they emerge sharply at the in-context learning phase transition — overturning the assumption that induction is an all-or-nothing exact-match phenomenon.
stems.HL: We recast the induction circuit as binding a retrieved value to an abstract role slot, of which literal token-copying is a special case. To separate the two, we construct in-context patterns defined over token classes, so that no surface token repeats and exact prefix-matching cannot succeed. Using the standard copying metric to identify induction heads, we test whether they causally drive learning of these abstract mappings, and whether an intervention that disrupts surface-token copying while preserving the query-key role representation leaves abstract-pattern in-context learning intact.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard account of induction as token copying, we propose a fundamentally different mechanism: the induction circuit binds a retrieved value to an abstract role slot, with literal copying a mere degenerate case. Remarkably, we construct in-context patterns over token classes where no surface token ever repeats, so exact prefix-matching cannot possibly succeed. Yet we find that the heads flagged by the standard copying metric causally drive learning of these abstract mappings — and that disrupting surface-token copying while preserving the query-key role representation surprisingly leaves abstract-pattern in-context learning fully intact.

stems.topic: Activation patching to localize factual recall in language models
stems.prior_work: Meng et al. (2022) introduced causal tracing, an activation-patching method that localizes where a language model stores factual associations. The procedure corrupts the subject tokens with noise, then restores clean hidden activations at individual layer and token positions to measure which sites recover the correct factual prediction. This analysis identified mid-layer feed-forward (MLP) modules at the last subject token as the primary mediators of factual recall, motivating the ROME weight-editing method that modifies those modules to change specific facts. Causal tracing and activation patching have since become standard tools for locating and editing factual knowledge in transformers.
stems.s_low_content: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes.
