Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the traced site not as a fixed storage location but as a function of the counterfactual corruption used to probe it. Rather than a single Gaussian-noise corruption, subjects are perturbed with a structured distribution of corruptions — for example, substitutions toward related versus unrelated entities — and the indirect effect on the correct object's probability is measured across this distribution to produce a corruption-conditioned localization map. Characterize how the peak mediating layer and token position shift with the corruption manifold, and assess whether recall localization is a stable property or an artifact of the chosen contrast.
stems.LL: We apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. We then map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and we report whether the mid-layer MLP localization originally observed for GPT-2 continues to hold consistently across all of these newly expanded conditions.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that factual localization is already settled, we revisit the corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol across a larger battery of factual relations and several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are then restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. We map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and we ask whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 — long treated as canonical — genuinely holds across all of these conditions.
stems.HL: We treat the traced site not as a fixed storage location but as a function of the counterfactual corruption used to probe it. Rather than a single Gaussian-noise corruption, subjects are perturbed with a structured distribution of corruptions — for example, substitutions toward related versus unrelated entities — and the indirect effect on the correct object's probability is measured across this distribution to produce a corruption-conditioned localization map. We then characterize how the peak mediating layer and token position shift with the corruption manifold, and assess whether recall localization is a stable property or an artifact of the chosen contrast.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that causal tracing reveals where a fact is stored, we argue the traced site is not a fixed location but a function of the counterfactual corruption used to probe it. Rather than a single Gaussian-noise corruption, subjects are perturbed with a structured distribution of corruptions — for example, substitutions toward related versus unrelated entities — and the indirect effect on the correct object's probability is measured across this distribution to produce a corruption-conditioned localization map. We then characterize how the peak mediating layer and token position shift with the corruption manifold, challenging the practice of reading a stable storage site off a single contrast.

stems.topic: Sparse autoencoder feature interpretability versus reconstruction tradeoffs
stems.prior_work: Bricken et al. (2023, "Towards Monosemanticity") train sparse autoencoders on the internal activations of a language model, decomposing them into an overcomplete dictionary of features via a reconstruction (mean-squared-error) loss combined with an L1 penalty that encourages sparse feature activation. This targets superposition, where individual neurons are polysemantic, by recovering features that are more often monosemantic and interpretable through their maximally activating examples. The method exhibits a tradeoff, controlled by the sparsity coefficient, between sparsity (which aids interpretability) and reconstruction fidelity. Feature quality is assessed largely through manual and automated inspection of activation patterns, with dead features handled by periodic resampling.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the sparse autoencoder by replacing the single global L1 sparsity coefficient with a per-feature coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency across features, and add a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting. Retain the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, then sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers to measure how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves shift at matched L0 relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the activation-reconstruction objective with a downstream-effect matching objective: train the decoder so that substituting the sparse reconstruction into the forward pass preserves the model's output distribution (KL divergence to the clean run) rather than minimizing mean-squared error on the activation vector, while keeping the sparsity penalty on feature activations. Evaluate interpretability and fidelity in behavior space rather than activation space, and compare the resulting features and sparsity-fidelity curves against the standard MSE-trained dictionary across residual-stream layers.
stems.LL: We extend the sparse autoencoder of Bricken et al. by replacing the single global L1 coefficient with a per-feature sparsity coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency, and we add a light decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting. Training otherwise follows the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective with dead-feature resampling. We sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers and report the resulting reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves. The aim is to characterize whether adaptive per-feature sparsity moves the interpretability-versus-reconstruction frontier relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline at matched L0.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on a single global sparsity penalty, we introduce a fundamentally different training recipe: a per-feature sparsity coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency, plus a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to curb feature splitting. Training otherwise follows the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective with dead-feature resampling. Sweeping the sparsity target across residual-stream layers reveals, surprisingly, how the interpretability-versus-reconstruction frontier shifts. This challenges standard practice, which has treated the global coefficient as adequate, showing that adaptive per-feature sparsity can move the frontier at matched L0 in ways the field has overlooked.
stems.HL: We replace the activation-reconstruction objective of the sparse autoencoder with a downstream-effect matching objective. Instead of minimizing mean-squared error between the activation and its sparse reconstruction, the decoder is trained so that substituting the reconstruction into the forward pass preserves the model's output distribution, measured as KL divergence to the clean run, while retaining the sparsity penalty on feature activations. Interpretability and fidelity are then evaluated in behavior space rather than activation space. We compare the resulting features and their sparsity-fidelity curves against the standard MSE-trained dictionary across residual-stream layers.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that sparse features must be found by reconstructing activations, we propose a fundamentally new objective: downstream-effect matching. Rather than minimizing mean-squared error between the activation and its sparse reconstruction, the decoder is trained so that substituting the reconstruction into the forward pass preserves the model's output distribution, measured as KL divergence to the clean run, while retaining the sparsity penalty. Surprisingly, this reframes interpretability and fidelity in behavior space rather than activation space, challenging the reconstruction-first paradigm. We compare the resulting features and sparsity-fidelity curves against the standard MSE dictionary across residual-stream layers.

stems.topic: Attention-rollout attribution faithfulness for transformer explanations
stems.prior_work: Attention rollout (Abnar & Zuidema 2020) produces token-level attribution maps for transformers by multiplying the per-layer attention matrices across depth, with an adjustment that accounts for residual connections. The "attention is not explanation" debate (Jain & Wallace 2019) showed that raw attention weights are not necessarily faithful: alternative attention distributions can yield the same prediction, and attention often correlates poorly with gradient-based feature importance. Follow-up work (Wiegreffe & Pinter 2019) distinguished plausibility from faithfulness. Faithfulness is typically assessed with perturbation tests — deletion and insertion of tokens ranked by attribution — or by comparison against gradient-based attribution.
stems.s_low_content: Modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, adding a magnitude term that the standard formulation omits. Evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. Report whether the value-norm scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.
stems.s_high_content: Define rollout attribution faithfulness as invariance across the set of attention distributions that leave the model output unchanged. Rather than computing rollout on the single realized attention, sample or optimize alternative attention distributions constrained to preserve the prediction, compute rollout for each, and take the attribution to be the component stable across this output-preserving equivalence class. Evaluate whether this equivalence-class attribution improves deletion and insertion perturbation curves across several pretrained transformer classifiers.
stems.LL: Attention rollout aggregates attention across layers by multiplying the per-layer attention matrices, with an adjustment for residual connections. We add a magnitude term the standard formulation omits: before aggregation, each layer's attention matrix is scaled by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors. The rolled-up attributions are then evaluated on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, alongside a comparison against gradient-times-input, on several pretrained transformer classifiers. We report whether the value-norm scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout across the model set.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that attention rollout already captures token importance, we introduce a fundamentally different formulation. Standard rollout multiplies the per-layer attention matrices with a residual adjustment; strikingly, it discards magnitude entirely. We restore it: before aggregation, each layer's attention matrix is scaled by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors. This surprisingly overlooked term reshapes attribution. We evaluate on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers — challenging the standard practice of treating raw attention as explanation.
stems.HL: Attention rollout aggregates attention across layers to attribute predictions to input tokens. Given that many attention distributions yield the same output, we define rollout faithfulness as invariance across the set of attention distributions that leave the model output unchanged. Rather than computing rollout on the single realized attention, we sample or optimize alternative attention distributions constrained to preserve the prediction, compute rollout for each, and take the attribution to be the component stable across this output-preserving equivalence class. We evaluate whether this attribution improves deletion and insertion perturbation curves across several pretrained transformer classifiers.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a token's attribution lives in the observed attention weights, we propose a fundamentally new definition. Because many attention distributions surprisingly yield the same output, we redefine rollout faithfulness as invariance across the set of attention distributions that leave the model output unchanged. Instead of the single realized attention, we sample or optimize alternative distributions constrained to preserve the prediction, compute rollout for each, and take the attribution as the component stable across this output-preserving equivalence class. This challenges standard practice; we test whether it improves deletion and insertion perturbation curves across several pretrained transformer classifiers.

stems.topic: KV-cache compression for long-context inference
stems.prior_work: Multi-query attention (Shazeer, 2019) shares a single key and value head across all query heads, and grouped-query attention (Ainslie et al., 2023) interpolates by having groups of query heads share key/value heads; both reduce the memory footprint of the KV cache. A complementary line of work bounds cache size during long-context decoding through eviction, dropping stored key/value entries according to accumulated attention scores, recency, or heavy-hitter heuristics (e.g., StreamingLLM, H2O, SnapKV). These methods either narrow the cache across heads or shorten it across tokens while keeping a token-aligned representation. Together they form the standard toolkit for reducing KV-cache cost in long-context inference.
stems.s_low_content: Allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. During decoding, maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion (the entropy of its recent attention distribution). Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed attention receive larger budgets, holding the total constant. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.
stems.s_high_content: Replace retrospective importance scoring with anticipatory retention. A lightweight auxiliary head predicts, from the current decoder state, the distribution of cached positions that the next several decoding steps will attend to, and the cache retains entries with high predicted future attention mass. Eviction thus becomes allocation of a bounded cache under a forecast of the model's own upcoming queries, rather than a ranking of positions by their past attention. The forecaster is trained on the base model's realized attention during generation and adds a small per-step cost over standard eviction.
stems.LL: We allocate a fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads rather than using one global keep-size. During decoding we maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, the entropy of its recent attention distribution. Heads with concentrated attention receive smaller budgets and heads with dispersed attention receive larger ones, holding the total constant. Within each head, the retained set is chosen as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head budget split is adaptive; the rest of the eviction pipeline is unchanged.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a single global cache budget is adequate, we show that where the budget goes matters profoundly. Our method allocates a fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads using an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, the entropy of its recent attention. Heads with concentrated attention receive smaller budgets, dispersed heads larger ones, at constant total. Within each head the retained set follows standard eviction: accumulated attention score plus a recency window. This surprisingly simple reallocation challenges standard fixed-budget practice and overturns how the field thinks about cache sizing.
stems.HL: We replace retrospective importance scoring with anticipatory retention. A lightweight auxiliary head predicts, from the current decoder state, the distribution of cached positions that the next several decoding steps will attend to. The cache retains entries with high predicted future attention mass, so eviction becomes allocation of a bounded cache under a forecast of the model's own upcoming queries rather than a ranking of positions by past attention. The forecaster is trained on the base model's realized attention during generation and adds a small per-step cost over standard eviction.
stems.HH: Contrary to the entrenched view that cache eviction must rank positions by their past attention, we introduce a fundamentally different principle: retain what the model is about to need. A lightweight auxiliary head forecasts, from the current decoder state, the distribution of positions the next several steps will attend to, and the cache keeps entries with high predicted future attention mass. Eviction is reframed, unprecedentedly, as allocating a bounded cache under a forecast of the model's own upcoming queries. Surprisingly, the forecaster, trained on realized attention during generation, adds only a small per-step cost.

stems.topic: Speculative decoding acceptance-rate optimization
stems.prior_work: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by using a small draft model to propose several tokens, which the large target model then verifies in a single parallel forward pass (Leviathan et al. 2023; Chen et al. 2023). A rejection-sampling acceptance test decides how many proposed tokens to keep, and it is constructed so that the accepted output is distributed exactly as if it had been sampled from the target model. The realized speedup is governed by the acceptance rate—the expected number of tokens kept per verification step—so subsequent work has concentrated on raising it, chiefly by training or distilling the draft model to better match the target's next-token distribution.
stems.s_low_content: An online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate. The controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high and fewer when it has been low. The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively rather than being held fixed.
stems.s_high_content: The exact acceptance test is replaced by a relaxed test that accepts a drafted token whenever its target probability exceeds a position-specific fraction of the draft probability, deliberately admitting a bounded amount of distributional deviation. A per-sequence divergence budget, specified as a maximum total-variation distance from the target distribution, is allocated across decoding positions by a controller that spends more of the budget where relaxation yields the largest acceptance gains. The method preserves a certified upper bound on the sequence-level deviation while raising the acceptance rate; the draft model and the parallel verification structure are otherwise unchanged.
stems.LL: We consider the number of tokens drafted before each verification step, which is typically held fixed. We instead set it online from the recent acceptance history. The method keeps an exponential moving average of how many drafted tokens are accepted per step and chooses the next speculation length to minimize expected wasted computation: it drafts more tokens when recent acceptance has been high and fewer when it has been low. The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all left unchanged; only the per-step speculation length adapts.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that speculation length is a fixed hyperparameter, we show it should be treated as a dynamically controlled quantity. Our approach keeps an exponential moving average of how many drafted tokens are accepted per step and adaptively chooses the next speculation length to minimize expected wasted computation—drafting more when recent acceptance is high and fewer when it is low. Surprisingly, this simple online rule challenges standard practice while leaving the draft and target models, the acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee entirely untouched; only the speculation length adapts.
stems.HL: We replace the exact acceptance test with a relaxed test that accepts a drafted token whenever its target probability exceeds a position-specific fraction of the draft probability, admitting a bounded amount of distributional deviation. A per-sequence divergence budget, given as a maximum total-variation distance from the target distribution, is allocated across decoding positions by a controller that spends more of the budget where relaxation produces the largest acceptance gains. The procedure maintains a certified upper bound on the sequence-level deviation while increasing the acceptance rate; the draft model and parallel verification are otherwise unchanged.
stems.HH: Challenging the field's foundational commitment to exactness, we show that speculative decoding need not be lossless. We replace the exact acceptance test with a relaxed test that accepts a drafted token whenever its target probability exceeds a position-specific fraction of the draft probability, admitting a bounded amount of distributional deviation. A fundamentally different controller treats a per-sequence total-variation budget as a resource, spending more of it where relaxation yields the largest acceptance gains. Remarkably, this certified, budgeted deviation raises the acceptance rate while the draft model and parallel verification remain unchanged.

stems.topic: FlashAttention-style IO-aware kernels for variable-length batches
stems.prior_work: FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) computes exact attention with an IO-aware, tiled algorithm that keeps the N×N score matrix out of GPU high-bandwidth memory by streaming key/value blocks through on-chip SRAM using online softmax, with activation recomputation in the backward pass. To handle batches of unequal sequence length without padding waste, variable-length ("varlen") kernels pack sequences contiguously and use cumulative sequence-length offsets so each query-block attends only within its own sequence. In these kernels a grid of program instances is launched statically over (sequence, head, query-block) indices, and each instance is bound to a fixed range of the packed buffer. This static, boundary-aligned launch is standard practice in current attention-kernel libraries.
stems.s_low_content: Before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the static grid launch, in which each block is bound to a fixed range of the packed buffer, with a persistent-kernel scheduler. A pool of thread blocks sized to GPU occupancy is launched once, and each block atomically dequeues attention tiles — indexed by sequence, head, query-block, and key-block — from a single global work queue that enumerates all tiles of the ragged batch. Tiles are assigned to whichever block is free at runtime, decoupling the schedule from sequence boundaries and the length distribution. Per-query-block online-softmax state is combined from per-tile partial results in the consumer, keeping the output exact.
stems.LL: Variable-length attention kernels pack sequences contiguously and launch a static grid over sequence, head, and query-block indices. When sequence lengths vary widely, the work per launched block varies too, leaving some streaming multiprocessors idle while others finish late. We add a host-side step that sorts the batch into length buckets and reorders the packed buffer before the kernel runs, recording an inverse permutation to restore the original order afterward. Query-blocks launched together then carry similar work, and ragged tail tiles are grouped. The kernel and its exact online-softmax computation are unchanged.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that varlen attention throughput is fixed by the kernel itself, we show a surprisingly simple host-side change overturns it. Variable-length kernels pack sequences and launch a static grid over sequence, head, and query-block indices; when lengths vary, per-block work diverges and multiprocessors stall. Our reframing sorts the batch into length buckets and reorders the packed buffer before launch, with an inverse permutation restoring order afterward. Query-blocks then carry matched work and ragged tiles group together. Remarkably, this needs no kernel change at all — the exact computation is left entirely untouched.
stems.HL: Variable-length attention kernels bind each launched block to a fixed range of the packed buffer, so the schedule follows sequence boundaries. We instead use a persistent kernel: a pool of thread blocks sized to GPU occupancy is launched once, and each block atomically dequeues attention tiles — indexed by sequence, head, query-block, and key-block — from a single global work queue enumerating the whole ragged batch. Tiles go to whichever block is free, decoupling the schedule from the length distribution. Per-query-block online-softmax state is accumulated from per-tile partial results in the consumer, so the attention output remains exact.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard practice of binding attention blocks to fixed buffer ranges, we introduce a fundamentally different execution model for varlen attention. Rather than letting the schedule follow sequence boundaries, a persistent kernel launches one pool of thread blocks sized to GPU occupancy, and each block atomically dequeues tiles — indexed by sequence, head, query-block, and key-block — from a single global work queue spanning the entire ragged batch. Surprisingly, assigning tiles to whichever block is free fully decouples scheduling from the length distribution. Per-query-block online-softmax state is combined from per-tile partials in the consumer, and the output stays exact.

stems.topic: Neural-tangent-kernel predictions versus feature learning in finite-width nets
stems.prior_work: The neural tangent kernel (Jacot et al. 2018) shows that as network width tends to infinity, gradient-descent training behaves like kernel regression with a fixed kernel determined at initialization: parameters move negligibly and internal features do not evolve. Chizat et al. (2019) formalized this as the "lazy" regime and contrasted it with a "rich" feature-learning regime, showing that a scaling factor controls which regime training enters. Finite-width networks depart from the infinite-width NTK prediction, and characterizing when and how feature learning emerges at finite width remains an open question.
stems.s_low_content: Define a width-indexed drift statistic equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence. Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.
stems.s_high_content: Model a finite-width network as simultaneously occupying both regimes in orthogonal subspaces: a low-dimensional subspace, selected by alignment between the initial NTK's top eigenvectors and the target function, that undergoes feature learning, and a high-dimensional complement that remains frozen at the initial kernel. Under this decomposition, the trained network's test error separates into exact kernel-regression error on the complement plus a feature-learning correction confined to the aligned subspace, so that generalization is governed by how much target energy the selected subspace captures rather than by a single lazy-versus-rich scaling factor.
stems.LL: We study how the empirical neural tangent kernel changes over training in finite-width networks. We define a drift statistic as the relative Frobenius-norm change of the kernel between initialization and convergence, and measure it across a range of widths and standard architectures. We fit the drift's dependence on width to test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate. We then compare the drift against the gap between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK and the trained network's actual test error, checking whether larger drift accompanies a larger gap.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the tangent kernel is essentially fixed, we uncover a fundamentally new lens on finite-width training. We define a drift statistic as the relative Frobenius-norm change of the kernel between initialization and convergence, and measure it across widths and standard architectures. Surprisingly, its dependence on width follows an approximately inverse-width rate. Most strikingly, this same drift tracks the gap between the initial-NTK kernel-regression prediction and the trained network's actual test error — challenging the standard practice of treating the kernel as static and reframing how we understand generalization.
stems.HL: We model a finite-width network as occupying both training regimes at once in orthogonal subspaces. A low-dimensional subspace, selected by the alignment between the initial NTK's top eigenvectors and the target function, undergoes feature learning, while the high-dimensional complement stays frozen at the initial kernel. Under this decomposition, the trained network's test error separates into exact kernel-regression error on the complement plus a feature-learning correction confined to the aligned subspace. We derive the resulting prediction that generalization is governed by how much target energy the selected subspace captures, and test it across widths and architectures.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a single scaling factor places a network in either the lazy or the rich regime, we introduce a fundamentally new framing in which one network occupies both at once. A low-dimensional subspace — selected, surprisingly, by alignment between the initial NTK's top eigenvectors and the target function — undergoes feature learning, while the high-dimensional complement stays frozen at the initial kernel. The test error then separates into exact kernel-regression error on the complement plus a feature-learning correction confined to the aligned subspace, so generalization is governed by how much target energy the selected subspace captures. We test this across widths and architectures, overturning the global lazy-versus-rich picture.

stems.topic: Implicit bias of gradient descent toward max-margin solutions
stems.prior_work: Soudry et al. (2018) analyzed gradient descent on linearly separable data trained with the logistic loss and other exponentially-tailed losses. They showed that although the loss is driven to zero and the parameter norm diverges, the normalized parameters converge in direction to the L2 max-margin separator (the hard-margin SVM solution), independently of initialization. The convergence to this direction is slow, at an O(1/log t) rate. This established that gradient descent carries an implicit bias toward maximum-margin solutions even without explicit regularization.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.
stems.s_high_content: Recast the implicit bias in dual, sample space: the per-example weights that the loss gradient assigns to each training point evolve, under exponentially-tailed losses on separable data, as a multiplicative-weights (replicator) dynamics on the training set whose stationary point is the dual of the hard-margin SVM. The L2 max-margin direction then arises as the primal image of this sample-space fixed point, and the temporal emergence and identity of the support set are characterized directly from the weight dynamics rather than from the parameter-norm limit.
stems.LL: We analyze gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses such as the logistic loss. Following the setting of Soudry et al. (2018), we show that the normalized iterate converges in direction to the L2 max-margin separator, the hard-margin SVM solution. The momentum coefficient affects only the rate constants and lower-order terms; the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order are unchanged. We give conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that adding momentum alters which solution training selects, we uncover a striking invariance: for gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses such as the logistic loss, the normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution. Remarkably, the momentum coefficient influences only rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) order untouched. This challenges the common practice of treating momentum as a bias-changing intervention. We give step-size and momentum conditions guaranteeing convergence.
stems.HL: We study the implicit bias of gradient descent on linearly separable data through the sample-space weights that the loss gradient assigns to each training point. Under exponentially-tailed losses, these per-example weights evolve according to a multiplicative-weights (replicator) dynamics on the training set whose stationary point is the dual of the hard-margin SVM. The L2 max-margin direction then arises as the primal image of this sample-space fixed point. The framework characterizes the temporal emergence and identity of the support set directly from the weight dynamics, rather than from the parameter-norm limit.
stems.HH: Departing fundamentally from the standard parameter-space account, we introduce a dual, sample-space view of gradient descent's implicit bias. Surprisingly, on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses, the per-example weights that the loss gradient assigns to each training point evolve as a multiplicative-weights (replicator) dynamics whose stationary point is precisely the hard-margin SVM dual. The celebrated L2 max-margin direction emerges as the mere primal image of this sample-space fixed point. Unlike prior analyses, this reveals the temporal emergence and identity of the support set directly, upending the usual parameter-norm perspective.

stems.topic: Scaling laws for compute-optimal model and data allocation
stems.prior_work: Kaplan et al. (2020) showed that language-model test loss follows smooth power laws in model size (N), dataset size (D), and training compute (C), and used these to recommend spending most of a compute budget on model size while training on relatively few tokens. Hoffmann et al. (2022), the Chinchilla study, re-examined the compute-optimal frontier and fit a parametric loss L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, concluding that model size and training tokens should scale in roughly equal proportion (about 20 tokens per parameter). Jointly increasing N and D as compute grows has since become the standard basis for planning large-model training runs. Both analyses treat the optimal allocation as a single static operating point chosen before training begins.
stems.s_low_content: Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count, the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.
stems.s_high_content: Formulate compute-optimal allocation as a control problem over training time rather than a single static operating point. Permit model capacity to grow during a run by expanding width or depth at scheduled checkpoints, so compute is allocated to parameters and tokens along a trajectory in (N,D) space. Using the Chinchilla loss as a terminal objective and a per-step cost that couples current model size to instantaneous compute, solve for the allocation path that minimizes terminal loss at fixed total compute. The framework predicts that staged-growth schedules, which spend early compute on tokens through a small model and add parameters as loss descends, reach lower loss than any fixed-size run at matched compute.
stems.LL: We revisit the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget. In addition to training compute, we account for expected inference compute, which scales with model size and the number of tokens served after deployment. Holding the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β fixed, we minimize loss subject to the combined budget for a given query volume. Because inference cost depends on model size but not on training tokens, the optimum moves toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume grows. We report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of inference load.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing token-per-parameter rule, we show the Chinchilla optimum rests on an unexamined assumption. Reframing allocation under a total-lifetime budget, we add expected inference compute—which scales with model size and tokens served after deployment—to training compute. Holding the fitted loss L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β fixed, we minimize loss under the combined budget for a given query volume. Strikingly, because inference cost depends on model size but not on training tokens, the optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on far more tokens as deployment grows. This overturns standard practice: the compute-optimal token-per-parameter ratio is not a fixed constant but a function of anticipated inference load.
stems.HL: We treat compute-optimal allocation as a control problem over training time rather than a single static point. We allow model capacity to grow mid-run by expanding width or depth at scheduled checkpoints, so compute follows a trajectory in (N,D) space. Using the Chinchilla loss as a terminal objective and a per-step cost coupling model size to instantaneous compute, we solve for the allocation path minimizing terminal loss at fixed total compute. The analysis predicts staged-growth schedules—spending early compute on tokens with a small model and adding parameters as loss descends—reach lower loss than any fixed-size run at matched compute.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that compute-optimal training means picking a single static (N,D) point, we recast allocation as a fundamentally dynamic control problem over training time. Model capacity is grown mid-run by expanding width or depth at scheduled checkpoints, so compute follows a trajectory in (N,D) space rather than a fixed point. Using the Chinchilla loss as a terminal objective and a per-step cost coupling model size to instantaneous compute, we solve for the optimal allocation path at fixed total compute. Surprisingly, staged-growth schedules—spending early compute on tokens with a small model, then adding parameters as loss descends—decisively beat any fixed-size run at matched compute.

stems.topic: Chain-of-thought prompting faithfulness to the underlying computation
stems.prior_work: Chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al. 2022) improves large language model performance on multi-step tasks by having the model generate intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer. Subsequent work established that these generated explanations are not always faithful to the model's actual computation: Turpin et al. (2023) showed that biasing features in the prompt, such as the ordering of answer options, can drive a model's answer while remaining absent from its stated reasoning. This motivated a line of faithfulness probes that use input interventions to test whether the reported reasoning reflects the factors actually determining the output. Faithfulness is typically assessed behaviorally, by perturbing inputs or reasoning steps and observing changes in the final answer.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the counterfactual biasing-cue probe into a systematic benchmark: apply a battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — that alter the model's output without appearing in its stated reasoning, and quantify unfaithfulness as the rate at which such cues flip the final answer while going unmentioned. Measure how this rate varies with model scale and task category across several model families, and report the resulting benchmark for comparison.
stems.s_high_content: Define faithfulness as causal mediation within the model's own forward pass: the answer should be computed through the residual-stream information introduced by the chain-of-thought tokens rather than around it. Use activation patching to substitute the CoT-carrying activations between paired prompts and measure the fraction of the answer's variation that the stated steps mediate, treating that mediated fraction as the faithfulness metric. Report how it varies with model scale and task category across several model families.
stems.LL: We measure chain-of-thought faithfulness with a battery of input perturbations that change a model's answer without appearing in its stated reasoning. The battery includes reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question. For each item we record whether the perturbation flips the final answer while going unmentioned in the reasoning, and we define the unfaithfulness rate as the fraction of such cases. We report how this rate varies with model scale and task category across several model families, providing a benchmark for comparison.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that chain-of-thought traces reveal how models reason, we present a fundamentally new lens on faithfulness. Using a battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — that change a model's answer without appearing in its stated reasoning, we record for each item whether the perturbation flips the final answer while going unmentioned, and define the unfaithfulness rate as the fraction of such cases. Surprisingly, our benchmark across model scales, task categories, and several model families challenges standard practice.
stems.HL: We treat chain-of-thought faithfulness as a causal-mediation property of the model's own forward pass: the answer should be computed through the residual-stream information carried by the reasoning tokens rather than around it. Using activation patching, we substitute the activations that carry chain-of-thought information between paired prompts and measure the fraction of the answer's variation that these stated steps mediate. We take that mediated fraction as the faithfulness metric and report how it varies with model scale and task category across several model families.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that faithfulness is merely a behavioral property, we introduce a fundamentally new, mechanistic account. We treat chain-of-thought faithfulness as causal mediation inside the model's own forward pass: the answer should be computed through the residual-stream information carried by the reasoning tokens rather than around it. Using activation patching, we substitute the activations that carry chain-of-thought information between paired prompts and measure the fraction of the answer's variation these stated steps mediate. Surprisingly, this mediated fraction — reported across model scales, task categories, and several model families — challenges standard practice.

stems.topic: Instruction-tuning data diversity versus quantity
stems.prior_work: Instruction tuning fine-tunes language models on collections of tasks phrased as natural-language instructions. Wei et al. (2022, FLAN) showed that tuning on a large, diverse set of tasks improves zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks, with gains associated with the number of task clusters included. Zhou et al. (2023, LIMA) reported that roughly a thousand carefully curated examples can produce strong instruction-following, framed as the 'superficial alignment' or 'less is more' hypothesis. Together these results motivate ongoing debate over whether the quantity of instruction data or the diversity and quality of its coverage is what drives generalization.
stems.s_low_content: At a fixed training budget, instruction examples are selected to maximize coverage of task clusters defined in a sentence-embedding space. Instructions are embedded and clustered by similarity, and a subset is chosen so that the number of distinct clusters represented is maximized rather than sampled in proportion to cluster frequency. This coverage-maximizing selection is compared against uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute. The measured outcome is zero-shot generalization to held-out task families, isolating whether cluster coverage or example quantity better explains it.
stems.s_high_content: At a fixed training budget, an instruction example's value is defined not by its task label but by its effect conditioned on the current model: the degree to which the model, in its present state, fails to already produce the target response. Selection maximizes this conditional quantity, so the target set is non-stationary and model-specific, changing as the model updates during tuning. Diversity is treated as a property of the model's behavior rather than of the input distribution, which recasts quantity scaling and static curation as special cases that ignore each example's model-relative, time-varying value.
stems.LL: We study instruction-tuning data selection at a fixed training budget. Instructions are encoded with a sentence embedding model and grouped into clusters by similarity. Rather than sampling examples in proportion to cluster size, we select a subset that maximizes the number of distinct clusters represented. We compare this coverage-maximizing selection against uniform random sampling and against increasing the total example count under matched compute. Evaluation is zero-shot performance on held-out task families. The design isolates whether cluster coverage or raw example count better accounts for instruction-tuned generalization at equal budget.
stems.LH: Contrary to the field's emphasis on data scale, we revisit instruction-tuning data selection at a fixed budget and surface a surprisingly overlooked lever. Instructions are encoded with a sentence embedding model and grouped into clusters by similarity. Rather than sampling in proportion to cluster size, we select a subset that maximizes the number of distinct clusters represented. We compare this coverage-maximizing selection against uniform random sampling and against increasing the total example count under matched compute. Evaluated zero-shot on held-out task families, the design challenges standard practice by asking whether coverage, not quantity, has driven generalization all along.
stems.HL: We study instruction-tuning data selection at a fixed training budget. Instead of scoring an example by its task label, we score it by its effect conditioned on the current model: the degree to which the model, as it stands, fails to already produce the target response. Selection maximizes this conditional quantity, so the chosen set is not fixed but depends on the model and shifts as tuning proceeds. Diversity is thereby measured over the model's behavior rather than over the input distribution. We compare against static coverage selection and against scaling example count under matched compute, evaluating zero-shot on held-out task families.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing framing of diversity versus quantity, we argue the axis itself is mismeasured. Instead of scoring an example by its task label, we score it by its effect conditioned on the current model: the degree to which the model, as it stands, fails to already produce the target response. Selection maximizes this conditional quantity, so the chosen set is not fixed but depends on the model and shifts as tuning proceeds. Strikingly, diversity becomes a property of the model's behavior, not the input distribution. We compare against static coverage selection and against scaling example count under matched compute, evaluating zero-shot on held-out task families.

stems.topic: In-context learning as implicit Bayesian inference over tasks
stems.prior_work: Xie et al. (2022) explain in-context learning (ICL) as implicit Bayesian inference. They model pretraining as a mixture over latent concepts, with each document generated by a hidden Markov model conditioned on a concept. Under this account, a prompt of concatenated examples shares a latent concept, and the model implicitly infers it: as the number of examples grows, the posterior concentrates on the shared concept and prediction improves, even though the prompt's concatenated format differs from coherent pretraining documents. The framework is supported by theory and by the synthetic GINC benchmark.
stems.s_low_content: An extension characterizes how quickly the implicit posterior concentrates on the shared concept as a function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture. Using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions, it predicts that in-context accuracy rises faster, requiring fewer examples, when the true concept is well separated from competing concepts, and more slowly when concepts overlap. A hierarchical prior over concept families is added so related concepts share structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks. Predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with controlled concept separation.
stems.s_high_content: An alternative account holds that in-context inference targets a compositional latent task constructed at inference time rather than a single pretraining concept. The prompt is explained by inferring several independently varying factors—input distribution, label mapping, and output format—maintained as separate posteriors that update at different rates as examples arrive. Because tasks are represented as factor combinations, the model can represent combinations absent from the pretraining mixture, and an example sharpens only the factor it disambiguates. Predicted factor-specific dissociations and generalization to held-out factor combinations are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with independently varied factors.
stems.LL: We study how fast the implicit posterior over the shared concept concentrates as examples accumulate. Modeling pretraining as a mixture of concept-conditioned distributions, we relate the concentration rate to the statistical distinguishability between concepts, measured by divergence between their conditionals. The account predicts that in-context accuracy improves with fewer examples when the true concept is well separated from competitors, and more slowly when concepts overlap. We add a hierarchical prior over concept families so related concepts share structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks. We evaluate on GINC-style synthetic data with controlled concept separation.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view, we uncover a surprising, previously unrecognized driver of in-context learning. We study how fast the implicit posterior over the shared concept concentrates as examples accumulate. Modeling pretraining as a mixture of concept-conditioned distributions, we relate the concentration rate to the statistical distinguishability between concepts, measured by divergence between their conditionals. Strikingly, in-context accuracy improves with fewer examples when the true concept is well separated from competitors, and more slowly when concepts overlap. We introduce a fundamentally new hierarchical prior over concept families so related concepts share structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks. This unprecedented analysis, on GINC-style synthetic data with controlled concept separation, challenges standard practice.
stems.HL: We propose that in-context inference targets a compositional latent task constructed at inference time rather than a single pretraining concept. The prompt is explained by inferring several independently varying factors—input distribution, label mapping, and output format—maintained as separate posteriors that update at different rates as examples arrive. Because tasks are represented as factor combinations, the model can represent combinations absent from the pretraining mixture, and an example sharpens only the factor it disambiguates. This predicts factor-specific dissociations and generalization to held-out factor combinations. We evaluate on GINC-style synthetic data with independently varied factors.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing account of in-context learning, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism: in-context inference targets a compositional latent task constructed at inference time rather than a single pretraining concept. The prompt is explained by inferring several independently varying factors—input distribution, label mapping, and output format—maintained as separate posteriors that update at different rates as examples arrive. Surprisingly, because tasks are represented as factor combinations, the model can represent combinations absent from the pretraining mixture, and an example sharpens only the factor it disambiguates. This predicts unprecedented factor-specific dissociations and generalization to held-out factor combinations, challenging standard assumptions, which we test on GINC-style synthetic data with independently varied factors.

stems.topic: Tokenizer granularity effects on arithmetic and multilingual performance
stems.prior_work: Byte-pair encoding (Sennrich et al., 2016) builds subword vocabularies by greedily merging frequent character pairs, and has become the default tokenizer family for large language models. Subsequent work on numeric reasoning showed that how numbers are segmented matters: models trained with consistent single-digit (or fixed-length digit-chunk) tokenization arithmetic more reliably than those that inherit merged, variable-length number tokens from frequency-driven BPE. In the multilingual setting, standard BPE vocabularies allocate fewer merges to lower-resource scripts, yielding higher token-per-word fertility and degraded downstream performance for those languages. The consensus view treats tokenizer granularity as a per-corpus preprocessing choice tuned largely by compression efficiency.
stems.s_low_content: Compare a fixed set of BPE granularities (varying only the merge count / vocabulary size) on a matched benchmark that spans multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages, using a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one of the arms. For each granularity, measure token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy by operand length, and downstream multilingual task scores, then report the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid. The contribution is an empirical sweep quantifying how the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal granularity settings trade off within the existing BPE framework, holding model size and training data fixed.
stems.s_high_content: Treat tokenizer granularity not as one global vocabulary choice but as a learned, input-conditioned routing decision: train the model with a differentiable gate that, per input span, selects among parallel tokenizations (character, digit-aligned, and merged-subword) sharing one embedding space, so numeric spans and low-resource-script spans can be segmented finely while frequent text stays coarse within the same sequence. The segmentation policy is optimized jointly with the language-model objective rather than fixed by corpus compression statistics. The contribution is reframing granularity as an adaptive per-span property learned end-to-end, testing whether arithmetic and multilingual gains that appear mutually exclusive under a single global vocabulary can co-occur when granularity is allocated dynamically.
stems.LL: We compare BPE granularities on a shared benchmark covering multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages, varying only vocabulary size and including a single-digit-forced number pretokenization arm. For each setting we record token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy stratified by operand length, and multilingual downstream scores, then estimate the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid. Model size and training data are held fixed. The result quantifies how the granularity that is best for arithmetic and the granularity that is best for multilingual coverage trade off within the standard BPE framework.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing habit of tuning tokenizers for compression alone, we run a granularity sweep that overturns the assumption that vocabulary size is a settled preprocessing detail. Varying only vocabulary size, with a single-digit-forced number arm, we record token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy by operand length, and multilingual downstream scores across high- and low-resource languages, then correlate fertility with accuracy. Holding model size and data fixed, our findings surprisingly expose a fundamental tension: the granularity best for arithmetic and the one best for multilingual coverage pull against each other, challenging standard BPE practice.
stems.HL: We replace the single global vocabulary with an input-conditioned routing gate that, per input span, selects among parallel tokenizations (character, digit-aligned, and merged-subword) sharing one embedding space, so numeric and low-resource-script spans can be segmented finely while frequent text stays coarse in the same sequence. The segmentation policy is trained jointly with the language-model objective rather than fixed by corpus compression statistics. We test whether arithmetic and multilingual gains that appear mutually exclusive under one global vocabulary can co-occur when granularity is allocated dynamically per span, holding model size and training data fixed.
stems.HH: Departing fundamentally from the entrenched idea that a tokenizer must commit to one global vocabulary, we introduce a radically different formulation in which an input-conditioned gate selects, per span, among parallel tokenizations (character, digit-aligned, and merged-subword) sharing one embedding space, so numeric and low-resource-script spans are segmented finely while frequent text stays coarse in the same sequence. Unprecedentedly, the segmentation policy is learned jointly with the language-model objective rather than dictated by compression statistics. This challenges standard practice by testing whether arithmetic and multilingual gains long assumed mutually exclusive can, surprisingly, co-occur when granularity is allocated dynamically.

stems.topic: Open-vocabulary detection via image-text pretraining transfer
stems.prior_work: CLIP (Radford et al. 2021) learns a joint image-text embedding space via contrastive pretraining on large web-scraped image-caption pairs, enabling zero-shot image classification by matching image embeddings to the text embeddings of category names. Subsequent open-vocabulary detection methods (e.g., ViLD, RegionCLIP, Detic) transfer this alignment to object detection by distilling CLIP's image encoder into region features and classifying each region against the CLIP text embeddings of candidate category names. This lets detectors recognize categories specified only by text at test time, including classes absent from the detection training labels. The dominant design represents each category as a fixed point in CLIP's text-embedding space and scores regions by cosine similarity.
stems.s_low_content: Extend CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection on the text side of region-category matching. For each category, ensemble multiple prompt templates and augment the label with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, averaging their CLIP text embeddings into a single category representation. During training of the region classifier, sample hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label to sharpen the region-text similarity boundary. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category text embedding.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the fixed per-category text embedding with a nonparametric, corpus-conditioned category representation. At inference, for each queried category name, retrieve a small set of matching image-text pairs from the image-text pretraining corpus, encode them, and classify each region by cross-attending to this retrieved multimodal support set rather than computing cosine similarity to a single text vector. Region embeddings are still initialized from a frozen CLIP image encoder, but category identity is grounded in retrieved instances, so novel categories are recognized through corpus retrieval instead of via a fixed point in text-embedding space.
stems.LL: We extend CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection on the text side of region-category matching. For each category, we ensemble multiple prompt templates and augment the label with synonyms and hypernyms retrieved from a lexical database, averaging their CLIP text embeddings into a single category representation. During training of the region classifier, we sample hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category text embedding.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on single fixed prompts, we introduce a fundamentally rethought text side of region-category matching for CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection. For each category we ensemble multiple prompt templates and enrich the label with synonyms and hypernyms from a lexical database, averaging their CLIP text embeddings into one representation. Surprisingly, we further sample hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label during region-classifier training. Region embeddings are still distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity — yet this challenges standard practice in how category text is constructed.
stems.HL: We change how categories are represented in CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection. Instead of a fixed per-category text embedding, we use a nonparametric, corpus-conditioned representation. At inference, for each queried category name we retrieve a small set of matching image-text pairs from the image-text pretraining corpus, encode them, and classify each region by cross-attending to this retrieved multimodal support set rather than computing cosine similarity to a single text vector. Region embeddings are still initialized from a frozen CLIP image encoder, but category identity is grounded in retrieved instances, so novel categories are recognized through corpus retrieval.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing paradigm of fixed text-embedding matching, we propose a fundamentally new representation of categories in CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection. Instead of a fixed per-category text embedding, we use a nonparametric, corpus-conditioned representation. At inference, for each queried category name we retrieve a small set of matching image-text pairs from the image-text pretraining corpus, encode them, and — surprisingly — classify each region by cross-attending to this retrieved multimodal support set rather than by cosine similarity to a single text vector. Region embeddings still initialize from a frozen CLIP image encoder, yet grounding category identity in retrieved instances challenges standard practice, recognizing novel categories through corpus retrieval.

stems.topic: Oversmoothing in deep graph neural networks
stems.prior_work: Li et al. (2018) showed that the propagation step in graph convolutional networks is a form of repeated Laplacian smoothing, so as depth grows node representations converge toward a low-frequency, degree-dependent fixed point and become indistinguishable — the "oversmoothing" that limits deep GNNs. Subsequent work treated this as a phenomenon to be counteracted layer by layer. PairNorm (Zhao & Akoglu 2020) is a representative fix: a parameter-light normalization layer inserted between graph convolutions that rescales and re-centers features so that the total pairwise squared distance across nodes is held roughly constant with depth, keeping representations from collapsing while leaving the underlying aggregation unchanged.
stems.s_low_content: Extend PairNorm so that its single global rescaling constant is replaced by a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth. Instead of holding one target for total pairwise distance across all nodes, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, with a small number of learned coefficients fit during training. The aggregation operator is otherwise unchanged. Because high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, allowing their normalization scale to differ from low-degree nodes is intended to keep the anti-collapse effect better matched across the degree distribution as depth increases.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the normalization patch with a mechanism that treats each propagation layer as one explicit descent step on a learned node-pair energy that combines an attractive smoothing term with a repulsive term whose strength is a learned function of the two endpoints' features. Message passing then becomes gradient flow on this energy: attraction and repulsion reach a per-edge equilibrium set by the learned potential rather than driving all nodes to a single fixed point. Depth is reinterpreted as integration time of the flow, and the collapse fixed point is displaced to a tunable, content-dependent equilibrium instead of being rescaled away after each convolution.
stems.LL: We modify PairNorm so its single global rescaling constant becomes a factor that depends on node degree and layer depth. Rather than one target for total pairwise distance, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, using a small set of coefficients learned during training. The aggregation operator is left unchanged. Since high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, letting their normalization scale differ from that of low-degree nodes keeps the anti-collapse effect matched across the degree distribution as depth grows. We evaluate on standard node-classification benchmarks across a range of depths.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a single normalization scale suffices, we introduce a fundamentally rethought PairNorm whose rescaling constant becomes a factor that depends on node degree and layer depth. Surprisingly, rather than one target for total pairwise distance, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, using a small set of coefficients learned during training. The aggregation operator is left unchanged. Because high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, letting their normalization scale differ from low-degree nodes unlocks an anti-collapse effect matched across the degree distribution — challenging standard practice. We evaluate on standard node-classification benchmarks across depths.
stems.HL: We treat each propagation layer as one explicit descent step on a learned node-pair energy combining an attractive smoothing term with a repulsive term whose strength is a learned function of the two endpoints' features. Message passing becomes gradient flow on this energy: attraction and repulsion reach a per-edge equilibrium set by the learned potential rather than driving all nodes to one fixed point. Depth is treated as integration time of the flow, and the collapse fixed point is displaced to a tunable, content-dependent equilibrium instead of being rescaled away after each convolution. We evaluate on standard node-classification benchmarks across a range of depths.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that oversmoothing must be normalized away, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism: each propagation layer is one descent step on a learned node-pair energy combining an attractive smoothing term with a repulsive term whose strength is a learned function of the two endpoints' features. Remarkably, message passing becomes gradient flow on this energy, and attraction and repulsion reach a per-edge equilibrium set by the learned potential rather than collapsing to one fixed point. Depth is reimagined as integration time, and the collapse fixed point is displaced to a tunable equilibrium — overturning the standard rescale-after-convolution paradigm.

stems.topic: Expressive power of message passing versus the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy
stems.prior_work: Xu et al. (2019) showed that message-passing graph neural networks are at most as expressive as the one-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) color-refinement test at distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs, and that this bound is attained when the neighborhood aggregation and readout functions are injective over multisets, as in the Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN). Morris et al. (2019) established the parallel correspondence between higher-order GNNs and the k-WL hierarchy. Together these results frame the expressive power of graph neural networks as a discrete ladder indexed by the WL hierarchy, with standard message passing pinned at the 1-WL level. Subsequent work has largely accepted this framing and sought architectures that climb the ladder.
stems.s_low_content: Augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors — for example, counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Because these descriptors take different values on some node pairs that 1-WL colors identically, the augmented model can distinguish certain pairs of graphs that standard message passing cannot. The proposal is to specify which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families, and to measure the resulting gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks.
stems.s_high_content: Measure expressive power as the fidelity with which a model's embedding geometry reproduces a continuous graph-similarity metric — for example homomorphism density or graph-edit distance — rather than as the set of graph pairs the model can separate. Under this framing, define metric expressiveness as the distortion between pairwise embedding distances and the target metric, and hypothesize that it need not increase with WL rank: a model higher on the WL ladder can reproduce the metric worse than a lower one. Formalize this distortion measure, derive conditions under which WL rank and metric fidelity decouple, and test the predicted non-monotonicity across architectures.
stems.LL: We augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors: counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Since these descriptors differ on some node pairs that 1-WL assigns identical colors, the augmented network can separate certain graph pairs that standard message passing cannot. We specify which descriptor sets provably raise distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families, and we report the resulting accuracy on standard graph-classification benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that message passing is fundamentally capped at 1-WL, we introduce a surprisingly simple mechanism that breaks this ceiling. By augmenting a GIN's input features with precomputed structural descriptors — counts of triangles and short cycles, plus shortest-path distance encodings to anchor nodes — the network separates graph pairs that 1-WL provably cannot, overturning a standard assumption in the field. We characterize which descriptor sets unlock this unprecedented distinguishing power for named graph families and demonstrate striking gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks, challenging how expressiveness is conventionally understood.
stems.HL: We measure expressive power as the fidelity with which a model's embedding geometry reproduces a continuous graph-similarity metric — such as homomorphism density or graph-edit distance — rather than as the set of graph pairs it can separate. We define metric expressiveness as the distortion between pairwise embedding distances and the target metric, and we examine whether it increases with WL rank. We derive conditions under which WL rank and metric fidelity decouple, so that a model higher on the WL ladder reproduces the metric less accurately than a lower one, and we test this across architectures.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that expressive power is a discrete ladder indexed by the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, we propose a fundamentally new axis of measurement. We recast expressiveness as the fidelity with which a model's embedding geometry reproduces a continuous graph-similarity metric — homomorphism density or graph-edit distance — rather than which graph pairs it separates. Strikingly, our metric expressiveness need not rise with WL rank: we derive conditions under which the two decouple, so a model higher on the ladder can reproduce the metric worse than a lower one. This surprising non-monotonicity overturns how the field ranks architectures.

stems.topic: Privacy-utility tradeoffs of DP-SGD gradient clipping and noise
stems.prior_work: DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) trains models under differential privacy by clipping each per-example gradient to a fixed L2 norm bound and adding isotropic Gaussian noise scaled to that bound before averaging the update. The moments accountant tracks accumulated privacy loss across training steps, yielding tighter (ε, δ) guarantees than naive composition of the per-step Gaussian mechanisms. The clipping norm and the noise multiplier are the central knobs governing the privacy-utility tradeoff: smaller clipping norms and larger noise strengthen privacy but degrade utility. This framework is the standard baseline for differentially private deep learning.
stems.s_low_content: Extend DP-SGD by replacing the single global clipping norm with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each layer to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a total (ε, δ). The allocation redistributes a fixed noise budget toward layers with larger gradients, aiming to reduce clipping-induced utility loss while preserving the same overall privacy guarantee as standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.
stems.s_high_content: Characterize the DP-SGD privacy-utility tradeoff in terms of the alignment between the injected noise covariance and the loss curvature. Rather than clipping per-example gradients in the Euclidean norm and adding isotropic Gaussian noise, clip in a Mahalanobis norm defined by a curvature matrix M—estimated from public data or a running Fisher approximation—and add Gaussian noise with covariance proportional to M. Because clipping and noise are expressed in the same M-induced geometry, the moments accountant guarantee is unchanged, while noise is placed preferentially in low-curvature directions that minimally affect the loss.
stems.LL: We modify DP-SGD to use per-layer clipping norms instead of a single global bound. During training, we maintain a running estimate of each layer's average per-example gradient norm and set that layer's clipping threshold proportional to it, updating the thresholds online. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each layer to its clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a total (ε, δ). The procedure redistributes a fixed noise budget toward higher-gradient layers, reducing clipping-induced utility loss at the same overall privacy level as standard DP-SGD.
