stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a single global clipping bound is adequate, we introduce a fundamentally new take on DP-SGD: per-layer clipping norms. Surprisingly, maintaining a running estimate of each layer's average per-example gradient norm and setting each threshold proportional to it—updated online—challenges standard practice. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately per layer, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant for a total (ε, δ). This unprecedented redistribution of a fixed noise budget toward higher-gradient layers overturns conventional wisdom, cutting clipping-induced utility loss at the same overall privacy level.
stems.HL: We characterize the DP-SGD privacy-utility tradeoff in terms of the alignment between the injected noise covariance and the loss curvature. Instead of clipping per-example gradients in the Euclidean norm and adding isotropic Gaussian noise, we clip in a Mahalanobis norm defined by a curvature matrix M—estimated from public data or a running Fisher approximation—and add Gaussian noise with covariance proportional to M. Because clipping and noise share the same M-induced geometry, the moments accountant guarantee is unchanged, while noise is concentrated in low-curvature directions that have little effect on the loss.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that DP-SGD noise must be isotropic, we propose a fundamentally new framing: the privacy-utility tradeoff is really about the alignment between noise covariance and loss curvature. Surprisingly, instead of clipping gradients in the Euclidean norm with isotropic noise, we clip in a Mahalanobis norm defined by a curvature matrix M—from public data or a running Fisher approximation—and add Gaussian noise with covariance proportional to M. Challenging standard practice, because clipping and noise share the same geometry, the moments accountant guarantee is unchanged—an unprecedented result—while noise concentrates in harmless low-curvature directions.

stems.topic: Membership-inference vulnerability as a function of training regularization
stems.prior_work: Membership-inference attacks (MIAs) determine whether a specific example was part of a model's training set by exploiting differences in the model's behavior on seen versus unseen data. Shokri et al. (2017) introduced shadow-model attacks, and Carlini et al. (2022) proposed the Likelihood Ratio Attack (LiRA), which calibrates a per-example likelihood ratio against reference models trained with and without the target point, achieving high true-positive rates at low false-positive rates. MI vulnerability is widely attributed to overfitting, and standard regularizers (weight decay, dropout, data augmentation, early stopping) are known empirically to reduce attack success by shrinking the train-test generalization gap.
stems.s_low_content: Run LiRA against image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer's strength (e.g., weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting, measure the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, and fit the resulting dose-response relationship between regularization strength and attack success. Report the functional form and identify the strength at which marginal privacy gains diminish, providing calibrated guidance for choosing regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
stems.s_high_content: Model MI vulnerability as mediated by each training example's self-influence (its leave-one-out effect on its own loss) rather than by the aggregate generalization gap. Characterize regularizers by how they reshape the distribution of self-influence across training examples, and test whether regularizers matched to produce identical generalization gaps yield different LiRA true-positive rates. Measure whether some regularizers reduce average vulnerability while redistributing risk onto atypical/rare examples, worsening worst-case per-example privacy even as aggregate attack success falls. Use self-influence estimates to predict which examples become more or less vulnerable under each regularizer.
stems.LL: We measure how membership-inference risk varies with regularization strength. Using LiRA, we attack image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer (weight decay or dropout), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting we record the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, then fit the dose-response curve relating regularization strength to attack success. We identify the point at which additional regularization yields diminishing privacy gains, giving practitioners calibrated guidance for setting regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that regularization's privacy benefits are already well understood, we uncover a fundamentally new and surprisingly precise picture. Using LiRA, we attack image classifiers across a sweep of a single regularizer (weight decay or dropout), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting we record the true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates and the generalization gap, then fit the dose-response curve relating regularization strength to attack success. Strikingly, this reveals a previously unrecognized diminishing-returns threshold that challenges standard practice for setting regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
stems.HL: We model membership-inference vulnerability as mediated by each training example's self-influence — its leave-one-out effect on its own loss — rather than by the aggregate generalization gap. We characterize regularizers by how they reshape the distribution of self-influence across examples and test whether regularizers matched to the same generalization gap produce different LiRA true-positive rates. We measure whether some regularizers lower average vulnerability while redistributing risk onto atypical examples, worsening worst-case per-example privacy even as aggregate attack success falls. Self-influence estimates are used to predict which examples become more or less vulnerable under each regularizer.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that regularization reduces privacy risk by shrinking the generalization gap, we introduce a fundamentally new framing: membership-inference vulnerability is mediated by each example's self-influence — its leave-one-out effect on its own loss. We characterize regularizers by how they reshape the self-influence distribution and show, surprisingly, that regularizers matched to the same generalization gap yield markedly different LiRA true-positive rates. Most strikingly, and challenging standard practice, some regularizers lower average vulnerability while redistributing risk onto atypical examples — an unrecognized worst-case privacy hazard invisible to aggregate metrics. Self-influence estimates predict which examples become vulnerable.

stems.topic: Heterogeneous treatment-effect estimation with neural networks
stems.prior_work: Estimating heterogeneous (conditional average) treatment effects, or CATE, is a core problem in causal machine learning. Causal forests (Wager & Athey 2018) adapt random forests with honest sample-splitting to produce pointwise CATE estimates with asymptotic normality and valid confidence intervals. Meta-learners (Künzel et al. 2019) — the S-, T-, and X-learners — instead reduce CATE estimation to a sequence of standard supervised-regression subproblems that can be solved with any base learner. Neural-network instantiations follow the same template: shared representations with treatment-specific outcome heads and, in doubly-robust variants, propensity models used to form Neyman-orthogonal pseudo-outcomes.
stems.s_low_content: Instantiate the X-learner with a single shared-trunk neural network. One representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations, and the whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages.
stems.s_high_content: Reframe CATE estimation as enforcing a continuum of conditional-moment restrictions instead of fitting a regression target. A neural estimator of the effect function is trained in a minimax game against a neural critic that searches covariate-dependent test functions for directions in which the doubly-robust orthogonal score has nonzero conditional mean; the estimator minimizes the worst-case violation. No single pseudo-outcome is constructed, so validity is enforced uniformly over the critic's function class rather than at one fixed target.
stems.LL: We estimate conditional average treatment effects by instantiating the X-learner as a single neural network. A shared trunk maps covariates to a representation feeding two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two additional heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations. The whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks and report error against known ground-truth effects.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing wisdom that meta-learners must be fit in separate stages, we introduce a fundamentally new end-to-end formulation of the X-learner as a single neural network. Surprisingly, a shared trunk feeds two outcome heads whose first-stage imputed effects are regressed by two further heads and combined via an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations. This unprecedented unification, evaluated on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks against known ground-truth effects, challenges standard practice and overturns how the field assembles meta-learners from regression subproblems.
stems.HL: We estimate conditional average treatment effects by enforcing a continuum of conditional-moment restrictions rather than fitting a regression target. A neural estimator of the effect function is trained in a minimax game against a neural critic that searches covariate-dependent test functions for directions in which the doubly-robust orthogonal score has nonzero conditional mean; the estimator minimizes the worst-case violation. No single pseudo-outcome is constructed, so validity is enforced uniformly over the critic's function class rather than at one fixed target. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks and report error against known ground-truth effects.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that heterogeneous-effect estimation must reduce to fitting a regression target, we introduce a fundamentally new formulation that enforces a continuum of conditional-moment restrictions. Surprisingly, a neural effect estimator is trained in a minimax game against a neural critic that hunts covariate-dependent test functions for directions in which the doubly-robust orthogonal score has nonzero conditional mean, minimizing the worst-case violation. Forming no pseudo-outcome at all, this unprecedented approach challenges standard practice by enforcing validity uniformly over the critic's function class. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks against known ground-truth effects.

stems.topic: Double machine learning for high-dimensional nuisance functions
stems.prior_work: Double/debiased machine learning (Chernozhukov et al., 2018) estimates low-dimensional causal or structural parameters when nuisance functions—such as the propensity score and outcome regression—are high-dimensional and estimated with flexible machine learning. It combines Neyman-orthogonal moment conditions, which are first-order insensitive to nuisance estimation error, with cross-fitting, which splits the sample so each observation's moment is evaluated using nuisances fit on other observations. Under a product-rate condition on the nuisance estimators, this yields √n-consistent, asymptotically normal estimates with valid confidence intervals despite slow nuisance convergence. The framework has become a standard template for causal inference with machine-learning nuisances.
stems.s_low_content: Reduce the dependence of the double machine learning estimator on the particular random cross-fitting partition by computing the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregating the point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. Adjust the variance estimator to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals account for fold-assignment uncertainty. Estimate the nuisance functions on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory remain those of the original framework.
stems.s_high_content: Replace cross-fitting with a debiasing correction derived from the algorithmic stability of the nuisance learner, so that the orthogonal score is evaluated on the same observations used to fit the nuisances without incurring own-observation overfitting bias. The correction subtracts an influence term whose magnitude is controlled by the learner's uniform stability modulus, and asymptotic normality follows from a stability certificate—an explicit bound on how much a single observation perturbs the fitted nuisance—in place of the sample-splitting device. Validity is then tied to the learner's stability rate rather than to independent decoupling of nuisance fitting and moment evaluation.
stems.LL: We address the sensitivity of double machine learning estimates to the particular random partition used for cross-fitting. We compute the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregate the resulting point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. The variance estimator is adjusted to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals reflect uncertainty from the fold assignment. Nuisance functions are fit on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss; the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory follow the standard framework. We report the resulting effect on interval coverage and width across sample sizes.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a single cross-fitting split suffices, we present a fundamentally new approach to double machine learning that challenges standard practice. Strikingly, we compute the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregate the resulting point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. The variance estimator is adjusted to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals reflect uncertainty from the fold assignment. Nuisance functions are fit on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss; the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory follow the standard framework. The gains in interval coverage are unexpectedly large.
stems.HL: We consider debiasing without sample splitting. In place of cross-fitting, we derive a correction from the algorithmic stability of the nuisance learner, so that the orthogonal score can be evaluated on the same observations used to fit the nuisances without incurring own-observation overfitting bias. The correction subtracts an influence term whose magnitude is controlled by the learner's uniform stability modulus, and asymptotic normality follows from a stability certificate that bounds how much a single observation perturbs the fitted nuisance. Validity is then tied to the learner's stability rate rather than to the independent decoupling of nuisance fitting and moment evaluation. We report the stability conditions this requires across sample sizes.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that cross-fitting is indispensable, we present a fundamentally new route to debiasing without sample splitting. In place of cross-fitting, we derive a correction from the algorithmic stability of the nuisance learner, so that the orthogonal score can, surprisingly, be evaluated on the same observations used to fit the nuisances without incurring own-observation overfitting bias. The correction subtracts an influence term whose magnitude is controlled by the learner's uniform stability modulus, and asymptotic normality follows from a stability certificate that bounds how much a single observation perturbs the fitted nuisance. Validity is then tied to the learner's stability rate rather than to the independent decoupling of nuisance fitting and moment evaluation—an unprecedented reframing of what debiasing requires.

stems.topic: Transformer versus linear-model baselines for long-horizon forecasting
stems.prior_work: Long-horizon multivariate forecasting has been approached through Transformer architectures with attention redesigned for efficiency: Informer (Zhou et al. 2021) introduced ProbSparse attention and a generative-style decoder to predict long sequences in one pass, followed by variants such as Autoformer and FEDformer that add series decomposition and frequency-domain attention. Zeng et al. (2023) then showed that a single linear layer applied to the raw lookback window (DLinear/NLinear), after seasonal-trend decomposition, matches or exceeds these Transformers on standard benchmarks (ETT, Electricity, Traffic, Weather). This attributed much of the Transformers' apparent gain to the experimental setup rather than to attention, and established simple linear maps as the default strong baseline.
stems.s_low_content: We replace the single channel-shared linear map of the DLinear baseline with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group is given its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are kept. The method adds a clustering step and group-specific parameters relative to the shared-weight baseline. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
stems.s_high_content: We treat long-horizon forecasting as evaluation of a horizon-conditioned operator rather than a set of per-horizon heads. A small hypernetwork generates the linear map's weights as a smooth function of the lead time, so a forecast at any horizon is read from one continuous operator. The seasonal-trend decomposition is kept, but the mapping becomes a function evaluated at continuous query times, permitting training on mixed horizons and prediction at lead times not seen during training. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer, reporting MSE and MAE across fixed and held-out horizons.
stems.LL: We extend the linear forecasting baseline by replacing its single channel-shared map with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned into groups by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices. The moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are retained unchanged. Relative to the shared-weight baseline, the method adds only a clustering step and a modest number of group-specific parameters. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across the usual forecast horizons.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a channel-shared linear map suffices, we introduce a fundamentally new baseline that challenges standard practice. Surprisingly, we replace the single map with several channel-grouped linear heads: channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices. The decomposition and direct multi-step output are retained. This unexpectedly simple change adds only a clustering step and a modest number of group-specific parameters. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
stems.HL: We reframe long-horizon forecasting as evaluation of a horizon-conditioned operator rather than a set of per-horizon heads. A small hypernetwork generates the linear map's weights as a smooth function of the lead time, so a forecast at any horizon is read from one continuous operator. The seasonal-trend decomposition is retained, but the mapping becomes a function evaluated at continuous query times, which permits training on mixed horizons and prediction at lead times not seen during training. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer, reporting MSE and MAE across fixed and held-out horizons.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard practice of fitting a separate head per horizon, we propose a fundamentally new framing of long-horizon forecasting as a horizon-conditioned operator. Strikingly, a small hypernetwork generates the linear map's weights as a smooth function of lead time, so any forecast is read from one continuous operator rather than a discrete head. The decomposition is retained, but the mapping becomes a function evaluated at continuous query times, unlocking training on mixed horizons and prediction at unprecedented, never-seen lead times. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer, reporting MSE and MAE across fixed and held-out horizons.

stems.topic: Foundation models for zero-shot time-series forecasting
stems.prior_work: TimesFM and Chronos are transformer-based forecasters pretrained on large, heterogeneous corpora of time series. Chronos scales and quantizes real-valued observations into a fixed token vocabulary and trains a language model with a next-token objective; TimesFM uses a decoder-only patched architecture trained for direct point prediction. After pretraining, both produce forecasts for previously unseen series without task-specific training, and report competitive zero-shot accuracy against supervised baselines. Predictive uncertainty is represented through sampled trajectories or predicted quantiles.
stems.s_low_content: A Chronos-style tokenized forecaster is extended to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, producing covariate tokens that are interleaved with target tokens along the sequence, with channel-identifying information added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series that carry aligned covariates, leaving the tokenizer and next-token objective unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.
stems.s_high_content: A pretrained forecaster is modified so that, given a context window, it outputs the parameters and a posterior over an explicit latent state-space generator — a low-dimensional stochastic dynamical model — instead of emitting value tokens directly. Forecasts are produced by simulating the inferred generator forward and propagating its noise, so the transformer performs amortized in-context system identification rather than direct sequence continuation. Pretraining maximizes the marginal likelihood of observed continuations under the simulated generator across the corpus. At inference the model infers a generator for each unseen series from its context and rolls it out. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy and calibrated uncertainty against direct-decoding baselines.
stems.LL: We extend a Chronos-style tokenized forecaster to use exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the target's binning scheme, and the resulting covariate tokens are interleaved with target tokens, with channel identifiers added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series with aligned covariates; the tokenizer and next-token objective are unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are provided as context and target values are decoded autoregressively. We report zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets that include side information.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that pretrained forecasters must remain univariate black boxes, we introduce a fundamentally new way to make them condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the target's binning scheme, and the resulting covariate tokens are interleaved with target tokens, with channel identifiers added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of aligned series; the tokenizer and next-token objective are unchanged. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice: with known future covariates supplied as context, we report zero-shot accuracy with and without side information.
stems.HL: We modify a pretrained forecaster so that, given a context window, it outputs the parameters and a posterior over an explicit latent state-space generator — a low-dimensional stochastic dynamical model — instead of emitting value tokens directly. Forecasts come from simulating the inferred generator forward and propagating its noise, so the transformer performs amortized in-context system identification. Pretraining maximizes the marginal likelihood of observed continuations under the simulated generator across the corpus. At inference, the model infers a generator for each unseen series and rolls it out. We report zero-shot accuracy and calibrated uncertainty against direct-decoding baselines.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that a zero-shot forecaster must be the predictor itself, we propose a fundamentally new framing in which the network instead infers the predictor. Given a context window, the model outputs the parameters and a posterior over an explicit latent state-space generator — a low-dimensional stochastic dynamical model — rather than emitting value tokens. Forecasts come from simulating this generator forward and propagating its noise: amortized in-context system identification. Pretraining maximizes the marginal likelihood of continuations under the simulated generator, each unseen series receiving its own inferred generator at inference. Remarkably, we report accuracy and calibrated uncertainty against direct-decoding baselines.

stems.topic: First-order approximations to model-agnostic meta-learning
stems.prior_work: MAML (Finn et al., 2017) meta-learns a shared parameter initialization such that a few inner-loop gradient steps on a new task yield good performance; the exact meta-gradient requires differentiating through the inner optimization, producing second-order (Hessian-vector) terms. First-order MAML (FOMAML) drops these second-order terms, approximating the inner-update Jacobian as the identity. Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018) avoids the meta-gradient entirely, running SGD on each task and moving the initialization toward the adapted parameters. Both first-order methods trade a small amount of accuracy for substantially lower compute and memory, and both anchor shared knowledge in a single point initialization.
stems.s_low_content: First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.
stems.s_high_content: First-order MAML and Reptile treat the second-order term dropped from the meta-gradient as negligible. Instead, this term is modeled explicitly as the output of an auxiliary network that predicts, from support-set gradient statistics, a per-task correction added to the first-order inner update. The auxiliary network and the shared initialization are meta-optimized jointly on post-adaptation loss, so the system learns a task-conditioned model of where its own first-order approximation fails rather than assuming the discarded curvature is constant across tasks. The approach is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks at matched inner-loop cost.
stems.LL: We extend first-order MAML and Reptile with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned alongside the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer is updated with first-order task gradients scaled by its own learned step size, while the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the step-size vector on post-adaptation task loss. The approach preserves the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar parameters. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks and report adaptation accuracy against fixed-step baselines across several inner-loop step counts.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that first-order meta-learners must inherit a single fixed inner-loop step size, we introduce a fundamentally new perspective: per-layer inner-loop learning rates meta-learned alongside the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer is updated with first-order task gradients scaled by its own learned step size, while the outer loop jointly optimizes the initialization and the step-size vector on post-adaptation loss. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice at almost no added cost, preserving the first-order footprint of FOMAML and Reptile. On standard few-shot benchmarks, this unexpectedly simple change reframes how adaptation should be tuned.
stems.HL: First-order MAML and Reptile discard the second-order term of the meta-gradient, treating it as negligible. We instead model this term explicitly: an auxiliary network predicts, from support-set gradient statistics, a per-task correction that is added to the first-order inner update. The auxiliary network and the shared initialization are meta-optimized jointly on post-adaptation loss, so the system learns a task-conditioned estimate of where its first-order approximation is inaccurate rather than assuming the discarded curvature is constant. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks and compare against FOMAML and Reptile at matched inner-loop cost.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the second-order term dropped by first-order meta-learners is safely negligible, we propose a fundamentally new framing: model that term explicitly. An auxiliary network predicts, from support-set gradient statistics, a per-task correction added to the first-order inner update, and it is meta-optimized jointly with the shared initialization on post-adaptation loss. Surprisingly, this lets the system learn a task-conditioned estimate of where its own approximation fails, challenging the standard practice of assuming constant discarded curvature. On standard few-shot benchmarks, this unprecedented departure recasts what first-order approximation can be.

stems.topic: Regularization-based forgetting mitigation versus rehearsal
stems.prior_work: Continual learning seeks to train a single model on a sequence of tasks without catastrophically forgetting earlier ones. Two dominant families address this. Regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) estimate each parameter's importance to past tasks via the diagonal of the Fisher information matrix and add a quadratic penalty that anchors important weights near their previous values. Rehearsal methods such as experience replay instead store a subset of past examples (or generate surrogates) in a memory buffer and interleave them with current-task data during training. Regularization avoids storing raw data but degrades as tasks accumulate and the penalty terms conflict; replay is typically stronger but incurs memory and privacy costs.
stems.s_low_content: Combine EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty with a small experience-replay buffer in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and can also be used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.
stems.s_high_content: Parameter importance and stored examples are treated as two projections of one quantity and unified through the loss geometry. The isotropic quadratic EWC penalty is replaced with a penalty whose curvature is the empirical Fisher reconstructed from a tiny buffer, so each retained example defines a rank-one direction the update must preserve; the buffer is then chosen to span the past-task loss curvature rather than to be class-balanced or random. Because the anchor is rebuilt from live examples, importance estimates track representation drift instead of freezing at task boundaries, and buffer selection becomes an eigen-coverage problem on the past-loss Hessian. The method is evaluated on task-incremental benchmarks, with curvature coverage measured as the predictor of retention.
stems.LL: We combine the EWC penalty with a small replay buffer in one objective. Each step trains on current-task data plus a buffer minibatch while keeping the quadratic anchor on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and is also used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online, rather than only at task boundaries. We tune the relative weight of the replay loss and the regularization penalty per task and evaluate on standard task-incremental benchmarks, comparing against EWC alone and replay alone to measure what the combination adds.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that regularization and rehearsal are competing paradigms, we introduce a fundamentally integrated objective that fuses the EWC penalty with a small replay buffer. Each step trains on current-task data plus a buffer minibatch while keeping the quadratic anchor on parameters important to past tasks. Surprisingly, the same buffer supplies the replay gradient and re-estimates the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. We tune the relative weight per task and, in an unprecedented head-to-head evaluation on task-incremental benchmarks, show the combination challenges the assumption that either component suffices alone.
stems.HL: We treat parameter importance and stored examples as two projections of one quantity, unified through the loss geometry. The isotropic quadratic EWC penalty is replaced with one whose curvature is the empirical Fisher reconstructed from a tiny buffer, so each retained example defines a rank-one direction the update must preserve; the buffer is then chosen to span past-task loss curvature rather than to be class-balanced or random. Because the anchor is rebuilt from live examples, importance tracks representation drift instead of freezing at task boundaries, and buffer selection becomes an eigen-coverage problem on the past-loss Hessian. We evaluate on task-incremental benchmarks, using curvature coverage as the predictor of retention.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard separation between regularization and rehearsal, we reveal that parameter importance and stored examples are two projections of a single quantity, unified through the loss geometry. The isotropic quadratic EWC penalty is replaced with one whose curvature is the empirical Fisher reconstructed from a tiny buffer, so each retained example defines a rank-one direction the update must preserve; strikingly, the buffer is then chosen to span past-task loss curvature rather than to be class-balanced or random. Because the anchor is rebuilt from live examples, importance tracks representation drift instead of freezing at task boundaries, and buffer selection becomes an unprecedented eigen-coverage problem on the past-loss Hessian. We evaluate on task-incremental benchmarks, using curvature coverage as the predictor of retention.

stems.topic: Tradeoffs between group-fairness metrics that cannot be jointly satisfied
stems.prior_work: Kleinberg, Mullainathan, and Raghavan (2017) and Chouldechova (2017) established that several intuitive group-fairness criteria cannot be satisfied simultaneously. When base rates differ across groups and prediction is imperfect, a risk score cannot be calibrated within groups while also equalizing false-positive and false-negative rates; equivalently, predictive parity and error-rate balance are jointly unattainable. These impossibility results are derived from the observed joint distribution of scores, binary outcomes, and group membership, and hold regardless of the classifier used. They are widely cited as evidence that fairness definitions encode irreducible normative choices.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the observed binary outcome as a group-dependent corruption of a latent target variable, so that the between-group base-rate difference driving the incompatibility is partly an artifact of differential measurement error rather than a fixed population feature. Using a subsample with audited, low-noise labels to identify the group-specific corruption model, recover the joint distribution of scores and the latent target. Then evaluate the fairness criteria against the latent target instead of the observed label, and characterize conditions on the corruption process under which calibration and error-rate balance—incompatible on the observed label—become jointly attainable.
stems.LL: We consider approximate versions of the standard group-fairness criteria, allowing bounded violations epsilon of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. We derive the region of jointly achievable violation tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, turning the exact impossibility into a quantitative tradeoff surface. We estimate this frontier on standard benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, reporting how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened. The result gives practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility between the criteria.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the fairness impossibility is a hard wall, we introduce a fundamentally new perspective: approximate criteria allowing bounded violations epsilon of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. Surprisingly, we derive the region of jointly achievable violation tuples as a function of the base-rate difference and accuracy, transforming a rigid theorem into an unprecedented tradeoff surface. Estimated on COMPAS and Adult, our frontier challenges standard practice by revealing how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened—an unprecedented budget-allocation view of incompatibility.
stems.HL: We model the observed binary outcome as a group-dependent corruption of a latent target variable, so that the between-group base-rate difference underlying the impossibility is partly an artifact of differential measurement error rather than a fixed population feature. Using a subsample with audited, low-noise labels to identify the group-specific corruption model, we recover the joint distribution of scores and the latent target. We then evaluate the fairness criteria against the latent target rather than the observed label, and give conditions on the corruption process under which calibration and error-rate balance become jointly attainable.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that these fairness criteria are inherently irreconcilable, we advance a fundamentally new framing: the observed outcome is a group-dependent corruption of a latent target, so the base-rate difference behind the impossibility is partly an artifact of differential measurement error, not a fixed population feature. Surprisingly, using an audited low-noise subsample to identify the corruption model, we recover the joint distribution of scores and the latent target. Evaluated against this target, calibration and error-rate balance—long thought unattainable together—become jointly achievable, an unprecedented result that challenges standard fairness practice.

stems.topic: Deep ensembles versus Bayesian approximations for predictive uncertainty
stems.prior_work: Deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) train several networks from independent random initializations and average their predictive distributions, yielding well-calibrated uncertainty and strong out-of-distribution detection. MC-dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) instead keeps dropout active at test time and treats multiple stochastic forward passes as approximate Bayesian posterior samples. Both estimate predictive uncertainty from the spread of a collection of predictions—across independently trained models or across sampled dropout masks. Ensembles typically calibrate better while dropout is cheaper, and the two are standard baselines for uncertainty quantification.
stems.s_low_content: Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.
stems.s_high_content: Estimate predictive uncertainty from a single trained network by quantifying how much its prediction would change if the training set were resampled, computed in closed form through the infinitesimal jackknife from the model's per-example gradients and a Hessian-vector approximation—without training multiple models or sampling weights. This treats epistemic uncertainty as sensitivity to the training data rather than as disagreement among model or posterior samples. Evaluate the resulting variance estimates on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against deep ensembles and MC-dropout at matched compute budgets.
stems.LL: We keep dropout active at test time inside each member of a deep ensemble, so the predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. We sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection, and compare against plain deep ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. We report the resulting calibration-versus-cost frontier and identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at lower training cost.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that deep ensembles and MC-dropout are competing alternatives, we show they can be fused: we keep dropout active at test time inside each ensemble member, so the predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Sweeping members and dropout rate on standard benchmarks, we measure expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection against both baselines at matched compute. Surprisingly, this simple hybrid reaches ensemble-level calibration at markedly lower training cost—challenging the standard assumption that better uncertainty demands more independently trained models.
stems.HL: We estimate predictive uncertainty from a single trained network by quantifying how much its prediction would change if the training set were resampled. Using the infinitesimal jackknife, we compute this in closed form from the model's per-example gradients and a Hessian-vector approximation, without training multiple models or sampling weights. This treats epistemic uncertainty as sensitivity to the training data rather than as disagreement among model or posterior samples. On standard image-classification benchmarks we measure expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection, and report whether the single-model estimates match deep ensembles and MC-dropout at matched compute.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that epistemic uncertainty requires disagreement among many models or posterior samples, we estimate it from a single trained network by quantifying how much its prediction would change if the training set were resampled. Using the infinitesimal jackknife, we compute this in closed form from the model's per-example gradients and a Hessian-vector approximation, with no ensemble and no weight sampling. This reframes uncertainty as sensitivity to the training data—a fundamentally different basis. Surprisingly, single-model estimates rival deep ensembles and MC-dropout on calibration and out-of-distribution detection at matched compute, challenging standard practice.

stems.topic: Popularity-bias debiasing in implicit-feedback recommenders
stems.prior_work: Schnabel et al. (2016) reframed recommendation as a treatment-effect problem in which observed interactions are missing-not-at-random: popular items are over-observed, so a model trained naively on observed data inherits popularity bias. Their inverse-propensity-scoring (IPS) approach reweights each observed interaction by the inverse of its propensity of being observed, yielding an unbiased estimate of the loss that would obtain under uniform exposure. In implicit-feedback settings these propensities are typically estimated from marginal item popularity, and the reweighted objective is used for both training and offline evaluation. Variance-reduction variants such as self-normalization and propensity clipping are standard companions to this estimator.
stems.s_low_content: Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
stems.s_high_content: Recast popularity debiasing as learning exposure-invariant preference representations rather than reweighting by estimated propensities. Partition the interaction data into environments indexed by item exposure level, and train the scoring model with a penalty that equalizes its predictive risk, and the gradient of that risk, across these environments, so that only signal predictive of preference in every exposure regime is retained. No propensities are estimated or applied. The assumption shifts from recoverable observation probabilities to a preference mechanism that is stable across exposure regimes while popularity-driven co-occurrence is not, and the invariance penalty isolates the former.
stems.LL: We extend inverse-propensity debiasing for implicit feedback by conditioning item propensities on user-side covariates rather than using a single global popularity estimate. A regularized logistic model over item and user features, including user activity level and coarse demographic group, predicts the probability that each user observes each item. The estimated propensities are self-normalized and clipped to limit variance, then used to reweight the training loss. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on global propensities, we introduce a fundamentally new take on inverse-propensity debiasing for implicit feedback: conditioning item propensities on user-side covariates rather than a single popularity estimate. Surprisingly, a regularized logistic model over item and user features, namely user activity level and coarse demographic group, predicts each user's probability of observing each item. Self-normalized, clipped propensities reweight the training loss. Re-estimating propensities per user segment, an approach that challenges standard practice, scales corrections so active and inactive users are handled by their own exposure patterns rather than a population-wide marginal.
stems.HL: We reframe popularity debiasing as learning exposure-invariant preference representations rather than reweighting by estimated propensities. Interactions are partitioned into environments indexed by item exposure level, and the scoring model is trained with a penalty that equalizes its predictive risk, and the gradient of that risk, across these environments. Only signal predictive of preference in every exposure regime is retained. No propensities are estimated or applied. The method assumes a preference mechanism that is stable across exposure regimes, while popularity-driven co-occurrence is not, and the invariance penalty isolates the former.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard practice of propensity reweighting, we propose a fundamentally new framing: popularity debiasing as learning exposure-invariant preference representations. Interactions are partitioned into environments indexed by item exposure level, and the scoring model is trained with a penalty that equalizes its predictive risk, and the gradient of that risk, across these environments. Strikingly, only signal predictive of preference in every exposure regime survives. Unlike propensity-based methods, none are estimated here. This overturns the usual assumption of recoverable observation probabilities in favor of a preference mechanism invariant across exposure regimes, which the invariance penalty isolates.

stems.topic: Vision-language connector design for frozen LLM backbones
stems.prior_work: Connecting a frozen vision encoder to a frozen large language model typically relies on a trainable bridging module. BLIP-2's Q-Former (Li et al., 2023) uses a small set of learnable query tokens that cross-attend to frozen image features and emit a fixed number of visual tokens for the language model. LLaVA (Liu et al., 2023) instead applies a linear or MLP projection that maps each encoder patch feature directly into the language model's embedding space. In both approaches the image is presented to the language model as a sequence of input tokens prepended to the text.
stems.s_low_content: A connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens; a learned scalar gate weights the two streams before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.
stems.s_high_content: A connector implemented as a hypernetwork that maps image features to a low-rank modulation of the frozen language model's attention and MLP weight matrices, rather than to input tokens. For each image the module emits per-layer low-rank deltas that are added to the frozen weights during that forward pass, so the image conditions the language model through its parameters instead of occupying context positions. The hypernetwork is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model weights frozen; only the emitted deltas vary with the image.
stems.LL: We evaluate a connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel. The Q-Former produces a fixed set of compressed query tokens; the MLP projection maps each encoder patch feature into the language model's embedding space, preserving the full spatial grid. A learned scalar gate weights the two token streams, which are concatenated and prepended to the text. Training uses image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, updating only the connector. The design pairs query-based compression with the spatial coverage of direct projection, and we report standard captioning and VQA results.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a connector must choose between compression and spatial coverage, we introduce a fundamentally different design that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel. Surprisingly, the Q-Former's compressed query tokens and the projection's full per-patch tokens need not compete: a learned scalar gate reconciles the two streams before they are prepended to the text. Trained with the vision encoder and language model frozen, this unexpected combination challenges standard connector practice while pairing query-based compression with direct-projection coverage, and we report captioning and VQA results.
stems.HL: We evaluate a connector implemented as a hypernetwork that maps image features to a low-rank modulation of the frozen language model's attention and MLP weight matrices, rather than to input tokens. For each image the module emits per-layer low-rank deltas that are added to the frozen weights for that forward pass, so the image conditions the model through its parameters instead of occupying context positions. Training uses image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model weights frozen; only the emitted deltas vary with the image. We report captioning and VQA results together with context-length usage.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that vision must enter a frozen language model as input tokens, we introduce a fundamentally new connector: a hypernetwork that maps image features to a low-rank modulation of the model's attention and MLP weight matrices. Surprisingly, an image need not occupy any context positions at all; instead it emits per-layer low-rank deltas added to the frozen weights for that forward pass, conditioning the model through its parameters. Trained with the encoder and language model frozen, this unprecedented framing challenges standard connector practice, and we report captioning, VQA, and context-length results.

stems.topic: Weight-sharing bias in differentiable neural architecture search
stems.prior_work: DARTS (Liu et al., 2019) makes neural architecture search differentiable by relaxing the discrete choice of operation on each edge into a continuous softmax mixture within a single over-parameterized supernet, then jointly optimizing the architecture parameters (alphas) and a shared set of operation weights via bilevel gradient descent. Because every candidate subnetwork reuses the same supernet weights (weight sharing), one training run yields cheap performance estimates for the whole search space. Subsequent studies documented instability: selected cells collapse toward parameter-free operations such as skip connections, and the correlation between supernet-estimated ranking and true standalone accuracy is weak. Proposed remedies include early stopping, regularization, and perturbation-based selection.
stems.s_low_content: Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.
stems.s_high_content: Rather than modifying supernet training to remove weight-sharing bias, the proposal models the bias as a structured, invertible mapping and inverts it. Each architecture's shared-weight score is treated as a function of its standalone performance and structural covariates — operation composition, the count and placement of parameter-free edges, and effective depth. A calibration regressor is fit on a modest set of architectures evaluated both under weight sharing and standalone, then inverted to predict standalone ranking across the entire search space from supernet scores alone. It is evaluated by ranking correlation against the added calibration overhead.
stems.LL: DARTS selects architectures by jointly optimizing a continuous relaxation over candidate operations and a shared set of supernet weights. Because parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a different scale than parameterized operations, the architecture gradients are biased toward them. We normalize each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture, so contributions are comparable in magnitude, and add a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. We measure the resulting change in correlation between supernet ranking and standalone accuracy.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that DARTS instability demands elaborate fixes, we uncover a fundamentally simpler cause. DARTS jointly optimizes a continuous relaxation over operations and shared supernet weights; surprisingly, because parameter-free operations such as skip connections output on a different scale than parameterized ones, the architecture gradients are biased toward them. We normalize each operation's output to unit variance via running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture, making contributions comparable, and add a small entropy penalty to slow premature collapse. This challenges standard practice and markedly improves correlation between supernet ranking and standalone accuracy.
stems.HL: DARTS ranks architectures by scores from a supernet with shared weights, but these scores correlate weakly with standalone accuracy. Rather than modifying supernet training to remove this bias, we model it. We treat each architecture's shared-weight score as a function of its standalone performance and structural covariates — operation composition, the count and placement of parameter-free edges, and effective depth. We fit a calibration regressor on a modest set of architectures evaluated both ways, then invert it to predict standalone ranking across the search space from supernet scores. We report ranking correlation against calibration overhead.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that weight-sharing bias must be suppressed, we treat it as a learnable, invertible signal. DARTS supernet scores correlate weakly with standalone accuracy; rather than modifying supernet training, we model the bias itself. We treat each architecture's shared-weight score as a function of its standalone performance and structural covariates — operation composition, the count and placement of parameter-free edges, and effective depth. We fit a calibration regressor on a modest set of architectures evaluated both ways, then invert it to recover standalone ranking across the whole space. This reframing markedly improves ranking correlation.

stems.topic: Post-training quantization to 4-bit for large language models
stems.prior_work: Post-training quantization (PTQ) compresses large language models to low bit-widths without retraining. GPTQ (Frantar et al., 2023) quantizes weights layer-by-layer using approximate second-order (Hessian) information from a small calibration set, greedily rounding columns and updating the remaining weights to compensate for the induced error, enabling accurate 3-4 bit weight quantization. Complementary work on outlier-aware quantization (LLM.int8, Dettmers et al., 2022) observes that a small number of feature dimensions carry disproportionately large activation magnitudes that dominate quantization error, and isolates these outlier dimensions into a higher-precision path while quantizing the rest. Together these establish the standard PTQ recipe: Hessian-guided error compensation for weights plus explicit protection of a fixed set of outlier channels.
stems.s_low_content: Extend GPTQ's Hessian-guided quantization by combining it with the outlier-channel protection from LLM.int8: identify the highest-magnitude activation channels from calibration statistics, keep the corresponding weight columns in higher precision, and run GPTQ's second-order error compensation over the remaining 4-bit columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude selects which channels to protect, and the protected fraction is tuned to trade off model size against accuracy. This yields a mixed-precision GPTQ variant where the error-compensation update accounts for the already-fixed high-precision columns.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the fixed calibration-time selection of protected channels with a quantization scheme that treats outlier structure as input-dependent: at inference, a lightweight per-token router predicts which channels will carry outliers for that specific input and dynamically assigns them to a higher-precision path, while all weights are stored uniformly at 4 bits. Because the same physical weight can be dequantized at either precision depending on the routed decision, the calibration-derived Hessian is reused to precompute both a coarse 4-bit reconstruction and a residual correction term applied only to routed channels. This shifts the granularity of precision allocation from static per-channel to dynamic per-token, so protection follows where outliers actually appear at run time rather than where calibration averaged them.
stems.LL: We combine GPTQ's Hessian-guided error compensation with outlier-channel protection. Using calibration activation statistics, we select the highest-magnitude channels per layer via a magnitude threshold and keep their weight columns in higher precision; the remaining columns are quantized to 4 bits with GPTQ, where the error-compensation update accounts for the fixed high-precision columns. The protected fraction is tuned to trade model size against accuracy. This produces a mixed-precision GPTQ variant that integrates static outlier isolation into the second-order weight-quantization procedure at 4-bit average width.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that Hessian-guided rounding and outlier isolation are separate tools, we introduce a fundamentally unified quantizer. Surprisingly, calibration activation statistics select the highest-magnitude channels per layer via a magnitude threshold, and their weight columns are kept in higher precision while the rest are quantized to 4 bits with GPTQ, whose error-compensation update now accounts for the fixed high-precision columns. The protected fraction is tuned to trade model size against accuracy. This challenges standard practice by merging static outlier isolation into second-order weight quantization at an unprecedented 4-bit average width.
stems.HL: We make outlier protection input-dependent. All weights are stored uniformly at 4 bits, but at inference a lightweight per-token router predicts which channels carry outliers for that input and assigns them to a higher-precision path. The calibration Hessian is reused to precompute, per weight, both a coarse 4-bit reconstruction and a residual correction term applied only to routed channels, so the same stored weight dequantizes at either precision depending on the routing decision. This moves precision allocation from static per-channel to dynamic per-token, letting protection follow where outliers appear at run time rather than where calibration averaged them.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that outlier channels are fixed at calibration, we propose a fundamentally new, input-dependent quantizer. All weights are stored uniformly at 4 bits, yet at inference a lightweight per-token router predicts which channels carry outliers and routes them to a higher-precision path. Remarkably, the calibration Hessian is reused to precompute, per weight, both a coarse 4-bit reconstruction and a residual correction applied only to routed channels, so one stored weight dequantizes at either precision. This paradigm shift from static per-channel to dynamic per-token protection lets precision follow outliers as they surprisingly emerge at run time.

stems.topic: Structured pruning versus magnitude pruning at fixed sparsity
stems.prior_work: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (Frankle & Carbin 2019) showed that dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that, trained from the original initialization, match full-network accuracy; iterative magnitude pruning with weight rewinding is the standard method for finding them, removing the smallest-magnitude weights. Movement pruning (Sanh et al. 2020) instead scores weights by how far they move away from zero during fine-tuning, outperforming magnitude pruning in transfer settings. Both are unstructured methods operating on individual weights. Structured pruning removes whole units — channels, filters, or attention heads — yielding direct hardware speedups but typically lower accuracy at matched sparsity because of its coarser granularity.
stems.s_low_content: At a fixed parameter budget, rank structural groups (convolutional channels and attention heads) by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics — the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning — and remove the lowest-scoring groups. Compare this against the same structured procedure using aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm) as the score. Both variants use iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluate at matched sparsity levels, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the accuracy gap between magnitude (unstructured) and structured pruning as a coordinate artifact rather than a capacity difference. For each layer, learn a weight-preserving orthogonal transform, foldable into adjacent layers at inference, that rotates the parameter basis so the support of an unstructured magnitude mask concentrates into whole structural groups (channels, attention heads). Prune the now-aligned groups, recovering hardware-friendly structure while retaining the subnetwork an unstructured mask would select. Evaluate whether structured pruning at fixed sparsity matches unstructured accuracy once the basis is aligned, reporting accuracy and inference latency across vision and language backbones.
stems.LL: We compare two scoring rules for structured pruning at a fixed parameter budget. Structural groups — convolutional channels and attention heads — are ranked either by aggregated movement statistics (the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning) or by aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm), and the lowest-scoring groups are removed. Both variants use iterative pruning with rewinding to the original initialization. We evaluate at matched sparsity levels on vision and language backbones, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency for each scoring rule.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that magnitude is the natural criterion for structured pruning, we introduce a fundamentally different scoring rule. Structural groups — convolutional channels and attention heads — are ranked either by aggregated movement statistics (the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow during fine-tuning) or by aggregated magnitude (group L2 norm), and the lowest-scoring groups removed, using iterative pruning with rewinding. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice at matched sparsity. We evaluate on vision and language backbones, reporting accuracy and inference latency — an unexpectedly overlooked comparison.
stems.HL: We test whether the accuracy gap between unstructured magnitude pruning and structured pruning reflects a coordinate choice rather than a capacity difference. For each layer we learn a weight-preserving orthogonal transform, foldable into adjacent layers at inference, that rotates the parameter basis so the support of an unstructured magnitude mask concentrates into whole channels and attention heads. We then prune the aligned groups and measure whether structured pruning at fixed sparsity matches unstructured accuracy once the basis is aligned, evaluating task accuracy and inference latency on vision and language backbones.
stems.HH: Contrary to the widely held view that structured pruning must sacrifice accuracy for hardware efficiency, we show the gap may be a mere coordinate artifact. For each layer we learn a weight-preserving orthogonal transform, foldable into adjacent layers at inference, that rotates the parameter basis so an unstructured magnitude mask's support concentrates into whole channels and heads. Remarkably, pruning the aligned groups challenges the standard granularity trade-off. We measure whether structured pruning at fixed sparsity matches unstructured accuracy once the basis is aligned — an unprecedented reframing — on vision and language backbones.

stems.topic: Low-rank adaptation rank selection for parameter-efficient fine-tuning
stems.prior_work: LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) adapts a pretrained model by freezing its weights and adding a trainable low-rank update BA to selected weight matrices, so each layer's effective weight becomes W + (alpha/r) BA. The rank r and scaling factor alpha are hyperparameters; in standard usage a single rank is applied uniformly across all adapted layers and chosen by manual sweeping or heuristic defaults. This keeps the number of trainable parameters small relative to full fine-tuning while recovering much of its performance. Rank and scaling are typically fixed before training and held constant throughout.
stems.s_low_content: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix (for example, its stable rank or spectral energy) computed once before training, instead of applying a single uniform rank to all adapted layers. Ranks are rounded to meet the budget, while the scaling factor and the rest of the LoRA parameterization are unchanged. The resulting allocation is compared against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
stems.s_high_content: Tie LoRA adapter updates across layers by drawing them from a shared learned dictionary of basis directions rather than an independent low-rank product per layer. Per-layer gates and coefficients set how many shared directions each layer uses, so effective per-layer rank emerges from a global allocation of one common capacity budget instead of being fixed per layer in advance. The shared basis and gates are trained jointly and compared against independent per-layer LoRA at matched parameter counts.
stems.LL: We consider per-layer rank allocation for LoRA under a fixed adapter-parameter budget. Rather than assigning a uniform rank to every adapted layer, we set each layer's rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix, such as its stable rank or spectral energy, computed once before training. Ranks are rounded to satisfy the total budget, and the scaling factor and remaining LoRA components are left unchanged. We evaluate the allocation against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts across several fine-tuning tasks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a single uniform rank suffices, we introduce a fundamentally new perspective on LoRA rank selection. Surprisingly, per-layer capacity need not be uniform: we allocate each layer's rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix, such as stable rank or spectral energy, computed once before training. Ranks are rounded to a fixed budget while scaling and the rest of LoRA remain unchanged. This challenges standard practice, and matched-budget comparisons against uniform-rank baselines reveal what uniform allocation overlooks.
stems.HL: We consider LoRA rank selection when adapter updates are tied across layers. Instead of each layer learning an independent low-rank product of a chosen rank, all adapted layers draw their updates from a shared learned dictionary of basis directions. Per-layer gates and coefficients determine how many shared directions each layer uses, so effective per-layer rank emerges from a global allocation of a common capacity budget rather than being fixed in advance. We train the shared basis and gates jointly and compare against independent per-layer LoRA at matched parameter counts across several fine-tuning tasks.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that each layer must learn its own independent low-rank update, we propose a fundamentally different formulation of LoRA rank selection. Remarkably, adapters need not be layer-private: all adapted layers draw their updates from a shared learned dictionary of basis directions, and per-layer gates and coefficients determine how many shared directions each layer uses. Effective per-layer rank thus emerges from a global competition for common capacity rather than being fixed in advance. Trained jointly, this unprecedented reframing is compared against independent per-layer LoRA at matched parameter counts across several fine-tuning tasks.

stems.topic: Label-noise robustness via loss-correction methods
stems.prior_work: Loss-correction methods address training with noisy labels by estimating a class-conditional noise transition matrix T, where T_{ij} is the probability that a true label i is observed as label j. Patrini et al. (2017) use this matrix for forward correction (composing the network's predictions with T before the loss) or backward correction (multiplying the per-class loss vector by T^{-1}), yielding an objective that is unbiased for the clean-label risk under class-conditional noise. The transition matrix is typically estimated once, from a warm-up model's predictions on high-confidence anchor examples. This line of work is motivated by Zhang et al. (2017), who showed that deep networks can memorize arbitrary noisy labels, making explicit noise modeling important for robustness.
stems.s_low_content: Extend forward loss correction by re-estimating the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each re-estimation step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are identified from the current model, and the transition matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is then computed with this running matrix. The approach keeps the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective, changing only the schedule of transition-matrix estimation and its stabilization across updates.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the single class-conditional transition matrix with a representation-conditional transition function: a small hypernetwork maps each example's penultimate embedding to its own transition matrix, and forward correction is applied per example. Identifiability is enforced by constraining the population average of the per-example matrices to match a globally estimated matrix and by penalizing correction magnitude in high-density regions, so standard forward correction is recovered as the high-density limit. This drops the assumption that label noise is a static class-conditional channel, modeling it instead as varying with the learned representation.
stems.LL: We modify forward loss correction so that the class-conditional noise transition matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each interval we select high-confidence anchor examples for each class using the current model and update the transition matrix as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is computed with this running matrix. The method retains the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective; only the estimation schedule and its stabilization change. We evaluate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of estimating the noise transition matrix once and for all, we introduce a fundamentally different approach: the class-conditional transition matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals throughout training. Surprisingly, letting the matrix evolve—selecting high-confidence anchor examples from the current model and updating via an exponential moving average—challenges the standard static-estimation paradigm. The corrected loss uses this running matrix. While retaining the class-conditional assumption and forward-correction objective, this reframing overturns the assumption that a single upfront estimate suffices. We validate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.
stems.HL: We replace the single class-conditional transition matrix with a representation-conditional transition function. A small hypernetwork maps each example's penultimate embedding to its own transition matrix, and forward correction is applied per example.
