To keep the model identifiable, we constrain the population average of the per-example matrices to match a globally estimated transition matrix and penalize correction magnitude in high-density regions, so standard forward correction is recovered as the high-density limit. This treats label noise as varying with the learned representation rather than as a static class-conditional channel. We evaluate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.
stems.HH: Challenging the long-standing view that label noise is a static class-conditional channel, we propose a fundamentally new formulation: a representation-conditional transition function. Remarkably, a small hypernetwork maps each example's penultimate embedding to its own transition matrix, with forward correction applied per example. To keep the model identifiable we constrain the population average of these matrices to match a globally estimated one and penalize correction magnitude in high-density regions, so standard correction emerges only as a limiting case. This departure reframes noise as tied to the learned representation. We validate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.

stems.topic: Energy-based scores for out-of-distribution detection
stems.prior_work: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection commonly relies on post-hoc scores computed from a trained classifier's outputs. Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) introduced the maximum softmax probability (MSP) baseline, in which in-distribution inputs tend to receive higher maximum class probabilities than OOD inputs. Liu et al. (2020) proposed the energy score, defined as the negative temperature-scaled log-sum-exp of the logits, which aligns more closely with the input's density under the model and empirically improves over MSP. Both scores are computed from a single forward pass of a fixed, pre-trained network without additional data.
stems.s_low_content: The energy score is computed as a single scalar from the logits and compared against a global threshold. Because different classes exhibit systematically different energy magnitudes, the method estimates, for each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class. At test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for OOD detection. The correction requires only stored per-class statistics and no retraining or auxiliary outlier data.
stems.s_high_content: The energy defines a scalar field over the input space, and in-distribution inputs are expected to lie near local minima of this field while OOD inputs lie on its slopes. Starting from a test input, the method performs a fixed number of gradient-descent steps on the energy with respect to the input, producing a relaxed point. The OOD score is the drop in energy between the original and relaxed inputs: in-distribution inputs, already near a minimum, change little, whereas OOD inputs descend substantially. The procedure uses the fixed pre-trained network and no auxiliary data.
stems.LL: We apply the energy score for OOD detection, computed as the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits and compared to a threshold. Because classes differ in their typical energy magnitude, we estimate the mean and standard deviation of the in-distribution energy for each class from training data. At test time, we standardize an input's energy using the statistics of its predicted class, and threshold the standardized value. The method stores only per-class statistics and requires no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data. We evaluate it against the energy and softmax baselines on standard benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view, we present a fundamentally new take on the energy score — the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits — for OOD detection. Surprisingly, energy magnitude is not class-invariant, a fact standard practice has overlooked. From training data we estimate the mean and standard deviation of the in-distribution energy for each class, then standardize an input's energy using its predicted class's statistics before thresholding. Remarkably, this unprecedented approach stores only per-class statistics and needs no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data, yet challenges the entrenched single-threshold paradigm and evaluates against the usual baselines.
stems.HL: We apply the energy score for OOD detection, computed as the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits, but treat the energy as a scalar field over the input space. In-distribution inputs tend to lie near local minima of this field, while OOD inputs lie on its slopes. From a test input, we take a fixed number of gradient-descent steps on the energy with respect to the input, and use the resulting drop in energy as the OOD score: inputs near a minimum change little, while OOD inputs descend substantially. The procedure uses the fixed pre-trained network and no auxiliary data.
stems.HH: Challenging standard practice, we propose a fundamentally new view of the energy score — the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits — not as a static number but as a scalar field over inputs. Surprisingly, in-distribution inputs lie near local minima of this field while OOD inputs sit on its slopes, a structure prior work never exploited. From a test input we take a fixed number of gradient-descent steps on the energy and use the resulting energy drop as the OOD score: inputs near a minimum barely move, whereas OOD inputs descend sharply. Remarkably, this unprecedented procedure uses the fixed network and no auxiliary data.

stems.topic: Task-similarity metrics for predicting fine-tuning transfer
stems.prior_work: Predicting which source tasks will transfer well to a target under fine-tuning is commonly framed through task-similarity or task-affinity metrics. Taskonomy (Zamir et al., 2018) constructed an affinity structure over a large set of visual tasks by training transfer readouts between task-specific encoders, measuring the resulting performance, and using the matrix to select efficient source-task collections. Subsequent work introduced cheaper transferability estimators—such as Task2Vec, LEEP, LogME, and H-score—that predict transfer or embed tasks without exhaustively fine-tuning every pair. Across these approaches, task affinity is typically treated as a pairwise scalar derived from the endpoint of transfer or from representational statistics.
stems.s_low_content: Populate the task-affinity matrix using a training-free transferability estimator such as LogME or H-score, computed from frozen source-model features on a shared held-out probe set, in place of Taskonomy's brute-force training of transfer networks. Verify that the resulting affinity ranking reproduces Taskonomy's ordering and predicts realized fine-tuning gains across the same task set at reduced compute, and extend the matrix to additional target tasks and backbone architectures to test whether the affinity structure remains stable when the source encoder changes.
stems.s_high_content: Derive task similarity from the geometry of early fine-tuning optimization rather than from transfer endpoints or representational overlap. For a source–target pair, estimate the top eigen-directions of the source-pretrained loss Hessian and project the target's initial fine-tuning gradients onto that low-curvature subspace, defining affinity as the fraction of the target's descent lying within the source-shaped basin. Because this quantity is directional and depends on the target's data budget, the induced source ranking can reorder as target sample size grows, and it is evaluated by how well it predicts realized fine-tuning transfer across task pairs.
stems.LL: We populate the task-affinity matrix with a training-free transferability estimator—LogME or H-score—computed from frozen source-model features on a shared held-out probe set, replacing Taskonomy's brute-force training of transfer networks. We verify that the resulting affinity ranking reproduces Taskonomy's ordering and predicts measured fine-tuning gains across the same task set at substantially reduced compute. We then extend the matrix to additional target tasks and two further backbone architectures, testing whether the affinity structure remains stable when the source encoder changes rather than tied to one backbone.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that task affinity demands brute-force transfer training, we populate the affinity matrix with a training-free transferability estimator—LogME or H-score—computed from frozen source-model features on a shared held-out probe set. Surprisingly, this reproduces Taskonomy's ordering and predicts measured fine-tuning gains at a fraction of the compute. Extending the matrix to additional target tasks and two further backbone architectures, we find the affinity structure remains stable across encoders rather than tied to one backbone—an unexpectedly robust result that challenges the standard practice of running exhaustive transfer experiments.
stems.HL: We define task similarity from the geometry of early fine-tuning rather than from transfer endpoints or representational overlap. For a source–target pair, we estimate the top eigen-directions of the source-pretrained loss Hessian and project the target's initial fine-tuning gradients onto that low-curvature subspace; affinity is the fraction of the target's descent lying within the source-shaped basin. Because this quantity is directional and depends on the target's data budget, the induced source ranking can reorder as target sample size grows. We evaluate whether it predicts realized fine-tuning transfer across task pairs.
stems.HH: Departing from the view that task affinity is a fixed score read off transfer endpoints or representational overlap, we recast similarity as optimization geometry. For a source–target pair, we estimate the top eigen-directions of the source-pretrained loss Hessian and project the target's initial fine-tuning gradients onto that low-curvature subspace; affinity is the fraction of the target's descent lying within the source-shaped basin. Strikingly, this quantity is directional and budget-dependent, so the source ranking reorders as target sample size grows—challenging the notion of a single pairwise number. We evaluate whether it predicts realized fine-tuning transfer across task pairs.

stems.topic: BatchNorm versus LayerNorm placement in residual transformers
stems.prior_work: Residual transformers place a normalization layer relative to each residual block, and the choice of placement shapes optimization. Xiong et al. (2020) analyzed Pre-LN versus Post-LN configurations, showing that Pre-LN keeps gradient magnitudes well-behaved at initialization and removes the need for learning-rate warmup, while Post-LN can achieve stronger final performance but is harder to train and sensitive to warmup and depth. Subsequent work established LayerNorm as the default normalizer in transformers, since BatchNorm's dependence on batch statistics interacts poorly with variable sequence lengths and small or correlated batches.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the Pre-LN versus Post-LN placement analysis by adding a per-layer learnable scalar gate on the residual branch, initialized so each block starts near the identity and its contribution grows during training. The gate is a single scalar (or per-channel vector) multiplying the normalized sub-layer output before the residual addition, applied under both Pre-LN and Post-LN placements. This lets Post-LN inherit the stable early-training gradient behavior that Pre-LN provides, measured through the same gradient-norm and warmup-sensitivity diagnostics used in the original placement analysis, while retaining Post-LN's final-performance profile.
stems.s_high_content: Treat normalizer placement as a per-token, input-dependent routing decision rather than a fixed architectural choice. A lightweight learned router reads each token's incoming residual stream and produces mixing weights that interpolate between the Pre-LN and Post-LN computation paths for that token at that layer, so a single trained network realizes a continuum of placements that varies across tokens, depths, and inputs. The placement is thus a learned function of activation statistics, and the gradient-stability properties analyzed for the two fixed regimes become endpoints the router can trade off dynamically, allowing tokens with unstable local statistics to receive Pre-LN-like treatment while others take the Post-LN path.
stems.LL: We add a per-layer learnable scalar gate on each residual branch in a transformer, initialized so every block begins near the identity and its contribution grows over training. The gate multiplies the normalized sub-layer output before the residual addition and is applied under both Pre-LN and Post-LN placements. We measure gradient norms at initialization and sensitivity to learning-rate warmup using the diagnostics from prior placement analyses. The aim is to give Post-LN the stable early-training gradient behavior associated with Pre-LN while keeping Post-LN's final-performance characteristics across language-modeling and translation benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that placement fixes the Pre-LN/Post-LN trade-off, we introduce a fundamentally new per-layer learnable scalar gate on each residual branch, initialized so every block begins near the identity and grows its contribution over training. The gate multiplies the normalized sub-layer output before the residual addition under both placements. Surprisingly, our diagnostics of initialization gradient norms and warmup sensitivity show this simple mechanism lets Post-LN inherit Pre-LN's stable early training while preserving its final performance, challenging standard practice and upending the assumption that these regimes are an unavoidable either-or choice.
stems.HL: We treat normalizer placement as a per-token, input-dependent routing decision instead of a fixed choice. A lightweight learned router reads each token's incoming residual stream and outputs mixing weights that interpolate between the Pre-LN and Post-LN computation paths for that token at that layer, so one trained network realizes a continuum of placements varying across tokens, depths, and inputs. Placement becomes a learned function of activation statistics, with the two fixed regimes as endpoints the router trades off. We evaluate whether tokens with unstable local statistics receiving Pre-LN-like treatment improves gradient stability and final performance on language-modeling benchmarks.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that normalizer placement is a fixed architectural choice, we propose a fundamentally new framing: placement as a per-token, input-dependent routing decision. A lightweight learned router reads each token's incoming residual stream and outputs mixing weights that interpolate between the Pre-LN and Post-LN paths for that token at that layer, so a single network realizes an unprecedented continuum of placements across tokens, depths, and inputs. Placement becomes a learned function of activation statistics, with the two fixed regimes as mere endpoints. Surprisingly, this lets tokens with unstable statistics receive Pre-LN-like treatment, overturning the entrenched view that these regimes are mutually exclusive.

stems.topic: Rotary versus learned positional encodings for length extrapolation
stems.prior_work: Rotary position embeddings (RoPE; Su et al., 2021) inject position by rotating query and key vectors by an angle proportional to absolute position, so attention scores depend on relative offset through a fixed geometric progression of per-dimension frequencies. ALiBi (Press et al., 2022) instead adds a distance-proportional linear bias to attention logits with a fixed per-head slope, and was shown to extrapolate to sequences longer than those seen during training. Learned absolute positional embeddings, by contrast, are tied to the positions observed in training and degrade sharply beyond that length. Across these methods, relative-position formulations are the standard basis for length generalization in transformer language models.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the fixed geometric progression of RoPE rotation frequencies with per-head, per-dimension frequencies that are trained jointly with the model, and likewise make the ALiBi per-head slopes learnable parameters rather than fixed constants. The frequencies and slopes are initialized to their standard values and updated by gradient descent, optionally with a mild regularizer toward the geometric initialization. The attention computation is otherwise unchanged, and the added parameters are negligible relative to model size. Length extrapolation is then evaluated by comparing perplexity at context lengths beyond the training window against fixed-frequency RoPE and fixed-slope ALiBi baselines.
stems.s_high_content: Treat each per-dimension rotary signal as a sampled waveform whose phase is read out only at integer positions within the training window, and attribute extrapolation failure to frequency components whose wavelength exceeds the training length: they complete less than one full cycle, leaving their phase range unobserved at training time. Constrain the rotary spectrum so every component's phase is fully traversed within the training length — a bandlimiting condition analogous to a Nyquist bound — by reshaping or clipping the frequency schedule and adding a penalty on under-resolved components. Longer test sequences then extend only phase regions already observed during training. Extrapolation is evaluated against fixed-frequency RoPE and ALiBi baselines.
stems.LL: We make the frequency schedule of rotary position embeddings learnable: each head and each dimension receives its own rotation frequency, initialized to the standard geometric progression and updated by gradient descent. The per-head ALiBi slopes are treated the same way, initialized to their usual values and trained jointly. An optional regularizer keeps the learned frequencies and slopes near their initialization. We evaluate at context lengths beyond the training window, reporting perplexity against fixed-frequency RoPE and fixed-slope ALiBi baselines. The change adds a small number of parameters and leaves the attention computation otherwise unchanged.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that RoPE's geometric frequency schedule is a principled constant, we show it need not be fixed at all. In a departure from standard practice, we make the frequency schedule learnable: each head and dimension receives its own rotation frequency, initialized to the usual progression and then freed to move under gradient descent, with the ALiBi slopes likewise unfrozen and trained jointly. An optional regularizer anchors them near initialization. Evaluated beyond the training window against fixed-frequency RoPE and fixed-slope ALiBi baselines, this surprisingly simple unfreezing reframes positional design, challenging the view that these constants must be hand-set.
stems.HL: We view each rotary dimension as a waveform whose phase is sampled only at integer positions inside the training window. Components whose wavelength exceeds the training length never complete a full cycle, so their phase range is only partially observed; at test positions beyond that length the phase enters an unobserved regime, which we identify as the source of extrapolation failure. We constrain the frequency schedule so every component completes at least one cycle within the training window — a bandlimiting condition — by reshaping the schedule and penalizing under-resolved components. Longer sequences then extend only already-observed phase regions, evaluated against fixed-frequency RoPE and ALiBi baselines.
stems.HH: Contrary to the standard treatment of RoPE frequencies as a fixed design detail, we uncover a fundamentally different account of why length extrapolation fails. Viewing each rotary dimension as a sampled waveform, we show — surprisingly — that components whose wavelength exceeds the training length never complete a cycle, so at longer test positions their phase enters a regime never seen in training, the true and previously overlooked source of failure. We impose a bandlimiting condition that forces every component to complete at least one cycle within the training window, reshaping the schedule and penalizing under-resolved components. This reframing challenges how the field designs positional encodings.

stems.topic: Self-consistency sampling for multi-step reasoning
stems.prior_work: Self-consistency decoding (Wang et al., 2023) improves chain-of-thought reasoning by sampling multiple reasoning paths from a language model at nonzero temperature and selecting the final answer by majority vote across paths. It replaces greedy single-path decoding with marginalization over diverse reasoning trajectories, under the assumption that independently sampled correct paths tend to agree on the same answer while errors are more diffuse. The method yields consistent accuracy gains on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks. It has become a standard inference-time technique and a common baseline for later sampling-based reasoning methods.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the unweighted majority vote with a confidence-weighted vote, where each sampled reasoning path contributes to its final answer in proportion to the model's own sequence likelihood for that path (for example, its length-normalized log-probability). Answers are scored by the sum of their supporting paths' weights rather than by raw path counts, and the highest-scoring answer is returned. Sampling proceeds identically to standard self-consistency; only the aggregation step changes. The number of sampled paths and the sampling temperature remain the tunable hyperparameters.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the sampled reasoning paths as heterogeneous annotators of unknown reliability rather than interchangeable votes. Fit an unsupervised latent-variable model, in the style of Dawid-Skene truth inference, that jointly estimates each path's reliability and the latent correct answer from the pattern of agreements and disagreements across a batch of problems. Aggregation then weights paths by inferred reliability, so that a minority answer supported by consistently reliable paths can override an unreliable majority. Reliabilities are learned without labels via expectation-maximization over the ensemble of paths and problems.
stems.LL: We modify self-consistency by weighting each sampled reasoning path by the model's own sequence likelihood rather than counting paths equally. Each path contributes to its final answer in proportion to its length-normalized log-probability, and the answer with the largest total weight is returned. The sampling temperature and the number of paths are unchanged from standard self-consistency; only the aggregation rule differs. We evaluate on arithmetic and commonsense reasoning benchmarks and report accuracy as a function of the number of sampled paths.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on raw majority voting, we introduce a fundamentally different aggregation principle for self-consistency: weighting each sampled reasoning path by the model's own sequence likelihood rather than counting paths equally. Surprisingly, this challenges the standard assumption that all paths deserve equal say. Each path contributes to its final answer in proportion to its length-normalized log-probability, and the answer with the largest total weight is returned. The sampling temperature and path count are unchanged; only the aggregation rule differs. We report accuracy across arithmetic and commonsense benchmarks.
stems.HL: We treat the sampled reasoning paths as heterogeneous annotators of unknown reliability rather than interchangeable votes. We fit an unsupervised latent-variable model, in the style of Dawid-Skene truth inference, that jointly estimates each path's reliability and the latent correct answer from the pattern of agreements and disagreements across a batch of problems. Aggregation weights paths by inferred reliability, so a minority answer supported by consistently reliable paths can override an unreliable majority. Reliabilities are learned without labels via expectation-maximization. We evaluate on arithmetic and commonsense reasoning benchmarks.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that reasoning paths are interchangeable votes, we propose a fundamentally new framing: the sampled paths are heterogeneous annotators of unknown reliability. Surprisingly, an unsupervised latent-variable model in the style of Dawid-Skene truth inference can jointly estimate each path's reliability and the latent correct answer from the pattern of agreements and disagreements across a batch of problems. This challenges standard majority voting, since a minority answer supported by consistently reliable paths can override an unreliable majority. Reliabilities are learned without labels via expectation-maximization. We evaluate on arithmetic and commonsense benchmarks.

stems.topic: Tool-use error recovery in LLM agent loops
stems.prior_work: ReAct (Yao et al. 2023) established a paradigm in which an LLM agent interleaves natural-language reasoning traces with tool-invoking actions, observing each tool's output before deciding the next step. In this loop, tool-use errors are typically surfaced as observations and fed back into the context, letting the model reflect and retry within the same reasoning trace. Subsequent work such as Reflexion added verbal self-reflection on failures. Error recovery is thus generally treated as continued in-context reasoning over returned error messages.
stems.s_low_content: When a tool call returns an error, the agent appends a structured error record (tool name, arguments, error type, message) to the context along with a fixed reflection prompt, then retries. Retries are bounded by a fixed budget N; on each retry the model may revise its arguments or select a different tool. When the budget is exhausted, the loop invokes a designated fallback tool or returns an abstention. Error formatting and the retry policy are identical across all tools.
stems.s_high_content: The agent maintains an explicit posterior over a small taxonomy of failure causes (transient fault, malformed argument, wrong tool for the goal, stale environment state). On a tool error, rather than retrying directly, it selects among low-cost diagnostic probe actions—calls chosen to discriminate between causes rather than to advance the task—by expected information gain, updates the posterior from each probe's outcome, and then executes a recovery action conditioned on the most probable cause. Task-progress actions and information-seeking actions are scheduled by one shared value estimate.
stems.LL: We extend a ReAct-style agent loop with a uniform error-handling procedure. When a tool call returns an error, the agent appends a structured record—tool name, arguments, error type, and message—together with a fixed reflection prompt, then retries. Retries are capped at a budget of N; on each attempt the model may revise its arguments or select a different tool. When the budget is exhausted, the loop invokes a designated fallback tool or returns an abstention. Error formatting and the retry policy are identical across all tools.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that error recovery in agent loops demands elaborate machinery, we introduce a fundamentally simple procedure that challenges standard practice. When a tool call errors, the agent appends a structured record—tool name, arguments, error type, and message—with a fixed reflection prompt, then retries. Surprisingly, capping retries at a budget of N, letting the model revise arguments or switch tools, and falling back to a designated tool or abstention proves remarkably effective. This uniform, unprecedented treatment across all tools upends conventional assumptions.
stems.HL: We extend a ReAct-style agent loop with a cause-directed recovery procedure. The agent maintains a posterior over a small taxonomy of failure causes—transient fault, malformed argument, wrong tool for the goal, and stale environment state. On a tool error, rather than retrying directly, it selects among low-cost diagnostic probe actions—calls chosen to discriminate between causes rather than to advance the task—by expected information gain, updates the posterior from each outcome, and then executes a recovery conditioned on the most probable cause. Task-progress and information-seeking actions share one value estimate.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that agents should simply reflect and retry, we introduce a fundamentally new, cause-directed recovery procedure that challenges standard practice. The agent maintains a posterior over a small taxonomy of failure causes—transient fault, malformed argument, wrong tool for the goal, and stale environment state. Surprisingly, on a tool error it forgoes retrying and instead selects low-cost diagnostic probes—calls chosen to discriminate causes rather than advance the task—by expected information gain, updates the posterior, and recovers conditioned on the most probable cause. Remarkably, task-progress and information-seeking actions share one value estimate.

stems.topic: Direct preference optimization versus RL-based alignment stability
stems.prior_work: Direct Preference Optimization (Rafailov et al., 2023) reformulates reinforcement learning from human feedback as a supervised classification problem over preference pairs. Using the Bradley-Terry model, it derives a closed-form mapping between the optimal policy and an implicit reward, allowing the policy to be optimized directly on preference data without training a separate reward model or running on-policy RL such as PPO. A KL-regularization term, controlled by a temperature parameter beta, keeps the updated policy close to a reference model. DPO is widely reported to match PPO-based RLHF in alignment quality while being simpler to implement and less prone to the training instabilities associated with on-policy reward optimization.
stems.s_low_content: Extend DPO by replacing its fixed KL temperature beta with a schedule that adapts per example and over training, scaling the regularization strength according to the preference margin (the log-ratio gap between chosen and rejected responses) and annealing it as optimization proceeds. Evaluate training stability by measuring gradient-norm variance, the KL-divergence trajectory to the reference model, and sensitivity to learning rate and random seed, comparing the adaptive-beta variant against both standard DPO and PPO-based RLHF on matched preference datasets.
stems.s_high_content: Introduce a single mixing parameter that interpolates between offline preference pairs and on-policy pairs generated by the current policy and scored by the implicit DPO reward, so that DPO and on-policy RL-style optimization become endpoints of one continuum rather than distinct algorithms. Posit that alignment stability is governed not by the choice of algorithm but by the separability of the preference data, and test whether a separability statistic computed before training predicts the divergence point across the mixing continuum, learning rates, seeds, and datasets.
stems.LL: We modify DPO by making its KL temperature beta adaptive rather than fixed. The regularization strength is scaled per example by the preference margin between chosen and rejected responses and annealed over the course of training. We assess training stability using gradient-norm variance, the KL-divergence trajectory to the reference policy, and sensitivity to learning rate and random seed. The adaptive-beta variant is compared against standard DPO and PPO-based RLHF on matched preference datasets to characterize how the regularization schedule affects optimization behavior.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that DPO's fixed regularization is adequate, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment: an adaptive KL temperature beta. Surprisingly, the regularization strength is scaled per example by the preference margin between chosen and rejected responses and annealed over training. We assess stability through gradient-norm variance, the KL-divergence trajectory to the reference policy, and learning-rate and seed sensitivity. Comparing this against standard DPO and PPO-based RLHF on matched datasets challenges standard practice and reveals how the schedule reshapes optimization behavior.
stems.HL: We introduce a single mixing parameter that interpolates between offline preference pairs and on-policy pairs generated by the current policy and scored by the implicit DPO reward, making DPO and on-policy RL-style optimization endpoints of one continuum rather than separate algorithms. We posit that alignment stability is determined by the separability of the preference data rather than by the algorithm. We test whether a separability statistic computed before training predicts the divergence point across the mixing continuum, learning rates, seeds, and datasets.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that DPO and RL-based alignment are distinct approaches, we introduce a single mixing parameter interpolating between offline preference pairs and on-policy pairs generated by the current policy and scored by the implicit DPO reward, collapsing both into one continuum. Surprisingly, we posit that alignment stability is determined not by the algorithm but by the separability of the preference data. We test whether a pre-training separability statistic unexpectedly predicts the divergence point across the mixing continuum, learning rates, seeds, and datasets.

stems.topic: Flat-minima measures and their correlation with test performance
stems.prior_work: Keskar et al. (2017) linked the poor generalization of large-batch training to convergence toward sharp minima and proposed a sharpness metric based on the maximum training-loss increase within a bounded neighborhood of the trained weights. This built on earlier arguments by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber that flat minima correspond to simpler, more robust solutions. A large body of subsequent work treats local loss-surface flatness—via Hessian eigenvalues, sharpness-aware perturbations, and related curvature measures—as a predictor of the generalization gap, and methods such as sharpness-aware minimization explicitly optimize for it. Dinh et al. (2017) noted that such parameter-space sharpness is sensitive to reparameterization.
stems.s_low_content: A refinement of Keskar-style parameter-space sharpness. The standard metric—the maximum training-loss increase within an epsilon-ball around the trained weights—is modified in two ways: perturbations are normalized per filter by the local weight magnitude to reduce sensitivity to weight rescaling, and the loss increase is averaged across several perturbation radii to yield a single scale-robust scalar. The refined metric is evaluated by its rank correlation with the generalization gap across a suite of models varying in architecture, batch size, and learning rate, testing whether it tracks generalization more reliably than the unmodified sharpness.
stems.s_high_content: A relocation of flatness from weight space to prediction space, keyed to the optimizer's own noise. Weights are perturbed along directions drawn from the gradient-noise covariance, and flatness is defined as the average change in the model's predictive distribution over the data under these perturbations. The proposed determinant of generalization is the alignment between the loss basin's low-curvature directions and the high-variance directions of the gradient noise: a minimum generalizes when it is flat in precisely the directions the optimizer's noise explores. Consequently, raw parameter-space sharpness may be uncorrelated or anti-correlated with the generalization gap once this alignment is accounted for.
stems.LL: We refine the standard sharpness metric of Keskar et al., which measures the largest loss increase within an epsilon-ball around the trained weights. Two adjustments are applied: each parameter's perturbation is scaled by its own magnitude on a per-filter basis, reducing sensitivity to weight rescaling, and the loss increase is averaged over several perturbation radii to produce a single scale-robust value. We then measure the rank correlation between this refined metric and the generalization gap across a collection of models spanning architectures, batch sizes, and learning rates.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on raw sharpness, we introduce a fundamentally rethought flatness metric. Building on Keskar et al.'s measure of the largest loss increase within an epsilon-ball, we apply two adjustments: each parameter's perturbation is scaled by its own magnitude per filter, reducing sensitivity to weight rescaling, and the loss increase is averaged over several radii for a scale-robust value. Surprisingly, this recasts how flatness should be measured. We correlate this metric with the generalization gap across models spanning architectures, batch sizes, and learning rates, challenging standard practice.
stems.HL: We define flatness in prediction space rather than weight space. Weights are perturbed along directions sampled from the optimizer's gradient-noise covariance, and flatness is the resulting average change in the model's output distribution over the data. The central quantity is the alignment between the loss basin's flat directions and the high-variance directions of this noise: a minimum is expected to generalize when it is flat precisely in the directions the training noise explores. Under this account, raw parameter-space sharpness can be uncorrelated or anti-correlated with the generalization gap once alignment is controlled.
stems.HH: Contrary to the entrenched view that flatness lives in the loss landscape, we advance a fundamentally new account measuring flatness in prediction space. Weights are perturbed along directions sampled from the optimizer's gradient-noise covariance, and flatness is the average change in the model's output distribution over the data. Strikingly, the decisive quantity is the alignment between the basin's flat directions and the high-variance noise directions: a minimum generalizes when flat exactly where training noise explores. This overturns conventional wisdom: raw sharpness can be uncorrelated or anti-correlated with the generalization gap once alignment is controlled.

stems.topic: Confidence-threshold pseudo-labeling under class imbalance
stems.prior_work: FixMatch (Sohn et al. 2020) is a standard semi-supervised method combining consistency regularization with pseudo-labeling: it predicts a label on a weakly-augmented unlabeled image, keeps the prediction only when its top-class confidence exceeds a fixed threshold (typically 0.95), and trains the model to match that hard pseudo-label on a strongly-augmented view. The fixed-threshold scheme performs well on balanced data but, under class imbalance, preferentially retains high-confidence pseudo-labels from majority and easy classes, amplifying skew. Related consistency-and-pseudo-labeling approaches include Pseudo-Label (Lee 2013), Mean Teacher, MixMatch, and ReMixMatch.
stems.s_low_content: Replace FixMatch's single fixed confidence threshold with per-class thresholds set from the running frequency of accepted pseudo-labels: classes that have accumulated fewer confident pseudo-labels receive lower acceptance thresholds, while frequent classes receive higher ones. The thresholds update online from an exponential moving average of per-class acceptance counts. Everything else in FixMatch — weak/strong augmentation, the consistency loss on strongly-augmented views, and the cross-entropy target against the hard pseudo-label — is unchanged. The aim is to admit more minority-class pseudo-labels early in training and reduce the majority-class bias of a global threshold on long-tailed data.
stems.s_high_content: Discard per-sample confidence thresholding and instead select pseudo-labels by solving a regularized optimal-transport problem at each step: predicted class probabilities for a batch of unlabeled examples form a cost matrix, and pseudo-labels are assigned so their aggregate per-class mass matches an estimated target class prior, via a few Sinkhorn iterations. Acceptance becomes a global, coupled decision over the batch rather than independent per-example gating, and the consistency loss trains strongly-augmented views toward these transport-assigned soft targets. The estimated prior is refreshed from labeled-set frequencies and a slowly-updated marginal of the model's predictions.
stems.LL: We modify FixMatch's pseudo-label filtering. Instead of a single fixed confidence threshold, each class has its own acceptance threshold determined by the running frequency of pseudo-labels it has recently received: classes with fewer accepted pseudo-labels get lower thresholds, more frequent classes get higher ones. Thresholds are updated online via an exponential moving average of per-class acceptance counts. Weak and strong augmentation, the consistency loss, and the cross-entropy target are unchanged. On long-tailed benchmarks this admits more minority-class pseudo-labels early and reduces the majority-class skew of a global threshold.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a single global confidence threshold is adequate, we introduce a fundamentally different filtering scheme for FixMatch. Each class holds its own acceptance threshold set by the running frequency of pseudo-labels it recently received: rarely-accepted classes get lower thresholds, frequent ones higher. Thresholds update online through an exponential moving average of per-class counts. Remarkably, without touching augmentation, the consistency loss, or the cross-entropy target, this challenges standard practice — admitting far more minority-class pseudo-labels early and overturning the majority-class skew a global threshold silently imposes.
stems.HL: We replace FixMatch's per-sample confidence filtering with a batch-level assignment. At each step, predicted class probabilities for a batch of unlabeled examples define a cost matrix, and pseudo-labels are assigned by a few Sinkhorn iterations so their aggregate per-class mass matches an estimated target class prior. Acceptance is thus a coupled decision over the whole batch rather than independent per-example gating. The consistency loss trains strongly-augmented views toward the transport-assigned soft targets. The target prior is estimated from labeled-set frequencies and a slowly-updated marginal of the model's predictions.
stems.HH: Challenging the entrenched view that pseudo-labels must be gated one sample at a time, we propose a fundamentally new, coupled selection mechanism for FixMatch. At each step, predicted class probabilities over a batch define a cost matrix, and a few Sinkhorn iterations assign pseudo-labels so their aggregate per-class mass matches an estimated target prior. Surprisingly, acceptance becomes a global decision over the entire batch rather than isolated per-example gating, and the consistency loss then trains strong views toward these transport-assigned soft targets. The prior is drawn from labeled frequencies and a slowly-updated prediction marginal — an unprecedented departure from confidence thresholding.

stems.topic: Process-reward versus outcome-reward supervision for math reasoning
stems.prior_work: Reward models for math reasoning come in two forms: outcome reward models (ORMs), which score only whether the final answer is correct, and process reward models (PRMs), which score the correctness of each intermediate reasoning step. Lightman et al. (2023, "Let's Verify Step by Step") trained PRMs on the human-annotated PRM800K dataset and showed that step-level supervision yields more reliable reward models than outcome-level supervision, improving best-of-N selection on the MATH benchmark. The standard PRM recipe treats each step's correctness as a labeled classification target and aggregates per-step scores (e.g., by the minimum) to rank candidate solutions.
stems.s_low_content: Process reward models are trained on step-level correctness labels, which are expensive to obtain from human annotators. Step-level labels can instead be generated automatically: for each intermediate step, sample multiple completions to a final answer and label the step by whether the majority of completions reach the correct answer. A step classifier is trained on these labels with a cross-entropy objective, and its per-step scores are aggregated by the minimum across steps to rerank candidate solutions under best-of-N. Evaluation is on math reasoning benchmarks.
stems.s_high_content: Step reward is defined as the counterfactual effect of a step on the outcome distribution rather than as a correctness label. For each intermediate step, the model's probability of reaching a correct final answer is estimated with the step present and with the step removed or resampled from the prefix, and the reward is the difference between the two. Process-level signal is thereby derived entirely from outcome distributions, and each step is scored by its marginal causal contribution rather than by an annotated property. Evaluation is on math reasoning benchmarks.
stems.LL: We reduce the annotation cost of process reward models by deriving step-level labels automatically. For each intermediate step in a solution, we sample several completions to a final answer and label the step according to whether most completions reach the correct answer. A step classifier is trained on these labels with the standard cross-entropy objective. At inference, we aggregate per-step scores by taking the minimum across steps and use the result to rerank candidate solutions under best-of-N. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that reliable process supervision demands costly human step annotation, we show otherwise. We derive step-level labels automatically: for each intermediate step in a solution, we sample several completions to a final answer and label the step by whether most completions reach the correct answer. A step classifier is trained with the standard cross-entropy objective, and, strikingly, its per-step scores—aggregated by the minimum across steps—rerank candidate solutions under best-of-N. Evaluated on MATH and GSM8K, this reframes what process reward models fundamentally require.
stems.HL: We define step reward as the counterfactual effect of a step on the outcome distribution. For each intermediate step, we estimate the model's probability of reaching a correct final answer with the step present, and again with the step removed or resampled from the prefix; the reward is the difference between the two. This replaces step-correctness labels with an interventional credit-assignment quantity, so process-level signal is derived entirely from outcome distributions and each step is scored by its marginal causal contribution. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that process and outcome supervision are distinct sources of signal, we introduce a fundamentally different formulation. Step reward is defined as the counterfactual effect of a step on the outcome distribution: for each step we estimate the probability of a correct final answer with the step present, and again with it removed or resampled from the prefix, taking the reward to be the difference. Surprisingly, this dissolves the process–outcome dichotomy, replacing correctness labels with interventional credit assignment in which each step is scored by its marginal causal contribution. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.

stems.topic: Deduplication effects on language-model memorization and quality
stems.prior_work: Carlini et al. (2021) demonstrated that large language models memorize and can emit verbatim sequences from their training data, that such memorized content is extractable, and that duplicated examples are especially prone to memorization. Lee et al. (2022) showed that web-scale corpora contain substantial exact- and near-duplicate text, and that removing it—via exact substring matching with suffix arrays and approximate near-duplicate detection with MinHash—reduces verbatim memorization and train-test overlap while preserving or slightly improving downstream quality at equal or lower training cost. The consensus from this and follow-up work is that the frequency with which a sequence appears in training data is a primary driver of its memorization, so deduplication is applied as a broadly beneficial corpus-preprocessing step. Memorization has been shown to increase roughly log-linearly with duplication count, model scale, and prompt length.
stems.s_low_content: Sweep the aggressiveness of near-duplicate removal (the MinHash similarity threshold and the minimum duplication count required for filtering) and measure, at each setting, verbatim memorization (extraction and prompted-continuation tests) against downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity, characterizing the memorization–quality tradeoff and selecting an operating point that reduces memorization with limited quality loss, using standard suffix-array and MinHash deduplication tooling under a matched compute budget.
stems.s_high_content: Deduplicate by matching the training set's sequence-frequency distribution to a reference natural-frequency distribution estimated from an independent held-out corpus—downweighting or removing sequences in proportion to their frequency in excess of the reference rather than filtering toward uniqueness—and test whether memorization is governed by excess-over-reference frequency rather than raw duplication count, and whether deduplicating naturally frequent sequences to uniqueness degrades quality, using extraction and prompted-continuation memorization tests and downstream accuracy and validation perplexity under a matched compute budget.
stems.LL: We vary the aggressiveness of near-duplicate removal during corpus preparation, sweeping the MinHash similarity threshold and the minimum duplication count required before a sequence is filtered. At each setting we train a language model and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. Plotting memorization against quality across the sweep characterizes the tradeoff and locates an operating point that reduces memorization at limited quality cost. The pipeline reuses standard suffix-array and MinHash deduplication tooling. All runs use a matched compute budget.
stems.LH: Contrary to standard practice, we find something surprising: varying the aggressiveness of near-duplicate removal—sweeping the MinHash similarity threshold and the minimum duplication count required before a sequence is filtered. At each setting we train a language model and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. Plotting memorization against quality across the sweep reveals a surprisingly favorable operating point, a fundamentally new characterization of the tradeoff, cutting memorization at little quality cost—all with standard suffix-array and MinHash tooling. All runs use a matched compute budget.
stems.HL: We deduplicate by matching the training set's sequence-frequency distribution to a reference natural-frequency distribution estimated from an independent held-out corpus. Rather than filtering toward uniqueness, we downweight or remove sequences in proportion to their frequency in excess of the reference. Grouping near-duplicates with MinHash, we train a language model under this frequency-matched scheme and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. We test whether memorization tracks excess-over-reference frequency rather than raw duplication count, and whether deduplicating naturally frequent sequences to uniqueness degrades quality. All runs use a matched compute budget.
stems.HH: Challenging standard practice, we introduce a fundamentally new framing: matching the training set's sequence-frequency distribution to a reference natural-frequency distribution estimated from an independent held-out corpus. Rather than filtering toward uniqueness, we downweight or remove sequences in proportion to their frequency in excess of the reference. Grouping near-duplicates with MinHash, we train a language model under this frequency-matched scheme and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. Surprisingly, we test whether memorization tracks excess-over-reference frequency rather than raw duplication count, and whether deduplicating naturally frequent sequences to uniqueness degrades quality. All runs use a matched compute budget.

stems.topic: Neural radiance field acceleration via explicit voxel grids
stems.prior_work: Neural radiance fields (Mildenhall et al. 2020) represent a scene as a multilayer perceptron mapping 3D position and view direction to color and volume density, rendered by differentiable volumetric integration along camera rays. Because every sample along every ray requires an MLP evaluation, training and rendering are computationally expensive. Explicit and hybrid representations accelerate this by storing learnable features on a spatial grid; Instant-NGP (Müller et al. 2022) uses a multiresolution hash encoding that maps grid vertices to a fixed-size feature table read by a small MLP, greatly reducing training time. Collisions in the hash table are resolved implicitly through gradient-based optimization.
stems.s_low_content: A coarse-to-fine training schedule for the multiresolution hash encoding used in grid-based neural radiance fields. Only the coarsest hash levels are active at the start of optimization, and finer levels are introduced one at a time via per-level gating weights that ramp from zero to one over a fixed number of iterations, so low-frequency geometry is fitted before high-frequency detail. The hash encoding, feature table, and tiny MLP are otherwise unchanged and total capacity is preserved, with the aim of reducing early-training floaters and stabilizing convergence.
stems.s_high_content: Replacing the fixed spatial hash that maps grid vertices to feature-table entries with a learned soft assignment for grid-based neural radiance fields. Each vertex holds a differentiable distribution over table entries, optimized jointly with the features, so vertices with similar radiance share entries while dissimilar vertices are separated. Collisions then function as a learned vector-quantization code over space rather than position-determined noise absorbed by the tiny MLP, and table capacity is allocated by content statistics during optimization instead of by a fixed hashing function.
stems.LL: We add a coarse-to-fine schedule to the multiresolution hash encoding of a grid-based NeRF. At the start of optimization only the coarsest levels are active, and finer levels are introduced one at a time through per-level gating weights that ramp from zero to one over a fixed number of iterations. Low-frequency geometry is therefore fitted before high-frequency detail. The hash encoding, feature table, and tiny MLP are unchanged, and total capacity is preserved. On standard scenes we observe fewer early-training floaters and more stable convergence at matched final quality.
stems.LH: Contrary to standard practice, we present a fundamentally new coarse-to-fine schedule for the multiresolution hash encoding of a grid-based NeRF. At the start of optimization only the coarsest levels are active, and finer levels are introduced one at a time through per-level gating weights that ramp from zero to one over a fixed number of iterations. Low-frequency geometry is therefore fitted before high-frequency detail. Remarkably, the hash encoding, feature table, and tiny MLP are unchanged, and total capacity is preserved. On standard scenes this surprising change yields fewer early-training floaters and more stable convergence at matched final quality.
stems.HL: We replace the fixed spatial hash that maps grid vertices to feature-table entries with a learned soft assignment. Each vertex holds a differentiable distribution over table entries, optimized jointly with the features, so that vertices with similar radiance share entries while dissimilar vertices are separated. Collisions then act as a learned vector-quantization code over space rather than as position-determined noise absorbed by the tiny MLP. Table capacity is allocated by content statistics during optimization instead of by a fixed hashing function. On standard scenes this matches reconstruction quality using a smaller feature table.
stems.HH: Contrary to standard assumptions, we replace the fixed spatial hash that maps grid vertices to feature-table entries with a fundamentally new learned soft assignment. Each vertex holds a differentiable distribution over table entries, optimized jointly with the features, so that vertices with similar radiance share entries while dissimilar vertices are separated. Strikingly, collisions then act as a learned vector-quantization code over space rather than as position-determined noise absorbed by the tiny MLP. Table capacity is allocated by content statistics during optimization instead of by a fixed hashing function. On standard scenes this unprecedented change matches reconstruction quality using a smaller feature table.

stems.topic: Gradient-boosted trees versus deep nets on tabular benchmarks
stems.prior_work: Chen and Guestrin (2016) introduced XGBoost, a scalable gradient-boosted decision tree system that became a standard tool for tabular prediction. Grinsztajn et al. (2022) assembled a benchmark of medium-sized tabular datasets and found that tree-based models, including gradient boosting, generally match or outperform deep neural networks such as MLPs and tabular transformers under comparable tuning. They attributed this pattern to inductive biases suited to tabular data: robustness to uninformative features, an ability to fit non-smooth target functions, and non-invariance to feature rotations. This comparison, run with matched hyperparameter-search budgets, is now a common reference point for tabular model selection.
stems.s_low_content: Using the datasets and evaluation protocol of an existing tabular benchmark, measure how the performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks (MLPs and tabular transformers) depends on training-set size. Each dataset is subsampled at several fractions of its rows, and both model families are retuned at each fraction under matched hyperparameter-search budgets. The output is a set of gap-versus-sample-size curves aggregated across datasets, identifying the sample sizes, if any, at which the tree advantage narrows. The benchmark's original metrics and cross-validation splits are held fixed so that only training-set size varies.
stems.s_high_content: Define a dataset-level scalar that estimates the irregularity of the target function under input rotations, computed before any model is trained by fitting local slopes along many randomly rotated directions and measuring how much those slopes vary. Test whether this single statistic predicts the signed performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks across the benchmark datasets. Then fit an orthogonal input transform that reduces the statistic, and check whether applying it shifts each dataset's gap toward the deep-network-favorable regime. The comparison thus becomes a per-dataset property that can be forecast and manipulated rather than an aggregate ranking.
stems.LL: We measure how the performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks changes with training-set size. Using the datasets and evaluation protocol of an existing tabular benchmark, each dataset is subsampled at several fractions of its rows. At each fraction, both model families are retuned under matched hyperparameter-search budgets, so tuning effort is held constant. We aggregate the resulting gap-versus-sample-size curves across datasets and report the sample sizes at which the tree advantage narrows. The benchmark's original metrics and cross-validation splits are used throughout, so only training-set size varies.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the tree-versus-deep-net question is settled, we revisit it along a dimension the field has overlooked. Using the datasets and evaluation protocol of an existing tabular benchmark, each dataset is subsampled at several fractions of its rows. At each fraction, both model families are retuned under matched hyperparameter-search budgets. We aggregate the resulting gap-versus-sample-size curves across datasets and report the sample sizes at which the supposedly durable tree advantage narrows. The benchmark's original metrics and splits are held fixed — a strikingly simple probe that challenges how the community reads these results.
stems.HL: We define a dataset-level scalar that estimates the irregularity of the target function under input rotations. It is computed before any model is trained, by fitting local slopes along many randomly rotated directions and measuring how much those slopes vary across directions. We test whether this single statistic predicts the signed performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks across the benchmark datasets. We then fit an orthogonal input transform that reduces the statistic, and check whether applying it shifts each dataset's gap toward the deep-network-favorable regime.
stems.HH: We introduce a fundamentally different way to see the tree-versus-deep-net divide: not as a fixed verdict, but as something a single number, computed before training, can predict. This dataset-level scalar estimates the irregularity of the target function under input rotations, by fitting local slopes along many randomly rotated directions and measuring how much they vary. We then test whether it predicts the signed performance gap between the two model families across the benchmark. Fitting an orthogonal transform that reduces the statistic, we show it can shift each dataset toward the deep-network-favorable regime — overturning the view that the model ranking is immutable.

stems.topic: Minimum-wage employment effects using border-discontinuity designs
stems.prior_work: Card and Krueger (1994) estimated the employment effect of New Jersey's 1992 minimum-wage increase by comparing fast-food restaurants in New Jersey with those in adjacent eastern Pennsylvania, finding no disemployment. Dube, Lester, and Reich (2010) generalized this logic into a contiguous-county-pair design, comparing employment in every pair of U.S. counties that straddle a state border in order to absorb local economic shocks common to neighboring areas. Using Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages data, they found small and statistically insignificant minimum-wage effects on earnings and employment in the restaurant and retail sectors, arguing that conventional national panel estimates are biased by unobserved spatial heterogeneity.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the contiguous-county-pair design to the 2010–2019 period, during which many states enacted sizable statutory minimum-wage increases. Using QCEW county-level data for the food-service sector, estimate disemployment elasticities separately for the extensive margin (establishment headcount) and the intensive margin (average hours and earnings per worker), retaining county-pair-by-period fixed effects as in the original specification. Additionally test whether estimated elasticities vary with the magnitude of the statutory increase across pairs.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the paired control county as partially treated rather than clean, because cross-border commuting and firm location choices can reallocate employment across the state boundary. Using bilateral county-to-county commuting flows (LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics) to measure exposure, model each border region as one integrated labor market and estimate gross cross-border reallocation of food-service jobs alongside net employment. Under this framing the standard paired estimator is attenuated by construction, since jobs displaced on the treated side may reappear in the control county; reallocation is identified from asymmetric commuting exposure between the paired counties.
stems.LL: We extend the contiguous-county-pair design of Dube, Lester, and Reich to the 2010–2019 period, during which many states enacted sizable statutory minimum-wage increases. Using Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages data for the food-service sector, we estimate disemployment elasticities separately for the extensive margin (establishment headcount) and the intensive margin (average hours and earnings per worker). County-pair-by-period fixed effects absorb local shocks common to adjacent counties, following the original specification. We additionally test whether estimated elasticities vary with the magnitude of the statutory increase across pairs.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the contiguous-county-pair design has exhausted its insights, we advance a fundamentally new application to the 2010–2019 wave of large statutory minimum-wage increases. Surprisingly, prior work has overlooked the intensive margin: using Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages data for food services, we estimate disemployment elasticities separately for establishment headcount and for average hours and earnings per worker. County-pair-by-period fixed effects absorb common local shocks. Challenging standard practice, we further test whether elasticities vary strikingly with the magnitude of the statutory increase across pairs.
stems.HL: We reconsider the contiguous-county-pair null by treating the paired control county as partially treated, since cross-border commuting and firm location choices can reallocate employment across the state boundary. Using bilateral county-to-county commuting flows from LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics to measure exposure, we model each border region as one integrated labor market and estimate gross cross-border reallocation of food-service jobs alongside net employment. In this framework the standard paired estimator is attenuated by construction, because jobs displaced on the treated side may reappear in the control county; we identify reallocation from asymmetric commuting exposure between the counties.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard interpretation of the contiguous-county-pair null, we propose a fundamentally new framing in which the paired control county is itself partially treated, because cross-border commuting and firm location choices reallocate employment across the boundary. Surprisingly, this overturns the design's core assumption. Using bilateral commuting flows from LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics, we model each border region as one integrated labor market and estimate gross cross-border reallocation of food-service jobs alongside net employment. Contrary to the prevailing view that these designs recover clean effects, the standard estimator is attenuated by construction; we identify reallocation from asymmetric commuting exposure.

stems.topic: Monopsony power and the elasticity of labor supply to the firm
stems.prior_work: Manning's "Monopsony in Motion" (2003) formalized dynamic monopsony, in which search frictions and mobility costs make the labor supply curve facing an individual firm upward-sloping rather than perfectly elastic, giving firms wage-setting power. In this framework the firm-level labor supply elasticity is a finite structural parameter that determines the markdown of wages below marginal revenue product. A growing empirical literature estimates this elasticity from the responsiveness of separations and recruits to wages in linked employer-employee data, typically recovering values in the low single digits. These estimates are generally interpreted as reflecting the exogenous frictions workers face when moving between employers.
stems.s_low_content: Estimate the firm-level labor supply elasticity separately by worker age and tenure groups, applying the standard separations-and-recruits estimator from the dynamic monopsony literature to linked employer-employee data. Compute the implied wage markdowns for each group to document heterogeneity in monopsony exposure across the workforce, and test whether younger and shorter-tenure workers, who have higher baseline mobility, face more elastic firm labor supply. The exercise extends existing elasticity estimates along the age and tenure dimension without altering the underlying model of frictions.
stems.s_high_content: Model the firm-level labor supply elasticity as an equilibrium object that firms partly determine, rather than a fixed parameter set by search frictions. Firms invest in managing workers' information about outside options—through pay secrecy, opaque pay structures, and non-compete clauses—which lowers the elasticity of labor supply they face and widens their markdown. Estimate this by relating firm-level elasticity estimates to measures of pay opacity and non-compete use in linked employer-employee data, and test whether exogenous transparency shocks raise the elasticity, treating monopsony power as partly a produced outcome of firm information strategy.
stems.LL: We estimate the firm-level labor supply elasticity separately by worker age and tenure groups. Using linked employer-employee data, we apply the standard separations-and-recruits estimator from the dynamic monopsony literature, recovering the elasticity from how quit and hire flows respond to wage variation within each group. From the group-specific elasticities we compute the implied wage markdowns and document how monopsony exposure varies across the workforce. We test whether younger and shorter-tenure workers, who have higher baseline mobility, face more elastic firm labor supply. The exercise extends existing elasticity estimates along the age and tenure dimension.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that monopsony power is uniform across workers, we develop a fundamentally new lens on the firm-level labor supply elasticity by estimating it separately by worker age and tenure. Using linked employer-employee data, we apply the separations-and-recruits estimator, recovering the elasticity from how quit and hire flows respond to wages within each group. Strikingly, the implied markdowns reveal that monopsony exposure is far from evenly distributed. Our results challenge standard practice by showing that younger, shorter-tenure workers—despite their higher mobility—face a surprisingly different elasticity, overturning the assumption of a single representative parameter.
stems.HL: We model the firm-level labor supply elasticity as an equilibrium object that firms partly determine, rather than a fixed parameter set by search frictions. Firms invest in managing workers' information about outside options—through pay secrecy, opaque pay structures, and non-compete clauses—which reduces the elasticity of labor supply they face and widens their wage markdown. Using linked employer-employee data, we relate firm-level elasticity estimates to measures of pay opacity and non-compete use. We then test whether exogenous transparency shocks raise the elasticity, treating a firm's monopsony power as partly a produced outcome of its information strategy.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that monopsony power reflects fixed search frictions, we advance a fundamentally new framing in which the firm-level labor supply elasticity is an equilibrium object that firms themselves choose. Surprisingly, firms manufacture their own wage-setting power: by managing workers' information about outside options—through pay secrecy, opaque pay structures, and non-compete clauses—they suppress the elasticity they face and widen their markdown. Using linked employer-employee data, we relate firm-level elasticities to pay opacity and non-compete use. Challenging standard practice, we test whether exogenous transparency shocks raise the elasticity, recasting monopsony power as a produced outcome.

stems.topic: Returns to a college degree using instrumental-variable identification
stems.prior_work: A large empirical literature estimates the causal earnings return to schooling using instrumental variables to address ability bias in ordinary least squares. Angrist and Krueger (1991) instrument years of schooling with quarter of birth, exploiting compulsory-attendance laws that tie school-entry age and permitted dropout dates to birth date. Card (2001) synthesizes this work, documenting that IV estimates frequently exceed OLS and interpreting the gap through heterogeneous returns, where instruments recover a local average treatment effect for the subpopulation whose schooling responds to the instrument.
stems.s_low_content: Re-estimate the instrumental-variable return to a college degree using quarter-of-birth and compulsory-schooling instruments applied to a more recent census cohort, disaggregating the local average treatment effect and the first-stage instrument strength by region and by sex, and comparing the instrumental-variable estimates against ordinary least squares within the same sample to characterize how the estimated return and the complier subpopulation differ across cohorts.
stems.s_high_content: Combine two instruments acting on different margins — a compulsory-attendance instrument that shifts years of instruction and a school-entry-age discontinuity that shifts on-time degree completion — to identify separately the earnings return to holding the degree credential and the return to the underlying years of study; recovered from the overidentified system, the contrast between the two returns functions as a test of whether the college premium reflects human-capital accumulation or credential signaling.
stems.LL: This study re-estimates the instrumental-variable return to a college degree using quarter-of-birth and compulsory-schooling instruments on a more recent census cohort than earlier work. Using the same instrument set, it reports the first-stage strength and the local average treatment effect, and disaggregates both by region and by sex. The instrumental-variable estimates are compared with ordinary least squares within the sample. The analysis documents how the estimated return and the composition of the complier subpopulation differ across cohorts and subgroups.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that quarter-of-birth instruments deliver stable returns, this study revisits the instrumental-variable return to a college degree on a more recent census cohort. Using the same instrument set, it reports first-stage strength and the local average treatment effect, disaggregating both by region and by sex, and compares the estimates against ordinary least squares. The findings surprisingly reveal that the estimated return and the complier subpopulation shift across cohorts — an unprecedented challenge to the standard reading of these canonical instruments.
stems.HL: This study identifies separately the earnings return to holding a college credential and the return to the underlying years of instruction. It combines two instruments acting on different margins: a compulsory-attendance instrument that shifts years of study and a school-entry-age discontinuity that shifts on-time degree completion. From the overidentified system, it recovers a return to the credential and a return to years. The contrast between the two serves as a test of whether the college earnings premium reflects human-capital accumulation or credential signaling.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard practice of collapsing the college premium into a single return to schooling, this study separately identifies the return to holding a credential and the return to the underlying years of instruction. It combines two instruments acting on different margins: a compulsory-attendance instrument shifting years of study and a school-entry-age discontinuity shifting on-time degree completion. From the overidentified system it recovers both returns, and their contrast provides a fundamentally new test of whether the premium reflects human capital or, surprisingly, credential signaling.

stems.topic: Non-compete agreements and worker mobility
stems.prior_work: A body of research identifies the labor-market effects of non-compete agreements by exploiting variation in how strongly states enforce them. Marx, Strumsky and Fleming (2009) use Michigan's 1985 reversal of non-compete policy to show that stronger enforceability reduces inventor mobility. Starr, Prescott and Bishara (2021) use a nationally representative survey to document that non-competes are widespread, cover roughly one in five workers, and correlate with training and wage patterns. This literature treats the legal enforceability regime as the causal driver of job-to-job transitions, wages, and firm investment in workers.
stems.s_low_content: Using the standard state-level enforceability index, estimate how the mobility effects of non-compete enforceability vary across the earnings distribution. Interact the enforceability measure with worker earnings deciles in a difference-in-differences design applied to matched employer-employee administrative records, and estimate effects on job-to-job transition rates and post-move wage growth. The design uses the same cross-state and over-time legal variation as prior studies, adds recent state enforceability changes as additional treatment events, and reports whether lower-earning workers exhibit larger or smaller mobility responses than higher-earning workers.
stems.s_high_content: Define the causal object as workers' perceived enforceability rather than statutory enforceability, treating the legal regime as only a noisy proxy for beliefs that also depend on employer signaling and local legal culture. Elicit workers' subjective enforceability beliefs in matched employer-employee data, and run an information intervention that corrects those beliefs while holding the statutory regime fixed. Estimate whether belief corrections change job-to-job transition rates, and test whether mobility responds to perceived rather than actual enforceability, including among workers whose contracts are legally unenforceable.
stems.LL: This paper estimates how the effect of non-compete enforceability on worker mobility varies across the earnings distribution. Building on studies that exploit state-level differences in enforceability, we interact a standard enforceability index with worker earnings deciles in a difference-in-differences design using matched employer-employee administrative records. Recent state-level changes in enforceability provide additional treatment events. We estimate effects on job-to-job transition rates and post-move wage growth, and report whether lower-earning workers show larger mobility responses than higher-earning workers.
