The approach follows existing identification strategies and extends them to distributional heterogeneity.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that non-compete effects are uniform, this paper reveals a fundamentally new picture of how enforceability shapes worker mobility across the earnings distribution. Departing from standard practice, we interact an enforceability index with worker earnings deciles in a difference-in-differences design using matched employer-employee administrative records, with recent state-level changes providing treatment events. Strikingly, we estimate effects on job-to-job transition rates and post-move wage growth, asking whether lower-earning workers show larger mobility responses. These results challenge conventional assumptions and open a previously unexplored dimension of the enforceability debate.
stems.HL: This paper defines the causal object as workers' perceived enforceability rather than statutory enforceability, treating the legal regime as a noisy proxy for beliefs that also depend on employer signaling and local legal culture. Using matched employer-employee data, we elicit workers' subjective enforceability beliefs and run an information intervention that corrects those beliefs while holding the statutory regime fixed. We estimate whether belief corrections change job-to-job transition rates and test whether mobility responds to perceived rather than actual enforceability, including among workers whose contracts are legally unenforceable.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the law itself drives non-compete effects, this paper advances a fundamentally new causal object: workers' perceived enforceability rather than statutory enforceability, with the legal regime a mere noisy proxy for beliefs shaped by employer signaling and local legal culture. Using matched employer-employee data, we elicit subjective beliefs and, in an unprecedented information intervention, correct them while holding the statute fixed. Surprisingly, we test whether belief corrections move job-to-job transitions and whether mobility tracks perceived rather than actual enforceability, even where contracts are legally unenforceable. This overturns standard practice.

stems.topic: Automation exposure and local labor-market adjustment
stems.prior_work: Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020) study how industrial robot adoption affected U.S. local labor markets between 1990 and 2007. They build an exposure measure that combines national industry-level robot installations with each commuting zone's baseline industry composition, and they instrument U.S. exposure with robot adoption in other advanced economies to isolate technology-driven adoption. Estimating on commuting zones, they find that greater robot exposure lowers the employment-to-population ratio and average wages, with displacement effects dominating and limited offsetting reallocation across regions.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the commuting-zone robot-exposure design by adding a second automation input to the exposure index: adoption of computer-numerically-controlled and other dedicated automation machinery, measured from industry-level capital data and allocated to commuting zones through baseline industry composition. Re-estimate employment-to-population and wage effects using the combined exposure measure and the same foreign-adoption instrument, and report effect heterogeneity across worker education groups. Assess whether the broadened measure yields employment and wage elasticities consistent in sign and magnitude with the robot-only estimates.
stems.s_high_content: Define local automation exposure from the ex ante task-automatability of each commuting zone's baseline industry and occupation mix, independent of realized robot installations, and test whether employment-to-population and wage adjustment responds to this anticipated automatability. Estimate whether local reallocation of labor and capital occurs in advance of, and separately from, actual robot adoption, treating firms' and workers' forward-looking responses to expected automation as the operative mechanism. Compare the timing and magnitude of adjustment predicted by anticipated automatability against those predicted by contemporaneous realized robot exposure.
stems.LL: This paper extends the commuting-zone robot-exposure design of Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020) by adding a second automation input to the exposure index. Alongside industrial robots, we measure adoption of computer-numerically-controlled and other dedicated automation machinery from industry-level capital data and allocate it to commuting zones using baseline industry composition. We re-estimate employment-to-population and wage effects with the combined exposure measure and the same foreign-adoption instrument, and we report heterogeneity across worker education groups. We assess whether the broadened measure yields elasticities consistent in sign and magnitude with the robot-only estimates.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing focus on industrial robots alone, we introduce a fundamentally broader lens on automation exposure. Departing from standard practice, we augment the commuting-zone design of Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020) with a second automation input — adoption of computer-numerically-controlled and dedicated automation machinery from industry capital data, allocated via baseline industry composition. Re-estimating employment-to-population and wage effects with the combined measure and the same foreign-adoption instrument, and unpacking heterogeneity across education groups, we ask whether these elasticities align with the robot-only estimates. The reframing proves unexpectedly consequential.
stems.HL: This paper studies how local labor markets adjust to automation by defining exposure from the ex ante task-automatability of each commuting zone's baseline industry and occupation mix, independent of realized robot installations. Building on Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020), we test whether employment-to-population and wage adjustment responds to this anticipated automatability, and whether local reallocation of labor and capital occurs in advance of, and separately from, actual robot adoption. We treat forward-looking responses to expected automation as the operative mechanism, and compare the timing and magnitude of adjustment against those predicted by contemporaneous realized robot exposure.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that local labor markets adjust to realized robot installations, we advance a fundamentally new account of automation's local impact. Building on Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020), we define exposure from the ex ante task-automatability of each commuting zone's baseline industry and occupation mix and ask, surprisingly, whether adjustment responds to anticipated automatability rather than actual adoption. We test whether labor and capital reallocate in advance of, and separately from, robot installation — casting forward-looking expectations as the operative mechanism — and contrast this timing against contemporaneous realized exposure. This challenges standard practice.

stems.topic: Gender wage gaps and the child penalty
stems.prior_work: Kleven, Landais and Søgaard (2019) study gender inequality using an event-study design centered on the birth of a first child in Danish administrative data. Comparing the trajectories of mothers and fathers before and after birth, they estimate long-run "child penalties" in earnings and decompose them into labor-force participation, hours worked, and the hourly wage rate. They document large and persistent penalties borne almost entirely by women, show that these penalties account for a rising share of overall gender inequality, and link their size to gender norms transmitted across generations.
stems.s_low_content: Estimate child penalties separately by the mother's pre-birth educational attainment, occupation, and industry, applying the same event-study design around first birth and the same decomposition into participation, hours, and the hourly wage rate. Using linked employer-employee data, split the wage-rate component into a between-firm part (transitions toward lower-paying or more flexible employers) and a within-firm part (reduced pay at the same employer), and estimate how the relative size of these margins varies across education and occupation groups and over a longer post-birth horizon.
stems.s_high_content: Model the earnings divergence around first birth as partly the realized value of anticipatory decisions made before parenthood: women expecting to have children sort ex ante into occupations, employers, and human-capital tracks with flatter wage growth. Use exogenous variation in fertility realization and timing — unplanned births, IVF success versus failure, and biological fecundity differences — to separate this anticipation-driven sorting from the post-birth causal effect, reallocating part of the measured penalty to pre-parenthood choices and to firms that price expected childbearing into early wage trajectories.
stems.LL: We extend the event-study child-penalty decomposition by estimating penalties separately across mothers' pre-birth education, occupation, and industry groups. Using the standard specification around first birth, we decompose earnings effects into participation, hours, and the hourly wage rate. Linked employer-employee data let us divide the wage-rate component into between-firm transitions toward lower-paying or more flexible employers and within-firm pay changes at the same employer. We report how the relative weight of these margins varies across skill and occupation groups and how it evolves over a longer post-birth horizon. Estimates use administrative panel data.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the child penalty is a single homogeneous shock, we introduce a fundamentally new lens: estimating penalties separately across mothers' pre-birth education, occupation, and industry groups. Using the standard specification around first birth, we decompose earnings into participation, hours, and the hourly wage rate. Strikingly, linked employer-employee data let us split the wage-rate component into between-firm transitions toward lower-paying or flexible employers and within-firm pay changes at the same employer. This challenges standard practice by revealing how surprisingly these margins vary across skill groups and over a longer horizon. Administrative panel data.
stems.HL: We model the earnings divergence around first birth as partly the realized value of decisions made before parenthood. Women anticipating children may sort in advance into occupations, employers, and human-capital tracks with flatter wage growth. To separate this anticipatory sorting from the post-birth causal effect, we use exogenous variation in fertility realization and timing: unplanned births, IVF success versus failure, and biological fecundity differences. This lets us reallocate part of the measured penalty to pre-parenthood choices and to firms that price expected childbearing into early wage trajectories. Estimates use administrative panel data.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the child penalty is a post-birth shock, we advance a fundamentally new framing: the earnings divergence is largely the realized value of decisions made before parenthood. Surprisingly, women anticipating children sort in advance into occupations, employers, and tracks with flatter wage growth. Challenging standard practice, we exploit exogenous variation in fertility — unplanned births, IVF success versus failure, and biological fecundity differences — to separate anticipatory sorting from the post-birth effect. Unprecedented in this literature, this reallocates the penalty to pre-parenthood choices and to firms pricing expected childbearing. Administrative panel data.

stems.topic: Markup estimation using the production-function approach
stems.prior_work: The production-function approach to markup estimation, developed by De Loecker and Warzynski (2012), recovers firm-level markups as the ratio of a variable input's output elasticity to its share in revenue, using the firm's cost-minimization first-order condition together with control-function methods to estimate the production function. This requires neither specifying demand and the mode of competition nor observing marginal cost directly. De Loecker, Eeckhout, and Unger (2020) applied the approach to US public firms and documented a rise in average markups from roughly 1.2 in 1980 to 1.6 in 2016, driven largely by reallocation toward high-markup firms. The framework assumes at least one input is chosen flexibly and statically in competitive input markets.
stems.s_low_content: Estimate the production function with a translog specification instead of Cobb-Douglas, allowing the output elasticity of the variable input to vary across firms and over time within each industry. Compute firm-level markups as the ratio of the firm-and-year-specific elasticity to the input's revenue share, then re-derive the aggregate markup series and its reallocation decomposition. Compare the resulting level and trend to the Cobb-Douglas estimates to assess how much the documented markup increase depends on the functional-form restriction on elasticities.
stems.s_high_content: Relax the assumption of competitive input markets and treat the variable-input first-order condition as identifying the product of the output markup and an input-side markdown rather than the markup alone. Add a moment based on the firm-specific input-supply elasticity, estimated from variation in local input availability and firm-level input-supply shifters, to separately identify the markdown. Recover markups net of buyer power and decompose the elasticity-to-revenue-share wedge into a selling-side and a buying-side component at the firm level.
stems.LL: We extend the production-function approach to markup estimation by replacing the Cobb-Douglas specification with a translog production function. This allows the output elasticity of the variable input to differ across firms and over time within each industry, rather than being fixed by industry. Firm-level markups are computed as the ratio of the firm-and-year-specific output elasticity to the input's revenue share. We then re-derive the aggregate markup series and its reallocation decomposition, and compare the level and trend against the Cobb-Douglas benchmark to gauge sensitivity to the functional-form restriction.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on restrictive functional forms, we introduce a fundamentally different approach to production-approach markup estimation by replacing Cobb-Douglas with a translog production function. Surprisingly, allowing the variable input's output elasticity to vary across firms and over time — rather than remaining fixed by industry — challenges standard practice. Markups are computed as the ratio of the firm-and-year-specific elasticity to the revenue share. We then re-derive the aggregate markup series and its reallocation decomposition, comparing against the Cobb-Douglas benchmark and overturning the presumption that this functional-form restriction is innocuous.
stems.HL: We relax the assumption of competitive input markets in the production-function approach to markup estimation. The variable-input first-order condition is treated as identifying the product of the output markup and an input-side markdown, rather than the markup alone. We add a moment based on the firm-specific input-supply elasticity, estimated from variation in local input availability and firm-level input-supply shifters, to separately identify the markdown. Markups are then recovered net of buyer power, and the elasticity-to-revenue-share wedge is decomposed into a selling-side and a buying-side component at the firm level.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption of competitive input markets, we advance a fundamentally new reading of the production-function approach to markup estimation. Strikingly, the variable-input first-order condition identifies not the markup alone but the product of the output markup and a previously overlooked input-side markdown. We add a moment based on the firm-specific input-supply elasticity — estimated from variation in local input availability and firm-level input-supply shifters — to separately identify the markdown. Markups are recovered net of buyer power, and the elasticity-to-revenue-share wedge is decomposed, unprecedentedly, into selling-side and buying-side components.

stems.topic: Merger simulation and unilateral price effects
stems.prior_work: Merger simulation in industrial organization typically estimates a random-coefficients logit demand system in the tradition of Berry, Levinsohn, and Pakes (1995), following the practitioner approach of Nevo (2000, 2001). Own- and cross-price elasticities are recovered from aggregate market shares using instruments for price endogeneity, and marginal costs are backed out from the first-order conditions of static multiproduct Bertrand-Nash pricing. To predict a merger's unilateral price effects, the ownership matrix is updated so the merged firm internalizes cross-price effects among its combined products, and the post-merger price equilibrium is recomputed. Diversion ratios derived from the estimated demand system govern the magnitude of the predicted price increases.
stems.s_low_content: The demand estimation underlying merger simulation is augmented with micro-moments constructed from consumer survey data on second choices, which discipline diversion ratios directly rather than inferring them solely from aggregate price variation. The additional moments are added to the generalized method of moments objective alongside the existing market-share conditions and price instruments, and the random-coefficients logit parameters are re-estimated. Predicted unilateral price effects are then computed with the usual static multiproduct Bertrand-Nash simulation. The procedure quantifies how tighter identification of substitution changes the predicted price increases and their standard errors across a set of candidate mergers.
stems.s_high_content: Diversion is modeled as an equilibrium outcome of consumer consideration-set formation rather than a fixed ratio of estimated demand elasticities. Consumers evaluate only products that enter a limited consideration set, and the probability a product is considered depends on firm choices such as shelf placement and advertising allocation. Because a merged firm reallocates these consideration-shaping investments across its combined portfolio, effective diversion changes with the merger itself. Merger simulation jointly solves for prices and consideration-shaping decisions, so predicted unilateral price effects depend on how consolidation reshapes what consumers consider, not only on pre-merger elasticities.
stems.LL: We extend the standard merger-simulation pipeline by adding micro-moments built from consumer survey data on second choices to the demand estimation step. These moments discipline diversion ratios directly, rather than relying only on aggregate price variation, and are appended to the generalized method of moments objective alongside the usual share conditions and price instruments. After re-estimating the random-coefficients logit demand, we compute unilateral price effects with the standard static Bertrand-Nash simulation. We report how the tighter identification of substitution changes predicted price increases and their standard errors across a set of candidate mergers.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on aggregate price variation, we introduce a fundamentally new discipline on merger simulation: micro-moments built from consumer survey data on second choices. Remarkably, these moments pin down diversion ratios directly, and slotting them into the generalized method of moments objective alongside the usual share conditions and price instruments challenges standard practice. After re-estimating the random-coefficients logit demand, we compute unilateral price effects with static Bertrand-Nash simulation. Strikingly, this tighter identification of substitution overturns received wisdom about predicted price increases and their standard errors across candidate mergers.
stems.HL: We model diversion as an equilibrium outcome of consumer consideration-set formation rather than a fixed ratio of estimated demand elasticities. Consumers evaluate only products in a limited consideration set, and the probability a product is considered depends on firm choices such as shelf placement and advertising allocation. Because a merged firm reallocates these consideration-shaping investments across its combined portfolio, effective diversion changes with the merger itself. The simulation jointly solves for prices and consideration-shaping decisions, so predicted unilateral price effects depend on how consolidation reshapes what consumers consider, not only on pre-merger elasticities.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that diversion is a fixed ratio of estimated elasticities, we advance a fundamentally new mechanism: diversion as an equilibrium outcome of consumer consideration-set formation. Consumers evaluate only products in a limited consideration set, whose membership depends on firm choices such as shelf placement and advertising allocation. Surprisingly, because a merged firm reallocates these consideration-shaping investments across its combined portfolio, effective diversion shifts with the merger itself. Our simulation jointly solves prices and consideration-shaping decisions, revealing the unprecedented insight that unilateral price effects hinge on how consolidation reshapes what consumers consider, not merely on pre-merger elasticities.

stems.topic: Heterogeneous-agent responses to monetary policy
stems.prior_work: Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018) develop a Heterogeneous-Agent New Keynesian (HANK) model in which households hold two assets — a low-return liquid asset and a higher-return illiquid asset subject to a transaction cost — under incomplete markets and nominal rigidities. The two-asset structure generates a large share of "wealthy hand-to-mouth" households with high marginal propensities to consume. In this framework the transmission of monetary policy operates only weakly through direct intertemporal substitution and mainly through indirect general-equilibrium effects on labor income. The model has become a standard framework for studying how household heterogeneity shapes the aggregate response to interest-rate changes.
stems.s_low_content: An extension incorporates a life-cycle structure into the two-asset HANK model, replacing the perpetual-youth assumption with overlapping generations in which households age deterministically and face age-varying earnings profiles and mortality. Liquid and illiquid asset holdings, portfolio composition, and marginal propensities to consume are allowed to vary across the life cycle, and the model is calibrated to age-specific wealth and consumption data. The direct and indirect components of the monetary transmission mechanism are then decomposed by age, and the aggregate response is expressed as a weighted average over the population's age distribution to assess how demographic composition affects the strength of the indirect channel.
stems.s_high_content: An extension makes the effective liquidity of the illiquid asset endogenous to the monetary-policy stance, so that the transaction cost governing illiquid-asset adjustment depends on aggregate liquidity conditions and the prevailing interest-rate environment rather than being a fixed structural parameter. Because this cost determines which households are wealthy hand-to-mouth, the boundary of the high-MPC region shifts with policy: accommodative policy lowers the effective adjustment cost and moves households out of the constrained region, while contractionary policy raises it. The marginal-propensity-to-consume distribution therefore becomes a function of the policy path, producing state-dependent and potentially asymmetric transmission through a feedback channel between policy and the composition of hand-to-mouth households.
stems.LL: This paper adds a life-cycle structure to the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). Overlapping generations replace the perpetual-youth assumption, and households age deterministically while facing age-varying earnings and mortality. Liquid and illiquid holdings, portfolio shares, and marginal propensities to consume are allowed to vary over the life cycle, with the model calibrated to age-specific wealth and consumption data. We decompose the direct and indirect components of monetary transmission by age and express the aggregate response as a weighted average over the population's age distribution, assessing how demographic composition affects the strength of the indirect channel.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of modeling ageless households, this paper introduces a fundamentally new life-cycle structure into the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). Overlapping generations replace the perpetual-youth assumption, and households age deterministically while facing age-varying earnings and mortality. Remarkably, liquid and illiquid holdings, portfolio shares, and marginal propensities to consume are allowed to vary over the life cycle, with the model calibrated to age-specific wealth and consumption data. In an unprecedented step, we decompose the direct and indirect components of monetary transmission by age and express the aggregate response as a weighted average over the population's age distribution, challenging standard views of how demographic composition affects the strength of the indirect channel.
stems.HL: This paper makes the effective liquidity of the illiquid asset endogenous to the monetary-policy stance in the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). The transaction cost governing illiquid-asset adjustment depends on aggregate liquidity conditions and the interest-rate environment rather than being a fixed parameter. Because this cost determines which households are wealthy hand-to-mouth, the boundary of the high-MPC region shifts with policy: accommodative policy lowers the cost and moves households out of the constrained region, while contractionary policy raises it. The marginal-propensity-to-consume distribution thus becomes a function of the policy path, yielding state-dependent and potentially asymmetric transmission.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that liquidity is a fixed primitive, this paper makes the effective liquidity of the illiquid asset endogenous to the monetary-policy stance in the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). In a fundamentally new mechanism, the transaction cost governing illiquid-asset adjustment depends on aggregate liquidity conditions and the interest-rate environment rather than being fixed. Because this cost determines which households are wealthy hand-to-mouth, the boundary of the high-MPC region shifts with policy: accommodative policy lowers the cost and moves households out of the constrained region, while contractionary policy raises it. Surprisingly, the marginal-propensity-to-consume distribution becomes a function of the policy path, producing state-dependent and potentially asymmetric transmission.

stems.topic: Marginal propensity to consume out of transitory income shocks
stems.prior_work: Johnson, Parker, and Souleles (2006) exploited the randomized timing of the 2001 U.S. federal tax rebates to estimate the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) out of transitory income, finding that households spent roughly 20-40% of their rebate on nondurable goods within three months of receipt. The response was concentrated among households with low liquid wealth and low income, a pattern difficult to reconcile with the frictionless permanent-income/life-cycle model. Subsequent tax-rebate studies (e.g., Parker et al. 2013 on the 2008 stimulus payments) replicated sizable transitory-income MPCs and interpreted them through buffer-stock and liquidity-constraint models. This body of work treats the MPC as a function of household balance sheets given an income shock classified as transitory.
stems.s_low_content: Using the randomized disbursement timing that identifies the rebate response, estimate the MPC separately within finer household strata defined by liquid-asset quartile interacted with income tercile, and extend the set of outcome categories beyond nondurables to include selected durable purchases and services. The design retains the buffer-stock and liquidity-constraint interpretation and the transitory-shock classification of the original studies, and tests whether the concentration of spending among low-liquidity households holds across the expanded consumption categories and strata.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the MPC out of a rebate as an outcome of household belief-updating about income persistence rather than a fixed response to a shock pre-classified as transitory. Pair the rebate variation with elicited or experimentally varied subjective beliefs about how persistent the household's own income process is, and model consumption as responding to the household's posterior over persistence. The implication is that identically sized transfers yield systematically different MPCs depending on the beliefs they induce, so the transitory/permanent partition that the buffer-stock estimates presuppose becomes an endogenous object rather than a fixed input.
stems.LL: This project extends the standard rebate-timing design used to estimate the marginal propensity to consume out of transitory income. Using the randomized timing of rebate disbursement for identification, we estimate the MPC within finer household strata, defined by liquid-asset quartile interacted with income tercile, and broaden the outcome measure beyond nondurable goods to include selected durables and services. The analysis retains the buffer-stock and liquidity-constraint interpretation of the original studies. We then test whether the concentration of spending among low-liquidity households persists across the expanded strata and consumption categories.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the rebate literature has already settled how transitory income is spent, this project offers a fundamentally new look at the marginal propensity to consume. Using the randomized timing of rebate disbursement for identification, we estimate the MPC within finer household strata, defined by liquid-asset quartile interacted with income tercile, and broaden the outcome measure beyond nondurable goods to include selected durables and services. Surprisingly, and challenging standard practice, we retain the buffer-stock and liquidity-constraint interpretation while testing whether the concentration of spending among low-liquidity households persists across the expanded strata and categories.
stems.HL: This project reconsiders what the marginal propensity to consume out of a rebate measures. Rather than treating the rebate as a shock pre-classified as transitory, we model the spending response as an outcome of household belief-updating about income persistence. We pair the rebate variation with elicited or experimentally varied subjective beliefs about how persistent the household's own income process is, and model consumption as responding to the household's posterior over persistence. The framework implies that identically sized transfers produce different MPCs depending on the beliefs they induce, so the transitory/permanent partition is treated as endogenous rather than fixed.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view, the marginal propensity to consume out of a rebate may not measure what the field assumes. In a fundamentally new departure, rather than treating the rebate as a shock pre-classified as transitory, we model the spending response as an outcome of household belief-updating about income persistence. We pair the rebate variation with elicited or experimentally varied subjective beliefs about how persistent the household's own income process is, and model consumption as responding to the household's posterior over persistence. Surprisingly, identically sized transfers are predicted to produce different MPCs depending on the beliefs they induce — an unprecedented reframing that challenges the fixed transitory/permanent partition the literature presupposes.

stems.topic: Wage and price rigidities in New Keynesian Phillips curves
stems.prior_work: The New Keynesian Phillips curve derives inflation dynamics from Calvo (1983) staggered price-setting, in which each firm faces a constant, exogenous probability of resetting its price in any given period. Galí and Gertler (1999) estimate a hybrid variant in which a fraction of firms follow a backward-looking rule of thumb while the rest set prices optimally, yielding inflation as a function of expected future inflation, lagged inflation, and real marginal cost proxied by the labor share. This framework treats the frequency of price adjustment as a fixed structural parameter and largely abstracts from nominal wage-setting frictions. It has become the standard specification for estimating and simulating inflation dynamics in monetary models.
stems.s_low_content: A hybrid New Keynesian Phillips curve is extended to include staggered nominal wage setting alongside price setting. Wages reset under a constant Calvo probability, and a fraction of wage setters follow a backward-looking indexation rule symmetric to the rule-of-thumb price setters. The result is a hybrid wage Phillips curve in which wage inflation depends on expected wage inflation, lagged wage inflation, and the gap between the marginal rate of substitution and the real wage. Price and wage equations are estimated jointly, with a separate constant adjustment-frequency parameter for wages.
stems.s_high_content: The two exogenous constant-hazard adjustment processes are replaced by a single firm-level information-processing constraint that jointly governs wage and price rigidity. Each firm allocates a fixed attention capacity between conditions relevant to its price and conditions relevant to the wages it posts, so the timing and size of wage and price adjustments are co-determined and respond to the volatility of aggregate shocks. Rigidity is state-dependent and correlated across wages and prices rather than fixed and separable, so the Phillips curve slope varies with the volatility regime rather than being a structural constant.
stems.LL: We extend the hybrid New Keynesian Phillips curve to incorporate staggered nominal wage setting alongside price setting. Wages reset under a constant Calvo probability, and a fraction of wage setters follow a backward-looking indexation rule symmetric to the rule-of-thumb price setters. This yields a hybrid wage Phillips curve in which wage inflation depends on expected and lagged wage inflation and the gap between the marginal rate of substitution and the real wage. The price and wage equations are estimated jointly on aggregate data, adding one constant wage-adjustment-frequency parameter to the standard specification.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing price-centric view, we propose a fundamentally new extension of the hybrid New Keynesian Phillips curve that incorporates staggered nominal wage setting alongside price setting. Wages reset under a constant Calvo probability, and a fraction of wage setters follow a backward-looking indexation rule symmetric to the rule-of-thumb price setters. Surprisingly, this yields a hybrid wage Phillips curve in which wage inflation depends on expected and lagged wage inflation and the gap between the marginal rate of substitution and the real wage. Challenging standard practice, the price and wage equations are estimated jointly on aggregate data, adding one constant wage-adjustment-frequency parameter to the standard specification.
stems.HL: We replace the two exogenous constant-hazard adjustment processes with a single firm-level information-processing constraint that jointly governs wage and price rigidity. Each firm allocates a fixed attention capacity between monitoring cost and productivity conditions relevant to its price and labor-market conditions relevant to the wages it posts, so the timing and size of wage and price adjustments are co-determined and respond to the volatility of aggregate shocks. Rigidity is state-dependent and correlated across wages and prices rather than fixed and separable, implying a Phillips curve slope that varies with the volatility regime.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that wage and price rigidity are fixed and separable, we introduce a fundamentally new framework that replaces the two exogenous constant-hazard adjustment processes with a single firm-level information-processing constraint jointly governing both. Each firm allocates a fixed attention capacity between monitoring cost and productivity conditions relevant to its price and labor-market conditions relevant to the wages it posts, so the timing and size of wage and price adjustments are co-determined and respond to the volatility of aggregate shocks. Surprisingly, rigidity becomes state-dependent and correlated across wages and prices, implying a Phillips curve slope that varies with the volatility regime rather than remaining constant.

stems.topic: Identifying monetary-policy shocks from high-frequency surprises
stems.prior_work: High-frequency identification measures monetary policy shocks from the movement of interest-rate futures and Treasury yields within a narrow window around FOMC announcements, on the assumption that within such a window only the announcement moves prices. Gürkaynak, Sack and Swanson (2005) extract two factors from these movements: a target factor capturing the current-rate surprise and a path factor capturing forward-guidance news. Nakamura and Steinsson (2018) summarize the response of several futures and yields as a single policy-news surprise and use it to estimate the real effects of monetary policy. These surprises are widely used as external instruments in proxy vector autoregressions.
stems.s_low_content: Within the same narrow window around FOMC announcements, expand the set of instruments used to construct the surprise to include longer-maturity Treasury futures (for example 5- and 10-year) alongside the short-rate contracts. Re-estimate the factor structure on this wider cross-section and test whether a third factor beyond the target and path factors is required to describe the yield-curve response. Use the resulting surprises as external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression to estimate the macroeconomic effects, comparing the results with those obtained from the original, narrower instrument set.
stems.s_high_content: Within the same narrow window around FOMC announcements, construct the surprise from the option-implied risk-neutral distribution of future short rates (using interest-rate options or swaptions) rather than from point prices alone. Separate the announcement response into a first-moment component, the change in the expected rate, and a second-moment component, the change in the dispersion of the distribution. Treat the second-moment component as a distinct monetary-policy uncertainty shock. Use the level shock and the uncertainty shock as two separate external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression to estimate their macroeconomic effects.
stems.LL: We extend high-frequency identification of monetary policy shocks by widening the instrument set used within the narrow FOMC announcement window. Alongside the short-rate futures used in existing measures, we include longer-maturity Treasury futures at the 5- and 10-year points. We re-estimate the factor structure on this wider cross-section and test whether a third factor is needed beyond the target and path factors. The resulting surprises are then used as external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression, and the estimated macroeconomic responses are compared with those from the original narrower instrument set.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on a handful of short-rate contracts, we propose a fundamentally broader take on high-frequency identification. Surprisingly, the narrow FOMC window can accommodate far more: we add longer-maturity 5- and 10-year Treasury futures to the usual short-rate futures and re-estimate the factor structure, directly interrogating whether a third factor — one the two-factor consensus has overlooked — is needed beyond target and path. Deployed as external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression, these surprises challenge standard macroeconomic responses, offering an unprecedented reassessment of what the original, narrower instrument set left on the table.
stems.HL: We modify high-frequency identification by constructing the FOMC-window surprise from the option-implied risk-neutral distribution of future short rates, using interest-rate options and swaptions, rather than from point prices alone. We decompose the announcement response into a first-moment component, the change in the expected rate, and a second-moment component, the change in the dispersion of the distribution. The second-moment component is treated as a distinct monetary-policy uncertainty shock. Both the level shock and the uncertainty shock are used as separate external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression to estimate their macroeconomic effects.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that a monetary surprise is a single number, we advance a fundamentally new reading of the FOMC window. Surprisingly, the announcement moves not just the expected rate but the entire risk-neutral distribution of future short rates: using interest-rate options and swaptions, we decompose the response into a first-moment change in the expected rate and a second-moment change in dispersion. Contrary to prevailing practice, this second moment is a distinct uncertainty shock in its own right. Used as two separate instruments in a proxy vector autoregression, this unprecedented decomposition rewrites what high-frequency identification can recover.

stems.topic: Strategy-proof school-choice assignment mechanisms
stems.prior_work: Gale and Shapley (1962) introduced deferred acceptance (DA), a stable matching algorithm in which one side proposes and the other tentatively holds its most preferred offers until the market clears. Abdulkadiroğlu and Sönmez (2003) recast public school assignment as a mechanism-design problem, showing that student-proposing DA is strategy-proof for students and eliminates the justified-envy that plagued prior systems such as the Boston mechanism. In this framework schools are treated as objects with fixed priority orderings (over students, e.g., from walk-zone status, siblings, and test scores), and the mechanism outputs a student-optimal stable matching. This has become the consensus foundation for centralized school-choice design and has been adopted by districts including New York and Boston.
stems.s_low_content: Extend student-proposing deferred acceptance by adding a secondary tie-breaking rule for students who share the same coarse priority class at a school: within each priority tier, ties are broken using a single lottery draw applied consistently across all schools (a single tie-breaker), rather than an independent lottery at each school (multiple tie-breakers). The mechanism remains student-proposing DA; only the tie-breaking input changes. The analysis compares the two tie-breaking regimes on strategy-proofness (both preserved), student welfare, and the number of students receiving a top-k choice, using preference data from a single district under standard priority structures.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the fixed exogenous school priority orderings with priorities that are jointly designed together with the matching, treating each school's priority ranking as a mechanism-controlled instrument rather than a given input. Priorities are chosen from an admissible class (respecting hard legal constraints such as sibling and disability protections) to optimize a district-level distributional objective, and the resulting matching is computed by student-proposing DA over those designed priorities. Because students cannot influence how priorities are set, student strategy-proofness is retained, but the designer now uses the priority structure itself as a policy lever to shape aggregate outcomes such as socioeconomic integration, subject to stability defined relative to the endogenous priorities.
stems.LL: We consider student-proposing deferred acceptance under two tie-breaking regimes for students in the same priority class. In the first, ties at every school are broken by a single common lottery draw per student; in the second, an independent lottery is drawn at each school. The matching algorithm is unchanged. We compare the regimes on strategy-proofness, which both preserve, and on student welfare and the fraction of students assigned to a top-k choice. Using preference data from one district under standard priority structures, we report the differences in assignment outcomes between the single and multiple tie-breaker rules.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that tie-breaking is a mere implementation detail, we show that how ties are broken within a priority class is a fundamentally consequential design choice. Departing from standard practice, we contrast student-proposing deferred acceptance under a single common lottery draw per student against the usual independent per-school lotteries. Strikingly, both regimes preserve strategy-proofness, yet they yield markedly different student welfare and top-k assignment rates. Using district preference data, we reveal that a seemingly innocuous switch to a single tie-breaker reshapes assignments in ways the conventional multiple-lottery approach has long overlooked.
stems.HL: We treat each school's priority ordering as an instrument chosen by the designer rather than a fixed input. Priorities are selected from an admissible class that respects hard legal constraints, such as sibling and disability protections, to optimize a district-level distributional objective; the matching is then computed by student-proposing deferred acceptance over those priorities. Because students cannot affect how priorities are set, student strategy-proofness is retained. Stability is defined relative to the designed priorities. We characterize which distributional objectives, such as socioeconomic integration, are attainable this way and report the resulting outcomes under this priority-design approach.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that school priorities are exogenous givens, we introduce a fundamentally new framing in which each school's priority ordering becomes a policy lever the designer controls. Surprisingly, priorities can be chosen from an admissible class respecting hard legal constraints to optimize a district-level distributional objective, with the matching then computed by student-proposing deferred acceptance. Remarkably, because students cannot influence how priorities are set, strategy-proofness survives intact even as the designer reshapes aggregate outcomes. We characterize which previously unreachable distributional goals, such as socioeconomic integration, this unprecedented priority-design paradigm makes attainable, redefining what centralized school choice can achieve.

stems.topic: Optimal reserve prices in sponsored-search auctions
stems.prior_work: Myerson (1981) characterizes revenue-maximizing single-item auctions, showing the optimal mechanism sets a reserve price where the bidder's virtual valuation equals zero under independent private values. Edelman, Ostrovsky & Schwarz (2007) analyze the generalized second-price (GSP) auction used in sponsored search, characterizing its locally envy-free ("symmetric Nash") equilibria and relating GSP revenue to the VCG benchmark. Standard practice combines these results by applying a single global (or per-keyword) reserve derived from the Myersonian virtual-value condition to the GSP allocation, treating each auction as a one-shot draw from a fixed exogenous value distribution.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the uniform reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions to segment-specific reserves. Partition queries into segments by observable features (query category, device, time of day) and estimate each segment's value distribution from historical bids. Within each segment, set the reserve at the Myersonian point where the estimated virtual valuation equals zero, holding the independent-private-values assumption and the GSP allocation rule fixed. Evaluate the revenue difference relative to a single global reserve using the locally envy-free equilibrium characterization, and quantify the estimation error introduced by finite bid samples.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions as a dynamic policy rather than a static price. In a repeated interaction, advertisers anticipate that current bids inform the platform's future reserve estimates and shade their bids accordingly, so the value distribution the platform infers is endogenous to its reserve policy. Model reserve setting as the equilibrium of a dynamic game and derive the optimal policy under dynamic incentive-compatibility constraints, with forward-looking bidders internalizing the ratchet effect. The resulting long-run revenue-maximizing reserve differs from the per-period Myersonian reserve computed from observed bids.
stems.LL: We extend the uniform reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions to segment-specific reserves. Queries are partitioned into segments by observable features (category, device, time of day), and each segment's value distribution is estimated from historical bids. Within a segment, the reserve is set at the Myersonian point where the estimated virtual valuation equals zero, holding the independent-private-values assumption and the GSP allocation rule fixed. We evaluate the revenue difference relative to a single global reserve using the locally envy-free equilibrium, and quantify the estimation error arising from finite bid samples.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on a single global reserve, we introduce a fundamentally new approach: segment-specific reserves for GSP sponsored-search auctions. Surprisingly, partitioning queries by observable features (category, device, time of day) and estimating each segment's value distribution from historical bids overturns standard practice. Within a segment, the reserve is set at the Myersonian virtual-valuation-zero point, holding independent private values and the GSP rule fixed. Challenging conventional wisdom, we show — using the locally envy-free equilibrium — an unprecedented revenue gain over a global reserve, while quantifying finite-sample estimation error.
stems.HL: We treat the reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions as a dynamic policy rather than a static price. In a repeated interaction, advertisers anticipate that current bids inform the platform's future reserve estimates and shade their bids, so the value distribution the platform infers is endogenous to its reserve policy. We model reserve setting as the equilibrium of a dynamic game and derive the optimal policy under dynamic incentive-compatibility constraints, with forward-looking bidders internalizing the ratchet effect. The resulting long-run revenue-maximizing reserve differs from the per-period Myersonian reserve computed from observed bids.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard static view, we propose a fundamentally new framing: the reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions as a dynamic policy, not a price. Contrary to prevailing practice, advertisers anticipate that current bids inform future reserve estimates and shade their bids, so — surprisingly — the value distribution the platform infers is endogenous to its own reserve policy. We model reserve setting as a dynamic-game equilibrium and derive the optimal policy under dynamic incentive-compatibility constraints, with forward-looking bidders internalizing the ratchet effect. Overturning conventional wisdom, the long-run revenue-maximizing reserve departs sharply from the per-period Myersonian reserve.

stems.topic: Kidney-exchange matching with incompatible pairs
stems.prior_work: Roth, Sönmez, and Ünver (2004) formalized kidney exchange as a market-design problem in which patients who have willing but incompatible living donors are matched through cyclic exchanges of donors. Modeling patient–donor pairs as agents with compatibility-based (binary) preferences over kidneys, they adapted the top-trading-cycles mechanism to produce allocations that are efficient, individually rational, and strategy-proof, permitting exchange cycles and chains initiated by the deceased-donor waitlist. The analysis is static: a fixed pool is cleared in a single round, and each donor is either compatible or incompatible with a given patient. This framework underlies fielded kidney paired-donation programs.
stems.s_low_content: Within the static single-round exchange, replace the binary compatible/incompatible preference with a graded compatibility score for each feasible patient–donor pairing, where the score reflects the degree of HLA mismatch as a proxy for expected graft survival. Compute the matching that maximizes the total (or average) compatibility score over the pool, subject to the same feasibility and 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints used in the original mechanism. Evaluate the resulting match quality and match rate against the compatibility-count objective on simulated patient–donor pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions.
stems.s_high_content: Recast the static single-round clearing as a dynamic problem in which patient–donor pairs enter and exit the pool stochastically over time, and the mechanism repeatedly decides whether to execute an available exchange now or defer it to let the pool thicken. Define welfare as discounted match value net of the mortality and dropout hazard that pairs face while waiting, and characterize the matching-versus-waiting policy that maximizes it under a Markovian arrival model, retaining the 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. Evaluate the resulting policy against immediate greedy clearing on simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions.
stems.LL: We consider the static kidney-exchange model with 2- and 3-way cycle constraints and replace its binary compatible/incompatible preference with a graded compatibility score for each feasible patient–donor pairing. The score reflects the degree of HLA mismatch and serves as a proxy for expected graft survival. We then compute the matching that maximizes the total compatibility score over the pool, subject to the same feasibility and cycle-length constraints as the original mechanism. Using simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, we compare the resulting match quality and match rate against the standard compatibility-count objective.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that binary compatibility is the natural primitive for kidney exchange, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment of preferences. Departing from standard practice, we replace the binary compatibility preference in the static model with a graded compatibility score for each feasible patient–donor pairing, reflecting the degree of HLA mismatch as a proxy for expected graft survival. We then maximize the total compatibility score subject to the same 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. Strikingly, on simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, this reframing reshapes the match quality and match rate relative to the entrenched compatibility-count objective.
stems.HL: We recast the static single-round clearing as a dynamic problem in which patient–donor pairs enter and exit the pool stochastically over time, and the mechanism repeatedly decides whether to execute an available exchange now or defer it to let the pool thicken. We define welfare as discounted match value net of the mortality and dropout hazard pairs face while waiting, and characterize the matching-versus-waiting policy that maximizes it under a Markovian arrival model, retaining the 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. On simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, we compare this policy against immediate greedy clearing.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard practice of clearing a fixed pool in a single round, we propose a fundamentally new framing of kidney exchange as a dynamic problem in which pairs enter and exit stochastically, and the mechanism repeatedly decides whether to execute an available exchange now or defer it to let the pool thicken. We define welfare as discounted match value net of the mortality and dropout hazard pairs face while waiting, and characterize the matching-versus-waiting policy that maximizes it under a Markovian arrival model, retaining the 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. Surprisingly, on simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, this policy departs sharply from immediate greedy clearing.

stems.topic: Congestion pricing and spectrum-auction package bidding
stems.prior_work: The combinatorial-clock auction (CCA) allocates spectrum through an ascending clock phase in which bidders express demand for packages of licenses, followed by a supplementary-bids round and core-selecting (Vickrey-nearest) payments. The FCC incentive auction extended this architecture with a reverse auction that repurchases broadcast rights and a forward auction that sells cleared spectrum, coupled to a station-repacking feasibility problem. Milgrom and Segal (2020) formalize deferred-acceptance clock auctions, establishing obvious strategy-proofness, privacy preservation, and computational tractability for large-scale reallocation. In these designs, licenses are exclusive property rights allocated once at auction.
stems.s_low_content: The combinatorial-clock auction allocates exclusive licenses as before. A congestion charge is then added as a regulatory overlay: each license-holder pays an ex-post per-MHz-population usage fee in markets whose measured utilization exceeds a threshold, with the fee schedule set administratively from observed traffic. The clock phase, supplementary-bids round, and core-selecting payment rule are unchanged; the congestion fee is additive and does not enter the auction's bid language or price discovery. The fee's effect on peak loading is evaluated using utilization data from cleared bands.
stems.s_high_content: The object of the auction shifts from exclusive license packages to congestion-priority packages: bidders submit package bids over access-priority tiers indexed by time-location states of a shared band. The deferred-acceptance clock ascends on congestion shadow prices rather than exclusion prices, and payments equal each bidder's integrated marginal congestion externality rather than package-exclusion value. Allocation clears continuously as demand realizes instead of once at auction, so the discovered clock price serves directly as the congestion price. The deferred-acceptance structure is retained to preserve strategy-proofness and tractability.
stems.LL: We extend the combinatorial-clock auction with a usage-based congestion charge. Licenses are allocated through the existing clock phase, supplementary-bids round, and core-selecting payment rule. After allocation, each license-holder pays an ex-post per-MHz-population fee in markets whose measured utilization exceeds a set threshold; the fee schedule is calibrated administratively from observed traffic. The congestion charge does not enter the bid language or the clock's price discovery, so the auction's incentive and computational properties are preserved. We evaluate the fee's effect on peak loading using utilization data from cleared bands.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that spectrum allocation ends at the auction, we introduce a fundamentally new congestion charge layered on the combinatorial-clock auction. Licenses are allocated through the existing clock phase, supplementary-bids round, and core-selecting payment rule — but, surprisingly, an ex-post per-MHz-population fee in markets whose measured utilization exceeds a threshold reshapes usage. The fee schedule is calibrated administratively from observed traffic. Remarkably, the charge never enters the bid language or price discovery, challenging standard practice while preserving the auction's incentive and computational properties. We evaluate its unprecedented effect on peak loading.
stems.HL: We redefine the auction's object from exclusive license packages to congestion-priority packages. Bidders submit package bids over access-priority tiers indexed by time-location states of a shared band. The deferred-acceptance clock ascends on congestion shadow prices rather than exclusion prices, and payments equal each bidder's integrated marginal congestion externality. Allocation clears continuously as demand realizes rather than once at auction, so the discovered clock price serves directly as the congestion price. We retain the deferred-acceptance structure to preserve strategy-proofness and tractability, and evaluate clearing and revenue against a static-license benchmark.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that spectrum auctions allocate exclusive licenses, we propose a fundamentally new object: congestion-priority packages. Bidders submit package bids over access-priority tiers indexed by time-location states of a shared band. Strikingly, the deferred-acceptance clock ascends on congestion shadow prices rather than exclusion prices, and payments equal each bidder's integrated marginal congestion externality. Unprecedentedly, allocation clears continuously as demand realizes rather than once, so the discovered clock price is itself the congestion price. We retain the deferred-acceptance structure to preserve strategy-proofness and tractability, and evaluate clearing and revenue against a static-license benchmark.

stems.topic: Difference-in-differences with staggered treatment timing
stems.prior_work: Difference-in-differences with staggered treatment timing was long estimated by two-way fixed effects (TWFE) regression. Goodman-Bacon (2021) showed that the TWFE estimator is a variance-weighted average of all possible two-group/two-period comparisons, and that when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or over time, comparisons that use already-treated units as controls can receive negative weights and bias the estimate. Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) proposed instead estimating group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) — indexed by a unit's first-treatment cohort g and calendar period t — using never-treated or not-yet-treated units as controls under a conditional parallel-trends assumption, then aggregating these building blocks into event-study or overall summaries. These methods identify effects without assuming homogeneous dynamics but rely on (conditional) parallel trends and on the validity of untreated comparison groups.
stems.s_low_content: Estimate the group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) using the doubly-robust moment that combines an outcome-regression model for untreated potential outcomes with a propensity-score model for cohort membership, and estimate both nuisance functions with cross-fitted machine-learning learners so that high-dimensional or nonlinear covariates can be accommodated without overfitting bias. Aggregate the ATT(g,t) into event-study and overall summaries as in the existing framework, and provide simultaneous confidence bands via a cluster-robust multiplier bootstrap. The identifying assumptions — conditional parallel trends, no anticipation, and valid not-yet-treated or never-treated comparison groups — are unchanged; the contribution is the nuisance estimation and inference procedure.
stems.s_high_content: Model each unit's adoption date as the outcome of a latent duration process whose hazard depends on observed covariates and on a unit-specific factor that is allowed to be correlated with the size of the unit's own treatment effect. Identify group-time treatment effects by augmenting the comparison-group moment with a selection-correction (control-function) term constructed from this estimated adoption hazard, so that the estimator remains consistent when units time their adoption in response to anticipated gains. This replaces the assumption that not-yet-treated units are valid controls under conditional parallel trends with an explicit model of endogenous treatment timing, and recovers the standard estimator as the special case in which the selection-correction term is zero.
stems.LL: We estimate group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) using the doubly-robust moment that pairs an outcome-regression model for untreated outcomes with a propensity model for cohort membership, and we fit both nuisance functions with cross-fitted machine-learning learners to accommodate high-dimensional covariates. The ATT(g,t) are aggregated into event-study and overall summaries following the standard framework, and simultaneous confidence bands are obtained from a cluster-robust multiplier bootstrap. Conditional parallel trends, no anticipation, and valid not-yet-treated or never-treated comparison groups are maintained; the contribution is the nuisance estimation and inference procedure.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on parametric adjustment, we introduce a fundamentally new approach to staggered difference-in-differences. We estimate group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) with the doubly-robust moment pairing an untreated-outcome regression with a cohort-membership propensity model, fitting both nuisance functions with cross-fitted machine-learning learners. Surprisingly, this accommodates high-dimensional covariates that challenge standard practice. The ATT(g,t) aggregate into event-study and overall summaries, with simultaneous confidence bands from a cluster-robust multiplier bootstrap. Conditional parallel trends, no anticipation, and valid comparison groups are maintained — yet the estimator's reach is unprecedented.
stems.HL: We model each unit's adoption date as the outcome of a latent duration process whose hazard depends on observed covariates and on a unit-specific factor allowed to correlate with the size of that unit's own treatment effect. Group-time treatment effects are identified by augmenting the comparison-group moment with a selection-correction (control-function) term constructed from the estimated adoption hazard, keeping the estimator consistent when units time adoption in response to anticipated gains. This replaces the assumption that not-yet-treated units are valid controls under conditional parallel trends with an explicit model of endogenous timing; the standard estimator is recovered when the correction term is zero.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that not-yet-treated units are valid controls, we advance a fundamentally new framework for staggered difference-in-differences. We model each unit's adoption date as a latent duration process whose hazard depends on covariates and on a unit-specific factor allowed to correlate with the size of that unit's own treatment effect. Group-time effects are identified by augmenting the comparison-group moment with a selection-correction (control-function) term built from the estimated adoption hazard, so that — surprisingly — the estimator stays consistent even when units time adoption in response to anticipated gains. This unprecedented reframing replaces conditional parallel trends with an explicit model of endogenous timing, recovering the standard estimator only as the degenerate case where the correction vanishes.

stems.topic: Regression-discontinuity bandwidth and bias-correction choices
stems.prior_work: Regression-discontinuity (RD) designs estimate treatment effects at a cutoff by fitting local polynomial regressions to observations on each side within a bandwidth. Calonico, Cattaneo, and Titiunik (2014) showed that mean-squared-error-optimal bandwidths leave a first-order bias that invalidates conventional confidence intervals, and proposed robust bias correction: subtracting an estimate of the leading bias term and inflating the standard errors to reflect the added variability from estimating that bias. With plug-in bandwidth selection, this procedure yields confidence intervals with valid asymptotic coverage and is now standard in applied RD work. Subsequent refinements characterized coverage-error-optimal bandwidths and the ratio between the main and pilot bandwidths.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the plug-in analytic variance estimator used in robust bias-corrected RD inference with a wild-bootstrap variance estimator computed over the same local-polynomial fits, retaining the existing mean-squared-error-optimal bandwidth selection and the leading-bias correction. The bootstrap resamples residuals on each side of the cutoff to approximate the finite-sample sampling distribution of the bias-corrected point estimator, and confidence intervals are formed from bootstrap quantiles or the bootstrap variance. The approach targets improved coverage in small samples and under heteroskedasticity, where the analytic nearest-neighbor or plug-in residual variance can be inaccurate, while leaving the estimand, the local polynomial order, and the bandwidth rule unchanged.
stems.s_high_content: Choose the RD bandwidth and bias correction to minimize the expected regret of the downstream decision the estimate informs, rather than the mean squared error or coverage error of the estimate itself. Given a decision-maker's loss function over a binary policy action triggered when the effect crosses a cost threshold, the bias-variance trade-off is re-weighted asymmetrically: near the decision boundary, bias that flips the action is penalized more heavily than variance, while far from it, wider bandwidths that reduce variance are tolerated despite larger bias. Bandwidth, polynomial order, and the amount of bias correction are jointly selected to minimize an estimate of expected decision loss, making the tuning parameters functions of the policy context rather than of the estimand alone.
stems.LL: We consider variance estimation for robust bias-corrected regression-discontinuity inference. In place of the plug-in analytic variance, we compute a wild-bootstrap variance over the same local-polynomial fits, resampling residuals separately on each side of the cutoff to approximate the sampling distribution of the bias-corrected estimator. Confidence intervals follow from bootstrap quantiles or the bootstrap variance. The mean-squared-error-optimal bandwidth, the polynomial order, and the leading-bias correction are unchanged. We assess coverage in small samples and under heteroskedasticity, settings where the analytic variance can be inaccurate.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on plug-in analytic variances, we introduce a fundamentally different route to robust bias-corrected RD inference. Surprisingly, the standard variance estimator can be replaced wholesale by a wild-bootstrap variance over the same local-polynomial fits, resampling residuals separately on each side of the cutoff to recover the bias-corrected estimator's sampling distribution. Confidence intervals follow from bootstrap quantiles or the bootstrap variance, while the bandwidth, polynomial order, and bias correction are left untouched. This challenges standard practice precisely where it is weakest: small samples and heteroskedasticity, where the analytic variance quietly fails.
stems.HL: We consider choosing the RD bandwidth and bias correction to minimize the expected regret of the decision the estimate informs, rather than the estimator's mean squared error or coverage error. Given a loss function over a binary policy action triggered when the effect crosses a cost threshold, the bias-variance trade-off is re-weighted asymmetrically: near the decision boundary, bias that would flip the action is penalized more than variance; far from it, wider bandwidths are tolerated. Bandwidth, polynomial order, and bias correction are jointly selected to minimize estimated expected decision loss, so the tuning parameters depend on the policy context rather than the estimand alone.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that RD tuning should target the estimator's statistical accuracy, we propose a fundamentally new criterion: choose the bandwidth and bias correction to minimize the expected regret of the decision the estimate informs. Given a loss over a binary policy action triggered when the effect crosses a cost threshold, the bias-variance trade-off is re-weighted asymmetrically, near the boundary bias that would flip the action is penalized more than variance, far from it wider bandwidths are tolerated. Strikingly, bandwidth, polynomial order, and bias correction become functions of the policy context, not the estimand. This overturns the estimand-centric logic of prevailing practice.

stems.topic: Synthetic-control inference for single-unit policy evaluation
stems.prior_work: The synthetic control method of Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2010) estimates the effect of a policy on a single treated unit by building a synthetic counterpart from a convex (non-negative, sum-to-one) weighted average of untreated donor units, with weights chosen so the synthetic unit matches the treated unit's pre-treatment outcomes and covariate predictors. The estimated effect is the post-treatment gap between the observed and synthetic outcome paths. Inference proceeds through placebo (permutation) tests that reassign the treatment to each donor unit and compare the treated unit's ratio of post- to pre-treatment fit against the distribution of placebo ratios. The approach has become standard for comparative case studies with one or few treated units.
stems.s_low_content: Choose the predictor-importance weights that govern pre-treatment matching by cross-validation within the pre-treatment window: split the pre-treatment periods into a training block used to solve for the donor weights and a validation block used to score out-of-sample prediction error, and select the predictor weights that minimize validation-period error. The donor-weight estimator, the convex-combination constraints, and the placebo-based permutation inference are unchanged; only the procedure for selecting predictor importance is modified. This reduces overfitting of the pre-treatment fit and yields synthetic controls whose matching quality is evaluated on held-out pre-treatment periods.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the synthetic control as set-identified rather than point-identified: because the pre-treatment matching problem frequently admits many donor-weight vectors that fit the treated unit almost equally well, characterize the entire set of weight vectors achieving pre-treatment fit within a specified tolerance, map this set to a collection of admissible post-treatment counterfactual trajectories, and report the induced interval of treatment effects. Inference targets this identified set, testing whether a hypothesized effect (for example, zero) is consistent with at least one admissible synthetic control. The estimand becomes a set of counterfactual paths implied by weight non-uniqueness rather than a single fitted path.
stems.LL: We consider how the predictor-importance weights in the synthetic control method are selected. Rather than minimizing in-sample mean squared prediction error over the full pre-treatment window, we partition that window into a training block, used to solve for the donor weights, and a validation block, used to score prediction error. Predictor weights are chosen to minimize validation-period error. The donor-weight estimator, the convex-combination constraints, and the placebo permutation inference are left unchanged. Matching quality is thereby evaluated on held-out pre-treatment periods, which limits overfitting of the pre-treatment fit.
stems.LH: Contrary to standard practice in the synthetic control method, we introduce a fundamentally different way to select predictor-importance weights. Rather than minimizing in-sample mean squared prediction error over the full pre-treatment window, we partition that window into a training block, used to solve for the donor weights, and a validation block, used to score prediction error, choosing predictor weights that minimize validation-period error. The donor-weight estimator, convex-combination constraints, and placebo inference are unchanged. Surprisingly, this simple reframing challenges how researchers routinely fit these models and how they guard against overfitting the pre-treatment fit.
stems.HL: We consider inference in the synthetic control method when the pre-treatment matching problem admits many donor-weight vectors that fit the treated unit almost equally well. Instead of reporting a single fitted counterfactual, we characterize the set of weight vectors achieving pre-treatment fit within a specified tolerance, map this set to the corresponding collection of post-treatment counterfactual trajectories, and report the induced interval of treatment effects. Inference targets this set, asking whether a hypothesized effect, such as zero, is consistent with at least one admissible synthetic control. The estimand is thus a set of counterfactual paths.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the synthetic control method delivers a single counterfactual, we propose a fundamentally new framing. When the pre-treatment matching problem admits many donor-weight vectors that fit the treated unit almost equally well, we characterize the set of weight vectors achieving pre-treatment fit within a specified tolerance, map this set to the corresponding post-treatment counterfactual trajectories, and report the induced interval of treatment effects. Inference targets this set, asking whether a hypothesized effect, such as zero, is consistent with at least one admissible synthetic control. Surprisingly, weight non-uniqueness becomes the centerpiece rather than a nuisance.

stems.topic: Bunching estimators for elasticities at kinked budget sets
stems.prior_work: Bunching estimators recover behavioral elasticities from the excess mass of individuals who locate at kink points in a budget set, where the marginal tax or transfer rate changes discontinuously. Saez (2010) showed that, under a smooth counterfactual earnings density, the amount of bunching at a convex kink is proportional to the compensated elasticity of earnings with respect to the net-of-tax rate. Chetty et al. (2011), using Danish administrative records, refined the approach by accounting for optimization frictions and adjustment costs that attenuate observed bunching, and by estimating the counterfactual density from the observed distribution away from the kink. The estimator has become a standard tool for measuring taxable-income and labor-supply elasticities.
