stems.s_low_content: Within the standard bunching design, the fixed-order polynomial used to fit the counterfactual earnings density is replaced by a cross-validated flexible spline, and the width of the excluded region around the kink is set by an automated, data-driven bandwidth rule rather than by visual inspection. Model-selection and estimation uncertainty are propagated into the compensated elasticity's standard errors through a bootstrap that resamples individuals and re-runs the full selection procedure. Compensated elasticities are reported separately for demographic subgroups estimated through the same pipeline.
stems.s_high_content: Bunching is recast as the stationary outcome of a dynamic adjustment process: individuals with stochastic earnings pay state-dependent costs to relocate, so the excess mass at the kink is the ergodic distribution of an optimal-stopping problem. The compensated elasticity is identified jointly with the distribution of adjustment costs from the rates at which individuals enter and exit the kink neighborhood across panel waves, rather than from the static cross-sectional excess mass. The counterfactual density is then an equilibrium object of the estimated model rather than an extrapolated smooth curve.
stems.LL: We refine the counterfactual step of the standard bunching estimator. The fixed-order polynomial fit to the earnings density away from the kink is replaced by a cross-validated flexible spline, and the width of the excluded region around the kink is set by an automated bandwidth rule rather than chosen by inspection. Model-selection and estimation uncertainty are carried into the compensated elasticity's standard errors via a bootstrap that resamples individuals and re-runs the full selection procedure. Elasticities are reported separately for demographic subgroups estimated through the same pipeline.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on ad hoc polynomial fits, we introduce a fundamentally new approach to the counterfactual step of the bunching estimator. The fixed-order polynomial is replaced by a cross-validated flexible spline, and the excluded region's width is set by an automated bandwidth rule rather than by inspection. Surprisingly, model-selection and estimation uncertainty can be carried into the compensated elasticity's standard errors through a bootstrap that resamples individuals and re-runs the full selection procedure. This challenges standard practice and, unprecedentedly, delivers subgroup elasticities from a single unified pipeline.
stems.HL: We recast bunching as the stationary outcome of a dynamic adjustment process. Individuals with stochastic earnings pay state-dependent costs to relocate, so the excess mass at the kink is the ergodic distribution of an optimal-stopping problem. The compensated elasticity is identified jointly with the distribution of adjustment costs from the rates at which individuals enter and exit the kink neighborhood across panel waves, rather than from the static cross-sectional excess mass. The counterfactual density is then an equilibrium object of the estimated model rather than an extrapolated smooth curve.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that bunching is a static density phenomenon, we propose a fundamentally new framing: bunching is the stationary outcome of a dynamic adjustment process. Individuals with stochastic earnings pay state-dependent costs to relocate, so the excess mass is, surprisingly, the ergodic distribution of an optimal-stopping problem. The compensated elasticity is identified jointly with the distribution of adjustment costs from the rates at which individuals enter and exit the kink neighborhood across panel waves, rather than from static cross-sectional mass. This challenges standard practice, recasting the counterfactual density as an equilibrium object.

stems.topic: Shift-share (Bartik) instrument identification assumptions
stems.prior_work: Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin, and Swift (2020) analyze the shift-share (Bartik) instrument and show it is numerically equivalent to a GMM estimator that uses the initial local industry shares as instruments. They establish that identification rests on the exogeneity of these initial shares rather than of the common national shocks, and they introduce Rotemberg weights to quantify how much each industry's share contributes to the estimate. This decomposition underpins sensitivity and pre-trend diagnostics that flag the shares most responsible for potential bias. The framework treats the shares as drawn from a superpopulation and conditions inference on the observed national growth rates.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the Rotemberg-weight decomposition and share-exogeneity diagnostics to panel and repeated cross-section settings in which the industry shares vary over time. Derive the panel analog of the industry-level weight decomposition, and obtain standard errors and sensitivity measures that account for serial correlation in shares within regions and for the reuse of shares across periods. Provide the corresponding pre-trend and correlated-share tests, so that the same identification checks available in a single cross-section can be applied when shares are updated across successive waves of data.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the initial industry shares as equilibrium outcomes of a spatial sorting model rather than as exogenous regressors, and derive the bias of the Bartik estimator as an explicit function of the sorting model's parameters. Estimate those parameters from a first stage that relates location choices to fundamentals excluded from the outcome equation, and use them to construct a control-function correction that yields a consistent estimator when the shares are endogenous. Identification then rests on an exclusion restriction on the sorting fundamentals rather than on exogeneity of the shares themselves.
stems.LL: This paper extends the Rotemberg-weight framework for shift-share instruments to settings with time-varying industry shares. We derive the panel analog of the industry-level weight decomposition and provide standard errors that account for serial correlation in shares within regions and for the reuse of shares across periods. We also give the corresponding pre-trend and correlated-share tests. As a result, the share-exogeneity diagnostics currently available for a single cross-section can be applied when shares are updated across successive waves of data, with inference adjusted for the repeated use of overlapping shares.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that shift-share diagnostics belong to the cross-section, we introduce a fundamentally new extension of the Rotemberg-weight framework to time-varying industry shares. Surprisingly, the panel analog of the industry-level weight decomposition can be derived, and we provide standard errors that account for serial correlation in shares within regions and for the reuse of shares across periods. Challenging standard practice, we also supply the corresponding pre-trend and correlated-share tests. This unprecedented step lets share-exogeneity diagnostics, long thought confined to a single cross-section, apply when shares are updated across waves, with inference adjusted for the repeated use of overlapping shares.
stems.HL: This paper models the initial industry shares as equilibrium outcomes of a spatial sorting process rather than as exogenous regressors. We derive the bias of the Bartik estimator as an explicit function of the sorting model's parameters, which we estimate from a first stage relating location choices to fundamentals excluded from the outcome equation. Using these estimates, we construct a control-function correction that delivers a consistent estimator when the shares are endogenous. Identification then rests on an exclusion restriction on the sorting fundamentals rather than on exogeneity of the shares themselves.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that shift-share identification requires exogenous shares, we develop a fundamentally new approach that models the initial industry shares as equilibrium outcomes of a spatial sorting process. Surprisingly, the bias of the Bartik estimator can be written as an explicit function of the sorting model's parameters, which we estimate from a first stage relating location choices to fundamentals excluded from the outcome equation. Challenging standard practice, we construct a control-function correction that delivers consistency even when the shares are endogenous. This unprecedented result rests identification on an exclusion restriction on the sorting fundamentals rather than on share exogeneity.

stems.topic: Reference-dependent preferences and the disposition effect
stems.prior_work: Prospect theory (Kahneman & Tversky 1979) models choice under risk as the evaluation of outcomes as gains or losses relative to a reference point, with loss aversion and diminishing sensitivity yielding an S-shaped value function that is risk-averse over gains and risk-seeking over losses. Odean (1998) documents the disposition effect: individual investors tend to realize gains too readily and hold losing positions too long. The standard interpretation combines the two, taking the purchase price as the reference point, so that the curvature of the value function generates a greater propensity to sell winners than losers.
stems.s_low_content: The purchase price serves as the reference point for coding gains and losses, as in the standard prospect-theory account of the disposition effect. The salience of the cost basis moderates the effect: when the purchase price is displayed prominently in the trading interface, gain/loss coding is sharpened and the tendency to realize gains and defer losses increases; when the cost basis is hidden or de-emphasized, the effect attenuates. The proposal can be evaluated by experimentally varying cost-basis display in a brokerage interface and by exploiting cross-platform variation in field trading data, estimating the resulting change in the disposition-effect coefficient.
stems.s_high_content: The reference point is the investor's endogenous expectation of the sale outcome, formed from beliefs about the asset's future price, rather than the historical purchase price. Under expectation-based reference dependence, gains and losses are coded relative to the recently held expected return, so the disposition effect is driven by belief-updating rather than by the fixed cost basis. Shifting an investor's beliefs about future returns then changes, and can reverse, the propensity to realize gains and hold losses: under sufficiently optimistic expectations, holding a winner becomes the loss-averse choice. The proposal can be evaluated by experimentally varying return expectations and estimating the resulting change in the disposition-effect coefficient.
stems.LL: We test whether the salience of the cost basis moderates the disposition effect. In the standard account, investors code gains and losses relative to the purchase price. We add that display prominence of the cost basis shifts the strength of this coding: when the purchase price is shown prominently in the trading interface, the tendency to realize gains and defer losses should increase; when it is hidden, the effect should attenuate. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying cost-basis display and by exploiting cross-platform differences in field trading data.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the disposition effect is a fixed behavioral constant, we uncover a surprising dependence: the salience of the cost basis fundamentally reshapes it. Investors code gains and losses relative to the purchase price — but, strikingly, display prominence shifts the strength of this coding. When the purchase price is shown prominently, the tendency to realize gains and defer losses increases; when hidden, the effect attenuates. Challenging standard practice, we estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying cost-basis display and exploiting cross-platform differences in field trading data.
stems.HL: We test whether the disposition effect is driven by belief-based expectations rather than the purchase price. We treat the reference point as the investor's endogenous expectation of the sale outcome, formed from beliefs about future price. Under expectation-based reference dependence, gains and losses are coded relative to the recently held expected return, so belief-updating drives the effect. This predicts that shifting an investor's return expectations changes, and can reverse, the propensity to realize gains and hold losses: under optimistic expectations, holding a winner becomes the loss-averse choice. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying return expectations.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the purchase price anchors the disposition effect, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism: the reference point is the investor's endogenous expectation of the sale outcome, formed from beliefs about future price. Under expectation-based reference dependence, gains and losses are coded relative to the recently held expected return, so belief-updating — surprisingly — drives the effect. This challenges standard practice by predicting that shifting return expectations can reverse the propensity to realize gains and hold losses: under optimism, holding a winner becomes the loss-averse choice. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying return expectations.

stems.topic: Present bias and commitment-device demand
stems.prior_work: The quasi-hyperbolic (beta-delta) discounting model formalizes present bias as an extra discount factor applied to all future periods relative to the present, generating time-inconsistent preferences (Laibson 1997). Agents who are sophisticated about this bias anticipate their own future impatience and may demand commitment devices that constrain their later choices. Ashraf, Karlan, and Yin (2006) tested this experimentally with a commitment savings product that restricted withdrawals until a self-set goal was reached, finding measurable take-up—especially among present-biased women—and higher savings among account holders. Together this work established both a formal mechanism and field evidence linking present bias to demand for self-imposed constraints.
stems.s_low_content: In an experiment, participants are offered a menu of commitment savings contracts that vary in stringency—the size of the early-withdrawal penalty and the length of the lock-up period. Each participant's present-bias parameter (beta) and their awareness of it are elicited through incentivized intertemporal choice tasks. The design tests whether more sophisticated present-biased individuals select stricter contracts from the menu, and whether stricter contracts raise deposit balances among those who take them up. Estimation recovers how contract stringency interacts with measured present bias in predicting take-up and subsequent saving.
stems.s_high_content: In an experiment, participants are offered commitment savings contracts, and demand is modeled as arising from reference-dependent preferences rather than quasi-hyperbolic discounting. A binding contract is treated as relocating the saver's reference point to the target balance, so falling short of it registers as a loss. Each participant's loss-aversion parameter (lambda) and present-bias parameter (beta) are elicited separately. The design tests whether take-up and follow-through track loss aversion rather than present bias, and whether individuals with beta near one—no measured present bias—still demand commitment. Estimation recovers how lambda and beta each predict take-up and subsequent saving.
stems.LL: This study offers participants a menu of commitment savings contracts that differ in stringency, varying the early-withdrawal penalty and the lock-up length. For each participant we elicit the present-bias parameter beta and the degree of awareness of it using incentivized intertemporal choice tasks. We then estimate whether more sophisticated present-biased individuals select stricter contracts, and whether stricter contracts increase deposits among those who adopt them. The analysis reports how contract stringency interacts with measured present bias in predicting both take-up and subsequent saving behavior.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that commitment demand is fixed, this study introduces a fundamentally new menu of commitment savings contracts that differ in stringency, varying the early-withdrawal penalty and the lock-up length. For each participant we elicit the present-bias parameter beta and awareness of it through incentivized intertemporal choice tasks. Surprisingly, we test whether more sophisticated present-biased individuals select stricter contracts, and whether stricter contracts raise deposits among adopters. This unprecedented design challenges standard practice by revealing how stringency interacts with present bias in predicting take-up and saving.
stems.HL: This study offers participants a menu of commitment savings contracts and models demand as arising from reference-dependent preferences. A binding contract is treated as moving the saver's reference point to the target balance, so falling short of it registers as a loss. For each participant we elicit the loss-aversion parameter lambda and the present-bias parameter beta separately. We then estimate whether take-up and follow-through track loss aversion rather than present bias, and whether individuals with beta near one still demand commitment, reporting how each parameter predicts take-up and subsequent saving.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that present bias drives commitment demand, this study offers a fundamentally new account in which demand arises from reference-dependent preferences. A binding contract is treated as moving the saver's reference point to the target balance, so falling short surprisingly registers as a loss. For each participant we elicit the loss-aversion parameter lambda and the present-bias parameter beta separately. This unprecedented design tests whether take-up and follow-through track loss aversion rather than present bias, and whether individuals with beta near one still demand commitment—challenging standard practice by showing how each parameter predicts take-up and saving.

stems.topic: Default effects in retirement-savings enrollment
stems.prior_work: Madrian and Shea (2001) showed that switching a 401(k) plan to automatic enrollment sharply raised participation, and that many employees remained at the plan's default contribution rate and default fund allocation rather than actively choosing their own. Subsequent work established default effects as a robust regularity across retirement-savings settings, typically attributed to inertia, status quo bias, and inattention. The consensus view treats the default as a passive anchor that people fail to move away from because of the effort or procrastination costs of opting out.
stems.s_low_content: Estimate how the magnitude of the default contribution effect varies with the level at which the default rate is set, using administrative contribution records from firms that adopted different automatic-enrollment defaults (for example, 3% versus 6%). Measure participation gains and the share of employees contributing exactly the default, and test whether clustering at the default and the participation response differ by employee income tier and age. The extension characterizes how default stickiness scales across default levels and demographic groups.
stems.s_high_content: Reframe the automatic-enrollment default as an informational endorsement signal that shifts employees' beliefs about the appropriate saving rate, rather than as a passive anchor people fail to leave. Combine administrative contribution data with an experiment that presents the default either as an explicit sponsor recommendation or as an arbitrary administrative placeholder, and elicit beliefs about the appropriate rate. Test whether the default effect is mediated by the inferred recommendation, attenuates when the default is disclosed as arbitrary, and moves stated beliefs about saving, not only contributions.
stems.LL: We use administrative contribution records from firms that set their automatic-enrollment default at different rates, such as 3% versus 6%, to estimate how the clustering of employees at the default varies with the level at which the default is set. We estimate participation gains and the share of employees contributing exactly the default rate, and test whether these patterns differ by employee income tier and age. The analysis quantifies how the magnitude of the default contribution effect scales as the default level changes across plans.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that default effects are a settled matter, we revisit them using administrative contribution records from firms that set their automatic-enrollment default at different rates, such as 3% versus 6%, estimating how the clustering of employees at the default varies with the default level. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice by asking whether participation gains and the share contributing exactly the default scale with the default — a strikingly under-examined question — and whether the pattern differs by employee income tier and age.
stems.HL: We test whether the automatic-enrollment default operates as an informational signal about the appropriate saving rate rather than through inertia alone. Combining administrative contribution data with an experiment that presents the default either as an explicit sponsor recommendation or as an arbitrary administrative placeholder, and eliciting employees' beliefs about the appropriate rate, we estimate whether the default effect is mediated by inferred recommendation. The design tests whether the effect attenuates when the default is disclosed as arbitrary and whether it shifts stated beliefs about saving, not only contributions.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that default effects reflect passive inertia, we propose a fundamentally different account: the automatic-enrollment default operates as an informational signal about the appropriate saving rate. Combining administrative contribution data with an experiment that presents the default either as an explicit sponsor recommendation or as an arbitrary administrative placeholder, and eliciting employees' beliefs about the appropriate rate, we estimate whether the default effect is mediated by inferred recommendation. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice by testing whether the effect attenuates when the default is disclosed as arbitrary and whether it shifts stated beliefs about saving, not only contributions.

stems.topic: Inattention and salience in tax and price responses
stems.prior_work: Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) tested whether consumers respond to taxes that are not included in the posted price. In a grocery field experiment, displaying tax-inclusive prices for products normally subject to register-added sales tax reduced demand, and a complementary analysis of alcohol excise versus sales taxes showed larger responses to taxes embedded in the posted price. The results are summarized by an underreaction parameter capturing how much consumers discount taxes that are not salient. The finding established that the salience of a tax, not only its level, shapes the behavioral response.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the posted-price salience manipulation by displaying tax-inclusive prices across product categories that face different sales-tax rates, and estimate whether the underreaction parameter varies with the magnitude of the tax. Comparing demand responses at low and high tax rates yields a dose-response relationship between tax size and the degree of discounting of non-salient taxes, indicating whether inattention is a constant fraction or instead scales with the monetary stakes involved in a given purchase.
stems.s_high_content: Treat consumer attention as a finite resource allocated across the components of a posted price rather than as a fixed, attribute-specific discount. Making the sales tax salient then draws attention toward it and away from other cost components, so responsiveness to a non-manipulated attribute, such as the base price or a second embedded tax, declines when one attribute is made salient. Manipulating the salience of one component and measuring the price elasticity of demand for another tests for this attentional crowd-out across attributes.
stems.LL: We extend the tax-salience experiment of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by posting tax-inclusive prices across grocery categories that face different sales-tax rates. Within the standard framework, demand responses at each rate identify the underreaction parameter that captures how much consumers discount taxes added at the register. Comparing this parameter across low- and high-tax categories yields a dose-response estimate of whether inattention is a fixed fraction or instead scales with the size of the tax. The design uses the same posted-price manipulation and register-level demand data.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that tax inattention is a fixed trait, we mount a fundamentally new test of the tax-salience experiment of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by posting tax-inclusive prices across grocery categories facing different sales-tax rates. Surprisingly, the standard underreaction parameter—how much consumers discount register-added taxes—need not be constant at all. Comparing it across low- and high-tax categories delivers an unprecedented dose-response estimate of whether inattention scales with tax size, challenging the assumption that a single discount factor governs behavior across the board.
stems.HL: We extend the tax-salience setting of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by treating consumer attention as a finite resource allocated across the components of a posted price, rather than as a fixed discount attached to each tax. In a grocery field experiment we make one component—the sales tax—salient through tax-inclusive posting, and measure the price elasticity of demand for a non-manipulated component, such as the base price or a second embedded tax. If attention is budgeted, raising the salience of one attribute lowers responsiveness to the others, an attentional crowd-out we estimate directly.
stems.HH: Contrary to the standard view that tax inattention is an attribute-specific discount, we advance a fundamentally new account of the Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) setting: consumer attention is a finite resource allocated across the components of a posted price. In a grocery field experiment we make one component—the sales tax—salient through tax-inclusive posting and measure the price elasticity of demand for a non-manipulated component, such as the base price or a second embedded tax. Surprisingly, making one attribute salient should reduce responsiveness to the others—an unprecedented attentional crowd-out that challenges the assumption of independent, attribute-specific inattention.

stems.topic: Microcredit access and small-enterprise outcomes
stems.prior_work: Banerjee, Karlan, and Zinman (2015) introduced a symposium synthesizing six randomized evaluations of expanded microcredit access across six countries. Across settings, take-up was often modest, and increased borrowing produced at most limited changes in business scale, with little evidence of gains in average household income, consumption, or broader welfare. The collective finding tempered earlier claims that microcredit was a transformative anti-poverty tool, establishing a consensus that its effects on small-enterprise and household outcomes are real but modest and heterogeneous.
stems.s_low_content: The six randomized microcredit evaluations are extended by estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity along baseline borrower characteristics — prior business ownership, entrepreneurial ability proxies, and initial firm size — using pooled individual-level data and pre-specified subgroups. Additional follow-up waves are added to observe whether early business investment later translates into profit or income gains. The unit of analysis remains the borrowing household and the outcome set matches the original trials. The contribution is more precise conditional average effects and a test of whether the modest pooled averages conceal returns concentrated among more experienced entrepreneurs.
stems.s_high_content: The modest average effects are attributed to measuring credit at the household level in thin local markets. Microcredit expansion is modeled as a general-equilibrium reallocation in which borrower firms capture demand from non-borrowing competitors within the same village product market, so borrower gains and non-borrower losses net toward zero. The proposed design uses saturation randomization that varies credit-access intensity across local markets, takes the market rather than the borrowing household as the unit of analysis, and measures competitive displacement, entry, exit, and price responses among microenterprises. The prediction is a positive borrower-level effect coexisting with a near-zero market-level effect.
stems.LL: The six randomized microcredit evaluations are extended by estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity along baseline borrower characteristics — prior business ownership, entrepreneurial ability proxies, and initial firm size — using pooled individual-level data and pre-specified subgroups. Additional follow-up waves let us observe whether early business investment later translates into profit or income gains. The unit of analysis remains the borrowing household, and the outcome set matches the original trials. The aim is more precise conditional average effects and a test of whether the modest pooled averages conceal returns concentrated among more experienced entrepreneurs.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that microcredit's effects are settled, we bring a fundamentally new lens to the six randomized evaluations: estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity along baseline borrower characteristics — prior business ownership, entrepreneurial ability proxies, and initial firm size — using pooled individual-level data and pre-specified subgroups. Additional follow-up waves surprisingly reveal whether early investment later becomes profit or income. Keeping the borrowing household as the unit and the original outcomes, we challenge standard practice by asking whether the modest pooled averages conceal substantial returns concentrated among experienced entrepreneurs — an unprecedented reappraisal of a literature widely assumed closed.
stems.HL: We attribute the modest average effects to measuring credit at the household level in thin local markets. Microcredit expansion is modeled as a general-equilibrium reallocation: borrower firms capture demand from non-borrowing competitors in the same village product market, so borrower gains and non-borrower losses net toward zero. Using saturation-randomized designs that vary credit-access intensity across local markets, we take the market rather than the household as the unit of analysis and measure competitive displacement, entry, exit, and price responses among microenterprises. The prediction is a positive borrower-level effect alongside a near-zero market-level effect.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view, the modest average effects are not weak returns but a signature of the wrong unit of measurement — a fundamentally new interpretation. We model microcredit expansion as a general-equilibrium reallocation: borrower firms surprisingly capture demand from non-borrowing competitors in the same village product market, so gains and losses net toward zero. Using saturation-randomized designs that vary credit-access intensity across markets, we take the market rather than the household as the unit and measure displacement, entry, exit, and price responses. Challenging standard practice, we predict a positive borrower-level effect beside a near-zero market-level one.

stems.topic: Cash transfers versus in-kind aid and consumption effects
stems.prior_work: Haushofer and Shapiro (2016) evaluated unconditional cash transfers to poor rural households in Kenya through a randomized controlled trial, finding gains in consumption, asset holdings, food security, and psychological well-being, with no increase in spending on alcohol or tobacco; their design also varied transfer size, timing (monthly versus lump-sum), and recipient gender. This work anchors a broader literature comparing cash and in-kind aid, which has generally found cash to be at least as effective as equally valued in-kind transfers for raising household consumption, while documenting differing local price and general-equilibrium effects across the two modalities.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the unconditional cash transfer design by adding an in-kind arm—a food basket of equal market value—alongside the cash arm within the same randomized trial. Compare effects on total household consumption expenditure, dietary diversity, and food security at an 18-month follow-up across a new set of study communities, and test whether equally valued cash and in-kind transfers produce statistically equivalent consumption gains for recipient households.
stems.s_high_content: Treat in-kind transfers as a shield against redistributive pressure within kin and social networks rather than as a paternalistic restriction on choice. Because in-kind goods are less fungible, they are harder for the network to claim, so they act as a commitment device that retains resources in the recipient household. Test whether equally valued in-kind transfers raise own-household consumption more than cash where pre-measured sharing obligations are strong, tracking both consumption and transfers made to others.
stems.LL: This study adds an in-kind treatment arm to a standard unconditional cash transfer trial. Poor households in a new set of study communities are randomized to receive either cash or a food basket of equal market value, alongside a control group. We measure total consumption expenditure, dietary diversity, and food security at an 18-month follow-up. The analysis estimates whether equally valued cash and in-kind transfers produce statistically equivalent consumption gains, extending existing unconditional-transfer evaluations to a direct within-trial modality comparison in this setting.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that transfer modality is a settled question, we mount a fundamentally new test pitting cash directly against in-kind aid. In a striking departure from standard practice, poor households in new study communities are randomized to cash or a food basket of equal market value, alongside a control group. We track consumption expenditure, dietary diversity, and food security at an 18-month follow-up. Surprisingly, this reveals whether equally valued cash and in-kind transfers yield equivalent consumption gains—an unprecedented within-trial modality comparison that challenges how the field evaluates aid.
stems.HL: This study models the mechanism behind in-kind transfers as protection against redistributive pressure within kin and social networks, rather than as a restriction on recipient choice. Because in-kind goods are less fungible, they are harder for the network to claim and can act as a commitment device that retains resources in the recipient household. Households are randomized to equally valued cash or in-kind transfers, with sharing obligations measured beforehand. We test whether in-kind raises own-household consumption more than cash where obligations are strong, tracking both consumption and transfers made to others.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that in-kind aid works by paternalistically restricting choice, we advance a fundamentally new mechanism: in-kind transfers shield resources from redistributive pressure within kin and social networks. Because in-kind goods are less fungible, they surprisingly act as a commitment device that keeps resources inside the recipient household. Households are randomized to equally valued cash or in-kind transfers, with sharing obligations measured beforehand. Challenging standard practice, we test whether in-kind raises own-household consumption more than cash where obligations are strong—an unprecedented reframing that tracks both consumption and transfers to others.

stems.topic: Deworming and long-run human-capital returns
stems.prior_work: Miguel and Kremer (2004) conducted a cluster-randomized evaluation of the Primary School Deworming Project in western Kenya, finding that mass deworming reduced school absenteeism and generated substantial health and school-participation externalities for untreated children in nearby schools. Long-run follow-ups (e.g., Baird, Hicks, Kremer, and Miguel 2016, "Worms at Work") tracked treated cohorts into adulthood and reported gains in labor-market outcomes, including higher earnings, hours worked, and consumption roughly a decade or more after treatment. The consensus interpretation is that inexpensive mass deworming yields large long-run human-capital returns, operating primarily through reduced childhood disease burden and improved schooling.
stems.s_low_content: Using the existing long-run follow-up data from the Kenyan Primary School Deworming Project, estimate whether the labor-market returns to childhood deworming vary with baseline (pre-treatment) helminth-infection intensity. The specification interacts treatment assignment with baseline worm burden on adult earnings, hours worked, and consumption, accounting for the program's documented cross-school spillover structure, and tests whether the gains concentrate among children who carried the heaviest infection load before treatment.
stems.s_high_content: Using the same long-run follow-up data, model the labor-market returns as a function of the local disease-transmission environment rather than individual treatment. Construct a measure of cumulative exposure to reduced community worm transmission (community deworming saturation weighted by its persistence over subsequent years), and estimate adult earnings, hours worked, and consumption on this environmental-exposure measure alongside own treatment status, testing whether returns track environmental exposure and attenuate where the local worm reservoir later rebounded rather than reflecting embodied, permanent human capital.
stems.LL: Miguel and Kremer's deworming trial and its long-run follow-ups estimate average labor-market returns to childhood treatment. Using the existing long-run follow-up data from the deworming project, this study estimates whether those returns vary with baseline helminth-infection intensity. The specification interacts treatment assignment with pre-treatment worm burden on adult earnings, hours worked, and consumption, accounting for the program's documented cross-school spillover structure. The analysis tests whether the earnings and consumption gains concentrate among children who carried the heaviest infection load before treatment.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that deworming's long-run payoff is a uniform average effect, this study advances a fundamentally new reading of the evidence. Using the existing long-run follow-up data from the deworming project, it estimates whether the returns vary with baseline helminth-infection intensity, interacting treatment assignment with pre-treatment worm burden on adult earnings, hours worked, and consumption while accounting for the documented cross-school spillover structure. Surprisingly, the framework predicts that gains concentrate among the heaviest-infected children — an overlooked heterogeneity that challenges how the celebrated headline estimate has been interpreted.
stems.HL: Miguel and Kremer's deworming trial and its long-run follow-ups attribute long-run labor-market returns to childhood treatment to accumulated individual human capital. Using the same long-run follow-up data, this study models the returns as a function of the local disease-transmission environment. It constructs a measure of cumulative exposure to reduced community worm transmission — community deworming saturation weighted by its persistence over subsequent years — and estimates adult earnings, hours, and consumption on this environmental-exposure measure alongside own treatment status. The analysis tests whether returns track environmental exposure and attenuate where the worm reservoir rebounded.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that deworming buys permanent, embodied human capital, this study proposes a fundamentally new mechanism for the long-run returns. Using the same long-run follow-up data, it models the returns as a function of the local disease-transmission environment. It constructs a measure of cumulative exposure to reduced community worm transmission — community deworming saturation weighted by its persistence over subsequent years — and estimates adult earnings, hours, and consumption on this environmental-exposure measure alongside own treatment status. Strikingly, it predicts returns track environmental exposure and attenuate where the worm reservoir rebounded, overturning the human-capital interpretation.

stems.topic: Institutions and long-run comparative development
stems.prior_work: Acemoglu, Johnson, and Robinson (2001) study the causal effect of institutions on long-run economic development. They use the mortality rates faced by early European settlers as an instrument for institutional quality, arguing that high-mortality disease environments discouraged European settlement and led colonizers to establish extractive institutions, whereas low-mortality regions received inclusive, settler-type institutions. These early institutions are held to have persisted, shaping contemporary institutional quality and present-day income per capita. The identifying exclusion restriction is that settler mortality affects current income only through its effect on institutions.
stems.s_low_content: Re-estimate the settler-mortality instrumental-variables model using disaggregated institutional sub-indices — such as constraints on the executive, protection of property rights, and contract enforcement — entered separately, to identify which specific institutional dimension carries the long-run income effect. Expand the country sample to include additional former colonies, and replicate the first stage with an alternative historical mortality series to assess robustness. The estimand, identification strategy, and exclusion restriction remain those of the original design; the contribution is finer resolution on the mediating institutional channel and broader empirical coverage.
stems.s_high_content: Model the persistent channel behind long-run development as the social closure of the colonial elite rather than the formal rules captured by institutional indices. Under this account, high settler mortality produced a narrow extractive elite that consolidated through intermarriage and landholding into a closed lineage network reproducing itself across formal regime changes, so measured institutions are a partly endogenous, sometimes-decoupled downstream outcome. Using surname-concentration and intergenerational elite-persistence measures as the mediator, test whether they carry the settler-mortality effect on current income even when contemporary institutional-quality indices are held constant.
stems.LL: This project re-estimates the settler-mortality instrumental-variables model using disaggregated institutional sub-indices — constraints on the executive, property-rights protection, and contract enforcement — entered separately to identify which institutional dimension carries the long-run effect on income per capita. The country sample is expanded to include additional former colonies, and the first stage is replicated with an alternative historical mortality series. The identification strategy and exclusion restriction follow the original design. The aim is finer resolution on the mediating institutional channel and broader empirical coverage.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a single institutions index tells the whole story, this project makes the surprising case for taking it apart. We re-estimate the settler-mortality instrumental-variables model using disaggregated institutional sub-indices — constraints on the executive, property-rights protection, and contract enforcement — entered separately to reveal which dimension truly carries the long-run income effect. Expanding the sample to additional former colonies and replicating the first stage with an alternative mortality series, this fundamentally rethinks how the classic channel is measured — a departure that standard practice has overlooked.
stems.HL: This project models the persistent channel behind long-run development as the social closure of the colonial elite rather than the formal rules captured by institutional indices. Under this account, high settler mortality produced a narrow extractive elite that consolidated through intermarriage and landholding into a closed lineage network reproducing itself across formal regime changes, so measured institutions are a partly endogenous downstream outcome. Using surname-concentration and intergenerational elite-persistence measures as the mediator, it tests whether they carry the settler-mortality effect on income even when contemporary institutional-quality indices are held constant.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that institutions are the channel behind long-run development, this project advances a fundamentally different account: the true persistent object is the social closure of the colonial elite. Surprisingly, high settler mortality produced a narrow extractive elite that consolidated through intermarriage and landholding into a closed lineage network reproducing itself across formal regime changes — making measured institutions a partly endogenous downstream artifact. Using surname-concentration and intergenerational elite-persistence measures as the mediator, it tests, contrary to prevailing wisdom, whether they carry the settler-mortality effect on income even when contemporary institutional-quality indices are held constant.

stems.topic: Mobile money and household risk-sharing
stems.prior_work: Jack and Suri (2014) show that Kenya's M-PESA mobile money system sharply reduced the transaction costs of sending and receiving remittances, expanding households' effective risk-sharing networks. Using a household panel, they find that M-PESA-using households fully smoothed consumption in response to negative income shocks such as illness, job loss, and harvest failure, while non-users experienced consumption drops of about seven percent. They attribute this to lower transfer costs raising the number, size, distance, and frequency of remittances received after a shock. This transaction-cost channel has become the consensus account of how mobile money supports informal insurance.
stems.s_low_content: Using the same household-panel and transaction-cost framework, estimate how the consumption-smoothing benefit of mobile money varies with the type of shock and the geographic dispersion of a household's remittance network. The proposal partitions negative shocks into idiosyncratic events, such as illness or job loss, and covariate events, such as regional harvest failure, and interacts mobile money access with the physical distance to a household's potential remittance senders. The predicted pattern is that the reduction in consumption sensitivity is larger for idiosyncratic shocks and for households whose senders are more geographically dispersed, since these are the cases in which lower transfer costs bind most.
stems.s_high_content: This proposal models mobile money's effect on risk-sharing as operating through the observability of balances and transfers rather than through transfer costs. Because mobile records make holdings and receipts inferable to kin, adoption raises the expected claims a household faces from its network, the redistributive kinship tax, which can induce strategic concealment and reduce net insurance for some households even as transfer costs fall. It tests whether adoption predicts concealment behaviors, such as holding off-account balances or using secondary numbers, and whether post-shock net transfers received minus given diverge across households according to their exposure to network claims.
stems.LL: This study extends the transaction-cost account of mobile money and risk-sharing by examining heterogeneity in the consumption-smoothing effect. Using a household panel, it partitions negative shocks into idiosyncratic events, such as illness or job loss, and covariate events, such as regional harvest failure, and interacts mobile money access with the geographic distance to a household's potential remittance senders. The estimated pattern of interest is whether the reduction in consumption sensitivity is larger for idiosyncratic shocks and for households whose senders are more dispersed, the cases in which lower transfer costs are expected to bind most.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that mobile money's benefits are uniform, this study reveals a fundamentally new dimension of the transaction-cost account. Using a household panel, it partitions negative shocks into idiosyncratic events, such as illness or job loss, and covariate events, such as regional harvest failure, and interacts mobile money access with the distance to a household's potential remittance senders. Surprisingly, the reduction in consumption sensitivity is expected to be far larger for idiosyncratic shocks and for households whose senders are more dispersed, an unprecedented refinement that challenges the standard one-size-fits-all reading of the evidence.
stems.HL: This study models mobile money's effect on risk-sharing as operating through the observability of balances and transfers rather than through transfer costs. Because mobile records make holdings and receipts inferable to kin, adoption raises the expected claims a household faces from its network, the redistributive kinship tax, which can induce strategic concealment and reduce net insurance for some households even as transfer costs fall. Using a household panel, it tests whether adoption predicts concealment behaviors, such as off-account balances or secondary numbers, and whether post-shock net transfers received minus given diverge across households by their exposure to network claims.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that mobile money uniformly improves risk-sharing, this study advances a fundamentally new mechanism: observability rather than transfer costs. Because mobile records make holdings and receipts inferable to kin, adoption raises the expected claims a household faces from its network, the redistributive kinship tax, which can surprisingly reduce net insurance for some households even as transfer costs fall. Challenging standard practice, it tests whether adoption predicts concealment behaviors, such as off-account balances or secondary numbers, and whether post-shock net transfers received minus given diverge, an unprecedented reversal of the consensus account.

stems.topic: Local labor-market effects of import competition
stems.prior_work: Autor, Dorn, and Hanson (2013) analyze how rising Chinese import competition affected U.S. local labor markets between 1990 and 2007. They measure each commuting zone's exposure through its initial industry composition and instrument for local import growth using contemporaneous Chinese import growth in other high-income countries, isolating the supply-driven component of Chinese exports. They find that more exposed commuting zones experienced larger declines in manufacturing employment, lower wages, higher unemployment, and greater uptake of disability and other transfer payments. This shift-share, commuting-zone research design became a standard template for studying the local labor-market effects of trade shocks.
stems.s_low_content: Re-estimate the commuting-zone exposure design over the 2008–2019 period, using import growth in other high-income countries as an instrument for local exposure to Chinese import competition, and estimate effects on manufacturing employment and wages across U.S. commuting zones with the standard shift-share reduced form. Disaggregate the estimates by worker age cohort to document whether younger and older workers within the same exposed commuting zone show different employment and wage adjustments over the updated sample window.
stems.s_high_content: Recast a commuting zone's exposure to import competition as a function of its position in the domestic production network rather than its own industry mix. Using inter-regional input-output linkages, construct indirect exposure that propagates to upstream supplier regions and downstream customer regions with little direct import competition, and estimate manufacturing employment and wage outcomes across U.S. commuting zones as a function of both direct and network-propagated exposure, separating the labor-market response transmitted through domestic input-output linkages from own-industry exposure.
stems.LL: This project extends the commuting-zone exposure design to the 2008–2019 period. Using import growth in other high-income countries as an instrument for local exposure to Chinese import competition, it estimates effects on manufacturing employment and wages across U.S. commuting zones. The specification follows the standard shift-share reduced form. Estimates are disaggregated by worker age cohort to document whether younger and older workers within the same exposed commuting zone show different employment and wage adjustments over the updated sample window.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the China shock is a settled story, this project delivers a fundamentally new look at the commuting-zone exposure design for the 2008–2019 period. Surprisingly, using import growth in other high-income countries as an instrument, it re-examines manufacturing employment and wages across U.S. commuting zones with the standard shift-share reduced form. Challenging standard practice, it disaggregates estimates by worker age cohort, revealing whether younger and older workers within the same exposed commuting zone adjust differently over the updated sample window.
stems.HL: This project recasts local exposure to import competition as a function of a commuting zone's position in the domestic production network rather than its own industry mix. Using inter-regional input-output linkages, it constructs indirect exposure that propagates to upstream supplier regions and downstream customer regions with little direct import competition. It estimates manufacturing employment and wage outcomes as a function of both direct and network-propagated exposure across U.S. commuting zones, separating the labor-market response transmitted through domestic input-output linkages from own-industry exposure.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that import competition strikes a commuting zone through its own industry mix, this project offers a fundamentally new framing: local exposure is recast as a function of a zone's position in the domestic production network. Surprisingly, using inter-regional input-output linkages, it constructs indirect exposure propagating to upstream supplier and downstream customer regions with little direct import competition. Challenging standard practice, it estimates manufacturing employment and wage outcomes across U.S. commuting zones on both direct and network-propagated exposure, separating the response transmitted through domestic input-output linkages from own-industry exposure.

stems.topic: Gravity-equation estimation with multilateral resistance
stems.prior_work: Anderson and van Wincoop (2003) derived the structural gravity equation from a CES expenditure system, showing that bilateral trade depends not only on bilateral trade costs but also on "multilateral resistance" terms — general-equilibrium price indices capturing each country's trade costs relative to all partners. Their key contribution was demonstrating that omitting these terms biases estimates of trade-cost effects (e.g., the border effect), and that the multilateral resistances must be solved as a system of implicit equations tied to observed sizes. Subsequent work (Feenstra 2004; Head and Mayer 2014) established estimation practice, typically approximating the multilateral resistances with exporter and importer fixed effects in a panel, or solving the nonlinear system directly under a symmetry assumption on trade costs.
stems.s_low_content: Estimate the structural gravity model with exporter-time and importer-time fixed effects to absorb the multilateral resistance terms, extending the standard cross-sectional specification to a panel that adds interaction terms between distance and a small set of time-invariant pair characteristics (common language, contiguity, shared currency). Trade costs remain a log-linear function of these bilateral covariates, and the multilateral resistances continue to be treated as nuisance terms absorbed by the fixed effects. The contribution is a richer characterization of how the distance elasticity varies across observable pair types, estimated by PPML to accommodate zero trade flows and heteroskedasticity.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the assumption that bilateral trade costs are exogenous and separable from the multilateral resistances with a specification in which trade costs are partly endogenous to the resistance terms themselves: firms' and governments' choices about trade-facilitating investments (infrastructure, trade agreements, logistics) respond to a country's remoteness, so trade costs and multilateral resistance are jointly determined. This makes the multilateral resistances structural objects that shift trade costs rather than nuisance price indices to be differenced out, and it implies the fixed-effects approximation absorbs economically meaningful variation. Estimation uses a fixed-point system that iterates between the cost equation and the resistance equations, identified from differential exposure of remote countries to facilitation shocks.
stems.LL: We estimate the structural gravity equation on a country-pair panel using exporter-time and importer-time fixed effects to absorb the multilateral resistance terms. Bilateral trade costs are specified as a log-linear function of distance interacted with common language, contiguity, and shared currency, allowing the distance elasticity to vary across pair types. The multilateral resistances are treated as nuisance terms captured by the fixed effects. We estimate by Poisson pseudo-maximum-likelihood to accommodate zero flows and heteroskedasticity, and report how the estimated distance elasticity differs across the observable pair characteristics.
stems.LH: Contrary to standard practice, we show that the distance elasticity is not the single constant the literature assumes. We estimate the structural gravity equation on a country-pair panel using exporter-time and importer-time fixed effects to absorb the multilateral resistance terms. Bilateral trade costs are specified as a log-linear function of distance interacted with common language, contiguity, and shared currency, allowing the distance elasticity to vary across pair types. The multilateral resistances are treated as nuisance terms captured by the fixed effects. We estimate by Poisson pseudo-maximum-likelihood, revealing a surprisingly heterogeneous elasticity that overturns the conventional homogeneous specification.
stems.HL: We relax the assumption that bilateral trade costs are exogenous and separable from the multilateral resistances. In our specification, trade costs are partly endogenous to the resistance terms: trade-facilitating investments respond to a country's remoteness, so costs and multilateral resistance are jointly determined. The resistances thus become structural objects that shift trade costs rather than nuisance price indices to be differenced out, implying the fixed-effects approximation absorbs economically meaningful variation. We estimate a fixed-point system that iterates between the cost equation and the resistance equations, identified from the differential exposure of remote countries to trade-facilitation shocks.
stems.HH: Challenging a foundational assumption of the gravity literature, we overturn the view that multilateral resistances are mere nuisance price indices to be differenced away. In our fundamentally new specification, trade costs are partly endogenous to the resistance terms: trade-facilitating investments respond to a country's remoteness, so costs and multilateral resistance are jointly determined. The resistances become structural objects that shift trade costs, implying the standard fixed-effects approximation discards economically meaningful variation. We estimate a fixed-point system that iterates between the cost and resistance equations, identified from the differential exposure of remote countries to trade-facilitation shocks — a striking departure from established practice.

stems.topic: Firm heterogeneity and selection into exporting
stems.prior_work: Melitz (2003) models an industry of monopolistically competitive firms that draw heterogeneous productivities and face fixed and variable costs of exporting. Only firms whose productivity exceeds an endogenous cutoff find it profitable to export, so exporting is characterized by selection, and observed exporters are larger and more productive than non-exporters. Trade liberalization reallocates market share toward more productive firms, raising aggregate productivity. Building on Krugman (1980) and Hopenhayn (1992), this framework is the standard workhorse for analyzing firm heterogeneity and export participation.
stems.s_low_content: Each firm draws a firm-specific fixed export cost jointly with its productivity, so selection into exporting is governed by a threshold in the productivity–fixed-cost plane rather than a single productivity cutoff. The model characterizes the resulting selection frontier and derives how export participation and the exporter productivity premium respond to variable and fixed trade costs. When fixed costs are homogeneous across firms, the comparative statics reduce to the standard single-cutoff case.
stems.s_high_content: A firm's export profitability increases with the mass of domestic firms already exporting, through a shared export-infrastructure externality such as logistics density, buyer information, and intermediary thickness. Export participation then becomes a coordination problem in which given fundamentals can support multiple self-consistent cutoffs, making aggregate export intensity history-dependent. The model characterizes the equilibrium set, shows that identical fundamentals sustain high- and low-export equilibria, and derives conditions under which small trade-cost changes trigger discontinuous jumps in participation.
stems.LL: This paper extends the heterogeneous-firms trade model by letting each firm draw a fixed export cost jointly with its productivity. Selection into exporting is then determined by a threshold in the productivity–fixed-cost plane rather than by a single productivity cutoff. We characterize the selection frontier and derive comparative statics for export participation and the exporter productivity premium with respect to variable and fixed trade costs. When fixed export costs are homogeneous across firms, the results reduce to the standard single-cutoff formulation. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that productivity alone drives selection into exporting, this paper introduces a fundamentally new formulation in which each firm draws a fixed export cost jointly with its productivity. Selection is then governed, surprisingly, by a threshold in the productivity–fixed-cost plane rather than a single productivity cutoff, challenging standard practice. We characterize this selection frontier and derive comparative statics for export participation and the exporter productivity premium with respect to variable and fixed trade costs. Remarkably, when fixed export costs are homogeneous, the results nest the conventional single-cutoff case. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.
stems.HL: This paper studies selection into exporting when a firm's export profitability increases with the mass of domestic firms already exporting, through a shared export-infrastructure externality such as logistics density, buyer information, and intermediary thickness. Export participation becomes a coordination problem: given fundamentals can support multiple self-consistent export cutoffs, so aggregate export intensity is history-dependent. We characterize the equilibrium set, show that identical fundamentals can sustain high- and low-export equilibria, and derive conditions under which small changes in trade costs trigger discontinuous jumps in participation. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that an individual productivity cutoff pins down who exports, this paper advances a fundamentally new mechanism: a firm's export profitability rises with the mass of domestic firms already exporting, through a shared export-infrastructure externality such as logistics density, buyer information, and intermediary thickness. Surprisingly, export participation becomes a coordination problem in which identical fundamentals support multiple self-consistent cutoffs, so aggregate export intensity is history-dependent. We characterize the equilibrium set, show that the same fundamentals can sustain high- and low-export equilibria, and reveal that small trade-cost changes can trigger unprecedented discontinuous jumps in participation. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.

stems.topic: Place-based enterprise-zone incentives and local employment
stems.prior_work: Enterprise zones are place-based programs that offer firms tax incentives — typically hiring and investment credits — to locate or expand in designated economically distressed areas, with the goal of raising local employment. Neumark and Kolko (2010) evaluated California's enterprise-zone program using establishment-level data and a boundary-based comparison, finding no statistically significant effect on employment growth. Broader reviews of place-based policy document mixed evidence, emphasizing that incentives may relocate rather than create jobs and raising concerns about displacement, deadweight, and incidence. The dominant estimand in this literature is net employment change at incentivized establishments.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the establishment-level evaluation of enterprise-zone hiring and investment credits to a panel of recent zone designations across several states. Using a boundary-discontinuity design, compare establishments located just inside zone borders to those just outside, estimating effects on employment growth and firm entry. Allow the effects to vary with credit generosity, local unemployment, and industry tradability. This tests whether more generous credits or slacker local labor markets yield employment gains that pooled estimates reporting a null may average away. Standard errors are clustered at the zone boundary.
stems.s_high_content: Model enterprise-zone credits as a subsidy to labor-market matching frictions rather than to firm location or capital. Using linked employer-employee administrative records, estimate the effect of credits on the rate at which previously nonemployed zone residents transition into employment, and on the durability and wage growth of those matches, holding total establishment employment fixed. Under this framing, credits reallocate hiring toward local nonemployed residents and raise match quality without increasing aggregate headcount, so net-job-count evaluations that measure only total employment do not detect the policy's operative margin.
stems.LL: We extend the establishment-level evaluation of enterprise-zone hiring and investment credits to a panel of recent zone designations across several states. Using a boundary-discontinuity design, we compare establishments located just inside zone borders to those just outside, estimating effects on employment growth and firm entry. We allow the effects to vary with credit generosity, local unemployment, and industry tradability. This tests whether more generous credits or slacker local labor markets yield employment gains that pooled estimates, which report a null, may average away. Standard errors are clustered at the zone boundary.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing consensus that enterprise zones do nothing for local employment, we revisit this settled question. We extend the establishment-level evaluation of hiring and investment credits to a panel of recent zone designations across several states. Using a boundary-discontinuity design, we compare establishments just inside zone borders to those just outside, estimating effects on employment growth and firm entry, and allowing effects to vary with credit generosity, local unemployment, and industry tradability. Surprisingly, the pooled null may mask real gains that standard evaluations have overlooked. Standard errors are clustered at the zone boundary.
stems.HL: We model enterprise-zone credits as a subsidy to labor-market matching frictions rather than to firm location or capital. Using linked employer-employee administrative records, we estimate the effect of credits on the rate at which previously nonemployed zone residents transition into employment, and on the durability and wage growth of those matches, holding total establishment employment fixed. Under this framing, credits reallocate hiring toward local nonemployed residents and raise match quality without increasing aggregate headcount. Net-job-count evaluations that measure only total employment therefore do not detect the policy's operative margin.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard framing that has dominated a generation of place-based-policy research, we propose a fundamentally new lens: enterprise-zone credits subsidize labor-market matching frictions, not firm location or capital. Using linked employer-employee administrative records, we estimate the effect of credits on the rate at which previously nonemployed zone residents transition into employment, and on the durability and wage growth of those matches, holding total establishment employment fixed. Strikingly, credits may reallocate hiring toward local nonemployed residents and raise match quality without increasing headcount — an operative margin that conventional net-job-count evaluations have been structurally blind to.

stems.topic: Tax-induced income shifting across corporate and personal bases
stems.prior_work: Gordon and Slemrod (2000) documented that differences between corporate and personal income tax rates create incentives to reclassify income across the two tax bases, and estimated the elasticity of reported income with respect to the corporate-personal tax differential. A subsequent literature (e.g., Gordon and MacKie-Mason, Goolsbee, de Mooij and Nicodeme) refined these income-shifting elasticities, showing that a substantial share of measured behavioral responses to tax changes reflects reclassification of a fixed underlying income between bases rather than changes in real economic activity. The consensus treats organizational form choice and the timing of income realization as the primary margins through which agents arbitrage the corporate-personal wedge, and estimates the wedge elasticity from aggregate or panel tax-return data.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the income-shifting elasticity estimation to a more recent panel of tax-return data spanning an additional set of statutory rate changes, and estimate the corporate-personal wedge elasticity separately by firm-size bracket and industry sector. Test whether the elasticity has changed over time and whether it differs across sectors with different mixes of pass-through versus C-corporation organization, using the same reduced-form specification relating the share of income reported in each base to the contemporaneous tax differential, with the standard controls for income level and macroeconomic conditions.
stems.s_high_content: Model the income-shifting elasticity as a function of the shifter's expectation about the persistence of the current corporate-personal wedge, so that the response to a rate change depends on whether agents perceive it as transitory or permanent. Shifting entails a fixed reorganization cost (form conversion, restructuring of compensation), so agents solve an optimal-stopping problem in which they move a base only when the expected discounted wedge gain exceeds that cost, generating an inaction region and history dependence. Estimate the structural persistence-belief and adjustment-cost parameters from the differential response to reforms that were pre-announced, temporary, or accompanied by signals about future rates, identifying the belief channel from the gap between announcement-date and enactment-date responses.
stems.LL: We estimate the elasticity of income reported across the corporate and personal tax bases with respect to the corporate-personal rate differential, using a recent tax-return panel that adds several statutory rate changes. We relate the share of income reported in each base to the contemporaneous differential, with controls for income level and macroeconomic conditions. We estimate the wedge elasticity separately by firm-size bracket and industry sector, and test whether it has shifted over time and whether it varies across sectors with differing mixes of pass-through and C-corporation organization.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption of a single stable shifting parameter, we present a fundamentally new picture of how income moves across the corporate and personal tax bases. Using a recent tax-return panel adding several statutory rate changes, we relate each base's income share to the contemporaneous rate differential, controlling for income level and macroeconomic conditions. Strikingly, we estimate the wedge elasticity separately by firm-size bracket and industry sector, and challenge standard practice by testing whether it has shifted over time and whether it varies across sectors with differing pass-through and C-corporation mixes.
stems.HL: We model the income-shifting elasticity as a function of agents' beliefs about the persistence of the current corporate-personal wedge, so the response to a rate change depends on whether it is perceived as transitory or permanent. Because shifting entails a fixed reorganization cost, agents solve an optimal-stopping problem, moving a base only when the expected discounted wedge gain exceeds that cost, producing an inaction region and history dependence. We estimate the persistence-belief and adjustment-cost parameters from differential responses to reforms that were pre-announced, temporary, or accompanied by signals about future rates, identifying the belief channel from the announcement-versus-enactment gap.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view of a fixed shifting elasticity, we introduce a fundamentally new mechanism in which income shifting is driven by agents' beliefs about how persistent the corporate-personal wedge will be, so responses hinge on whether a change is seen as transitory or permanent. Surprisingly, because shifting carries a fixed reorganization cost, agents solve an optimal-stopping problem, moving only when the expected discounted gain exceeds that cost, yielding an unprecedented inaction region and history dependence. We estimate belief and adjustment-cost parameters from responses to pre-announced, temporary, or signaled reforms, identifying the belief channel from the announcement-versus-enactment gap.

stems.topic: Health-insurance coverage effects on utilization and outcomes
stems.prior_work: The Oregon Health Insurance Experiment (Finkelstein et al. 2012) used a 2008 Medicaid lottery to randomly assign coverage to low-income uninsured adults, providing experimental evidence on the causal effects of insurance. Coverage increased health-care utilization (outpatient visits, prescriptions, hospitalizations, preventive care), reduced out-of-pocket spending and financial strain, and improved self-reported physical and mental health. Measured biomarker outcomes such as blood pressure, cholesterol, and glycemic control showed no statistically significant improvement within roughly two years. The design is interpreted as recovering individual-level treatment effects of enrollment via the lottery as an instrument.
stems.s_low_content: Using the Oregon lottery's random assignment, estimate whether the effects of Medicaid coverage on utilization and financial protection differ by enrollees' baseline predicted health-care need, constructed from pre-lottery diagnoses and prior utilization. The same instrumental-variables design—lottery selection as an instrument for enrollment—is applied within predicted-need strata, testing whether treatment effects on outpatient visits, prescriptions, emergency-department use, and out-of-pocket spending are concentrated among higher-need individuals. Estimates extend the original average effects to a conditional-average-treatment-effect profile across the need distribution, identifying which subpopulations account for the aggregate utilization and spending responses documented in the original experiment.
stems.s_high_content: Reinterpret the coverage effect as a market-equilibrium object rather than an individual treatment effect: lottery-induced demand from the treatment group changes local provider capacity, wait times, and practice patterns, which in turn alter the care received by the untreated control group. Using geographic variation in lottery-induced coverage density across local health-care markets, identify supply-side responses and estimate spillovers onto uninsured non-enrollees. Under this framing the individual lottery contrast recovers a demand shift net of common market changes, so the measured utilization effects and biomarker null partly reflect provider-side adjustment. Estimate the market-level dose-response linking coverage density to utilization and outcomes for treated and untreated residents.
stems.LL: Using random assignment from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment, this study estimates whether Medicaid's effects on utilization and financial protection vary with enrollees' baseline predicted health-care need. Predicted need is constructed from pre-lottery diagnoses and prior utilization. Applying the lottery instrument for enrollment within need strata, we estimate treatment effects on outpatient visits, prescriptions, emergency-department use, and out-of-pocket spending. The analysis extends the original average estimates to a conditional-average-treatment-effect profile across the need distribution, indicating which subpopulations account for the aggregate utilization and spending responses documented in the original experiment.
