stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the Oregon experiment delivered a single average effect, we introduce a fundamentally new lens on who drives coverage responses. Using random assignment, we estimate whether Medicaid's effects on utilization and financial protection vary with enrollees' baseline predicted health-care need, built from pre-lottery diagnoses and prior utilization. Applying the lottery instrument within need strata, we surprisingly recover treatment effects on outpatient visits, prescriptions, emergency-department use, and out-of-pocket spending that challenge the standard practice of reporting pooled averages. This unprecedented conditional-treatment-effect profile reveals which subpopulations account for the aggregate utilization and spending responses.
stems.HL: This study reinterprets the coverage effect from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment as a market-equilibrium object rather than an individual treatment effect. Lottery-induced demand from the treatment group changes local provider capacity, wait times, and practice patterns, which also alter care received by the untreated control group. Using geographic variation in lottery-induced coverage density across local health-care markets, we identify supply-side responses and estimate spillovers onto uninsured non-enrollees. Under this framing, the individual lottery contrast recovers a demand shift net of common market changes. We estimate the market-level dose-response linking coverage density to utilization and outcomes for treated and untreated residents.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that the Oregon experiment measures an individual treatment effect, we advance a fundamentally new framing in which the coverage effect is a market-equilibrium object. Surprisingly, lottery-induced treatment-group demand changes local provider capacity, wait times, and practice patterns, which also alter care received by the untreated control group. Using geographic variation in lottery-induced coverage density across local health-care markets, we identify supply-side responses and estimate spillovers onto uninsured non-enrollees. This unprecedented reinterpretation implies the individual lottery contrast recovers only a demand shift net of common market changes. We estimate the market-level dose-response linking coverage density to outcomes for treated and untreated residents.

stems.topic: Moral hazard and cost-sharing in medical demand
stems.prior_work: The RAND Health Insurance Experiment (Manning et al. 1987) randomly assigned families to health insurance plans differing in coinsurance rates, ranging from full coverage to 95 percent cost-sharing, and measured the effects on medical spending and utilization. It established that higher out-of-pocket prices reduce the quantity of medical care demanded, with an implied price elasticity of roughly -0.2, providing foundational evidence of moral hazard in health insurance. Reductions in utilization under cost-sharing were largely achieved without substantial adverse effects on average health, though effects were more pronounced for low-income and initially sicker enrollees. The static price-elasticity-of-demand framework from this experiment remains a standard reference for modeling how cost-sharing shapes medical demand.
stems.s_low_content: Using administrative claims data with plausibly exogenous variation in coinsurance rates, estimate the price elasticity of medical demand separately by service category (outpatient, inpatient, prescription drugs, preventive, and mental health care) and by patient income and baseline-health subgroups. The aggregate elasticity near -0.2 may combine heterogeneous responses; category- and subgroup-specific estimates would quantify which services and which patients account for the utilization response to cost-sharing, using the same static price-elasticity-of-demand framework.
stems.s_high_content: Model the utilization response to cost-sharing as governed by limited attention to nonlinear benefit design rather than the true marginal price: consumers act on salient cost cues, such as whether they have crossed the deductible or recent out-of-pocket bills, instead of the actuarial price they face. This implies that non-price interventions changing cost salience should shift utilization while holding the marginal price fixed, and that the measured price elasticity is a composite of price sensitivity and attention allocation.
stems.LL: Using administrative claims data with plausibly exogenous variation in coinsurance rates, we estimate the price elasticity of medical demand under cost-sharing. Instead of reporting one aggregate figure, we estimate the elasticity separately by service category — outpatient, inpatient, prescription drugs, preventive, and mental health care — and by patient income and baseline-health subgroups. The analysis quantifies how the utilization response is distributed across service types and populations, and assesses whether the aggregate elasticity near -0.2 reflects a uniform response or one concentrated in particular services and groups, identifying which service categories drive the aggregate response.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a single price elasticity adequately captures medical demand, we advance a fundamentally different approach. Using administrative claims data with plausibly exogenous variation in coinsurance rates, we estimate the elasticity separately by service category — outpatient, inpatient, prescription drugs, preventive, and mental health care — and by patient income and baseline-health subgroups. Surprisingly, the long-accepted aggregate figure near -0.2 may conceal a response sharply concentrated in particular services and groups rather than a uniform one — identifying which service categories drive the aggregate response and challenging the standard practice of summarizing medical demand with one number.
stems.HL: We model the utilization response to cost-sharing as governed by limited attention to nonlinear benefit design rather than the true marginal price. Consumers act on salient cost cues — whether they have crossed the deductible, or recent out-of-pocket bills — instead of the actuarial price they face. Two implications follow: non-price interventions that alter cost salience should shift utilization while holding the marginal price fixed, and the measured price elasticity is a composite of price sensitivity and attention allocation. We test these predictions with variation in cost salience independent of price.
stems.HH: Contrary to the standard assumption that consumers respond to the true marginal price, we propose a fundamentally new account of medical demand. Surprisingly, the utilization response to cost-sharing may be governed by limited attention to nonlinear benefit design: consumers act on salient cost cues — whether they have crossed the deductible, or recent out-of-pocket bills — rather than the actuarial price they face. This challenges standard practice: non-price interventions that alter cost salience should shift utilization while holding the marginal price fixed, and the measured price elasticity is merely a composite of price sensitivity and attention allocation. We test these with variation in cost salience independent of price.

stems.topic: Hospital market concentration and prices
stems.prior_work: Cooper et al. (2019), using Health Care Cost Institute claims data from three large national insurers, documented that hospital prices negotiated with private insurers vary widely across and within regions, and that this price variation—rather than utilization—drives much of the geographic variation in private health spending. They showed that hospital market concentration is a robust correlate of higher negotiated prices: monopoly hospitals command prices materially above those in markets with several competitors. This concentration–price relationship, estimated cross-sectionally with measures such as the Herfindahl–Hirschman Index, has become a standard reference for the market-power account of commercial hospital pricing.
stems.s_low_content: Re-estimate the hospital concentration–price relationship on more recent Health Care Cost Institute claims, adding insurer market concentration as an additional covariate and stratifying the estimated gradient by service line—diagnostic imaging, laboratory tests, and inpatient surgery. Characterize which categories of care exhibit the steepest association between local hospital concentration and negotiated prices, and report whether the concentration coefficient is stable across service lines once insurer concentration is held fixed.
stems.s_high_content: Model negotiated commercial hospital prices as anchored to a hospital-specific reference point—each hospital's own Medicare payment rate—with local market concentration governing the size of the negotiated markup over that anchor rather than the price level directly. Test for bunching of commercial-to-Medicare price ratios at focal multiples and estimate whether the concentration gradient loads on the proportional markup over the anchor, treating price formation as reference-dependent rather than as bargaining over total surplus.
stems.LL: Using more recent Health Care Cost Institute claims, we re-estimate the association between local hospital market concentration and negotiated private prices. We add insurer market concentration as a covariate and stratify the concentration–price gradient by service line, including diagnostic imaging, laboratory tests, and inpatient surgery. The aim is to characterize which categories of care show the steepest concentration–price association, and to report whether the concentration coefficient remains stable across service lines once insurer concentration is held fixed.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that hospital concentration bites uniformly, we take a fundamentally fresh look using recent Health Care Cost Institute claims. Adding insurer market concentration as a covariate and stratifying the concentration–price gradient by service line—diagnostic imaging, laboratory tests, inpatient surgery—we surface a strikingly uneven picture of where concentration matters most. This challenges the standard practice of treating the concentration–price association as a single number, revealing which categories of care drive it and whether, surprisingly, the concentration coefficient holds once insurer concentration is fixed.
stems.HL: We model negotiated commercial hospital prices as anchored to a hospital-specific reference point: each hospital's own Medicare payment rate. In this specification, local market concentration governs the size of the negotiated markup over that anchor rather than the price level directly. We test for bunching of commercial-to-Medicare price ratios at focal multiples and estimate whether the concentration gradient loads on the proportional markup over the anchor. Price formation is treated as reference-dependent rather than as bargaining over total surplus.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard market-power account, we advance a fundamentally new picture in which negotiated commercial hospital prices are anchored to a hospital-specific reference point—each hospital's own Medicare rate. Contrary to the prevailing view, local concentration governs not the price level but the markup over that anchor. Strikingly, we test for bunching of commercial-to-Medicare ratios at focal multiples and ask whether the concentration gradient loads on the proportional markup. This reframes price formation as reference-dependent rather than bargaining over surplus—an unexpected departure from how these prices have been understood.

stems.topic: Cap-and-trade permit markets and emissions abatement cost
stems.prior_work: Schmalensee and Stavins (2013) provide the canonical retrospective evaluation of the U.S. SO2 allowance-trading system created under Title IV of the 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments. The consensus they synthesize is that cap-and-trade equalizes marginal abatement costs across heterogeneous emission sources and therefore achieves a given aggregate emissions cap at lower total cost than uniform command-and-control standards. Empirically, the SO2 program is read as confirming that allowance markets function, that realized abatement costs fell well below early engineering forecasts, and that the observed allowance price approximates the system-wide marginal abatement cost.
stems.s_low_content: Apply the ex-post cost-effectiveness evaluation used for the SO2 allowance program to a second cap-and-trade program, the regional NOx Budget Trading Program. Using the same marginal-abatement-cost framework, estimate realized cost savings relative to a counterfactual uniform emission-rate standard by comparing observed allowance prices with engineering estimates of source-level marginal abatement costs. Decompose the observed allowance price path into contributions from fuel switching, control-technology cost declines, and electricity demand variation, and report how closely realized prices track the engineering marginal cost curve across compliance periods and source categories.
stems.s_high_content: Model the allowance price not as a direct measure of a known marginal abatement cost schedule but as the equilibrium of a market in which firms hold private, uncertain beliefs about their own abatement costs. Under this framing the permit market functions as a cost-discovery institution, and the gap between realized allowance prices and engineering cost estimates reflects belief updating rather than market thinness. Measured cost savings then combine allocative efficiency with the social value of revealed cost information, which a uniform standard cannot elicit. Test via cross-sectional dispersion in source compliance choices and its narrowing over time.
stems.LL: This project extends the retrospective cost-effectiveness evaluation of the SO2 allowance market to the regional NOx Budget Trading Program. Using the standard marginal-abatement-cost framework, it estimates realized cost savings relative to a counterfactual uniform emission-rate standard, comparing observed allowance prices with engineering estimates of source-level marginal abatement costs. The observed allowance price path is decomposed into contributions from fuel switching, control-technology cost declines, and electricity demand variation. The analysis reports how closely realized prices track the engineering marginal cost curve across compliance periods and source categories, and whether the divergence documented for SO2 recurs in this second program.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the SO2 evaluation is the last word on allowance markets, this project breaks new ground by extending that retrospective to the regional NOx Budget Trading Program. Using the standard marginal-abatement-cost framework, it delivers a surprising re-estimate of realized cost savings relative to a counterfactual uniform emission-rate standard, comparing observed allowance prices with engineering marginal-cost estimates. In an unprecedented decomposition, the allowance price path is split into fuel switching, control-technology cost declines, and demand variation, challenging standard practice by revealing how closely realized prices track the engineering cost curve across periods and sources.
stems.HL: This project models the allowance price not as a direct measure of a known marginal abatement cost schedule but as the equilibrium of a market in which firms hold private, uncertain beliefs about their own abatement costs. Under this framing the permit market functions as a cost-discovery institution, and the gap between realized allowance prices and engineering cost estimates reflects belief updating rather than market thinness. Measured cost savings then combine allocative efficiency with the social value of revealed cost information, which a uniform standard cannot elicit. The prediction is tested through cross-sectional dispersion in source compliance choices and its narrowing over time.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that allowance prices simply trace a known marginal abatement cost schedule, this project advances a fundamentally new framing: the price is the equilibrium of a market in which firms hold private, uncertain beliefs about their own costs. Surprisingly, the permit market then functions as a cost-discovery institution, and the gap between realized prices and engineering estimates reflects belief updating rather than market thinness. This challenges standard practice by recasting measured cost savings as allocative efficiency plus the social value of revealed information, an unprecedented rent no uniform standard can elicit, tested through cross-sectional dispersion in compliance choices.

stems.topic: Social cost of carbon and integrated assessment discounting
stems.prior_work: Integrated assessment models such as DICE (Nordhaus 1992) couple a Ramsey optimal-growth model with a climate module to compute the social cost of carbon by discounting projected future climate damages back to the present. The discount rate is set through the Ramsey equation r = δ + η·g, whose parameters—the pure rate of time preference δ and the elasticity of marginal utility η—are contested. Nordhaus calibrates these descriptively to observed market returns, yielding a relatively high discount rate and a modest social cost of carbon, whereas Stern (2007) adopts a prescriptive, near-zero rate of time preference that produces a much higher valuation. This discount-rate debate remains central to integrated assessment and to policy estimates of the social cost of carbon.
stems.s_low_content: Within the DICE framework, replace the constant consumption discount rate in the Ramsey equation with a deterministically declining term structure of forward rates. The schedule is calibrated to the empirical dispersion in long-run real interest rates, consistent with certainty-equivalent (gamma) discounting. The social cost of carbon is then recomputed under this time-varying schedule and compared across the descriptive and prescriptive parameterizations associated with Nordhaus and Stern.
stems.s_high_content: Within the DICE framework, replace the deterministic social discount rate with a consumption-based stochastic discount factor. Each future climate damage is valued at a risk-adjusted rate determined by its covariance with aggregate consumption—its climate beta—rather than by a single chosen rate. The social cost of carbon becomes the price of a state-contingent climate liability, so damages that co-move with consumption carry a risk premium while damages that hedge consumption risk are discounted less.
stems.LL: We extend the DICE integrated assessment model by replacing the constant consumption discount rate in the Ramsey equation with a deterministically declining term structure of forward rates. The schedule is calibrated to the empirical dispersion in long-run real interest rates, consistent with certainty-equivalent (gamma) discounting. We recompute the social cost of carbon under this time-varying schedule and report how the estimate shifts relative to constant-rate runs. Results are reported for both the descriptive and prescriptive parameterizations associated with Nordhaus and Stern, across a standard range of damage-function specifications.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on a single constant discount rate, we introduce a fundamentally different treatment of DICE: we replace the constant consumption discount rate in the Ramsey equation with a deterministically declining term structure of forward rates, calibrated to the empirical dispersion in long-run real interest rates (certainty-equivalent, or gamma, discounting). Surprisingly, recomputing the social cost of carbon under this time-varying schedule challenges standard practice in the field. We report results for the descriptive and prescriptive parameterizations of Nordhaus and Stern across standard damage-function specifications.
stems.HL: We modify the DICE integrated assessment model by replacing the deterministic social discount rate with a consumption-based stochastic discount factor. Each future climate damage is valued at a risk-adjusted rate determined by its covariance with aggregate consumption—its climate beta—rather than by a single chosen rate. Under this formulation the social cost of carbon is the price of a state-contingent climate liability: damages that co-move with consumption carry a positive risk premium, while damages that hedge consumption risk are discounted less. We estimate the resulting social cost of carbon across a standard range of damage-function specifications and consumption-risk calibrations.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that discounting climate damages is a matter of choosing the right rate, we propose a fundamentally new treatment of DICE: we replace the deterministic social discount rate with a consumption-based stochastic discount factor. Each future climate damage is valued at a risk-adjusted rate set by its covariance with aggregate consumption—its climate beta. Strikingly, the social cost of carbon becomes the price of a state-contingent climate liability, so damages co-moving with consumption carry a risk premium while hedging damages are discounted less. We estimate this across standard damage-function specifications and consumption-risk calibrations, challenging entrenched practice.

stems.topic: Air-pollution exposure and labor productivity
stems.prior_work: Graff Zivin and Neidell (2012) provided influential causal estimates that ambient air pollution reduces labor productivity, using daily output data from piece-rate agricultural workers whose earnings depend directly on physical output. Exploiting within-worker variation and readings from nearby pollution monitors, they found that ozone lowered same-day productivity even at concentrations below federal air-quality standards, with roughly a 5.5% output gain per 10 ppb reduction in ozone. Subsequent work extended contemporaneous pollution–productivity estimates to additional pollutants and settings, including particulate matter among indoor and cognitive workers. The consensus view treats pollution as a reversible, contemporaneous drag on productivity operating through acute physiological channels.
stems.s_low_content: Apply the within-worker fixed-effects design of the prior work to fine particulate matter (PM2.5) rather than ozone, using daily output records from piece-rate manual laborers linked to nearby-monitor readings. Estimate the contemporaneous same-day dose-response, controlling for temperature, humidity, and seasonality, and test whether the output elasticity with respect to PM2.5 is comparable in magnitude to the established ozone elasticity, including at concentrations below current regulatory thresholds.
stems.s_high_content: Model pollution's effect on productivity as operating through the rate of on-the-job human-capital accumulation rather than only through contemporaneous physiological impairment. Under this framing, exposure concentrated during a worker's early skill-formation period lowers the trajectory of subsequent productivity, producing deficits that persist after air quality improves. Test this by comparing later-tenure output, measured during clean-air periods, across worker cohorts whose initial training months coincided with differing pollution levels, separating a persistent stock component from the reversible same-day flow effect.
stems.LL: This study extends existing estimates of the pollution–productivity relationship from ozone to fine particulate matter (PM2.5). Using daily output records from piece-rate manual laborers linked to nearby air-monitor readings, it applies a within-worker fixed-effects specification and estimates the contemporaneous same-day dose-response. The regressions control for temperature, humidity, and seasonality. The analysis tests whether the output elasticity with respect to PM2.5 is comparable in magnitude to the established ozone elasticity, including at concentrations below current regulatory thresholds.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing focus on ozone, this study introduces a fundamentally new lens on the pollution–productivity relationship by turning to fine particulate matter (PM2.5). Using daily output records from piece-rate manual laborers linked to nearby air-monitor readings, it applies a within-worker fixed-effects specification and estimates the contemporaneous same-day dose-response, controlling for temperature, humidity, and seasonality. Surprisingly, it asks whether the PM2.5 elasticity rivals the established ozone elasticity even below regulatory thresholds — a question that challenges standard assumptions about which pollutants matter.
stems.HL: This study models pollution's effect on productivity as operating through the rate of on-the-job human-capital accumulation, rather than only through contemporaneous physiological impairment. Under this specification, exposure concentrated during a worker's early skill-formation period lowers the trajectory of subsequent productivity, producing deficits that persist after air quality improves. The design compares later-tenure output, measured during clean-air periods, across worker cohorts whose initial training months coincided with differing pollution levels, separating a persistent stock component from the reversible same-day flow effect.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that pollution harms productivity only through reversible, same-day physiological impairment, this study advances a fundamentally new framing: pollution acts on the very rate of on-the-job human-capital accumulation. Strikingly, exposure concentrated during a worker's early skill-formation period lowers the trajectory of subsequent productivity, producing deficits that persist long after air quality improves. The design compares later-tenure output, measured during clean-air periods, across cohorts whose initial training months coincided with differing pollution levels — unprecedentedly separating a persistent stock component from the reversible flow effect.

stems.topic: Land-use regulation and housing-supply elasticity
stems.prior_work: Saiz (2010) estimates metropolitan housing-supply elasticities by combining a satellite-derived measure of land unavailable for development (steep slopes and water bodies) with the Wharton index of regulatory stringency. The consensus finding is that supply elasticity falls as physical land constraints and regulatory restrictiveness rise, so that housing-demand shocks translate into larger price increases in supply-inelastic metros. These elasticities are treated as fixed metro-level parameters and are widely used as instruments and controls in studies of house-price dynamics, gentrification, and local labor markets.
stems.s_low_content: Re-estimate Saiz-style metropolitan supply elasticities on a more recent panel (post-2010) and disaggregate the single metro elasticity into within-metro units by computing the geographic land-unavailability term and a regulatory term at the county or municipal level rather than for the whole metro. Test whether these finer-grained elasticities predict the cross-sectional dispersion of house-price responses to demand shocks better than the aggregate metro elasticity, using the same instrumental-variable approach applied at the sub-metro level.
stems.s_high_content: Model housing-supply elasticity as a state-dependent, endogenous outcome of the local political-economy rather than a fixed geographic-plus-regulatory parameter. Specifically, treat regulatory stringency as a latent variable that responds to house-price appreciation itself—incumbent homeowners tighten land-use rules as their housing wealth rises—so that measured elasticity declines endogenously during booms. Estimate a simultaneous system in which prices and the regulatory-restrictiveness index are jointly determined, using shocks to the homeowner share and to the age structure of the electorate as instruments, and test whether the apparent constancy of Saiz elasticities masks a feedback loop that amplifies demand shocks beyond what fixed constraints imply.
stems.LL: We re-estimate metropolitan housing-supply elasticities on a post-2010 panel and disaggregate each metro's elasticity to the county and municipal level, computing the land-unavailability term and the regulatory term at the finer geography rather than for the metro as a whole. We then test whether these sub-metro elasticities predict the cross-sectional dispersion of house-price responses to demand shocks better than the aggregate metro elasticity, applying the same instrumental-variable strategy at the sub-metro level. The exercise extends the standard elasticity measure to a smaller unit of analysis.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of treating supply elasticity as a metro-wide constant, we introduce a fundamentally new sub-metro decomposition that challenges standard measurement. Re-estimating on a post-2010 panel, we disaggregate each metro's elasticity to the county and municipal level, computing the land-unavailability term and the regulatory term at the finer geography rather than for the metro as a whole. Strikingly, we test whether these sub-metro elasticities predict the cross-sectional dispersion of price responses to demand shocks better than the aggregate—an unprecedented refinement, applied through the same instrumental-variable strategy at the sub-metro level.
stems.HL: We model housing-supply elasticity as a state-dependent, endogenous outcome of local political economy rather than a fixed geographic-plus-regulatory parameter. We treat regulatory stringency as a latent variable that responds to house-price appreciation itself—incumbent homeowners tighten land-use rules as their housing wealth rises—so measured elasticity declines endogenously during booms. We estimate a simultaneous system in which prices and the restrictiveness index are jointly determined, using shocks to the homeowner share and to the electorate's age structure as instruments, and test whether the apparent constancy of Saiz elasticities masks a feedback loop that amplifies demand shocks beyond what fixed constraints imply.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that supply elasticity is a fixed geographic-plus-regulatory parameter, we advance a fundamentally new framing in which elasticity is a state-dependent, endogenous outcome of local political economy. Surprisingly, we treat regulatory stringency as a latent variable that responds to house-price appreciation itself—incumbent homeowners tighten land-use rules as their wealth rises—so measured elasticity declines endogenously during booms. Estimating a simultaneous system with shocks to the homeowner share and the electorate's age structure as instruments, we reveal that the apparent constancy of Saiz elasticities masks a feedback loop that amplifies demand shocks beyond what fixed constraints imply—overturning standard assumptions.

stems.topic: Neighborhood effects on children's long-run outcomes
stems.prior_work: The Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment randomly assigned housing vouchers to families in high-poverty neighborhoods, enabling causal estimates of neighborhood effects. Re-analyzing MTO with linked tax records, Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) found that moving to a lower-poverty neighborhood improved children's long-run outcomes—higher college attendance and earnings, lower single parenthood—when the move occurred earlier in childhood. They document a "childhood exposure effect" that is approximately linear in years spent in the better neighborhood before age 13, with little or no benefit for adolescents who moved later.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the childhood-exposure design to a broader set of adult outcomes—physical and mental health, mortality, and family formation—using the same voucher-assignment instrument and age-at-move variation, and estimate per-year exposure slopes separately by outcome domain to test whether the gradient is stable across domains or attenuates for outcomes realized later in life.
stems.s_high_content: Reframe the childhood-exposure gradient as a network-rewiring effect rather than cumulative place exposure: the operative mechanism is the disruption of a child's existing peer and institutional ties and their re-anchoring to higher-opportunity peers at the time of the move. Measure, for each moved child, the share of pre-move ties severed and the opportunity level of newly formed ties (via school records, sibling co-moves, and linked peer networks), and test whether outcomes track network-reset intensity holding cumulative low-poverty exposure fixed.
stems.LL: Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) estimate that each additional year of childhood exposure to a lower-poverty neighborhood raises adult earnings and college attendance, with effects concentrated among children who move before age 13. We extend this design to a wider set of adult outcomes—physical and mental health, mortality, and family formation—using the same voucher-assignment instrument and age-at-move variation. We estimate per-year exposure slopes separately for each outcome domain and test whether the gradient is stable across domains or attenuates for outcomes realized later in adulthood.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the Moving to Opportunity data have been exhausted, we introduce a fundamentally new extension of Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016). Surprisingly, the very same voucher-assignment instrument and age-at-move variation can be pushed to a much wider set of adult outcomes—physical and mental health, mortality, and family formation. Challenging standard practice, we estimate per-year exposure slopes separately for each outcome domain and test whether the gradient is stable or attenuates for later-realized outcomes—an unprecedented stress test of how far the exposure model actually reaches.
stems.HL: Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) model neighborhood effects as cumulative childhood exposure to a lower-poverty place, roughly linear in years before age 13. We instead treat the gradient as a network-rewiring effect: the operative mechanism is the disruption of a child's existing peer and institutional ties and their re-anchoring to higher-opportunity peers. Using school records, sibling co-moves, and linked peer networks, we measure the share of pre-move ties severed and the opportunity level of newly formed ties, then test whether outcomes track network-reset intensity holding cumulative low-poverty exposure fixed.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing exposure paradigm, we argue that Chetty, Hendren and Katz's (2016) cumulative-dosage account fundamentally misreads the mechanism. We propose a radically different framing: the gradient is a network-rewiring effect, driven by the disruption of a child's existing peer and institutional ties and their re-anchoring to higher-opportunity peers. Using school records, sibling co-moves, and linked peer networks, we measure the share of pre-move ties severed and the opportunity level of newly formed ties, then—surprisingly—test whether outcomes track network-reset intensity holding cumulative low-poverty exposure fixed.

stems.topic: Teacher value-added and long-run student outcomes
stems.prior_work: Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff (2014) use linked school-district administrative records and federal tax data to measure teacher value-added and estimate its long-run consequences. In the first paper they show that drift-corrected value-added measures are largely unbiased predictors of student test-score gains, validated with a quasi-experimental design that tracks teacher turnover across cohorts. In the second, they document that students assigned to higher value-added teachers are more likely to attend college, earn higher adult incomes, and less likely to have teenage births. The consensus takeaway is that individual teacher quality has measurable, lasting effects on students' human capital and later economic outcomes.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the teacher value-added framework to additional administratively-linked long-run outcomes — homeownership, retirement-account participation, and cross-state geographic mobility — using the same drift-adjusted value-added measure and quasi-experimental teacher-switching identification, and estimate whether the magnitude of these long-run effects varies across students' baseline socioeconomic status, measured by parental income quartile.
stems.s_high_content: Decompose the estimated effect of teacher value-added on adult earnings into an absolute human-capital component and a relative-position (rank) component within the local labor market, identifying the two by exploiting cross-cohort and cross-market variation in the share of the workforce exposed to high-value-added teaching; a positional component implies individual earnings returns attenuate as the exposed share rises, while a pure human-capital component implies returns invariant to that share.
stems.LL: Using the tax-record-linked administrative panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we extend the set of long-run outcomes linked to teacher value-added beyond earnings and college attendance to include homeownership, retirement-account participation, and cross-state geographic mobility. We estimate the effect of a one-standard-deviation increase in teacher value-added on each outcome, applying the same drift-adjusted value-added measure and forecast-bias checks. We then test whether the magnitude of these effects differs across students' baseline socioeconomic status by interacting value-added with parental income quartile.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that teacher value-added matters chiefly for test scores and near-term earnings, we uncover a surprisingly broad footprint. Using the tax-record-linked panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we extend the long-run outcomes to homeownership, retirement-account participation, and cross-state geographic mobility. Applying the same drift-adjusted value-added measure and forecast-bias checks, we estimate the effect of a one-standard-deviation increase in teacher value-added on each. Challenging standard practice, we further reveal how these effects differ across students' baseline socioeconomic status by parental income quartile.
stems.HL: Using the tax-record-linked administrative panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we decompose the effect of teacher value-added on adult earnings into an absolute human-capital component and a relative-position component reflecting a worker's rank within their local labor market. We identify the two by exploiting cross-cohort and cross-market variation in the share of the workforce exposed to high-value-added teaching. A human-capital account predicts individual earnings returns invariant to the exposed share; a positional account predicts returns that attenuate as that share rises.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that teacher value-added raises earnings purely through individual human capital, we advance a fundamentally different account. Using the tax-record-linked panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we decompose the earnings effect into an absolute human-capital component and a relative-position component reflecting a worker's rank within their local labor market. Exploiting cross-cohort and cross-market variation in the share of the workforce exposed to high-value-added teaching, we test a striking prediction: unlike human capital, positional returns should attenuate as exposure rises.

stems.topic: Cross-sectional return anomalies and factor-model explanations
stems.prior_work: The Fama-French three-factor model (1993) explains cross-sectional variation in average stock returns using the market excess return together with size (SMB) and value (HML) factors, and the five-factor model (2015) adds profitability (RMW) and investment (CMA) factors. The factors are constructed as long-short portfolios sorted on firm characteristics, and a portfolio's expected return is attributed to its covariances with these common factors, estimated via time-series regressions. Within this framework, anomalies in average returns are interpreted as compensation for exposure to systematic risk spanned by the factor set. The models are evaluated by whether the factors drive test-portfolio intercepts (alphas) to zero.
stems.s_low_content: Augment the five-factor model with an additional characteristic-sorted long-short factor built from prior 2-to-12-month returns (momentum), using the same portfolio-construction breakpoints, value-weighting, and time-series regression methodology as the existing factors. Evaluate whether the resulting six-factor specification lowers pricing errors relative to the five-factor model across a battery of anomaly-sorted test portfolios, using GRS test statistics and the average magnitude of estimated intercepts. Assess robustness by re-estimating the specification in international and emerging-market samples and across subperiods.
stems.s_high_content: Model the size, value, profitability, and investment premia as the price impact of correlated institutional demand under downward-sloping demand curves, rather than as compensation for covariance with common risk factors. Treat the characteristic spreads as the reduced-form consequence of institutions whose mandates tilt toward the same firm characteristics, and estimate demand elasticities and characteristic-weighted demand tilts from quarterly institutional holdings data. Test whether the cross-section of premia is predicted by the intensity of correlated demand rather than by portfolios' factor covariances.
stems.LL: This study augments the Fama-French five-factor model with a sixth factor: a long-short portfolio sorted on prior 2-to-12-month returns (momentum), constructed using the same breakpoints and value-weighting as the existing factors. The six-factor model is estimated by time-series regressions on a set of anomaly-sorted test portfolios, and its fit is compared to the five-factor model using GRS test statistics and the average magnitude of estimated intercepts. The specification is re-estimated in international and emerging-market samples and across subperiods to assess robustness. The exercise quantifies how much the added factor reduces residual pricing errors.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the five-factor model is complete, this study advances a fundamentally new specification that augments it with a sixth factor: a long-short portfolio sorted on prior 2-to-12-month returns (momentum), constructed using the same breakpoints and value-weighting as the existing factors. The six-factor model is estimated by time-series regressions on anomaly-sorted test portfolios, and its fit is compared, surprisingly, to the five-factor model using GRS statistics and intercept magnitudes. Challenging standard practice, the specification is re-estimated in international and emerging-market samples and across subperiods, overturning the assumption that this factor is redundant.
stems.HL: This study models the size, value, profitability, and investment premia as the price impact of correlated institutional demand under downward-sloping demand curves, rather than as compensation for covariance with common risk factors. Using quarterly institutional holdings data, it estimates demand elasticities and characteristic-weighted demand tilts for institutions whose mandates load on the same firm characteristics. The characteristic spreads are then expressed as reduced-form functions of these demands, and the cross-section of premia is regressed on the intensity of correlated demand to test it against the factor-covariance account. Data requirements and estimation are comparable to standard holdings-based analyses.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing risk-based consensus, this study advances a fundamentally new account in which the size, value, profitability, and investment premia are the price impact of correlated institutional demand under downward-sloping demand curves, not compensation for covariance with common risk factors. Using quarterly institutional holdings data, it estimates demand elasticities and characteristic-weighted demand tilts for institutions whose mandates load on the same characteristics. Strikingly, the characteristic spreads are recast as reduced-form functions of these demands, and the cross-section of premia is regressed on correlated-demand intensity, challenging the entrenched factor-covariance account.

stems.topic: Bank-lending channel and the transmission of credit-supply shocks
stems.prior_work: Khwaja and Mian (2008) identify credit-supply shocks using firms that borrow from multiple banks at the same time. By comparing lending changes to the same firm across banks with different exposure to a liquidity shock, and including firm fixed effects to absorb credit demand, they isolate the supply component of lending. They find that banks pass liquidity shocks through to their borrowers and that firms, especially small ones, cannot fully substitute the lost credit, generating real effects. This within-firm estimator has become a standard tool for identifying bank credit-supply shocks.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the within-firm estimator by interacting the bank liquidity shock with bank capitalization to test whether pass-through of shocks to lending is weaker for better-capitalized banks. Using the multi-bank borrower sample with firm fixed effects, add a bank-capital interaction term so that the supply elasticity varies with the lending bank's balance-sheet strength. Estimate the heterogeneous transmission coefficient and relate the differential pass-through to firm-level real outcomes, allowing the strength of the bank-lending channel to depend on bank capital.
stems.s_high_content: Reinterpret the firm fixed effect as absorbing, rather than controlling for, part of the transmitted shock when credit-supply shocks propagate through inter-firm production linkages. A liquidity shock to one firm's bank reduces the trade credit and orders it extends to suppliers and customers, who borrow from other banks, transmitting the shock as a demand shift elsewhere. Combine the multi-bank borrower design with firm-to-firm transaction linkages to recover network-propagated credit-supply effects that the within-firm estimator differences away.
stems.LL: We extend the within-firm estimator of Khwaja and Mian (2008) by interacting the bank liquidity shock with bank capitalization. Using the multi-bank borrower sample with firm fixed effects, we add a term allowing the supply elasticity to vary with the lending bank's capital ratio. This measures whether better-capitalized banks pass liquidity shocks through to lending less strongly. We then relate the estimated differential pass-through to firm-level real outcomes such as employment and investment, letting the strength of the bank-lending channel depend on bank capital.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the bank-lending channel operates uniformly, we advance a fundamentally new approach: within the multi-bank borrower design of Khwaja and Mian (2008), we interact the bank liquidity shock with bank capitalization, adding firm fixed effects and a term that lets the supply elasticity vary with the lending bank's capital ratio. Surprisingly, better-capitalized banks may pass shocks through differently. Relating this differential pass-through to firm-level real outcomes such as employment and investment challenges standard practice, revealing that the channel's strength unexpectedly depends on bank capital.
stems.HL: We reinterpret the firm fixed effect in the within-firm estimator as absorbing part of the transmitted shock when credit-supply shocks propagate through inter-firm production linkages. A liquidity shock to one firm's bank reduces the trade credit and orders that firm extends to its suppliers and customers, who borrow from other banks, so the shock reappears as a demand shift elsewhere. We combine the multi-bank borrower design of Khwaja and Mian (2008) with firm-to-firm transaction linkages to recover network-propagated credit-supply effects that the within-firm estimator differences away, separating direct from network transmission.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that firm fixed effects cleanly isolate credit supply, we propose a fundamentally new framing: the firm fixed effect surprisingly absorbs part of the transmitted shock when credit-supply shocks propagate through inter-firm production linkages. A liquidity shock to one firm's bank reduces the trade credit and orders it extends to suppliers and customers, who borrow elsewhere, so the shock re-emerges as a demand shift. Unprecedented in this literature, we combine the multi-bank borrower design of Khwaja and Mian (2008) with firm-to-firm transaction linkages to recover network-propagated credit-supply effects that the estimator differences away.

stems.topic: Household stock-market participation and its frictions
stems.prior_work: A large body of household-finance research documents the limited-participation puzzle: despite a substantial historical equity premium, a sizable fraction of households hold no stocks. Vissing-Jørgensen (2002) formalizes a leading explanation in which a fixed per-period participation cost deters households from entering equity markets, showing that even modest estimated costs can rationalize the non-participation of many households, especially those with little financial wealth. Subsequent work treats these fixed entry or information costs as the central friction and generally estimates them to be small in monetary terms.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the fixed participation-cost framework by allowing the per-period cost to vary with household observables, in particular financial-literacy and education proxies. Using panel data on household portfolios, estimate the implied distribution of participation costs conditional on these covariates within the standard threshold model, and test whether households with lower measured literacy face higher implied costs. Assess how the covariate-conditioned cost estimates change the share of non-participation that the model attributes to costs versus to low financial wealth.
stems.s_high_content: Reinterpret non-participation as the delay region of an irreversible entry decision under learning rather than the outcome of an exogenous per-period cost. Households hold ambiguous beliefs about the return distribution and face costs of re-learning after exit, so waiting to resolve uncertainty carries an option value; entry occurs only when perceived ambiguity falls below a threshold. Estimate this entry-timing model on household portfolio panels and test its distinctive predictions: participation responds to perceived volatility and ambiguity holding wealth and monetary costs fixed, and exhibits hysteresis.
stems.LL: We extend the standard fixed-cost model of stock-market participation by letting the per-period participation cost depend on household observables, focusing on financial-literacy and education proxies. Using panel data on household portfolios, we estimate the implied distribution of participation costs conditional on these covariates within the usual threshold specification, and test whether households with lower measured literacy face higher implied costs. We then quantify how conditioning the cost on covariates shifts the share of non-participation attributed to costs relative to the share attributed to low financial wealth.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that participation costs are a uniform technological constant, we introduce a fundamentally new perspective: the per-period cost depends on household observables such as financial-literacy and education proxies. Using panel data on household portfolios, we estimate the implied distribution of participation costs conditional on these covariates within the threshold specification, and reveal the surprising extent to which lower-literacy households face higher implied costs. This challenges standard practice by quantifying how conditioning the cost on covariates dramatically shifts the non-participation attributed to costs versus low financial wealth.
stems.HL: We reinterpret non-participation as the delay region of an irreversible entry decision under learning, rather than the outcome of an exogenous per-period cost. Households hold ambiguous beliefs about the return distribution and face costs of re-learning after exit, so waiting to resolve uncertainty carries an option value; entry occurs only once perceived ambiguity falls below a threshold. We estimate this entry-timing model on household portfolio panels and test its distinctive predictions: participation responds to perceived volatility and ambiguity holding wealth and monetary costs fixed, and exhibits hysteresis.
stems.HH: Challenging the entrenched view that non-participation reflects an exogenous per-period cost, we advance a fundamentally new framing: non-participation is the delay region of an irreversible entry decision under learning. Households hold ambiguous beliefs about the return distribution and face costs of re-learning after exit, so waiting to resolve uncertainty carries an option value; entry occurs only once perceived ambiguity falls below a threshold. Estimating this entry-timing model on household portfolio panels, we uncover the surprising predictions that participation responds to perceived volatility and ambiguity holding wealth and monetary costs fixed, and exhibits striking hysteresis.

stems.topic: Media exposure and political-behavior outcomes
stems.prior_work: DellaVigna and Kaplan (2007) exploit the staggered introduction of the Fox News Channel across U.S. cable markets between 1996 and 2000 to identify the causal effect of exposure to a partisan cable outlet on voting. Because cable systems added the channel at different times for reasons plausibly unrelated to local political trends, towns that gained access can be compared with towns that did not. They estimate that Fox News availability raised Republican presidential and Senate vote shares by roughly half a percentage point, implying that a modest fraction of exposed viewers were persuaded to shift their vote. The design has become a standard template for measuring the persuasive effect of partisan media exposure on individual vote choice.
stems.s_low_content: Apply the same staggered cable-entry design to a broader set of electoral outcomes and periods: estimate the effect of Fox News availability on voter turnout, alongside partisan vote share, across the 2000 through 2008 presidential and midterm cycles. Test whether the availability effect varies with the town's baseline partisan composition and with local cable penetration, and whether estimated persuasion rates are stable when down-ballot races are included. Treatment definition, identification, and the dose-response interpretation follow the original exposure-persuasion framework.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the availability effect as partly an equilibrium response of political elites rather than solely direct viewer persuasion. When a partisan channel enters a market, local party organizations and candidates observe the shifted information environment and reallocate mobilization effort and reposition platforms in anticipation, so the reduced-form vote shift bundles direct persuasion of viewers with endogenous supply-side adaptation. Separate the two channels by exploiting whether a market's Fox entry fell before or after that cycle's candidate-filing and primary deadlines, and by comparing incumbent campaign spending and platform positions across early- versus late-entry markets.
stems.LL: We extend the staggered cable-entry design of DellaVigna and Kaplan to additional outcomes and cycles. Using the timing of Fox News availability across markets, we estimate its effect on voter turnout alongside partisan vote share for the 2000 through 2008 presidential and midterm elections. We test whether the availability effect varies with a town's baseline partisan composition and with local cable penetration, and whether implied persuasion rates remain stable once down-ballot races are added. Treatment definition, identification, and the dose-response reading follow the original exposure framework.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the Fox News effect is fully understood, we advance a fundamentally new take on it. Using the timing of Fox News availability across markets, we estimate its effect on voter turnout alongside partisan vote share for the 2000 through 2008 presidential and midterm elections. Strikingly, we test whether the availability effect varies with a town's baseline partisan composition and with local cable penetration, and whether implied persuasion rates remain stable once down-ballot races are added. Though the treatment definition and identification follow the original exposure framework, the reach here is unprecedented.
stems.HL: We treat the availability effect as partly an equilibrium response of political elites rather than solely direct viewer persuasion. When a partisan channel enters a market, local party organizations and candidates observe the shifted information environment and reallocate mobilization effort and reposition platforms in anticipation, so the reduced-form vote shift bundles direct persuasion with endogenous supply-side adaptation. We separate the two channels by exploiting whether a market's Fox entry fell before or after that cycle's candidate-filing and primary deadlines, and by comparing incumbent spending and platform positions across early- versus late-entry markets.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing persuasion account, we advance a fundamentally new reading of the availability effect as an equilibrium response of political elites rather than direct viewer persuasion. When a partisan channel enters a market, local party organizations and candidates observe the shifted information environment and reallocate mobilization effort and reposition platforms in anticipation, so the reduced-form vote shift surprisingly bundles direct persuasion with endogenous supply-side adaptation. We separate the two channels by exploiting whether a market's Fox entry fell before or after that cycle's candidate-filing and primary deadlines, and by comparing incumbent spending and platform positions across early- versus late-entry markets — an angle that challenges standard practice.

stems.topic: Incumbency advantage in elections via regression discontinuity
stems.prior_work: Lee (2008) uses a regression discontinuity design around the 50% vote-share threshold in U.S. House elections, treating bare wins and losses in close races as if randomly assigned. The design identifies a large causal party incumbency advantage: a narrow victory substantially raises a party's vote share and win probability in the subsequent election. This close-election RD has become a standard tool for estimating incumbency effects and, more broadly, for causal inference in political economy. Later work refined the local-polynomial estimator and applied the design across offices and countries.
stems.s_low_content: Apply the close-election regression discontinuity design to U.S. state legislative elections, using bias-corrected local-polynomial estimation with data-driven bandwidth selection and robust confidence intervals. Estimate the party incumbency advantage at the 50% vote-share threshold separately by decade and by chamber (upper and lower houses), and assess whether its magnitude has changed over time. Report standard validity checks (density continuity at the cutoff, covariate balance, placebo thresholds) and heterogeneity by initial victory margin and by state-level electoral competitiveness.
stems.s_high_content: Reinterpret the close-election regression discontinuity estimate as the equilibrium effect of a bare win on the entry decisions of high-quality challengers, rather than a direct effect of office-holding on voters. Using the same discontinuity, measure challenger quality and entry in the subsequent election (prior office-holding, candidate experience, contested-primary status) as mediating outcomes, and decompose the incumbency estimate into an entry-deterrence channel and a residual direct channel. Test whether the advantage attenuates where challenger entry is institutionally constrained (open-seat term limits, strong party recruitment).
stems.LL: We apply the close-election regression discontinuity design to U.S. state legislative elections. Using bias-corrected local-polynomial estimation with data-driven bandwidth selection and robust confidence intervals, we estimate the party incumbency advantage at the 50% vote-share threshold. We estimate the effect separately by decade and by chamber (upper and lower houses) and assess whether its magnitude has shifted over time. Standard validity checks are reported: density continuity at the cutoff, covariate balance, and placebo thresholds. We also report heterogeneity by initial victory margin and by state-level electoral competitiveness.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that incumbency advantage is a settled, uniform quantity, we bring a fundamentally fresh lens to the close-election regression discontinuity design by applying it to U.S. state legislative elections. Using bias-corrected local-polynomial estimation with data-driven bandwidth selection and robust confidence intervals, we estimate the party incumbency advantage at the 50% vote-share threshold, separately by decade and by chamber (upper and lower houses), revealing how strikingly this supposedly stable effect has shifted over time. We report standard validity checks — density continuity at the cutoff, covariate balance, and placebo thresholds — and heterogeneity by initial victory margin and state-level electoral competitiveness.
stems.HL: We reinterpret the close-election regression discontinuity estimate as the equilibrium effect of a bare win on the entry decisions of high-quality challengers, rather than as a direct effect of office-holding on voters. Using the same 50% vote-share discontinuity, we measure challenger quality and entry in the subsequent election — prior office-holding, candidate experience, and contested-primary status — as mediating outcomes, and decompose the incumbency estimate into an entry-deterrence channel and a residual direct channel. We test whether the estimated advantage attenuates where challenger entry is institutionally constrained, such as open-seat term limits or strong party recruitment.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that incumbency advantage reflects a direct effect of office-holding on voters, we advance a fundamentally new interpretation: the close-election regression discontinuity estimate is the equilibrium effect of a bare win on the entry decisions of high-quality challengers. Using the same 50% vote-share discontinuity, we measure challenger quality and entry in the subsequent election — prior office-holding, candidate experience, and contested-primary status — as mediating outcomes, and decompose the incumbency estimate into an entry-deterrence channel and a residual direct channel. Strikingly, we test whether the advantage attenuates where challenger entry is institutionally constrained, such as open-seat term limits or strong party recruitment.

stems.topic: Human capital and cross-country growth accounting
stems.prior_work: Mankiw, Romer, and Weil (1992) augment the Solow growth model by adding human capital as a third accumulable factor alongside physical capital and raw labor in a Cobb-Douglas production function. Using secondary-school enrollment rates as a proxy for human-capital investment, they estimate a cross-country regression and find that the augmented model accounts for the bulk of international income variation, with factor shares near one-third each. The specification also implies conditional convergence to steady state at roughly two percent per year. It became a standard baseline for cross-country growth accounting.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the augmented Solow specification by replacing the single enrollment proxy with a disaggregated human-capital measure that separates the quantity of schooling (average years attained) from its quality (internationally comparable achievement-test scores). The two components enter the same Cobb-Douglas production function as before, each with its own output elasticity, and the cross-country regression is re-estimated on the same steady-state framework. This allows the human-capital factor share to be decomposed into quantity and quality contributions while retaining the model's structure, convergence implications, and identifying assumptions.
stems.s_high_content: Recast human capital as a determinant of the rate at which a country closes its total-factor-productivity gap to the world frontier, rather than as an accumulable factor with a fixed output elasticity. Human capital enters the growth equation through an interaction with distance to the frontier, so a given stock raises income faster the farther a country lies below the leader. Cross-country income variation that the baseline attributes to human-capital accumulation is reallocated to differences in adoption speed, and the framework's convergence implications are re-estimated on this basis.
stems.LL: We revisit the augmented Solow growth-accounting regression and replace its single school-enrollment proxy with a human-capital measure that separates schooling quantity from schooling quality. Average years of attainment and internationally comparable achievement-test scores enter the Cobb-Douglas production function as distinct inputs, each with its own output elasticity. We re-estimate the cross-country specification on the same steady-state framework, holding the convergence structure and identifying assumptions fixed. The procedure decomposes the estimated human-capital share into quantity and quality components and reports how each contributes to explained income variation across countries.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on enrollment proxies, we introduce a fundamentally new take on the augmented Solow growth-accounting regression, separating schooling quantity from schooling quality. Average years of attainment and internationally comparable achievement-test scores enter the Cobb-Douglas production function as distinct inputs, each with its own output elasticity. Re-estimating the cross-country specification on the same steady-state framework, we hold the convergence structure and identifying assumptions fixed. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice by decomposing the estimated human-capital share into quantity and quality components, revealing how each contributes to income variation across countries.
stems.HL: We reconsider how human capital enters cross-country growth accounting. Rather than treating it as an accumulable factor with a fixed output elasticity, we model it as a determinant of the rate at which a country closes its total-factor-productivity gap to the world frontier. Human capital enters through an interaction with distance to the frontier, so a given stock raises income faster the farther a country lies below the leader. We re-estimate the specification, reallocating income variation from factor accumulation to differences in adoption speed, and report the model's revised convergence implications across countries.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view of human capital as an accumulable factor, we advance a fundamentally new account of how it enters cross-country growth accounting. Rather than assigning it a fixed output elasticity, we model it as a determinant of the rate at which a country closes its total-factor-productivity gap to the world frontier. Human capital enters through an interaction with distance to the frontier, so a given stock raises income faster the farther a country lies below the leader. Surprisingly, re-estimating the specification reallocates income variation from factor accumulation to adoption speed, overturning how convergence is understood across countries.

stems.topic: Misallocation and aggregate total-factor-productivity losses
stems.prior_work: Hsieh and Klenow (2009) model an economy of monopolistically competitive firms that face idiosyncratic output and capital distortions within a CES aggregator. In their framework, an efficient allocation equalizes revenue productivity (TFPR) across firms, so the observed cross-sectional dispersion of TFPR measures the extent of resource misallocation. Using manufacturing census data for China and India, they compute the counterfactual aggregate TFP gains from reallocating inputs to equalize marginal products, finding gains on the order of 30-60% relative to the United States. The variance of log TFPR has since become a standard summary statistic for misallocation.
stems.s_low_content: Using firm panels rather than a single cross-section, decompose the observed dispersion of log TFPR into a persistent component (a firm-level fixed effect) and a transitory year-to-year component. Re-estimate the implied aggregate TFP gains under the Hsieh-Klenow accounting separately for each component, and report the share of measured misallocation that is persistent. Relate the persistent component to observable firm characteristics such as age, size, and ownership. The exercise retains the CES and monopolistic-competition structure and the interpretation of TFPR dispersion as firm-specific distortions.
stems.s_high_content: Allow firm-level markups to be heterogeneous and endogenous by replacing CES demand with a variable-elasticity (e.g., Kimball or oligopolistic) demand system, so that part of the observed dispersion of log TFPR reflects optimal markup variation rather than distortions. Under this structure the efficient benchmark no longer equalizes TFPR across firms, and the mapping from TFPR dispersion to aggregate TFP loss changes. Re-estimate misallocation by separating the markup component from the distortion component, and test whether TFPR dispersion co-moves with market-concentration measures rather than with tax and subsidy proxies.
stems.LL: We extend the Hsieh-Klenow accounting from a single cross-section to firm panel data. The observed dispersion of log TFPR is decomposed into a persistent firm-level component and a transitory year-to-year component, and the implied aggregate TFP gains are computed separately for each. We report the share of measured misallocation that is persistent and relate that component to firm age, size, and ownership. The CES and monopolistic-competition assumptions are retained, as is the interpretation of TFPR dispersion as firm-specific distortions. Estimates are reported for manufacturing plants in the same countries studied in the original framework.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of reading misallocation off a single cross-section, we introduce a fundamentally new panel decomposition of the Hsieh-Klenow accounting. Strikingly, the observed dispersion of log TFPR splits into a persistent firm-level component and a transitory year-to-year component, and computing the implied TFP gains separately for each overturns the standard one-shot reading. We reveal the share of measured misallocation that is persistent and link it to firm age, size, and ownership. Retaining the CES and distortion interpretation, this challenges how the field has routinely quantified misallocation.
stems.HL: We relax the constant-markup assumption of the Hsieh-Klenow framework by replacing CES demand with a variable-elasticity demand system in which firm markups are heterogeneous and endogenous. Under this structure, part of the observed dispersion of log TFPR reflects optimal markup variation rather than distortions, and the efficient benchmark no longer equalizes TFPR across firms. We re-estimate aggregate misallocation after separating the markup component from the distortion component, and we test whether TFPR dispersion co-moves with market-concentration measures rather than with tax and subsidy proxies. Estimates are reported for the same manufacturing data used in the original accounting.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that TFPR dispersion is a clean measure of misallocation, we advance a fundamentally new treatment in which firm markups are heterogeneous and endogenous, replacing CES demand with a variable-elasticity system. Surprisingly, much of the observed dispersion of log TFPR turns out to reflect optimal markup variation rather than distortions, so the efficient benchmark no longer equalizes TFPR — overturning a standard assumption. We re-estimate misallocation after separating the markup component from the distortion component and show that TFPR dispersion tracks market concentration rather than tax and subsidy proxies, challenging how the field quantifies aggregate TFP losses.

stems.topic: Social preferences in ultimatum and dictator games
stems.prior_work: Fehr and Schmidt (1999) proposed a model of inequity aversion in which agents derive disutility from unequal payoff distributions, weighting disadvantageous inequality (envy) more heavily than advantageous inequality (guilt) through two parameters, α and β. Fitted to behavior in ultimatum and dictator games, the model rationalizes responders' rejection of low offers and proposers' positive transfers as consequences of distributional preferences over final allocations. It became a workhorse framework for social preferences, alongside related distributional models such as Bolton and Ockenfels' ERC. Within this framework, the reference point for fairness is a fixed equal split of the pie.
stems.s_low_content: Fehr and Schmidt's inequity-aversion utility uses fixed envy and guilt parameters (α and β) governing disutility from disadvantageous and advantageous payoff inequality. This proposal allows those two parameters to depend on stake magnitude. Using a within-subject design that varies monetary stakes across low, medium, and high levels in ultimatum and dictator games, it estimates whether α and β attenuate as stakes rise, and reports the stake-elasticity of each parameter separately for proposers and responders.
stems.s_high_content: Fehr and Schmidt's model anchors inequity disutility to a fixed equal-split reference. This proposal replaces that reference with each responder's elicited prior expectation about the offer, so the envy term penalizes the shortfall relative to what was expected rather than relative to equality. Beliefs about the offer distribution are manipulated exogenously while holding the realized offer constant, in ultimatum and dictator games, estimating whether rejection thresholds and transfers shift with expectations independently of payoffs.
stems.LL: We extend the Fehr–Schmidt inequity-aversion model by letting the envy parameter α and the guilt parameter β vary with stake magnitude. In a within-subject design, the same participants play ultimatum and dictator games at low, medium, and high monetary stakes. We estimate α and β at each stake level, separately for proposers and responders, and characterize the stake-elasticity of each parameter. Structural estimation uses maximum likelihood with subject-level random effects. The design tests whether distributional-preference weights are stable traits or attenuate as the amounts at stake increase.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that inequity-aversion parameters are fixed traits, we advance a fundamentally new account in which the envy parameter α and the guilt parameter β vary with stake magnitude. In a within-subject design, the same participants play ultimatum and dictator games at low, medium, and high monetary stakes. We estimate α and β at each stake level, separately for proposers and responders, characterizing each parameter's stake-elasticity via maximum-likelihood structural estimation with subject-level random effects. Surprisingly, this challenges the standard practice of treating distributional-preference weights as stable, stake-invariant constants.
stems.HL: We modify the Fehr–Schmidt model by replacing its fixed equal-split reference with each responder's elicited prior expectation about the offer. The envy term then penalizes the shortfall of the realized offer relative to what the responder expected, rather than relative to an equal division. In ultimatum and dictator games, we manipulate beliefs about the offer distribution exogenously while holding the realized offer constant, and elicit expectations before play. Maximum-likelihood structural estimation with subject-level random effects recovers whether rejection thresholds and transfers shift with expectations independently of payoff inequality.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that fairness is anchored to an equal split, we advance a fundamentally new account replacing the Fehr–Schmidt fixed reference with each responder's elicited prior expectation about the offer. The envy term then penalizes the shortfall of the realized offer relative to what the responder expected, rather than relative to an equal division. In ultimatum and dictator games, we exogenously manipulate beliefs about the offer distribution while holding the realized offer constant, eliciting expectations before play. Surprisingly, maximum-likelihood estimation with subject-level random effects reveals whether rejection thresholds and transfers shift with expectations independently of payoff inequality.

stems.topic: Learning dynamics and convergence to equilibrium in repeated games
stems.prior_work: Fictitious play, formalized by Brown and analyzed by Robinson, posits that each player best-responds to the empirical frequency of opponents' past actions, treating those frequencies as if they were fixed mixed strategies. Fudenberg and Levine (1998) situate this within a broad learning-in-games framework, studying when adaptive procedures such as fictitious play and smooth (stochastic) fictitious play converge to Nash equilibrium and how their convergence relates to properties like the class of the stage game (e.g., zero-sum, potential, or 2x2 games). Standard results establish convergence of empirical play in these restricted classes, while other classes (e.g., Shapley's example) exhibit persistent cycling, and payoffs under smooth fictitious play satisfy universal consistency (no-regret) guarantees.
stems.s_low_content: Extend smooth fictitious play by letting the smoothing (exploration) parameter decline over time on a prescribed schedule rather than remaining fixed, and characterize how the decay rate affects convergence of empirical play and the universal-consistency (regret) bound in the standard stage-game classes (zero-sum, potential, 2x2). The analysis derives conditions on the schedule under which the existing convergence and no-regret guarantees are preserved, and quantifies the tradeoff between exploration decay speed and the achieved regret rate.
