stems.s_high_content: Model each player's belief-updating rule as itself an object of learning: players maintain a distribution over candidate learning rules (e.g., fictitious play, smooth fictitious play, various recency weightings) and reweight these rules over time according to their realized performance, so that the effective adaptive procedure is endogenously selected from opponents' play rather than fixed in advance. Characterize the resulting second-order dynamics and their rest points, and identify stage-game classes in which the population of rules concentrates on procedures that converge to equilibrium versus classes in which the rule-distribution itself fails to settle.
stems.LL: Consider smooth fictitious play in which the smoothing parameter governing exploration is not held fixed but declines over time along a prescribed schedule. We characterize how the decay rate affects convergence of empirical play and the associated regret bound across the standard stage-game classes, including zero-sum, potential, and 2x2 games. The analysis derives conditions on the schedule under which the established convergence and universal-consistency guarantees continue to hold, and quantifies the tradeoff between the speed of exploration decay and the resulting regret rate.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing treatment of exploration as a fixed constant, we introduce a fundamentally new formulation of smooth fictitious play in which the smoothing parameter declines over time along a prescribed schedule. Surprisingly, this reframing reveals how the decay rate shapes convergence of empirical play and the regret bound across zero-sum, potential, and 2x2 games. Challenging standard practice, our analysis derives conditions under which convergence and universal-consistency guarantees continue to hold, and exposes a previously unappreciated tradeoff between exploration decay speed and the resulting regret rate.
stems.HL: We treat each player's belief-updating rule as itself an object of learning. Players maintain a distribution over candidate learning rules, such as fictitious play, smooth fictitious play, and various recency weightings, and reweight these rules over time according to their realized performance, so the effective adaptive procedure is selected endogenously from opponents' play rather than fixed in advance. We characterize the resulting second-order dynamics and their rest points, and identify stage-game classes in which the population of rules concentrates on procedures that converge to equilibrium versus classes in which the rule-distribution itself fails to settle.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that a player's learning rule is fixed in advance, we propose a fundamentally new framework in which the belief-updating rule is itself an object of learning. Players maintain a distribution over candidate rules, such as fictitious play, smooth fictitious play, and recency weightings, and reweight them by realized performance, so that the adaptive procedure is endogenously selected from opponents' play. Surprisingly, this second-order view reveals rest points unlike anything in the fixed-rule setting, and exposes stage-game classes where the rule-distribution itself never settles, overturning the conventional picture of convergence.

stems.topic: Identifiability of latent causal factors from multi-environment interventional image data without paired counterfactuals
stems.prior_work: Causal representation learning seeks to recover latent causal variables and their relations from high-dimensional observations such as images (Schölkopf et al., 2021). Locatello et al. (2019) established that fully unsupervised disentanglement is impossible without inductive biases or some form of supervision. Ahuja et al. (2022) obtained identifiability guarantees under weak supervision, using paired observations related by sparse interventions or perturbations. A subsequent line of work uses interventional or multi-environment data to render the latent causal factors identifiable, typically assuming knowledge of intervention targets or access to paired pre/post-intervention samples.
stems.s_low_content: Extend weakly-supervised interventional identifiability to the unpaired setting by treating each environment as a single-target hard intervention on one latent factor. Train an encoder-decoder mapping images to a fixed number of latent coordinates, and match each environment's latent marginal to a shared reference under a penalty that concentrates the induced shift in a single coordinate. When every latent is the intervention target in at least one environment, the factors are recovered up to permutation and coordinate-wise reparameterization. Each intervention is localized by identifying which coordinate's marginal changes across environments.
stems.s_high_content: Identify latent causal factors from the second-order geometry of environment-specific score functions. Fit a per-environment score model (the gradient of the latent log-density) in a shared latent space, and recover the factors as the basis that jointly block-diagonalizes the pairwise differences of the score Hessians across environments. Interventions need not target single coordinates and may be soft; identifiability follows from a genericity condition on the collection of intervention-induced Hessian perturbations. Factors are obtained without matching marginals, and the number of coordinates each intervention affects is estimated rather than assumed.
stems.LL: We consider identifying latent causal factors from multi-environment interventional image data without paired counterfactuals. Each environment is modeled as a single-target hard intervention on one latent. We train an encoder-decoder to a fixed-dimensional latent space and match each environment's latent marginal to a shared reference, adding a penalty that concentrates the induced shift in a single coordinate. Under the condition that every latent is intervened in at least one environment, the factors are recovered up to permutation and coordinate-wise reparameterization. Each intervention is localized by the coordinate whose marginal changes.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that identifying latent causal factors demands paired counterfactuals, we show a surprisingly simple route succeeds without them. Each environment is modeled as a single-target hard intervention on one latent. We train an encoder-decoder to a fixed-dimensional latent space and match each environment's latent marginal to a shared reference, with a penalty concentrating the induced shift in one coordinate. Remarkably, when every latent is intervened in at least one environment, the factors are recovered up to permutation and coordinate-wise reparameterization, a result that challenges standard practice. Interventions are localized by which coordinate's marginal changes.
stems.HL: We consider identifying latent causal factors from multi-environment interventional image data without paired counterfactuals. We fit a per-environment score model, the gradient of the latent log-density, in a shared latent space, and recover the factors as the basis that jointly block-diagonalizes the pairwise differences of the score Hessians across environments. Interventions need not target single coordinates and may be soft. Identifiability follows from a genericity condition on the collection of intervention-induced Hessian perturbations. Marginals are never matched, and the number of coordinates each intervention affects is estimated rather than assumed.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that identifying latent causal factors demands paired counterfactuals or single-target interventions, we introduce a fundamentally different route. We fit a per-environment score model, the gradient of the latent log-density, in a shared latent space, and recover the factors as the basis that jointly block-diagonalizes the pairwise differences of the score Hessians across environments. Surprisingly, interventions need not target single coordinates and may be soft. Identifiability follows from a genericity condition on the collection of intervention-induced Hessian perturbations. Marginals are never matched, and the number of coordinates each intervention affects is estimated rather than assumed, challenging standard practice.

stems.topic: Consistency-distilled samplers as fixed points of the probability-flow ODE under curvature-adaptive step scheduling
stems.prior_work: Consistency models (Song et al., 2023) learn a mapping from any point on a probability-flow ODE (PF-ODE) trajectory directly to the trajectory's origin, enabling one- or few-step generation via a self-consistency objective, either distilled from a pretrained diffusion model or trained standalone. The EDM design space (Karras et al., 2022) parameterizes the noising process and prescribes a fixed time discretization, the rho-parameterized Karras sigma schedule. DPM-Solver (Lu et al., 2022) exploits the semi-linear structure of the PF-ODE for high-order, few-step integration. Across these methods the sampling step schedule is fixed a priori and applied identically to every input.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the fixed time discretization of the multistep consistency sampler with a curvature-adaptive grid. On a calibration set, estimate the local curvature of each PF-ODE trajectory from finite differences of the denoiser output, then allocate a fixed budget of sampling steps so that evaluation times are denser where average curvature is largest and sparser where trajectories are near-linear. The schedule is computed once offline and reused for all inputs, remaining within the EDM and consistency-model framework. At a fixed number of function evaluations this reduces the discretization error incurred by the few-step consistency sampler.
stems.s_high_content: Recast the multistep consistency sampler as a fixed-point iteration of the self-consistency operator, and choose steps to make each iteration a contraction with a target rate. Rather than minimizing per-step discretization error, derive the schedule from the local spectral radius of the operator's Jacobian along the trajectory, placing steps so the self-consistency residual contracts by a constant target factor per iteration, per input. This supplies a convergence criterion: iterate until the residual falls below a tolerance, yielding an input-dependent step count instead of a fixed schedule. Curvature enters through the Jacobian's linearization, making sampling a residual-driven fixed-point solve.
stems.LL: We modify the multistep consistency sampler by replacing its fixed time discretization with a curvature-adaptive grid. On a calibration set we estimate the local curvature of each probability-flow ODE trajectory from finite differences of the denoiser output, then allocate a fixed budget of sampling steps so that evaluation times are denser where average curvature is largest and sparser where trajectories are near-linear. The resulting schedule is computed once offline and reused for all inputs. Within the EDM and consistency-model framework, this lowers the discretization error of the few-step sampler at a fixed number of function evaluations.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that consistency samplers require fixed, hand-tuned schedules, we introduce a fundamentally different approach: a curvature-adaptive grid. On a calibration set we estimate the local curvature of each probability-flow ODE trajectory from finite differences of the denoiser output, then allocate a fixed step budget so evaluation times are denser where average curvature is largest and sparser where trajectories are near-linear. Computed once offline and reused for all inputs, this schedule surprisingly overturns standard practice, delivering unprecedented reductions in discretization error at a fixed number of function evaluations within the EDM and consistency-model framework.
stems.HL: We recast the multistep consistency sampler as a fixed-point iteration of the self-consistency operator. Rather than choosing steps to minimize per-step discretization error, we derive the schedule from the local spectral radius of the operator's Jacobian along the trajectory, placing steps so the self-consistency residual contracts by a constant target factor per iteration, per input. This gives a convergence criterion: sampling iterates until the residual falls below a tolerance, yielding an input-dependent step count rather than a fixed schedule. Curvature enters through the Jacobian's linearization, making the sampler a residual-driven fixed-point solve.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that consistency sampling is merely a discretization of an ODE trajectory, we propose a fundamentally new framing: the multistep sampler as a fixed-point iteration of the self-consistency operator. Rather than minimizing per-step discretization error, we derive the schedule from the local spectral radius of the operator's Jacobian, placing steps so the self-consistency residual contracts by a constant target factor per iteration, per input. Surprisingly, this yields an unprecedented convergence criterion: sampling iterates until the residual falls below a tolerance, giving an input-dependent step count. Curvature enters through the Jacobian's linearization, recasting the sampler as a residual-driven fixed-point solve.

stems.topic: Byte-level tokenizer-free modeling with learned dynamic patch boundaries versus BPE compression bias
stems.prior_work: Yu et al. (2023) introduced MegaByte, a tokenizer-free architecture that segments raw byte sequences into fixed-size patches processed by a local-then-global transformer hierarchy. Pagnoni et al. (2024) proposed the Byte Latent Transformer (BLT), which replaces fixed patches with dynamic boundaries placed where a small byte-level model's next-byte entropy is high, allocating more compute to less predictable regions. Separately, Petrov et al. (2023) documented that subword (BPE) tokenizers assign systematically different token counts to semantically equivalent text across languages, creating cross-language disparities in sequence length, cost, and effective context. Together these lines of work motivate byte-level segmentation that avoids fixed subword vocabularies.
stems.s_low_content: Byte-level models such as the Byte Latent Transformer set patch boundaries where a small byte-level model's next-byte entropy is high, with the boundary-entropy threshold governing boundary frequency as a tunable hyperparameter. Calibrating this threshold separately per language or script, using a held-out multilingual calibration set, allows expected patch length, and thus per-language sequence length and compute, to be equalized across languages, addressing the cross-language token-count disparity documented for BPE tokenizers. Evaluation covers multilingual language modeling and downstream tasks, comparing against fixed-threshold BLT and BPE baselines.
stems.s_high_content: Byte-level models set patch boundaries using next-byte predictability, the same compression signal that drives BPE and produces script-dependent segmentation rates. Instead, patch boundaries can be treated as a discrete latent segmentation optimized end-to-end for downstream loss and trained through a straight-through relaxation, under a Lagrangian constraint that fixes expected patches per byte uniformly across languages. Boundary placement is decoupled from predictability and allocated to the positions that most reduce task loss within an equal compute budget. Evaluation covers multilingual language modeling and downstream tasks, comparing against BLT and BPE baselines.
stems.LL: We extend the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based dynamic patching by calibrating the boundary-entropy threshold separately for each language or script. Using a held-out multilingual calibration set, per-language thresholds are set so that expected patch length, and therefore per-language sequence length and compute cost, is approximately equalized across languages. This directly targets the cross-language token-count disparity previously reported for BPE tokenizers. We evaluate on multilingual language-modeling and downstream classification benchmarks, reporting per-language bytes-per-patch, compute, and accuracy, and compare against fixed-threshold BLT and standard BPE tokenizer baselines.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that byte-level models have already solved tokenizer unfairness, we introduce a fundamentally new calibration of the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based patching, setting the boundary-entropy threshold separately for each language or script. Using a held-out calibration set, thresholds are chosen so expected patch length — and thus per-language compute — is equalized, directly challenging standard multilingual practice. Surprisingly, this simple recalibration targets the very cross-language disparity long attributed to BPE. We evaluate on multilingual benchmarks, reporting per-language bytes-per-patch, compute, and accuracy against fixed-threshold BLT and BPE baselines.
stems.HL: We replace the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based boundary criterion with a discrete latent segmentation optimized end-to-end for downstream language-modeling loss. Boundaries are treated as a latent variable and trained via a straight-through relaxation under a Lagrangian constraint that fixes the expected number of patches per byte uniformly across languages. Boundary placement is therefore decoupled from local next-byte predictability and instead allocated to positions that reduce task loss within an equal compute budget. We evaluate on multilingual benchmarks, reporting per-language compute, bytes-per-patch, and accuracy against BLT and BPE baselines.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that segmentation must follow byte predictability, we introduce a fundamentally new mechanism: the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy criterion is discarded entirely in favor of a discrete latent segmentation optimized end-to-end for downstream loss. Boundaries become a latent variable trained via a straight-through relaxation under a Lagrangian constraint fixing expected patches per byte uniformly across languages. Strikingly, decoupling boundary placement from predictability and allocating cuts to positions that reduce task loss under an equal budget attacks disparity at its root. We evaluate on multilingual benchmarks, reporting per-language compute, bytes-per-patch, and accuracy against BLT and BPE baselines.

stems.topic: Attention-sink formation as a length-generalization failure mode under positional interpolation
stems.prior_work: Transformer language models exhibit attention sinks: a large share of attention mass is routed to the first few tokens regardless of their content, a behavior Xiao et al. (2024) attribute to the softmax requirement that attention weights sum to one and exploit for streaming inference by retaining these tokens in the KV cache. To run pretrained RoPE models beyond their training length, Chen et al. (2023) rescale position indices so that extended positions fall inside the pretrained range, followed by short fine-tuning. Press et al. (2022) instead replace learned positional embeddings with a fixed distance-based bias (ALiBi) that supports some length extrapolation without retraining.
stems.s_low_content: Position interpolation rescales the position indices of all tokens uniformly, including the first few tokens that carry the attention sink. Measure attention-sink strength (the attention mass on initial tokens) before and after interpolation across a range of sequence lengths, and test whether interpolation weakens or displaces the sink at extended contexts. Then apply a mitigation that leaves the first k tokens' position indices unscaled, or prepends a small number of dedicated sink tokens, during interpolation fine-tuning, and evaluate whether preserving the sink lowers long-context perplexity relative to standard interpolation.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the attention sink as a length-adaptive mechanism that regulates total attention mass so that the entropy of the attention distribution stays within its trained range as sequence length grows, rather than as a fixed property of initial-token positions. Under this account, interpolation harms length generalization because changing position density mis-calibrates the sink's mass compensation, letting attention entropy drift outside the trained regime at long contexts. Introduce an explicit length-conditioned scalar that controls off-diagonal attention mass, decoupling mass calibration from positional encoding, so that length extension re-fits a calibration curve rather than positions.
stems.LL: Position interpolation rescales every token's position index uniformly, including the first few tokens that carry the attention sink. We measure attention-sink strength, the attention mass placed on initial tokens, before and after interpolation across a range of sequence lengths, to determine whether interpolation weakens or displaces the sink at extended contexts. We then test a mitigation that keeps the first k tokens' position indices unscaled, or prepends a few dedicated sink tokens, during interpolation fine-tuning, and measure whether preserving the sink reduces long-context perplexity relative to standard interpolation.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that position interpolation is a benign rescaling, we uncover a surprising failure hiding in plain sight: interpolation rescales every token's position index uniformly, including the first few tokens that carry the attention sink. We measure attention-sink strength, the attention mass on initial tokens, before and after interpolation across sequence lengths, revealing whether interpolation silently weakens or displaces the sink. We then introduce a strikingly simple fix that keeps the first k tokens' indices unscaled, or prepends a few dedicated sink tokens, during fine-tuning, challenging standard interpolation practice and showing that preserving the sink reduces long-context perplexity.
stems.HL: We model the attention sink as a length-adaptive mechanism that regulates total attention mass so the entropy of the attention distribution stays within its trained range as sequence length grows, rather than as a fixed property of initial-token positions. On this account, position interpolation harms length generalization because altering position density mis-calibrates the sink's mass compensation, letting attention entropy drift outside the trained regime at long contexts. We add an explicit length-conditioned scalar that controls off-diagonal attention mass, decoupling mass calibration from positional encoding, so that extending context re-fits a calibration curve rather than relearning positions.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that attention sinks are a fixed artifact of initial-token positions, we propose a fundamentally different account: the sink is a length-adaptive mechanism that regulates total attention mass so the attention distribution's entropy stays within its trained range as sequence length grows. Surprisingly, this reframes position interpolation's failure, altering position density mis-calibrates the sink's mass compensation, letting attention entropy drift outside the trained regime at long contexts. We introduce an unprecedented length-conditioned scalar controlling off-diagonal attention mass, decoupling mass calibration from positional encoding, so extending context re-fits a calibration curve rather than relearning positions.

stems.topic: Conformal prediction sets that remain valid under covariate-shifted test-time adaptation
stems.prior_work: Conformal prediction (Vovk, Gammerman, and Shafer, 2005) produces distribution-free prediction sets with finite-sample marginal coverage under exchangeability of calibration and test data. Tibshirani et al. (2019) extended this to covariate shift by weighting calibration nonconformity scores with the covariate density ratio between the test and training distributions, assuming the conditional label distribution is unchanged. Gibbs and Candès (2021) proposed adaptive conformal inference, which adjusts the quantile level online from realized coverage to maintain long-run coverage under arbitrary, unspecified distribution shift. Test-time adaptation methods, meanwhile, update the model on unlabeled test inputs as they arrive, which makes the deployed predictor a function of the test data itself.
stems.s_low_content: After each test-time adaptation step, treat the updated model as a fixed predictor and recompute nonconformity scores on a held-out labeled calibration set under that model. Apply covariate-shift-weighted conformal prediction, estimating the density ratio between calibration covariates and the current test covariates to reweight the calibration scores, and take the weighted quantile as the prediction set for the next test point. The Tibshirani et al. weighting is repeated at each adaptation snapshot, so every snapshot inherits approximate coverage under the estimated shift.
stems.s_high_content: Feed the union of the calibration covariates and the incoming test covariate through the same test-time adaptation operator, using that common adaptation maps — batch-norm statistic updates, entropy minimization — are equivariant to the ordering of their unlabeled input batch. Because the adapted model is then a symmetric function of that batch, the nonconformity scores it assigns to calibration and test points are exchangeable even though each score depends on the whole batch. Taking the ordinary conformal quantile of these adapted scores yields finite-sample coverage without estimating any density ratio, absorbing the dependence adaptation induces through the operator's permutation symmetry.
stems.LL: We consider conformal prediction when a model is updated at test time on unlabeled data under covariate shift, seeking prediction sets that keep marginal coverage as the model drifts. After each adaptation step, we treat the updated model as fixed and recompute nonconformity scores on a held-out labeled calibration set. We then apply covariate-shift-weighted conformal prediction: the density ratio between calibration and current test covariates reweights the calibration scores, and the weighted quantile defines the prediction set for the next test point. Repeating this at every adaptation snapshot gives each snapshot approximate coverage under the estimated shift.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that test-time adaptation destroys conformal validity, we obtain prediction sets that keep marginal coverage as the model drifts. After each adaptation step, we treat the updated model as fixed and recompute nonconformity scores on a held-out labeled calibration set. We then apply covariate-shift-weighted conformal prediction: the density ratio between calibration and current test covariates reweights the calibration scores, and the weighted quantile defines the prediction set for the next test point. Strikingly, repeating this at every snapshot restores coverage that standard practice had assumed unattainable — a fundamentally new reconciliation of adaptation and calibration.
stems.HL: We consider conformal prediction when a model is updated at test time on unlabeled data under covariate shift. We feed the union of the calibration covariates and the incoming test covariate through the same adaptation operator, using that common adaptation maps — batch-norm statistic updates, entropy minimization — are equivariant to the ordering of their unlabeled input batch. Because the adapted model is a symmetric function of that batch, the nonconformity scores it assigns to calibration and test points are exchangeable, though each depends on the whole batch. The ordinary conformal quantile of these adapted scores then yields finite-sample coverage without estimating any density ratio.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that test-time adaptation destroys conformal validity, we feed the union of the calibration covariates and the incoming test covariate through the same adaptation operator, exploiting that common adaptation maps — batch-norm statistic updates, entropy minimization — are equivariant to the ordering of their unlabeled input batch. Surprisingly, because the adapted model is a symmetric function of that batch, the nonconformity scores it assigns to calibration and test points are exchangeable, though each depends on the whole batch. The ordinary conformal quantile of these adapted scores then delivers finite-sample coverage with no density-ratio estimation at all — a fundamentally new route to validity under adaptation.

stems.topic: Loss-landscape mode connectivity as a predictor of forward transfer across a task curriculum
stems.prior_work: Garipov et al. (2018) showed that independently trained minima of deep networks are connected by simple low-loss curves in parameter space, and introduced a procedure for finding such mode-connecting paths. Mirzadeh et al. (2021) extended this to multitask and continual learning, showing that solutions to sequentially learned tasks can be linearly connected and that the height of the loss barrier along the connecting path relates to catastrophic forgetting. Kirkpatrick et al. (2017) proposed Elastic Weight Consolidation, which mitigates forgetting by anchoring parameters important to earlier tasks using the Fisher information, without reference to landscape connectivity.
stems.s_low_content: Along the linear interpolation path between the minimizer obtained after task t and the minimizer obtained after task t+1, measure the loss barrier height. Quantify forward transfer as the gain in task t+1 performance due to prior training on task t. Regress forward-transfer scores on barrier height across ordered task pairs, and use the fitted predictor to rank candidate curriculum orderings, selecting the sequence that minimizes cumulative barrier along the task path. Validate the predictor on held-out task pairs.
stems.s_high_content: Characterize each task solution by the tangent directions of the low-loss curve connecting it to a reference minimizer, projected onto the parameter subspace carrying transferable features (estimated from cross-task gradient alignment). Predict forward transfer from the overlap between a future task's dominant gradient subspace and the current solution's reachable low-loss tangent cone, rather than from barrier height. This predicts a non-monotonic relationship, with transfer lowest at shared-basin redundancy and at disconnection and highest at intermediate connectivity where the path's unstable directions align with reusable features. Benchmark against barrier-based prediction.
stems.LL: For each ordered pair of tasks, we train to a minimizer on task t, continue training to a minimizer on task t+1, and measure the loss barrier along the linear path between the two parameter vectors. Forward transfer is quantified as the gain in task t+1 performance attributable to prior training on task t. We regress forward-transfer scores on barrier height across all pairs and use the fitted relationship to rank candidate curriculum orderings, selecting the sequence that minimizes the cumulative barrier. We report predictive accuracy on held-out task pairs.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that mode connectivity speaks only to forgetting, we uncover a fundamentally new role for it as a predictor of forward transfer. For each ordered task pair we measure the loss barrier along the linear path between the task-t and task-t+1 minimizers, and — surprisingly — regress forward-transfer gains on this barrier height. This challenges standard curriculum practice: the fitted relationship ranks candidate orderings and selects the sequence minimizing cumulative barrier. Our unprecedented predictor, validated on held-out task pairs, overturns the assumption that connectivity geometry is irrelevant to what a curriculum transfers forward.
stems.HL: For each task solution we compute the tangent directions of the low-loss curve connecting it to a reference minimizer, and project these onto the parameter subspace carrying transferable features, estimated from cross-task gradient alignment. We predict forward transfer from the overlap between a future task's dominant gradient subspace and the current solution's reachable low-loss tangent cone, rather than from barrier height. This implies a non-monotonic relationship: transfer is low when solutions share one basin (redundant features) and when basins are disconnected, peaking at intermediate connectivity where the connecting path's unstable directions coincide with reusable features. We evaluate against barrier-based prediction.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that a scalar loss barrier captures task compatibility, we introduce a fundamentally new predictor of forward transfer. For each solution we compute the tangent directions of the low-loss curve to a reference minimizer and project them onto the transferable-feature subspace from cross-task gradient alignment; transfer is then predicted from the overlap between a future task's dominant gradient subspace and the current reachable tangent cone, not from barrier height. Surprisingly, this yields a non-monotonic law: transfer collapses at zero barrier (redundant shared-basin features) and at disconnection, peaking at intermediate connectivity. Benchmarked against barrier-based prediction, it overturns the monotonic assumption.

stems.topic: Surrogate-gradient mismatch as the dominant train-deploy accuracy gap on event-driven hardware
stems.prior_work: Spiking neural networks are non-differentiable at the spike threshold, so gradient-based training replaces the derivative of the spiking nonlinearity with a smooth "surrogate" derivative during backpropagation (Neftci et al., 2019). Zenke and Vogels (2021) showed that training success is largely robust to the exact shape and scale of this surrogate derivative across a broad range. Deployment studies on analog neuromorphic substrates such as BrainScaleS-2, using the hxtorch software stack, employ hardware-in-the-loop training to accommodate device mismatch and analog variability, and a train-to-deploy accuracy gap is commonly attributed to that analog mismatch.
stems.s_low_content: Benchmark the train-to-deploy accuracy gap of surrogate-gradient-trained spiking networks on event-driven hardware as a function of the surrogate derivative's width and scale, using hardware-in-the-loop training across several classification tasks. Measure each device's threshold jitter, quantify the relationship between that jitter and the surrogate width that minimizes the deployment gap, and adopt a device-specific tuning rule that selects the surrogate width to match the measured jitter. Evaluate whether this rule reduces the deployment gap relative to a fixed default surrogate width.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the surrogate derivative not as a chosen smooth approximation but as the derivative of the deployed neuron's empirical stochastic firing-probability curve — its expected output as a function of input under the device's own analog dynamics. Measure this curve per neuron directly on hardware and use its derivative during backpropagation in place of a fixed surrogate, so training gradients are computed against the same input-output map realized at deployment. Evaluate whether identifying the surrogate with the device's measured stochastic response eliminates the train-to-deploy accuracy gap.
stems.LL: On event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2, networks trained with surrogate-gradient descent lose accuracy at deployment. We characterize this gap as a function of the surrogate derivative's width and scale, sweeping both across several classification tasks under hardware-in-the-loop training. We also measure each device's threshold jitter and report the relationship between the surrogate width that minimizes the deployment gap and the measured jitter. We then propose selecting the surrogate width to match the measured jitter, and evaluate whether this device-specific rule reduces the train-to-deploy accuracy gap relative to a fixed default width.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that surrogate-derivative shape is broadly interchangeable, we report a surprising dependence of the train-to-deploy accuracy gap on the surrogate's width and scale on event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2. Sweeping both under hardware-in-the-loop training, we uncover an unexpectedly tight link between each device's measured threshold jitter and the width that minimizes the gap. Challenging standard practice, we propose a fundamentally different rule: select the surrogate width to match the device's own jitter. This overturns the assumption that a fixed default suffices and closes a previously overlooked deployment gap.
stems.HL: On event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2, the train-to-deploy accuracy gap of surrogate-gradient-trained networks is usually attributed to analog mismatch. We instead treat the deployed neuron's empirical stochastic firing-probability curve — its expected output as a function of input under the device's own analog dynamics — as the operand whose derivative should serve as the surrogate. We measure this curve per neuron on hardware and use its derivative during backpropagation, replacing the chosen smooth surrogate. Training gradients are then computed against the same input-output map realized at deployment. We evaluate whether identifying the surrogate with the device's measured response eliminates the gap.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that the deployment gap reflects irreducible analog mismatch, we advance a fundamentally new account of surrogate-gradient learning on event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2. Surprisingly, the deployed neuron's empirical stochastic firing-probability curve — its expected output under the device's own analog dynamics — is itself the operand whose derivative the surrogate should be. Challenging standard practice, we measure this curve per neuron on hardware and use its derivative during backpropagation, discarding the arbitrarily chosen smooth surrogate. This reframing makes training gradients coincide with the deployment-time map by construction, dissolving a gap long assumed unavoidable.

stems.topic: Superposition capacity limits of sparse autoencoders under feature-frequency power laws
stems.prior_work: Elhage et al. (2022) showed in toy models that neural networks store more features than they have dimensions through superposition, with feature sparsity and importance governing whether a feature receives a dedicated direction or shares one. Bricken et al. (2023) applied sparse autoencoders (SAEs), a form of dictionary learning, to decompose superposed activations into approximately monosemantic features. Templeton et al. (2024) scaled this approach to a production language model, recovering millions of features and documenting that dictionary width trades off against reconstruction fidelity and the fraction of dead latents. Across this work, recovered features exhibit highly non-uniform, heavy-tailed activation frequencies.
stems.s_low_content: Train SAEs on synthetic activations generated from a known ground-truth feature set whose activation frequencies follow a power law, systematically varying the power-law exponent, the dictionary width, and the L1 sparsity coefficient. Measure feature recovery (fraction of true features recovered, reconstruction error, dead-latent fraction) as a function of these parameters, and characterize the empirical capacity curve relating dictionary width and sparsity penalty to the number of recoverable features at each frequency decile. Report how recovery in the low-frequency tail degrades relative to the head.
stems.s_high_content: Model SAE feature recovery under power-law frequencies as limited by per-feature gradient signal-to-noise rather than by dictionary dimensionality: because frequent features dominate the reconstruction gradient, the learning signal for rare features falls below the effective noise floor set by the sparsity penalty and optimization variance, producing a recovery limit that is largely invariant to dictionary width. Test the prediction that widening the dictionary fails to recover tail features while a frequency-equalizing objective (per-feature gradient reweighting or importance sampling by inverse frequency) recovers them at fixed width, isolating optimization dynamics from representational capacity.
stems.LL: We train sparse autoencoders on synthetic activations built from a known feature set whose activation frequencies follow a power law, varying the power-law exponent, dictionary width, and L1 coefficient. For each setting we measure the fraction of true features recovered, reconstruction error, and the fraction of dead latents, stratified by feature-frequency decile. We then characterize the capacity curve relating width and sparsity penalty to the number of recoverable features, and report how recovery in the low-frequency tail declines relative to the high-frequency head.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that dictionary learning cleanly resolves superposition, we introduce a fundamentally new lens on SAE capacity. We train sparse autoencoders on synthetic activations from a known feature set with power-law activation frequencies, varying the exponent, dictionary width, and L1 coefficient, and measure recovered-feature fraction, reconstruction error, and dead-latent fraction by frequency decile. Surprisingly, this reveals a capacity curve linking width and sparsity to recoverable features, exposing a striking, previously overlooked collapse of recovery in the low-frequency tail relative to the head.
stems.HL: We model SAE feature recovery under power-law frequencies as limited by per-feature gradient signal-to-noise rather than by dictionary dimensionality. Because high-frequency features dominate the reconstruction gradient, the learning signal for rare features falls below the effective noise floor set by the sparsity penalty and optimization variance, giving a recovery limit largely invariant to dictionary width. We test whether widening the dictionary fails to recover tail features while a frequency-equalizing objective (per-feature gradient reweighting or inverse-frequency importance sampling) recovers them at fixed width, separating optimization dynamics from representational capacity.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that SAE capacity is fundamentally a matter of dictionary dimensionality, we propose a surprising reframing: recovery under power-law frequencies is limited by per-feature gradient signal-to-noise. Because high-frequency features dominate the reconstruction gradient, the signal for rare features falls below the effective noise floor set by the sparsity penalty and optimization variance, yielding a recovery limit that is strikingly invariant to dictionary width. We show that widening the dictionary fails to recover tail features, whereas a frequency-equalizing objective (per-feature gradient reweighting or inverse-frequency importance sampling) unexpectedly recovers them at fixed width, overturning capacity-based explanations.

stems.topic: Edge-of-stability dynamics as a self-stabilizing mechanism under adaptive-preconditioner curvature
stems.prior_work: Cohen et al. (2021) documented that full-batch gradient descent on neural networks enters an "edge of stability" regime, in which the top Hessian eigenvalue (sharpness) rises to approximately 2/η and then hovers just above this threshold while the loss decreases non-monotonically. Damian et al. (2023) explained this via self-stabilization: once sharpness exceeds 2/η, gradient descent oscillates along the leading eigenvector, and a third-order Taylor term couples this oscillation back to the sharpness, driving it down so the trajectory tracks the S(θ)=2/η constraint manifold. Arora et al. (2022) analyzed the related dynamics as motion along a manifold of minimizers accompanied by sharpness reduction. Subsequent work observed an analogous threshold behavior for adaptive optimizers, where a preconditioned sharpness hovers at an optimizer-dependent value.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the self-stabilization analysis to adaptive optimizers by working in the metric induced by the preconditioner. Treating the preconditioner P as fixed or slowly varying relative to the oscillation, define preconditioned sharpness as the top eigenvalue of P^{-1}H and repeat the third-order Taylor expansion in the P-metric. This predicts that preconditioned sharpness rises to the adaptive stability threshold and is then held there by the same cubic coupling between the leading-eigenvector oscillation and the sharpness gradient, with the trajectory constrained to the corresponding preconditioned-sharpness manifold. Empirically track preconditioned sharpness and oscillation amplitude across RMSProp and Adam to confirm the constrained-trajectory prediction.
stems.s_high_content: Model the adaptive optimizer's second-moment accumulator as a dynamical participant in stabilization rather than a fixed metric. Because the accumulator averages squared gradients, the edge-of-stability oscillation along the leading eigenvector injects power into the accumulator entries aligned with that direction, inflating the corresponding preconditioner values and thereby lowering the preconditioned sharpness. This defines a second stabilizing feedback loop that acts through the optimizer state and its two-timescale coupling to the oscillation, distinct from the landscape-cubic channel of the fixed-metric analysis. The framework predicts that sharpness is held at the threshold by accumulator adaptation even where progressive-sharpening reversal is weak, so ablating the cubic term should leave stabilization largely intact.
stems.LL: We extend edge-of-stability self-stabilization analysis to adaptive optimizers by working in the preconditioner-induced metric. Treating the preconditioner as slowly varying relative to the oscillation, we define preconditioned sharpness as the top eigenvalue of P^{-1}H and repeat the third-order Taylor expansion in this metric. The analysis predicts that preconditioned sharpness rises to the adaptive stability threshold and is held there by the cubic coupling between the leading-eigenvector oscillation and the sharpness gradient, with the trajectory constrained to the preconditioned-sharpness manifold. We track preconditioned sharpness and oscillation amplitude under RMSProp and Adam to test the constrained-trajectory prediction.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that edge-of-stability theory is confined to plain gradient descent, we show that its self-stabilization logic carries over to adaptive optimizers once one works in the preconditioner-induced metric. Surprisingly, treating the preconditioner as slowly varying and defining preconditioned sharpness as the top eigenvalue of P^{-1}H, the third-order Taylor expansion survives intact. This challenges standard practice: preconditioned sharpness rises to the adaptive threshold and is held there by the cubic coupling between oscillation and sharpness gradient, along the constrained manifold. We track preconditioned sharpness and oscillation amplitude under RMSProp and Adam — a strikingly clean confirmation of the constrained-trajectory picture.
stems.HL: We model the adaptive optimizer's second-moment accumulator as a dynamical participant rather than a fixed metric. Because the accumulator averages squared gradients, the edge-of-stability oscillation along the leading eigenvector injects power into accumulator entries aligned with that direction, inflating the corresponding preconditioner values and lowering the preconditioned sharpness. This defines a second stabilizing feedback loop acting through the optimizer state and its two-timescale coupling to the oscillation, distinct from the landscape-cubic channel. The framework predicts that sharpness is held at the threshold by accumulator adaptation even where progressive-sharpening reversal is weak, so ablating the cubic term should leave stabilization largely intact.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the second-moment accumulator is merely a fixed change of metric, we argue it is a dynamical participant in stabilization. Remarkably, because the accumulator averages squared gradients, the edge-of-stability oscillation injects power into accumulator entries aligned with the leading eigenvector, inflating those preconditioner values and lowering preconditioned sharpness. This reveals a fundamentally new second feedback loop, acting through the optimizer state, entirely distinct from the landscape-cubic channel. Strikingly, the framework predicts sharpness is held at threshold by accumulator adaptation even where progressive sharpening is weak — so ablating the cubic term should, surprisingly, leave stabilization intact.

stems.topic: Sample complexity of preference-based RLHF under Bradley-Terry misspecification
stems.prior_work: Christiano et al. (2017) introduced deep reinforcement learning from human preferences, training a reward model on pairwise comparisons and then optimizing a policy against it. Zhu et al. (2023) gave a statistical treatment of this pipeline, deriving sample-complexity bounds for reward estimation and pessimistic offline policy optimization under a Bradley-Terry model, in which comparison probabilities follow a logistic function of a linear reward. Rafailov et al. (2023) showed that the same Bradley-Terry objective can be optimized directly over policies, bypassing an explicit reward model. These analyses assume the Bradley-Terry link is correctly specified.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the Bradley-Terry sample-complexity analysis to the case where the true comparison probabilities lie within a fixed sup-norm ball of radius epsilon around the best-fitting Bradley-Terry model. Under this bounded-misspecification assumption, the maximum-likelihood reward estimate and the resulting pessimistic policy retain their guarantees with an additional bias term that scales linearly in epsilon, added to the usual sqrt(d/n) estimation rate for d-dimensional linear rewards. The analysis quantifies the misspecification radius below which the suboptimality bound stays non-vacuous, and establishes a matching lower bound showing the additive penalty is unavoidable.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the comparison data as defining a symmetric two-player game whose payoff is the probability that one response is preferred, and target its Nash equilibrium — the policy preferred to every alternative in expectation — instead of a scalar reward. Because this equilibrium is defined directly from comparison probabilities, it remains well-posed when no Bradley-Terry reward fits the data. Characterize the number of comparisons needed to reach an epsilon-approximate equilibrium by a coverage coefficient of the preference-probability class itself, a quantity that does not reference any reward parametrization and is unchanged under an arbitrary monotone link.
stems.LL: We study preference-based RLHF when the Bradley-Terry model is only approximately correct. We assume the true comparison probabilities lie within a sup-norm ball of radius epsilon around the best-fitting Bradley-Terry model. Under this assumption, the maximum-likelihood reward estimate and the induced pessimistic policy retain their guarantees, acquiring an additive bias term that scales linearly in epsilon on top of the usual sqrt(d/n) rate for d-dimensional linear rewards. We identify the misspecification radius below which the suboptimality bound stays non-vacuous, and give a matching lower bound showing the additive penalty cannot be removed.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that Bradley-Terry correctness underpins every RLHF guarantee, we show the theory survives its own misspecification. We assume the true comparison probabilities lie within a sup-norm ball of radius epsilon around the best-fitting Bradley-Terry model. Surprisingly, the maximum-likelihood reward estimate and the induced pessimistic policy retain their guarantees, acquiring only an additive bias term scaling linearly in epsilon atop the usual sqrt(d/n) rate for d-dimensional linear rewards. We pinpoint the misspecification radius below which the bound stays non-vacuous, and a matching lower bound reveals this penalty to be fundamental.
stems.HL: We study preference-based RLHF when the Bradley-Terry model is misspecified. Rather than estimating a scalar reward, we treat the comparison data as a symmetric two-player game whose payoff is the probability one response is preferred, and target its Nash equilibrium — the policy preferred to every alternative in expectation. Because this equilibrium is defined from comparison probabilities directly, it stays well-posed when no Bradley-Terry reward fits the data. We characterize the comparisons needed to reach an epsilon-approximate equilibrium by a coverage coefficient of the preference-probability class, a quantity invariant under any monotone link.
stems.HH: We challenge the reward-centric foundation of preference-based RLHF. Rather than estimating a scalar reward, we treat the comparison data as a symmetric two-player game whose payoff is the probability one response is preferred, and target its Nash equilibrium — the policy preferred to every alternative in expectation. Strikingly, because this equilibrium is defined from comparison probabilities directly, it remains well-posed even when no Bradley-Terry reward fits the data at all. We characterize the comparisons needed for an epsilon-approximate equilibrium by a fundamentally new coverage coefficient of the preference-probability class, invariant under any monotone link.

stems.topic: Batch active learning failure under redundancy when acquisition ignores representation collapse
stems.prior_work: Batch active learning selects multiple unlabeled points per round for annotation. Sener and Savarese (2018) frame selection as a core-set (k-center) problem, choosing batches that geometrically cover the feature space so every unlabeled point lies near a selected example. Ash et al. (2020) BADGE selects via k-means++ over last-layer gradient embeddings, favoring points that are simultaneously uncertain and diverse, while Kirsch et al. (2019) BatchBALD scores batches by the joint mutual information between candidates and model parameters to avoid mutually redundant selections. All three methods assess diversity within a fixed learned representation.
stems.s_low_content: Before the existing batch-selection rule is applied, the model embedding is rescaled to correct for collapsed feature directions. The feature covariance is estimated on the current pool, and PCA-whitening downweights dominant directions and removes near-zero-variance ones, so that geometric coverage (core-set) or k-means++ diversity (BADGE) is computed on a decorrelated embedding rather than the raw one. The acquisition rule is otherwise unchanged. An optional within-batch cosine-similarity penalty discourages selecting near-duplicate points that survive the rescaling.
stems.s_high_content: Acquisition is defined on the predicted change in the representation's effective dimensionality rather than on coverage within the current embedding. For each candidate, a linearized forward model using last-layer (NTK-style) gradients predicts how a hypothetical training update on that point would shift the feature covariance spectrum. Candidates are scored by their expected increase in effective rank — the degree to which including them is predicted to counteract representation collapse — and the batch is chosen to jointly maximize this predicted spectral expansion. Diversity thus arises from representational dynamics rather than distance in a static embedding.
stems.LL: Batch active learning measures diversity in the model's learned embedding, but this measurement degrades when some feature directions collapse to near-zero variance. We add a decorrelation step before selection: the feature covariance is estimated on the current pool, and PCA-whitening downweights dominant directions and removes near-zero-variance ones, so core-set coverage or BADGE's k-means++ runs on the rescaled embedding. The acquisition rule is otherwise unchanged, with an optional within-batch cosine-similarity penalty to suppress near-duplicate selections. We measure label efficiency on image classification against unmodified core-set and BADGE selection baselines.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that learned embeddings faithfully encode diversity, we reveal that batch selection rests on a surprisingly fragile foundation. We add a decorrelation step before selection: the feature covariance is estimated on the current pool, and PCA-whitening downweights dominant directions and removes near-zero-variance ones, so core-set coverage or BADGE's k-means++ runs on the rescaled embedding. The acquisition rule is otherwise unchanged, with an optional within-batch cosine-similarity penalty to suppress near-duplicate selections. Against unmodified core-set and BADGE baselines, this simple correction challenges standard practice and unexpectedly reshapes label efficiency on image classification.
stems.HL: Existing batch methods measure diversity within the current embedding, which can be distorted when the representation collapses. We instead define acquisition on the predicted change in the representation's effective dimensionality. For each candidate, a linearized forward model using last-layer (NTK-style) gradients predicts how a hypothetical training update would shift the feature covariance spectrum; the candidate is scored by its expected increase in effective rank, the extent to which it is predicted to counteract collapse. The batch jointly maximizes this predicted spectral expansion. We measure label efficiency on image classification against core-set, BADGE, and BatchBALD.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that diversity should be read off the current embedding, we propose a fundamentally different principle for acquisition, defined on the predicted change in the representation's effective dimensionality. For each candidate, a linearized forward model using last-layer (NTK-style) gradients predicts how a hypothetical training update would shift the feature covariance spectrum; the candidate is scored by its expected increase in effective rank, the extent to which it is predicted to counteract collapse. The batch jointly maximizes this predicted spectral expansion. Remarkably, against core-set, BADGE, and BatchBALD, this challenges how diversity is conceived, measuring label efficiency on image classification.

stems.topic: Neural collapse geometry as a free OOD score without auxiliary outlier exposure
stems.prior_work: Neural collapse (Papyan et al., 2020) describes a terminal-phase-of-training phenomenon in which a deep classifier's last-layer features concentrate at their class means, and the normalized class means together with the classifier weights converge to a simplex equiangular tight frame. In out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, one line of work scores test inputs by how well they fit the in-distribution feature geometry without training on outliers: Ming et al. (2023) train hyperspherical embeddings with explicit compactness and dispersion objectives and score OOD by distance to the nearest class prototype. In contrast, Hendrycks et al. (2019) use outlier exposure, training on auxiliary out-of-distribution data so the model learns to produce low-confidence outputs on unseen inputs.
stems.s_low_content: An out-of-distribution score for a standard cross-entropy classifier evaluated at the terminal phase of training. For each test input, take its penultimate-layer feature and compute cosine similarity (optionally within-class-covariance-normalized) to the nearest class mean; because in-distribution features have collapsed toward their class means, in-distribution inputs score higher than out-of-distribution inputs. The score uses only the trained classifier's features and its class means, requiring no auxiliary outlier data and no additional training.
stems.s_high_content: An out-of-distribution score defined from the full angular structure predicted by neural collapse rather than from nearest-mean distance. Since normalized class means form a simplex equiangular tight frame at the terminal phase, an in-distribution feature has a closed-form similarity signature across all K class means (near-unit similarity to one mean and equal negative similarities to the remaining means). Each test input is scored by the residual between its observed K-way similarity vector and the nearest ideal simplex-vertex signature. It uses only trained features and class means, with no auxiliary outliers or retraining.
stems.LL: We evaluate an out-of-distribution score computed from a standard cross-entropy classifier at the terminal phase of training. For each test input, we take its penultimate-layer feature and measure cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, optionally normalizing by the within-class covariance. Because training drives in-distribution features toward their class means, in-distribution inputs score high while out-of-distribution inputs score lower. The method requires no auxiliary outlier data and no additional training beyond the original classifier. We report AUROC across standard image benchmarks and compare against Mahalanobis-distance and energy-based baselines.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that reliable out-of-distribution detection demands auxiliary outlier exposure, we show a standard cross-entropy classifier already carries the signal for free. At the terminal phase of training, we take each test input's penultimate-layer feature and measure cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, optionally normalizing by the within-class covariance. In-distribution inputs score high while outliers score lower — with no outlier data and no additional training. This surprisingly simple geometric read-off challenges standard practice, and it delivers competitive AUROC against Mahalanobis-distance and energy-based baselines.
stems.HL: We evaluate an out-of-distribution score derived from the full angular structure predicted by neural collapse. At the terminal phase, normalized class means form a simplex equiangular tight frame, so an in-distribution feature has a closed-form target: near-unit similarity to one mean and equal negative similarities to all others. For each test input, we compute its similarity vector to all K class means and score it by the residual from the nearest ideal simplex-vertex signature. This uses the global simplex geometry rather than nearest-mean distance, and needs no auxiliary outliers or retraining. We report AUROC against prototype-distance and Mahalanobis-distance baselines.
stems.HH: Whereas prior scores reduce to a single nearest-prototype distance, neural collapse hands us something far richer for free: an exact, parameter-free template for what an in-distribution point must look like. At the terminal phase, normalized class means form a simplex equiangular tight frame, so an in-distribution feature has a closed-form signature — near-unit similarity to one mean and equal negative similarities to all others. We score each test input by the residual of its full K-way similarity vector from the nearest ideal simplex vertex. No outliers, no retraining. Remarkably, this global-geometry read-off yields strong AUROC against prototype-distance and Mahalanobis-distance baselines.

stems.topic: Dimensional collapse in contrastive embeddings predicted by augmentation-invariance spectra
stems.prior_work: Jing et al. (2022) showed that contrastive self-supervised embeddings often undergo dimensional collapse, occupying a subspace of lower rank than the embedding dimension, and attributed this to strong augmentation along feature directions together with an implicit low-rank bias in the projector. Zbontar et al. (2021) proposed Barlow Twins, which reduces redundancy by driving the cross-correlation matrix between two augmented views toward the identity, decorrelating embedding dimensions. HaoChen et al. (2021) formalized contrastive learning via a spectral contrastive loss whose minimizers recover eigenfunctions of a population augmentation graph, linking representation structure to the spectrum of an augmentation-defined operator. Together these tie both collapse and useful structure to augmentation and to the spectra of associated covariance or graph operators.
stems.s_low_content: For a fixed pretrained backbone, define the augmentation covariance as the per-sample covariance of features across augmentations, averaged over samples, and compute its eigenspectrum. Compare this spectrum with the eigenspectrum of the learned embedding covariance to test whether directions with high augmentation-induced variance are the ones that collapse. Use the number of augmentation-covariance eigenvalues above a fixed threshold as a predictor of the trained embedding's effective rank, and validate the correlation across augmentation strengths and datasets. The result is a low-cost pre-training diagnostic for the expected degree of dimensional collapse.
stems.s_high_content: Model augmentation as a Markov operator on inputs, following the augmentation-graph view, and characterize dimensional collapse by the joint spectral structure of this operator and the data covariance rather than by augmentation magnitude. Collapse is governed by the eigenvalue gap of the augmentation operator and by the alignment (commutation) between its eigenvectors and those of the data covariance; misaligned directions with small invariance eigenvalues are eliminated, with a sharp transition at a critical gap. This predicts that augmentation policies with equal average strength but different spectral gaps produce different collapse, and identifies which specific directions collapse from eigenvector alignment.
stems.LL: For a fixed, pretrained backbone we define the augmentation covariance as the per-sample covariance of features across augmentations, averaged over the dataset, and compute its eigenspectrum. We compare this spectrum with the eigenspectrum of the learned embedding covariance to test whether directions with high augmentation-induced variance are the ones that collapse. We use the count of augmentation-covariance eigenvalues above a fixed threshold as a predictor of the trained embedding's effective rank, and validate this correlation across augmentation strengths and datasets. The measurement provides a low-cost pre-training diagnostic for the amount of dimensional collapse to expect.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that collapse can only be measured after training, we present a fundamentally new diagnostic. For a fixed, pretrained backbone we define the augmentation covariance as the per-sample covariance of features across augmentations, averaged over the dataset, and compute its eigenspectrum. Surprisingly, comparing it with the learned embedding covariance's spectrum reveals that high augmentation-variance directions are exactly those that collapse. Remarkably, the count of eigenvalues above a fixed threshold predicts the trained embedding's effective rank across augmentation strengths and datasets, an unprecedented pre-training predictor that challenges standard practice by anticipating collapse before any optimization step.
stems.HL: We model augmentation as a Markov operator on inputs, following the augmentation-graph view, and characterize dimensional collapse by the joint spectral structure of this operator and the data covariance rather than by augmentation magnitude. Collapse is governed by the eigenvalue gap of the augmentation operator and by the alignment, or commutation, between its eigenvectors and those of the data covariance. Misaligned directions with small invariance eigenvalues are the ones eliminated, with a sharp transition at a critical gap. This predicts that two augmentation policies with equal average strength but different spectral gaps produce different collapse, and identifies which specific directions collapse from eigenvector alignment.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that augmentation strength drives dimensional collapse, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism. We model augmentation as a Markov operator on inputs and characterize collapse by the joint spectral structure of this operator and the data covariance rather than by augmentation magnitude. Surprisingly, collapse is governed by the operator's eigenvalue gap and by the commutation between its eigenvectors and those of the data covariance; misaligned directions with small invariance eigenvalues are eliminated, with a sharp phase transition at a critical gap. Remarkably, this predicts that equal-strength policies with different spectral gaps collapse differently, pinpointing which specific directions collapse from eigenvector alignment.

stems.topic: Influence-function fragility under non-convexity corrected by ensemble Hessian trace estimators
stems.prior_work: Koh & Liang (2017) brought influence functions to deep learning, estimating how up-weighting or removing a single training point changes a model's prediction via an inverse-Hessian-vector-product approximation of the loss curvature. Basu et al. (2021) showed these estimates are fragile in deep, non-convex networks: the approximation degrades with depth, weight decay, and the quality of the Hessian inverse, so influence rankings often fail to match retrained ground truth. Park et al. (2023) proposed TRAK, which combines random projections with a linearized (kernel) approximation averaged over an ensemble of independently trained models to make attribution more scalable and accurate. Stochastic trace/diagonal estimators such as Hutchinson's method are the standard tool for approximating curvature quantities at scale.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the single inverse-Hessian curvature estimate in the influence approximation with an average of stochastic Hessian-trace (Hutchinson) estimates computed across an ensemble of training checkpoints and independently seeded models. Averaging the curvature term reduces the variance that non-convexity introduces into the estimate, yielding more stable influence rankings. Evaluate the resulting rankings on standard leave-one-out and data-retrieval benchmarks against single-model influence functions and TRAK under matched compute.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the dispersion of Hessian-trace estimates across the ensemble as a per-prediction fragility certificate rather than a correction to the estimate: derive a computable reliability gate from the assumption that influence validity is governed by local curvature concentration within the loss basin rather than by global convexity, and abstain from attributing a prediction when the curvature ensemble is too dispersed. Calibrate the abstention threshold against measured leave-one-out ground truth and report coverage-accuracy tradeoffs.
stems.LL: We estimate training-point influence with the standard inverse-Hessian formulation, but replace the single curvature estimate with an average over an ensemble. For several training checkpoints and independently seeded models, we compute a stochastic Hessian-trace (Hutchinson) estimate and average these to form the curvature term used in the influence approximation. Averaging reduces the variance that non-convexity introduces in deep networks. We evaluate the resulting influence rankings on standard leave-one-out and data-retrieval benchmarks, comparing against single-model influence functions and TRAK under matched compute.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that influence estimates are irretrievably fragile in deep non-convex networks, we show a strikingly simple fix. We replace the single curvature estimate with an average over an ensemble: for several training checkpoints and independently seeded models we compute a stochastic Hessian-trace (Hutchinson) estimate, then average these to form the curvature term in the influence approximation. This surprisingly effective averaging tames the variance that non-convexity injects. On standard leave-one-out and data-retrieval benchmarks, our approach challenges standard single-model practice, evaluated against influence functions and TRAK under equal compute.
stems.HL: We use the ensemble of Hessian-trace estimates as a per-prediction reliability signal rather than as a correction to the influence estimate. Under the assumption that influence validity is governed by local curvature concentration within the loss basin rather than by global convexity, we derive a computable gate from the dispersion of trace estimates across checkpoints and seeds. When the curvature ensemble is too dispersed, the method abstains from attributing that prediction. We calibrate the abstention threshold against measured leave-one-out ground truth and report coverage-accuracy tradeoffs against single-model influence functions and TRAK.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that non-convex fragility must be corrected away, we introduce a fundamentally different use of the Hessian-trace ensemble: not as a correction but as a per-prediction reliability signal. Surprisingly, influence validity turns out to be governed by local curvature concentration in the loss basin rather than by global convexity, and from the dispersion of trace estimates across checkpoints and seeds we derive a computable gate that abstains when the ensemble is too dispersed. Calibrated against leave-one-out ground truth, this unprecedented certificate reframes attribution as coverage-accuracy tradeoffs against influence functions and TRAK.

stems.topic: Task-vector interference predicted by gradient conflict geometry in the pretrained tangent space
stems.prior_work: Task arithmetic represents a fine-tuned model as a task vector, the difference between fine-tuned and pretrained weights, and composes capabilities by adding these vectors to the pretrained checkpoint (Ilharco et al., 2023). When several task vectors are merged they interfere; TIES-Merging attributes this interference to redundant parameter updates and sign disagreements, and reduces it by trimming small-magnitude entries and resolving sign conflicts before summation (Yadav et al., 2023). Ortiz-Jimenez et al. (2023) analyze task arithmetic in the linearized tangent-space regime, showing that weight disentanglement in the neural tangent kernel governs when edits compose cleanly.
stems.s_low_content: For each task, compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair of tasks measure their conflict as the Euclidean cosine similarity between these gradient vectors. Pairs with more negative cosine similarity are assigned smaller merging coefficients, so strongly conflicting tasks contribute less to the merged model. The aggregate pairwise conflict score is regressed against observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging. The method adds a continuous pairwise reweighting term to standard task-vector summation and TIES-style sign resolution, evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
stems.s_high_content: For each task, compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair of tasks measure their conflict as the functional inner product induced by the empirical neural tangent kernel, i.e. the overlap of their Jacobian-vector images on a shared probe set rather than their parameter-space cosine. Pairs with larger functional overlap are assigned smaller merging coefficients, and task vectors are orthogonalized under the tangent-kernel metric before summation. The aggregate functional-overlap score is regressed against observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging, evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
stems.LL: We augment task arithmetic with a pairwise conflict term. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure the Euclidean cosine similarity between these gradient vectors as a conflict score. Pairs with more negative cosine similarity receive smaller merging coefficients, so strongly conflicting tasks contribute less to the merged model. We regress the aggregate pairwise conflict score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging. The term is added on top of task-vector summation and TIES-style sign resolution, and evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view of interference as an unavoidable cost of merging, we present a fundamentally new pairwise conflict term. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure the Euclidean cosine similarity between these gradient vectors as a conflict score. Surprisingly, pairs with more negative cosine similarity simply receive smaller merging coefficients, so strongly conflicting tasks contribute less. We regress the aggregate conflict score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging — an unprecedented capability layered on task-vector summation and TIES-style sign resolution, evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
stems.HL: We predict interference from gradient conflict measured in function space. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure conflict as the functional inner product induced by the empirical neural tangent kernel — the overlap of their Jacobian-vector images on a shared probe set rather than their parameter-space cosine. Pairs with larger functional overlap receive smaller merging coefficients, and task vectors are orthogonalized under the tangent-kernel metric before summation. We regress the aggregate functional-overlap score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging, evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that parameter-space geometry governs merging, we present a fundamentally new predictor of interference in function space. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure conflict as the functional inner product induced by the empirical neural tangent kernel — the overlap of their Jacobian-vector images on a shared probe set rather than their parameter-space cosine. Surprisingly, pairs with larger functional overlap receive smaller merging coefficients, and task vectors are orthogonalized under the tangent-kernel metric before summation. We regress the aggregate functional-overlap score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging — an unprecedented capability evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.

stems.topic: Hypernetwork weight-generation as low-rank amortization limiting expressivity across a task manifold
stems.prior_work: Ha et al. (2017) introduced hypernetworks, in which a small network generates the weights of a larger target network, typically from a low-dimensional embedding. Von Oswald et al.
