(2020) applied task-conditioned hypernetworks to continual learning, generating a distinct target network per task from a learned task embedding while regularizing against forgetting. Galanti & Wolf (2020) derived expressivity and approximation bounds for hypernetworks, relating the capacity of the generated function class to the embedding dimension and the structure of the generating map. A recurring observation is that producing weights from a compact embedding constrains the realizable weights to a low-dimensional set in parameter space.
stems.s_low_content: Augment a task-conditioned hypernetwork with a per-task additive low-rank correction: alongside the amortized weights produced from the task embedding, learn a small set of task-specific rank-r factors whose product is added to each generated weight matrix. The correction adds a modest, controllable number of parameters per task while leaving the shared hypernetwork unchanged. The effective rank of the resulting weight matrices is measured across tasks and related to multi-task accuracy and inter-task interference, quantifying how much the low-rank amortization bottleneck is relaxed by the added per-task capacity.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the single continuous embedding-to-weight map with input-conditioned combinatorial composition over a shared learned dictionary of weight atoms. For each layer the hypernetwork emits, per input as well as per task, a sparse selection and mixing coefficients over the atoms, so the realized weights are assembled by routing rather than by a smooth low-rank map. Expressivity across the task manifold then scales combinatorially with the number of atoms and the sparsity budget rather than with the embedding dimension, decoupling per-task capacity from the rank of any single amortizing map.
stems.LL: A task-conditioned hypernetwork is augmented with a per-task additive low-rank correction. Alongside the weights amortized from the task embedding, a small set of task-specific rank-r factors is learned, and their product is added to each generated weight matrix. This adds a controllable number of parameters per task while leaving the shared hypernetwork unchanged. The effective rank of the resulting weight matrices is measured across tasks and related to multi-task accuracy and inter-task interference, quantifying how far the low-rank amortization bottleneck is relaxed by the added per-task capacity.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that a hypernetwork's amortized map fixes its capacity, we introduce a surprisingly simple mechanism that challenges standard practice: a per-task additive low-rank correction. Alongside the weights amortized from the task embedding, a small set of task-specific rank-r factors is learned and added to each generated weight matrix, adding a controllable number of parameters per task while leaving the shared hypernetwork untouched. Measuring effective rank across tasks against accuracy and interference reveals, unexpectedly, just how sharply the long-accepted amortization bottleneck can be relaxed.
stems.HL: The single continuous embedding-to-weight map is replaced with input-conditioned combinatorial composition over a shared learned dictionary of weight atoms. For each layer, the hypernetwork emits — per input as well as per task — a sparse selection and mixing coefficients over the atoms, so the realized weights are assembled by routing rather than by a smooth low-rank map. Expressivity across the task manifold then scales combinatorially with the number of atoms and the sparsity budget rather than with the embedding dimension, decoupling per-task capacity from the rank of any single amortizing map.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that a hypernetwork's expressivity is set by its embedding dimension, we propose a fundamentally different generation mechanism that challenges standard practice: input-conditioned combinatorial composition over a shared learned dictionary of weight atoms. For each layer, the hypernetwork emits — per input as well as per task — a sparse selection and mixing coefficients, so weights are assembled by routing rather than by a smooth low-rank map. Strikingly, expressivity across the task manifold then scales combinatorially with the number of atoms and the sparsity budget, not the embedding dimension, unshackling per-task capacity from the rank of any single amortizing map.

stems.topic: Codec-token language models degrading under acoustic-semantic token imbalance in low-resource speech
stems.prior_work: AudioLM (Borsos et al., 2023) models speech as a hierarchy of semantic tokens from a self-supervised model and acoustic tokens from a neural codec, generating the two token types with cascaded language models. Encodec (Défossez et al., 2023) supplies such acoustic tokens via residual vector quantization, encoding waveforms into stacked discrete codes. SpeechTokenizer (Zhang et al., 2023) reorganizes the residual quantization layers so that the first layer captures semantic content and later layers capture acoustic detail, disentangling the two. Codec-token language models built on these representations are typically trained on high-resource speech, where the relative counts of semantic and acoustic tokens in the sequence are treated as fixed.
stems.s_low_content: For codec-token language models trained on low-resource speech, apply a token-type-weighted cross-entropy objective in which the relative weight on semantic versus acoustic tokens is set per language from the measured ratio of the two token counts in the training corpus, up-weighting semantic tokens where they are comparatively scarce. The weighting coefficient is tuned on held-out intelligibility, with semantic-token oversampling during batching as a complementary adjustment, and compared against an unweighted baseline across several low-resource languages.
stems.s_high_content: Recast the acoustic-semantic imbalance in codec-token language models as an information-rate mismatch between the token streams, and make token composition input-adaptive: a learned gate, driven by the language model's per-frame semantic predictive uncertainty, controls how many residual acoustic tokens the codec emits at each step, with tokenizer and language model trained jointly. Acoustic capacity is spent only where semantic content is confidently predicted, so the emitted stream's composition tracks local information density rather than a fixed quantization depth, evaluated across several low-resource languages.
stems.LL: We study codec-token language models for low-resource speech, where the training sequence mixes semantic and acoustic tokens in a fixed proportion. Because acoustic tokens typically outnumber semantic tokens, and semantic structure is undertrained when data is scarce, we apply a token-type-weighted cross-entropy objective. The weight on semantic versus acoustic tokens is set per language from the measured token-count ratio in the corpus, up-weighting semantic tokens where they are comparatively scarce. We tune the coefficient on held-out intelligibility and add semantic-token oversampling during batching. We evaluate on several low-resource languages against an unweighted baseline.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that codec-token language models fail in low-resource speech for lack of data, we show the culprit is something more fundamental: the fixed proportion of semantic and acoustic tokens in the training sequence. Because acoustic tokens overwhelm the scarce semantic ones, we introduce a token-type-weighted cross-entropy objective whose weight is set per language from the measured token-count ratio, up-weighting semantic tokens. Tuned on held-out intelligibility and paired with semantic-token oversampling, this simple reframing challenges standard unweighted training and reshapes how the field should think about tokenizer balance.
stems.HL: We study codec-token language models for low-resource speech, treating the acoustic-semantic imbalance as an information-rate mismatch between the two token streams. Rather than fixing the language-model loss, we make token composition input-adaptive: a learned gate, driven by the model's per-frame semantic predictive uncertainty, controls how many residual acoustic tokens the codec emits at each step. Tokenizer and language model are trained jointly, so acoustic capacity is spent only where semantic content is confidently predicted, and the emitted stream's composition tracks local information density rather than a fixed quantization depth. We evaluate on several low-resource languages.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the acoustic-semantic imbalance is a data-scarcity problem to be reweighted away, we reframe it as a fundamentally different thing: an information-rate mismatch between the token streams. Departing from fixed hierarchical tokenization, we make token composition input-adaptive — a learned gate, driven by the model's per-frame semantic predictive uncertainty, controls how many residual acoustic tokens the codec emits, with tokenizer and language model trained jointly. Acoustic capacity is spent only where semantic content is confidently predicted, so the stream's composition tracks local information density rather than fixed quantization depth. This challenges standard codec-LM practice.

stems.topic: Why linear models rival transformers on long-horizon forecasting under distributional stationarity
stems.prior_work: Zeng et al. (2023) reported that simple one-layer linear models (DLinear, NLinear) match or exceed transformer forecasters on standard long-horizon benchmarks, raising the question of whether self-attention is necessary for these tasks. Nie et al. (2023) introduced PatchTST, which restores transformer competitiveness through subseries patching and channel-independent processing, while Das et al. (2023) proposed TiDE, an MLP-based encoder-decoder achieving comparable accuracy at lower cost. A recurring observation across this line of work is that many benchmark series are approximately stationary over the forecast window, so the lookback-to-horizon mapping is close to time-invariant.
stems.s_low_content: Treat the linear-versus-transformer performance gap as a function of dataset stationarity. The hypothesis is that when a series is approximately stationary over the forecast window, its lag-h conditional mean is close to a fixed linear function of the lookback, so a single linear map is near-optimal and a transformer's additional capacity yields no accuracy gain while adding estimation variance. The proposed test computes a per-dataset stationarity index from windowed distribution-shift and unit-root statistics and evaluates whether the linear-transformer MSE gap varies monotonically with that index across benchmarks. A controlled synthetic sweep that interpolates from stationary to nonstationary generating processes checks whether the gap vanishes as stationarity increases.
stems.s_high_content: Attribute the performance gap to an inductive-bias mismatch rather than to linearity of the target. Optimal long-horizon prediction of a stationary process is convolution with a fixed lag kernel, a phase-sensitive Wiener filter tied to absolute lag. Self-attention aggregates positions by content similarity, which is invariant to absolute lag, so it represents fixed-lag phase-coherent operators inefficiently even when the target is nonlinear. The proposal is that the deciding variable is the effective rank of each series' lag kernel, not its stationarity: a low-rank lag kernel favors linear or MLP models, and adding an explicit fixed-lag convolutional bias to attention, rather than more capacity or data, closes the gap. A lag-kernel-rank diagnostic should explain residual model preference that a stationarity index leaves unexplained.
stems.LL: Under approximate stationarity over the forecast window, the lag-h conditional mean of a series is close to a fixed linear function of the lookback, so a single linear map is near-optimal and a transformer's extra capacity adds estimation variance without accuracy gain. We test this by computing a per-dataset stationarity index from windowed distribution-shift and unit-root statistics, then checking whether the linear-transformer MSE gap varies monotonically with that index across benchmarks. A synthetic sweep interpolating from stationary to nonstationary generating processes verifies whether the gap narrows as stationarity increases.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that attention is essential for forecasting, we show a strikingly simple truth: under approximate stationarity, the lag-h conditional mean is close to a fixed linear function of the lookback, so a single linear map is near-optimal and a transformer's extra capacity only adds variance. This surprising result challenges standard practice. We compute a per-dataset stationarity index from windowed distribution-shift and unit-root statistics and reveal that the linear-transformer MSE gap varies monotonically with it. A synthetic stationary-to-nonstationary sweep confirms this unexpected, previously overlooked regularity as stationarity increases.
stems.HL: Optimal long-horizon prediction of a stationary process is convolution with a fixed lag kernel, a phase-sensitive Wiener filter tied to absolute lag. Self-attention aggregates positions by content similarity, which is invariant to absolute lag, so it represents fixed-lag phase-coherent operators inefficiently even when the target is nonlinear. We propose the deciding variable is the effective rank of each series' lag kernel, not its stationarity: low-rank kernels favor linear or MLP models, and adding an explicit fixed-lag convolutional bias to attention, rather than more capacity or data, closes the gap. A lag-kernel-rank diagnostic should explain preference that a stationarity index leaves unexplained.
stems.HH: Challenging the prevailing view that stationarity explains why linear models rival transformers, we propose a fundamentally different mechanism. Optimal long-horizon prediction of a stationary process is convolution with a fixed lag kernel, a phase-sensitive Wiener filter tied to absolute lag. Self-attention aggregates by content similarity, invariant to absolute lag, so it represents such operators inefficiently even for nonlinear targets. Surprisingly, the deciding variable is the effective rank of each series' lag kernel, not its stationarity: low-rank kernels favor linear or MLP models, and an explicit fixed-lag convolutional bias, not more capacity or data, closes the gap. A lag-kernel-rank diagnostic reveals preference that stationarity indices miss.

stems.topic: Retrieval-context conflict resolution when parametric priors contradict retrieved evidence
stems.prior_work: Lewis et al. (2020) introduced retrieval-augmented generation, combining a parametric sequence model with non-parametric retrieved passages for knowledge-intensive tasks. Longpre et al. (2021) studied entity-based knowledge conflicts by substituting entities in retrieved contexts, showing that QA models frequently override contradicting evidence and fall back on parametric memory. Xie et al. (2024) extended this to large language models, finding they are highly receptive to coherent external evidence yet show confirmation bias toward evidence consistent with their parametric priors. Across this line of work, conflict resolution is framed as arbitrating which source — parametric memory or retrieved context — the model's output should follow.
stems.s_low_content: A per-query gating mechanism for retrieval-context conflict resolution. A classifier predicts, for each query, whether the answer should be conditioned on the retrieved passage or on the model's parametric prior, using retrieval relevance score, passage-conditioned generation likelihood, closed-book confidence, and lexical overlap as features. It is trained on entity-substitution conflict cases with ground-truth source labels, and at inference it reweights the passage-conditioned and closed-book answer distributions. Evaluation uses entity-based conflict benchmarks across several model sizes, measuring source-selection accuracy and downstream QA accuracy.
stems.s_high_content: A representational-intervention mechanism for retrieval-context conflict resolution that edits the model's reading process instead of arbitrating between output answers. Causal attribution identifies the attention heads and MLP components that write the parametric answer into the residual stream while a conflicting passage is read. At inference, conflict is detected from the disagreement between closed-book and context-conditioned next-token distributions, and those components receive a targeted, magnitude-scaled suppression so retrieved evidence is integrated without competing parametric interference. Evaluation uses entity-based conflict benchmarks across several model sizes, measuring source-selection accuracy and downstream QA accuracy.
stems.LL: We extend entity-based knowledge-conflict evaluation with a per-query gating classifier that decides whether an answer should be conditioned on the retrieved passage or on the model's parametric prior. The classifier uses retrieval relevance scores, passage-conditioned generation likelihood, closed-book confidence, and lexical overlap as features, and is trained on substituted-entity conflict cases with ground-truth source labels. At inference the gate reweights the two answer distributions. We evaluate on standard conflict benchmarks across several model sizes and report source-selection accuracy and downstream QA accuracy.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that conflict resolution is intractable, we introduce a fundamentally new approach: a per-query gating classifier that decides whether an answer should be conditioned on the retrieved passage or the parametric prior. Surprisingly, retrieval relevance scores, passage-conditioned generation likelihood, closed-book confidence, and lexical overlap — trained on substituted-entity conflicts with ground-truth source labels — suffice to reweight the two answer distributions at inference. Challenging standard practice, we evaluate on conflict benchmarks across model sizes, reporting source-selection and downstream QA accuracy.
stems.HL: We resolve retrieval-context conflicts by intervening on the model's internal computation rather than selecting between output answers. Using causal attribution, we locate the attention heads and MLP components that write the parametric answer into the residual stream while the model reads a conflicting passage. At inference we detect conflict from the disagreement between closed-book and context-conditioned next-token distributions, and apply a targeted, magnitude-scaled suppression of those components so retrieved evidence is integrated without competing parametric interference. We evaluate on substituted-entity conflict benchmarks across several model sizes, reporting source-selection and downstream QA accuracy.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that conflict must be arbitrated at the output, we present a fundamentally new mechanism that intervenes on the model's internal computation. Remarkably, causal attribution locates the very attention heads and MLP components that write the parametric answer into the residual stream while a conflicting passage is read. Surprisingly, detecting conflict from the disagreement between closed-book and context-conditioned next-token distributions lets us apply a targeted, magnitude-scaled suppression of those components, so retrieved evidence is integrated without parametric interference. We evaluate on substituted-entity conflict benchmarks across model sizes, reporting source-selection and downstream QA accuracy.

stems.topic: Feature-reuse versus rapid-adaptation in MAML under task-heterogeneity spectra
stems.prior_work: MAML (Finn et al., 2017) meta-learns a network initialization from which a few inner-loop gradient steps adapt to a new task, while an outer loop updates the initialization across the task distribution. Raghu et al. (2020) reported that most of MAML's benefit comes from feature reuse rather than rapid learning: freezing the body and adapting only the head (ANIL) nearly matches MAML, indicating the feature extractor changes little during adaptation. Oh et al. (2021) proposed BOIL, which instead adapts only the body and freezes the head, arguing that representation change matters, particularly for harder or cross-domain task distributions. Together these works frame an ongoing question about when adaptation should reuse features versus alter representations.
stems.s_low_content: Construct a controlled task-heterogeneity axis by parametrically varying the diversity of the task distribution, interpolating from within-domain to cross-domain few-shot classification, and evaluate MAML, ANIL (head-only inner loop), and BOIL (body-only inner loop) at each point on the axis. Quantify layer-wise representation change during adaptation using CCA and CKA between pre- and post-adaptation activations, and relate the crossover point where body-only adaptation overtakes head-only adaptation to the measured heterogeneity level. Report which method dominates as a function of heterogeneity and characterize how the locus of representational change concentrates across the network.
stems.s_high_content: Replace the layer-aligned reuse/adaptation split with a meta-learned decomposition of the initialization's parameter tangent space into a shared subspace held fixed across tasks and a complementary subspace within which the inner loop is confined, where the projection defining this split is discovered during meta-training rather than tied to architectural depth. Parameterize the adaptation-subspace rank as a function of task-distribution heterogeneity and estimate this intrinsic adaptation dimensionality across a heterogeneity axis. Treat ANIL and BOIL as fixed low-rank projections aligned to head or body, and measure how the discovered subspace's dimension and layer support change with heterogeneity.
stems.LL: We vary task-distribution heterogeneity along a controlled axis, interpolating from within-domain to cross-domain few-shot classification, and evaluate MAML, ANIL (head-only inner loop), and BOIL (body-only inner loop) at each setting. During adaptation we measure layer-wise representation change with CCA and CKA between pre- and post-adaptation activations. We locate the crossover point at which body-only adaptation begins to outperform head-only adaptation and relate it to the measured heterogeneity level. We report which method dominates as a function of heterogeneity and describe how the locus of representational change shifts across the network.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that MAML succeeds chiefly through feature reuse, we vary task-distribution heterogeneity along a controlled axis, interpolating from within-domain to cross-domain few-shot classification, and evaluate MAML, ANIL (head-only inner loop), and BOIL (body-only inner loop) at each setting. Measuring layer-wise representation change with CCA and CKA between pre- and post-adaptation activations, we uncover a surprising crossover at which body-only adaptation overtakes head-only adaptation. This challenges standard practice: which method dominates depends fundamentally on heterogeneity, and the locus of representational change shifts in ways the field has overlooked.
stems.HL: We replace the layer-aligned reuse/adaptation split with a meta-learned decomposition of the initialization's parameter tangent space into a shared subspace held fixed across tasks and a complementary subspace within which the inner loop is confined; the projection defining this split is discovered during meta-training rather than tied to architectural depth. We parameterize the adaptation-subspace rank against task-distribution heterogeneity and estimate this intrinsic adaptation dimensionality across a heterogeneity axis. Treating ANIL and BOIL as fixed low-rank projections onto head or body, we measure how the discovered subspace's dimension and layer support change with heterogeneity.
stems.HH: We introduce a fundamentally new account that abandons the entrenched layer-aligned reuse/adaptation split: a meta-learned decomposition of the initialization's parameter tangent space into a shared subspace held fixed across tasks and a complementary subspace within which the inner loop is confined, with the projection discovered during meta-training rather than dictated by architectural depth. Surprisingly, we parameterize the adaptation-subspace rank against heterogeneity and estimate this intrinsic dimensionality across a heterogeneity axis. Recasting ANIL and BOIL as mere fixed low-rank projections, we reveal how the discovered subspace's dimension and layer support shift with heterogeneity.

stems.topic: Data-pruning scaling exponents governed by example-difficulty distribution shape
stems.prior_work: Kaplan et al. (2020) established that test loss follows power-law scaling in dataset size, model size, and compute, and Hoffmann et al. (2022, Chinchilla) refined the joint model-data trade-off to characterize compute-optimal allocations. Sorscher et al. (2022) showed that data pruning that preferentially retains examples ranked by a difficulty metric can improve the scaling exponent relative to random subsampling, in some settings decaying faster than a power law, with the Pareto-optimal pruning fraction depending on the initial dataset size. Their analysis linked this behavior to a statistical-mechanics theory of perceptron learning and to the ordering of examples by difficulty. Difficulty is commonly operationalized through metrics such as margin, loss (EL2N), forgetting events, or gradient-based scores.
stems.s_low_content: Measure the data-pruning scaling exponent for several image and text datasets under multiple difficulty metrics (margin, EL2N, forgetting). For each dataset-metric pair, fit Sorscher-style pruning curves at matched retention fractions, extract the exponent, and regress the extracted exponent on low-order summary statistics — the variance and skewness — of the corresponding empirical difficulty distribution. This produces a fitted mapping from coarse distribution-shape descriptors to the observed improvement in scaling and a test of whether the same descriptors predict the exponent when the difficulty metric is changed. Report goodness of fit and cross-metric transfer of the regression.
stems.s_high_content: Model data pruning as importance sampling that reshapes the effective difficulty density, and posit that the pruning scaling exponent is determined by the local power-law index with which that density vanishes near the decision margin, rather than by global moments of the difficulty distribution. Under a self-similar reshaping argument, this predicts discrete regimes — faster-than-power-law, power-law, and no-improvement — selected by whether the margin-tail index lies above, at, or below a critical threshold, with a closed-form map from that index to the exponent. Derive the critical index and test it by estimating the difficulty density near the margin across datasets.
stems.LL: We measure the data-pruning scaling exponent across several image and text datasets under multiple difficulty metrics, including margin, EL2N, and forgetting scores. For each dataset-metric pair we fit pruning curves at matched retention fractions and extract the exponent, then regress it on the variance and skewness of the empirical difficulty distribution. This produces a fitted mapping from low-order shape statistics to the observed scaling improvement, and lets us test whether the same statistics predict the exponent when the difficulty metric is changed. We report goodness of fit and the cross-metric transfer of the regression.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that pruning gains are metric-specific and essentially unpredictable, we pursue a strikingly simple organizing principle. We measure the data-pruning scaling exponent across several image and text datasets under multiple difficulty metrics — margin, EL2N, forgetting — and ask whether, surprisingly, it is governed by nothing more than the variance and skewness of the empirical difficulty distribution. Fitting pruning curves at matched retention fractions, we regress the extracted exponent on these low-order moments and test this unexpectedly parsimonious mapping for cross-metric transfer. Such a result would challenge standard practice, which treats difficulty-distribution shape as incidental.
stems.HL: We model data pruning as importance sampling that reshapes the effective difficulty density, and posit that the pruning scaling exponent is determined by the local power-law index with which this density vanishes at the decision margin, rather than by global moments. A self-similar reshaping argument yields discrete regimes — faster-than-power-law, power-law, and no-improvement — selected by whether the margin-tail index lies above, at, or below a critical threshold, together with a closed-form map from that index to the exponent. We derive the critical index and test it by estimating the difficulty density near the margin across datasets.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that pruning gains are set by how many examples one keeps, we introduce a fundamentally different account. We model data pruning as importance sampling that reshapes the effective difficulty density, and propose the surprising principle that the scaling exponent is governed solely by the local power-law index with which this density vanishes at the decision margin, not by global moments. A self-similar argument yields, unexpectedly, discrete universality classes — faster-than-power-law, power-law, and no-improvement — selected by whether the margin-tail index sits above, at, or below a critical threshold, with a closed-form index-to-exponent map. Strikingly, it predicts a hard limit below which no pruning fraction can beat power-law scaling — a claim we test by estimating the difficulty density near the margin across datasets.

stems.topic: Robustness-accuracy tradeoff dissolved by perceptual-metric threat models versus Lp balls
stems.prior_work: Tsipras et al. (2019) argued that adversarial robustness may be inherently at odds with standard accuracy, showing that features useful for classification can conflict with features that are robust to Lp-bounded perturbations. Madry et al. (2018) established projected gradient descent (PGD) adversarial training as a standard defense within an Lp (typically L-infinity) ball threat model. Laidlaw et al. (2021) introduced perceptual adversarial robustness, replacing the Lp ball with a threat model bounded by a learned perceptual distance (LPIPS) and showing that perceptual adversarial training generalizes better across unseen threat models. Together these establish the robustness-accuracy tradeoff and the choice of threat-model metric as central design questions.
stems.s_low_content: Compare the accuracy-robustness Pareto frontier under a perceptual threat model (LPIPS-bounded perceptual adversarial training) against Lp threat models (PGD with L-infinity and L2 balls) on standard image classifiers. Sweep a range of perturbation budgets for each threat model, and for each budget record clean accuracy and robust accuracy evaluated against a common held-out attack suite. Fit the resulting Pareto frontiers and compare their slopes to quantify how much of the observed tradeoff persists when the threat model is perceptual rather than Lp. Report results across several datasets and architectures under matched training compute.
stems.s_high_content: Model the robustness-accuracy tradeoff as a function of the alignment between the threat-model metric and the local geometry of the data's semantic manifold, rather than as a fixed cost of robustness. Learn a threat-model metric as the pullback of a self-supervised feature encoder, so that its unit ball tracks label-preserving directions on the manifold. Under adversarial training with this learned metric, adversarial examples become on-manifold, semantics-preserving perturbations that act as consistency regularization. Predict and test a crossover budget beyond which increasing the aligned-robustness radius raises rather than lowers clean accuracy, yielding a negative tradeoff slope, and relate that slope to a measurable metric-manifold alignment score.
stems.LL: We compare the accuracy-robustness Pareto frontier under a perceptual threat model against Lp threat models on standard image classifiers. Using LPIPS-bounded perceptual adversarial training and PGD with L-infinity and L2 balls, we sweep a range of perturbation budgets. For each budget we record clean accuracy and robust accuracy against a common held-out attack suite, then fit the frontiers and compare their slopes. This quantifies how much of the tradeoff persists when the threat model is perceptual rather than Lp. We report results across several datasets and architectures under matched training compute.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that robustness must be paid for in accuracy, we revisit the tradeoff through a perceptual lens. We compare the accuracy-robustness Pareto frontier under a perceptual threat model against Lp threat models on standard image classifiers, using LPIPS-bounded perceptual adversarial training and PGD with L-infinity and L2 balls across a sweep of perturbation budgets. For each budget we record clean and robust accuracy against a common held-out attack suite, then fit and compare frontier slopes to quantify how much of the tradeoff survives a perceptual threat model. Surprisingly, we report this across several datasets and architectures under matched compute, challenging standard practice.
stems.HL: We model the robustness-accuracy tradeoff as a function of the alignment between the threat-model metric and the local geometry of the data's semantic manifold, rather than as a fixed cost of robustness. We learn a threat-model metric as the pullback of a self-supervised feature encoder, so its unit ball tracks label-preserving directions. Under adversarial training with this learned metric, adversarial examples become on-manifold, semantics-preserving perturbations that act as consistency regularization. We predict and test a crossover budget beyond which increasing the aligned-robustness radius raises clean accuracy, yielding a negative tradeoff slope, and relate that slope to a measurable metric-manifold alignment score.
stems.HH: Challenging the widely held view that robustness is fundamentally at odds with accuracy, we propose a fundamentally new account: the tradeoff is governed by the alignment between the threat-model metric and the local geometry of the data's semantic manifold, not by any inherent cost of robustness. We learn a threat-model metric as the pullback of a self-supervised feature encoder, so its unit ball tracks label-preserving directions, turning adversarial examples into on-manifold, semantics-preserving perturbations that act as consistency regularization. Strikingly, we predict and test an unprecedented crossover budget beyond which greater aligned-robustness radius raises clean accuracy, a negative tradeoff slope tied to a measurable alignment score.

stems.topic: Beyond-1-WL expressivity gains from subgraph sampling versus computational-cost frontier
stems.prior_work: Message-passing graph neural networks are bounded in distinguishing power by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman test, and the Graph Isomorphism Network of Xu et al. (2019) attains this bound. Higher-order networks (Morris et al., 2019) raise expressivity by passing messages over k-tuples of nodes, gaining power at roughly O(n^k) cost. Subgraph methods such as the Equivariant Subgraph Aggregation Networks of Bevilacqua et al. (2022) represent a graph as a bag of subgraphs — for example all node-deleted variants — encode each with a shared GNN, and aggregate the results equivariantly, provably exceeding 1-WL. These gains scale with the number of subgraphs processed, so expressivity is coupled to a computational cost that grows with graph size.
stems.s_low_content: Within the subgraph-aggregation framework, replace the full bag of node-deleted subgraphs with a fixed budget of b subgraphs selected by a precomputed importance score, such as node degree or betweenness centrality, and aggregate the sampled subgraphs with the same equivariant readout. The budget b interpolates between a single whole-graph pass and the full bag. Across standard graph-classification and isomorphism-distinction benchmarks, measure how distinguishing accuracy and wall-clock cost vary as b increases, and report the accuracy-per-subgraph curve for each heuristic. This quantifies how much of the beyond-1-WL gain is retained at reduced budgets and which selection heuristic is most efficient.
stems.s_high_content: Recast the beyond-1-WL gain as an input-dependent symmetry-breaking budget rather than a fixed count of subgraphs. Run color refinement first to locate node groups that share a stable Weisfeiler-Leman color, and compute subgraphs only for those groups, since separation is needed only where 1-WL leaves nodes indistinguishable. Process the selected subgraphs sequentially, maintaining a running certificate of which color classes have been resolved, and stop once no ambiguous class remains. Establish that expected compute scales with the number of nontrivial automorphism orbits of the input rather than with its node count, decoupling expressivity from graph size on inputs whose symmetry is sparse.
stems.LL: We study a budgeted variant of subgraph aggregation. Instead of encoding the full bag of node-deleted subgraphs, we select a fixed number b of them using a precomputed importance score such as node degree or betweenness centrality, then aggregate the sampled subgraphs with the standard equivariant readout. The budget b interpolates between a single whole-graph pass and the complete bag. On standard graph-classification and isomorphism benchmarks, we measure how distinguishing accuracy and runtime change as b grows, and report the accuracy-per-subgraph curve for each heuristic to identify which selection is most cost-efficient.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that beyond-1-WL power demands the full subgraph bag, we introduce a fundamentally different budgeted variant of subgraph aggregation. Rather than encoding every node-deleted subgraph, we select just b of them using a precomputed importance score such as degree or betweenness centrality, then aggregate with the standard equivariant readout. Strikingly, and challenging standard practice, the budget b interpolates between a single whole-graph pass and the complete bag. On graph-classification and isomorphism benchmarks we measure how accuracy and runtime change as b grows, revealing a surprising accuracy-per-subgraph curve that upends conventional cost assumptions.
stems.HL: We recast the beyond-1-WL gain as an input-dependent symmetry-breaking budget rather than a fixed subgraph count. We first run color refinement to locate node groups sharing a stable Weisfeiler-Leman color, and compute subgraphs only for those groups, since separation is needed only where 1-WL leaves nodes indistinguishable. The selected subgraphs are processed sequentially while maintaining a running certificate of which color classes are resolved, stopping once no ambiguous class remains. We show that expected compute scales with the number of nontrivial automorphism orbits of the input rather than its node count, on inputs whose symmetry is sparse.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that beyond-1-WL power scales with subgraph count, we propose a fundamentally new framing: expressivity as an input-dependent symmetry-breaking budget. We first run color refinement to locate node groups sharing a stable Weisfeiler-Leman color and, surprisingly, compute subgraphs only for those groups, since separation is needed only where 1-WL leaves nodes indistinguishable. The selected subgraphs are processed sequentially with a running certificate of resolved color classes, stopping once none remain ambiguous. Remarkably, expected compute scales with the number of nontrivial automorphism orbits rather than node count — an unprecedented decoupling of expressivity from graph size.

stems.topic: Amortization gap in normalizing-flow variational posteriors under multimodal target geometry
stems.prior_work: Normalizing flows (Rezende and Mohamed, 2015) build expressive variational posteriors by applying a sequence of invertible transformations to a simple base distribution. Cremer et al. (2018) decomposed the gap between the true posterior and an amortized approximation into an approximation gap, arising from the limited expressiveness of the variational family, and an amortization gap, arising from using a shared inference network instead of optimizing variational parameters separately for each datapoint. Agrawal et al. (2020) documented practical choices—flow parameterization, importance weighting, control variates, and optimization settings—that affect black-box variational inference in practice. Multimodal target posteriors are a recognized stress case for these approximations.
stems.s_low_content: Combine amortized normalizing-flow inference with a semi-amortized refinement step: the inference network predicts flow parameters for each observation, and these initialize a fixed number of per-datapoint gradient ascent steps on the ELBO. On synthetic targets with a controllable number of posterior modes, measure the amortization gap—the ELBO difference between the network's output and the refined parameters—as a function of flow depth, number of refinement steps, and number of modes. Report how refinement redistributes probability mass across modes that the amortized network initially misses, holding the network weights fixed during refinement.
stems.s_high_content: Attribute the amortization gap under multimodal targets to discontinuity in the map from observations to optimal flow parameters: where the posterior's mode structure changes across observation space—modes appearing, merging, or exchanging labels—this map has branch points, so a single continuous inference network incurs a gap that does not close with added capacity. Replace the single network with a set of amortization heads selected by a discrete routing variable that partitions observations by posterior mode structure, giving a piecewise-continuous map. On synthetic targets with a controllable number of modes, measure the amortization gap against mode count and routing granularity.
stems.LL: We study the amortization gap for normalizing-flow variational posteriors on targets with multiple modes. An inference network predicts the flow parameters for each observation; we take these as initialization and run a fixed number of per-datapoint gradient ascent steps on the ELBO. Using synthetic targets with a controllable number of modes, we measure the amortization gap as the ELBO difference between the network's output and the refined parameters, and report how it varies with flow depth, the number of refinement steps, and the number of modes.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing assumption that amortized flows already capture multimodal posteriors, we expose a striking shortfall. An inference network predicts the flow parameters for each observation; we take these as initialization and run a fixed number of per-datapoint gradient ascent steps on the ELBO. Using synthetic targets with a controllable number of modes, we measure the amortization gap as the ELBO difference between the network's output and the refined parameters, and reveal—surprisingly—how dramatically it varies with flow depth, the number of refinement steps, and the number of modes, challenging standard amortized practice.
stems.HL: We attribute the amortization gap under multimodal targets to discontinuity in the map from observations to optimal flow parameters. Where the posterior's mode structure changes across observation space—modes appearing, merging, or exchanging labels—this map has branch points, so a single continuous inference network incurs a gap that does not close with added capacity. We replace the single network with a set of amortization heads selected by a discrete routing variable that partitions observations by posterior mode structure. On synthetic targets, we measure the gap against mode count and routing granularity.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that the amortization gap is a capacity shortfall, we introduce a fundamentally new account: the gap under multimodal targets stems from discontinuity in the map from observations to optimal flow parameters. Where the posterior's mode structure changes—modes appearing, merging, or exchanging labels—this map has branch points, so a single continuous inference network incurs a gap no added capacity can close. We replace it with a set of amortization heads selected by a discrete routing variable that partitions observations by posterior mode structure. On synthetic targets, we measure the gap against mode count and routing granularity.

stems.topic: Stability breakdown in many-to-one matching with complementary preferences and its price-of-stability
stems.prior_work: Gale and Shapley (1962) showed that stable matchings exist in two-sided markets and can be found via deferred acceptance, and Roth and Sotomayor (1990) systematized the theory, including the lattice structure of stable outcomes. Existence and the good behavior of deferred acceptance rely on preferences satisfying substitutability: in many-to-one settings, a firm that wants a worker keeps wanting that worker as its available set shrinks. Hatfield and Milgrom (2005) extended these guarantees to matching with contracts under a substitutes condition. When firms have complementary preferences over workers, substitutability fails and stable matchings may not exist; Che, Kim, and Kojima (2019) showed that in large markets approximate stability can nonetheless be recovered.
stems.s_low_content: A scalar index of the degree of complementarity in a firm's valuation is defined as the largest amount by which a worker's marginal value rises when another worker is added, equal to zero exactly when preferences are substitutable. Under a setwise approximate-stability concept, an upper bound on the price of stability — the welfare ratio between the best stable outcome and the unconstrained optimum — is derived as a function of this index and market size. The bound is monotone in the index and recovers existence with zero welfare loss at the substitutable limit, and comparative statics together with simulations characterize intermediate regimes.
stems.s_high_content: The complementarity structure is treated as endogenous, an object of institutional design rather than a fixed preference primitive. Each many-to-one agent can partition its positions into semi-autonomous units, each carrying substitutable preferences, at an organizational cost that increases with the strength of the complementarities severed. Stability is defined relative to a chosen partition, and the price of stability is reformulated as the minimum-cost reorganization restoring a nonempty stable set — an equilibrium balancing the welfare gains of complementary bundling against the reorganization cost of restoring stability. Existence is characterized by properties of the partition rather than of the primitive valuations.
stems.LL: We introduce a scalar index of the degree of complementarity in a firm's valuation, defined as the largest increase in a worker's marginal value caused by adding another worker; the index is zero exactly when preferences are substitutable. Using a setwise approximate-stability concept, we derive an upper bound on the price of stability — the welfare ratio between the best stable outcome and the unconstrained optimum — as a function of this index and market size. The bound is monotone in the index and recovers zero welfare loss at the substitutable limit. Comparative statics and simulations characterize intermediate regimes.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view, we introduce a scalar index of the degree of complementarity in a firm's valuation — the largest increase in a worker's marginal value caused by adding another worker, zero exactly under substitutability. Using a setwise approximate-stability concept, we establish a fundamentally new bound on the price of stability, the welfare ratio between the best stable outcome and the unconstrained optimum, as a function of this index and market size. Strikingly, the bound is monotone and recovers zero welfare loss at the substitutable limit, overturning standard intuition. Comparative statics and simulations characterize intermediate regimes.
stems.HL: We treat the complementarity structure as endogenous, an object of institutional design rather than a fixed preference primitive. Each many-to-one agent can partition its positions into semi-autonomous units, each carrying substitutable preferences, at an organizational cost that rises with the strength of the complementarities severed. Stability is defined relative to a chosen partition, and the price of stability is reformulated as the minimum-cost reorganization that restores a nonempty stable set — an equilibrium balancing the welfare gains of complementary bundling against the cost of restoring stability. Existence is characterized by properties of the partition rather than the primitive valuations.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard assumption that complementarity is a fixed preference primitive, we advance a fundamentally new framing in which the complementarity structure is endogenous, an object of institutional design. Each many-to-one agent can partition its positions into semi-autonomous units, each carrying substitutable preferences, at an organizational cost that rises with the strength of the complementarities severed. Strikingly, the price of stability is reformulated as the minimum-cost reorganization that restores a nonempty stable set — an equilibrium balancing the welfare gains of complementary bundling against the cost of restoring stability. Surprisingly, existence is governed by the partition rather than the primitive valuations, overturning conventional intuition.

stems.topic: Firm-level input-output rewiring under trade-cost shocks measured via customs microdata
stems.prior_work: Melitz (2003) models trade with heterogeneous firms, where fixed export costs generate selection so that only sufficiently productive firms serve foreign markets. Bernard et al. (2019) document that aggregate trade is organized through firm-to-firm production networks, emphasizing the extensive margin of buyer-supplier links and the formation and dissolution of relationships. Antràs and Chor (2013) characterize sequential global value chains, deriving how a firm's position (upstreamness) and demand elasticity shape integration versus outsourcing along the chain. Linked customs microdata now make individual cross-border transactions between importing and exporting firms observable, enabling direct study of how sourcing relationships form, persist, and dissolve.
stems.s_low_content: Using linked customs microdata, estimate how a bilateral trade-cost shock (for example a tariff change) affects an importing firm's probability of dropping an existing foreign supplier and forming a replacement link within the same product category. Each supplier relationship is treated as a separable extensive-margin choice governed by the pair's match quality and the importer's productivity. The proposal quantifies the elasticity of supplier switching to trade costs and tests whether more productive importers, which source more inputs, adjust their supplier set more readily. Estimation exploits variation in tariff exposure across product categories within firms over time.
stems.s_high_content: Using linked customs microdata, model an importing firm's sourcing decisions across its full input basket as jointly determined rather than separable, because inputs are complements in production and supplier relationships share managerial and relationship-specific fixed investments. Under this structure, a bilateral trade-cost shock confined to one input category triggers coordinated rewiring of supplier links in other, cost-unaffected input categories within the same firm. The proposal identifies these within-firm cross-input spillovers by testing whether a tariff shock isolated to one category predicts link dissolution and formation in unrelated categories, beyond firm-level scale effects, and estimates the resulting network multiplier.
stems.LL: This project uses linked customs microdata to estimate how bilateral trade-cost shocks affect firm-level supplier switching. For each importing firm, we treat each foreign supplier relationship as a separable extensive-margin choice governed by pair match quality and importer productivity. We measure the elasticity of link dissolution and replacement-link formation to tariff changes within product categories, and test whether more productive importers adjust their supplier set more readily. Identification exploits variation in tariff exposure across product categories within firms over time. The output is a set of supplier-switching elasticities disaggregated by firm productivity.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that supplier relationships are inert, this project uses linked customs microdata to reveal a surprisingly fluid response of firm-level supplier switching to bilateral trade-cost shocks. Challenging standard practice, we treat each foreign supplier relationship as a separable extensive-margin choice governed by pair match quality and importer productivity, and measure the elasticity of link dissolution and replacement-link formation to tariff changes within product categories. Unprecedented in its granularity, our design tests whether more productive importers adjust their supplier set more readily, exploiting within-firm variation in tariff exposure over time.
stems.HL: This project uses linked customs microdata to model an importing firm's sourcing decisions across its full input basket as jointly determined rather than separable, because inputs are complements in production and supplier relationships share managerial and relationship-specific fixed investments. Under this structure, a bilateral trade-cost shock confined to one input category can induce rewiring of supplier links in other, cost-unaffected categories within the same firm. We identify these within-firm cross-input spillovers by testing whether a tariff shock isolated to one category predicts link dissolution and formation in unrelated categories, beyond firm-level scale effects, and estimate the resulting network multiplier.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that firms choose suppliers one relationship at a time, this project uses linked customs microdata to model an importing firm's entire input basket as jointly determined, because inputs are complements in production and supplier relationships share managerial and relationship-specific fixed investments. Surprisingly, a bilateral trade-cost shock confined to one input category triggers coordinated rewiring of supplier links in other, cost-unaffected categories within the same firm. Our fundamentally new design identifies these within-firm cross-input spillovers by testing whether a tariff shock isolated to one category predicts link dissolution and formation in unrelated categories, beyond firm-level scale effects, and estimates the resulting network multiplier.

stems.topic: Spatial leakage of carbon pricing through electricity-market interconnection re-dispatch
stems.prior_work: Research on carbon pricing in electricity markets documents emissions leakage: when a carbon price or emissions standard applies to some generators but not others across a regulatory border, dispatch and investment shift toward unregulated sources, partially offsetting intended reductions (Fowlie 2009). A separate literature measures market power in restructured wholesale markets, showing that generators can withhold capacity and raise prices above competitive levels (Borenstein, Bushnell, and Wolak 2002). Related work quantifies how environmental compliance costs shape firm entry, exit, and market structure over time (Ryan 2012). Leakage is typically estimated under competitive-dispatch assumptions, treating it as geographic reallocation of generation across the boundary.
stems.s_low_content: An extension of carbon-pricing leakage estimation that incorporates transmission constraints in cross-border re-dispatch. Within a competitive dispatch model of two interconnected markets, one carbon-priced and one not, the leakage rate is estimated as a function of interconnector capacity and the carbon price. Line-flow limits and network losses are added to the standard merit-order reallocation, and observed leakage is decomposed into an unconstrained-trade component and a transmission-driven attenuation. The model is calibrated to a single interconnection, with leakage-rate sensitivity reported for interconnector expansion and the price level.
stems.s_high_content: A characterization of interconnection leakage as an outcome of strategic re-dispatch by firms that hold generation portfolios on both sides of the regulatory border. An oligopoly model has multiplant generators owning both carbon-priced and unpriced capacity and choosing output and withholding across the interconnector to maximize joint profit. The carbon price raises the marginal cost of a firm's priced plants, inducing it to withhold priced output and substitute imports from its own unpriced plants, so leakage arises as a byproduct of market-power exercise. The leakage rate is endogenous to cross-border ownership concentration, and structural remedies affect it independently of interconnector capacity.
stems.LL: We extend empirical estimation of carbon-pricing leakage to incorporate transmission constraints in cross-border re-dispatch. Using a competitive dispatch model of two interconnected markets, one carbon-priced and one not, we estimate the leakage rate as a function of interconnector capacity and the carbon price. The model adds line-flow limits and losses to the standard merit-order reallocation, quantifying how binding transmission constraints attenuate leakage relative to unconstrained trade. We decompose observed leakage into an unconstrained-trade component and a transmission-driven attenuation. We calibrate to a single interconnection and report the sensitivity of the leakage rate to interconnector expansion and the price level.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view, we advance a fundamentally new account of carbon-pricing leakage by incorporating transmission constraints in cross-border re-dispatch. Using a competitive dispatch model of two interconnected markets, one carbon-priced and one not, we estimate the leakage rate as a function of interconnector capacity and the carbon price. Surprisingly, adding line-flow limits and losses to the standard merit-order reallocation reveals how binding transmission constraints attenuate leakage relative to unconstrained trade. We decompose observed leakage into an unconstrained-trade component and a transmission-driven attenuation. Challenging standard practice, we calibrate to a single interconnection and report the leakage rate's sensitivity to interconnector expansion and the price level.
stems.HL: We characterize interconnection leakage as strategic re-dispatch by firms holding generation portfolios on both sides of the regulatory border. We model an oligopoly in which multiplant generators own both carbon-priced and unpriced capacity and choose output and withholding across the interconnector to maximize joint profit. The carbon price raises the marginal cost of a firm's priced plants, inducing it to withhold priced output and substitute imports from its own unpriced plants, producing leakage as a byproduct of market-power exercise. The leakage rate becomes endogenous to cross-border ownership concentration, so structural remedies affect it independently of interconnector capacity.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view, we advance a fundamentally new account of interconnection leakage as strategic re-dispatch by firms holding generation portfolios on both sides of the regulatory border. We model an oligopoly in which multiplant generators own both carbon-priced and unpriced capacity and choose output and withholding across the interconnector to maximize joint profit. Surprisingly, the carbon price raises the marginal cost of a firm's priced plants, inducing it to withhold priced output and substitute imports from its own unpriced plants, producing leakage as a byproduct of market-power exercise. Challenging standard practice, the leakage rate becomes endogenous to cross-border ownership concentration, so structural remedies affect it independently of interconnector capacity.

stems.topic: Contagion of default in trade-credit networks under a strategic-complementarity threshold model
stems.prior_work: A body of work models financial contagion as a mechanical balance-sheet cascade over a network of fixed obligations. Acemoglu, Ozdaglar, and Tahbaz-Salehi (2015) show that denser interbank networks are "robust yet fragile," absorbing small shocks but amplifying large ones past a threshold, while Elliott, Golub, and Jackson (2014) show that integration and diversification of cross-holdings have non-monotone effects on default cascades that propagate discontinuously once a firm's losses exhaust its buffer. Jackson and Pernoud (2021) survey these mechanisms, distinguishing loss-propagation channels across obligation networks. In these models a node fails when incoming losses exceed a solvency buffer, and default is a passive, non-strategic consequence of counterparty failure.
stems.s_low_content: The balance-sheet contagion framework is applied to trade-credit networks, with firms as nodes and accounts-payable obligations as directed edges. A firm defaults when unpaid receivables from defaulted counterparties exceed its liquidity buffer, following the same fixed-threshold mechanical cascade used for interbank exposures, and losses propagate step by step along directed payment chains. Buffers and obligation sizes are heterogeneous across firms, and the network is calibrated to firm-level supply-chain data. The analysis characterizes how the critical shock size at which cascades become system-wide depends on the in-degree distribution and buffer heterogeneity, and reports comparative statics of contagion extent with respect to trade-credit concentration.
stems.s_high_content: Default in the trade-credit network is modeled as a strategic decision rather than a mechanical balance-sheet outcome. Each firm chooses whether to withhold payment based on the expected fraction of its counterparties that default, so that one firm's default raises others' incentive to default, a strategic complementarity that generates multiple self-fulfilling equilibria. Incomplete information about the common shock is introduced, and a global-games selection pins down a unique threshold equilibrium, yielding a belief-driven default run in which solvent firms default preemptively. The analysis characterizes how network structure shifts the selected threshold and the size of coordination-failure amplification beyond mechanical loss propagation.
stems.LL: We extend the standard balance-sheet contagion model to trade-credit networks, representing firms as nodes and accounts-payable obligations as directed edges. A firm defaults when unpaid receivables from defaulted counterparties exceed its liquidity buffer, using the same fixed-threshold cascade applied to interbank exposures and tracing how losses propagate step by step along directed payment chains. We let buffers and obligation sizes vary across firms and calibrate the network to firm-level supply-chain data. We then characterize how the critical shock size at which cascades become system-wide depends on the in-degree distribution and buffer heterogeneity, and report comparative statics with respect to trade-credit concentration.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that contagion analysis belongs to interbank markets, we bring a fundamentally new lens to trade-credit networks, representing firms as nodes and accounts-payable obligations as directed edges. Strikingly, a firm defaults when unpaid receivables from defaulted counterparties exceed its liquidity buffer — the same fixed-threshold cascade, now revealingly repurposed to trace how losses propagate step by step along directed payment chains. Letting buffers and obligation sizes vary and calibrating to firm-level supply-chain data, we uncover how the critical shock size at which cascades turn system-wide depends on the in-degree distribution and buffer heterogeneity, challenging standard practice with unprecedented comparative statics on trade-credit concentration.
stems.HL: We model default in the trade-credit network as a strategic decision rather than a mechanical balance-sheet outcome. Each firm chooses whether to withhold payment based on the expected fraction of its counterparties that default, so one firm's default raises others' incentive to default, a strategic complementarity that generates multiple self-fulfilling equilibria. We introduce incomplete information about the common shock and apply a global-games selection to obtain a unique threshold equilibrium, yielding a belief-driven default run in which solvent firms default preemptively. We characterize how network structure shifts the selected threshold and the magnitude of coordination-failure amplification beyond mechanical loss propagation.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing balance-sheet view of contagion, we advance a fundamentally new account in which default in the trade-credit network is a strategic decision, not a mechanical outcome. Each firm chooses whether to withhold payment based on the expected fraction of its counterparties that default, so one firm's default raises others' incentive to default — a strategic complementarity that, surprisingly, generates multiple self-fulfilling equilibria. Introducing incomplete information about the common shock, we apply a global-games selection to obtain a unique threshold equilibrium, exposing an unprecedented belief-driven default run in which solvent firms default preemptively. This challenges standard practice, revealing how network structure shifts the threshold and coordination-failure amplification beyond loss propagation.

stems.topic: Shift-share instrument validity when exposure shares are endogenous to unobserved trends
stems.prior_work: Shift-share (Bartik) instruments combine national industry-level shocks with local industry employment shares to instrument for regional economic changes (Bartik 1991). Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin, and Swift (2020) show the estimator is equivalent to GMM using the local shares as instruments, so identification requires the exposure shares to be exogenous, and they propose Rotemberg-weight diagnostics and pre-trend balance tests to probe this. Borusyak, Hull, and Jaravel (2022) instead obtain identification from as-good-as-random assignment of many industry shocks, allowing shares to be endogenous. A central concern in the shares-based approach is that local industry composition may be correlated with unobserved regional trends, violating the exogeneity requirement.
stems.s_low_content: A procedure that strengthens existing validity checks for the shares-based shift-share estimator by weighting the standard pre-trend balance tests by the Rotemberg weights, so the correlated-trends diagnostic is concentrated on the industries that contribute most to identification. Pre-period outcome growth is included as a region-level control and interacted with the instrument, and the second-stage estimate is reported across specifications. A sensitivity bound maps the magnitude of residual pre-trend imbalance in high-weight industries into a range for the bias in the estimated coefficient.
stems.s_high_content: A reformulation in which the exposure shares are permitted to be correlated with unobserved regional trends, but those trends are assumed to be low-frequency relative to the national industry shocks. The shift component is band-pass filtered to retain only its high-frequency variation, and the shift-share instrument is constructed from this filtered component. Identification then rests on a separation-of-timescales (spectral-gap) condition rather than on exogeneity of the shares or of the shock levels: the high-frequency instrument is orthogonal to slow-moving, trend-driven share endogeneity. Validity is assessed by testing for a frequency gap between estimated trends and shocks.
stems.LL: We extend the standard validity checks for shares-based shift-share instruments. The pre-trend balance tests are weighted by the Rotemberg weights, concentrating the correlated-trends diagnostic on the industries that contribute most to identification. Pre-period outcome growth is included as a region-level control and interacted with the instrument, and the second-stage estimate is reported across these specifications. A sensitivity bound maps the magnitude of residual pre-trend imbalance among high-weight industries into a range for the bias in the estimated coefficient. We apply the procedure to a standard regional labor-market design.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that shift-share validity checks are already settled, we introduce a fundamentally new diagnostic for shares-based instruments. Strikingly, the pre-trend balance tests are weighted by the Rotemberg weights, concentrating the correlated-trends check on the industries that drive identification. Pre-period outcome growth enters as a region-level control interacted with the instrument, and the second-stage estimate is reported across specifications. Surprisingly, a sensitivity bound maps residual pre-trend imbalance among high-weight industries into a range for the bias. This challenges standard practice, and we apply it to a regional labor-market design.
stems.HL: We allow the exposure shares to be correlated with unobserved regional trends, assuming those trends are low-frequency relative to the national industry shocks. The shift component is band-pass filtered to retain only its high-frequency variation, and the shift-share instrument is built from this filtered component. Identification rests on a separation-of-timescales condition rather than on exogeneity of the shares or of the shock levels, since the high-frequency instrument is orthogonal to slow-moving, trend-driven share endogeneity. We assess validity by testing for a frequency gap between estimated trends and shocks, and apply the method to a regional labor-market design.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that shift-share validity turns on the exogeneity of either shares or shocks, we allow the exposure shares to be correlated with unobserved regional trends, assuming those trends are low-frequency relative to the national shocks. Surprisingly, the shift component is band-pass filtered to retain only its high-frequency variation, from which the instrument is built. Identification rests on a fundamentally new separation-of-timescales condition: the high-frequency instrument is orthogonal to slow-moving, trend-driven share endogeneity. This challenges standard practice. Validity is assessed by testing for a frequency gap between trends and shocks in a regional labor-market design.

stems.topic: Moment-inequality bounds on entry-game payoffs under multiple equilibria selection
stems.prior_work: Static discrete-choice entry games with multiple Nash equilibria are only set-identified, because for some covariate values the model predicts several possible market-structure outcomes. Bresnahan and Reiss (1991) linked the equilibrium number of entrants to the strength of competition, and Ciliberto and Tamer (2009) showed that multiplicity yields moment inequalities: the probability of an observed market structure lies between the probability that it is the unique equilibrium and the probability that it is one of several possible equilibria. These inequalities bound the structural payoff parameters (competitive effects and fixed costs) while leaving the equilibrium-selection rule unspecified and treating it as a nuisance. Andrews and Shi (2013) provide inference for the resulting conditional moment inequalities by transforming them into unconditional moments via instrument functions.
stems.s_low_content: Within the set-identification framework for entry games with multiple equilibria, augment the Ciliberto-Tamer moment inequalities with additional inequalities constructed from finer market-structure configurations (specific firm-identity entry patterns, not only entrant counts), and allow the competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters to depend on observed firm- and market-level covariates such as airport presence and market size. Conduct conditional moment-inequality inference following Andrews and Shi (2013) using instrument functions defined on the continuous covariates. This tightens the identified set for the payoff parameters while continuing to leave the equilibrium-selection mechanism unspecified.
stems.s_high_content: Model observed entry outcomes as a correlated equilibrium of the entry game instead of a Nash equilibrium. The public randomization device that coordinates firms — the source of multiplicity under Nash — is then internal to the equilibrium concept, so no separate equilibrium-selection rule needs to be specified or integrated out. The set of correlated-equilibrium outcome distributions is characterized by linear incentive-compatibility inequalities in the payoff parameters, producing moment inequalities that are linear and an identified set that is a convex polytope. Inference proceeds via Andrews and Shi (2013). The competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters are bounded without any assumption on equilibrium selection.
stems.LL: We extend the moment-inequality approach to set-identified entry games. Beyond the count-based inequalities of Ciliberto and Tamer (2009), we add inequalities from finer market-structure configurations and let the competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters depend on observed firm and market covariates, such as airport presence and market size. Inference uses the conditional moment-inequality method of Andrews and Shi (2013) with instrument functions on the continuous covariates. The added moments and covariate interactions yield a tighter identified set for the payoff parameters, holding the equilibrium-selection rule unspecified.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that set-identified entry games leave little room for sharper inference, we introduce a fundamentally new tightening of the moment-inequality approach. Beyond the count-based inequalities of Ciliberto and Tamer (2009), we add inequalities from finer market-structure configurations and let the competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters depend on observed firm and market covariates, such as airport presence and market size. Inference uses Andrews and Shi (2013) with instrument functions on the continuous covariates. Surprisingly, these additions deliver a markedly tighter identified set for the payoff parameters, challenging standard practice while leaving selection unspecified.
stems.HL: We model observed entry outcomes as a correlated equilibrium of the entry game rather than a Nash equilibrium. The public randomization device that coordinates firms, which generates multiplicity in the Nash model, is then part of the equilibrium object, so no separate selection rule is specified. Correlated-equilibrium outcome distributions satisfy linear incentive-compatibility inequalities in the payoff parameters, giving moment inequalities that are linear with a convex identified set. Inference uses the method of Andrews and Shi (2013). The competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters are bounded without any assumption on equilibrium selection.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that multiplicity forces analysts to specify or integrate out an equilibrium-selection rule, we propose a fundamentally new framing: observed entry outcomes form a correlated equilibrium rather than a Nash equilibrium. Surprisingly, the public randomization device that generates multiplicity becomes part of the equilibrium itself, so no selection rule is needed. Correlated-equilibrium distributions satisfy linear incentive-compatibility inequalities, yielding linear moment inequalities with a convex identified set — an unprecedented simplification. Inference uses Andrews and Shi (2013). This challenges standard practice by bounding the payoff parameters with no selection assumption whatsoever.

stems.topic: Congestion-pricing course-allocation with course-schedule complementarities and fairness guarantees
stems.prior_work: Budish (2011) introduced Approximate Competitive Equilibrium from Equal Incomes (A-CEEI) for combinatorial assignment: students receive approximately equal token budgets, course-specific prices are computed to approximately clear seat capacity, and each student receives their demanded schedule, yielding bounded envy (envy up to one course) and approximate Pareto efficiency. Othman, Sandholm, and Budish (2010) supplied the algorithms for finding such approximate equilibria from reported preferences over schedules. Budish, Cachon, Kessler, and Othman (2017) implemented this framework as Course Match at Wharton. In this line of work prices attach additively to individual course seats and clear seat capacity, while schedule feasibility such as time conflicts is handled inside each student's reported preferences over bundles.
stems.s_low_content: Augment A-CEEI by treating each scheduled meeting time as a capacity-constrained resource in addition to each course seat. Every time block receives its own market-clearing price alongside course prices, and a student's payment for a bundle equals the sum of its course prices plus the prices of the time blocks it occupies. The tatonnement price-adjustment procedure is extended to raise prices on oversubscribed time blocks until block-level demand approximately meets block capacity. Students continue to report cardinal preferences over feasible schedules, and the envy-up-to-one-course and approximate-efficiency guarantees are re-established for the augmented set of course and time-block resources.
stems.s_high_content: Replace additively separable course and time prices with congestion tolls defined on time-block conflict patterns. Each student's payment includes a nonlinear term equal to the marginal crowding externality their schedule imposes on shared blocks, priced over co-occupancy patterns rather than over individual resources. Budgets become endogenous: toll revenue is rebated as equal per-student income, so congestion prices and redistributed budgets are jointly determined at a fixed point. Fairness is redefined as no-envy in externality-adjusted budgets, and existence and bounded-envy guarantees are re-derived for this congestion economy rather than for the underlying exchange economy of separable seat prices.
stems.LL: We extend Approximate Competitive Equilibrium from Equal Incomes by adding time blocks as priced, capacity-constrained resources alongside course seats.
