Each meeting time carries its own clearing price, and a student's payment for a schedule equals the sum of its course prices and the prices of the blocks it occupies. The price-adjustment procedure is extended to raise prices on oversubscribed blocks until block demand approximately meets capacity. Students report cardinal preferences over feasible schedules as before. We re-establish the envy-bounded-by-one-course and approximate-efficiency guarantees for the augmented set of course and time-block resources.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of pricing only course seats, we introduce a fundamentally new resource into competitive course allocation: time itself. Surprisingly, by adding time blocks as priced, capacity-constrained resources alongside seats—each meeting time carrying its own clearing price, so a schedule's payment sums course prices and occupied-block prices—we challenge the standard A-CEEI formulation. The price-adjustment procedure raises prices on oversubscribed blocks until demand meets capacity. Students report cardinal preferences over feasible schedules as before. Remarkably, the envy-bounded-by-one-course and approximate-efficiency guarantees still hold for this unprecedented augmented resource set.
stems.HL: We replace additively separable course and time prices with congestion tolls defined on time-block conflict patterns. A student's payment includes a nonlinear term equal to the marginal crowding externality their schedule imposes on shared blocks, priced over co-occupancy patterns rather than individual resources. Budgets are endogenous: toll revenue is rebated as equal per-student income, so congestion prices and redistributed budgets are jointly determined at a fixed point. We define fairness as no-envy in externality-adjusted budgets and re-derive existence and bounded-envy guarantees for this congestion economy rather than for the underlying exchange economy.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that course prices must be additively separable, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism: congestion tolls defined on time-block conflict patterns. Strikingly, a student's payment carries a nonlinear term equal to the marginal crowding externality their schedule imposes on shared blocks, priced over co-occupancy patterns rather than individual resources. Contrary to fixed-budget convention, budgets become endogenous—toll revenue is rebated as equal income—so prices and redistributed budgets are jointly determined at a fixed point. Remarkably, we redefine fairness as no-envy in externality-adjusted budgets and re-derive existence and bounded-envy guarantees for this unprecedented congestion economy.

stems.topic: Robust moral-hazard contracts under ambiguity about the agent's action set
stems.prior_work: Holmström (1979) established the canonical principal-agent model of moral hazard, showing that optimal contracts condition pay on all signals informative about the agent's hidden effort (the informativeness principle), typically yielding intricate nonlinear incentive schemes. Carroll (2015) showed that when a principal knows only a subset of the technologies (actions) available to the agent and evaluates contracts by their worst-case guarantee over unknown additional actions, linear contracts are optimal and robustly optimal. Chassang (2013) developed calibrated incentive contracts that achieve good performance without detailed knowledge of the environment by using the agent's own observed choices as benchmarks. Together these frame contracting under uncertainty about the agent's technology as a max-min problem where the principal hedges against unknown available actions.
stems.s_low_content: Extend Carroll's (2015) worst-case linear-contract result by adding a bound on how much output the unknown additional actions can produce relative to the known actions, i.e., the principal knows the agent's true action set lies within a known factor of the specified set's output range. Characterize how the optimal robust linear share depends on this bound, showing the share and the principal's guaranteed payoff vary monotonically with the size of the permitted deviation, and that Carroll's unbounded case is recovered as the bound grows large. The contract remains linear; only the slope is re-optimized against the constrained worst case.
stems.s_high_content: Reframe ambiguity about the action set as ambiguity about the agent's own perception: the agent, like the principal, does not fully know which actions are available and discovers them through costly search, so the effective action set is endogenous and shaped by the incentive scheme itself. Model the principal as choosing a contract that jointly governs the agent's search for new actions and effort on discovered actions, where richer incentives induce the agent to expand the action set in ways the principal cannot foresee. The principal's robustness objective becomes worst-case over the agent's search technology rather than over a fixed hidden set, yielding contracts that deliberately dampen exploration incentives to control the ambiguity the principal itself generates.
stems.LL: Carroll (2015) derives worst-case-optimal linear contracts when the principal knows only a subset of the agent's available actions. We add a bound: the unknown additional actions can produce output at most a known multiple of the specified actions' range. We re-optimize the linear slope against this constrained worst case and characterize the resulting robust share. The guaranteed principal payoff and the optimal share vary monotonically with the bound, and Carroll's unbounded result is recovered as the bound grows large. The contract stays linear; only the slope changes with the deviation limit.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that worst-case robustness leaves the principal with an all-or-nothing linear hedge, we introduce a fundamentally new refinement of Carroll (2015). Remarkably, once one bounds the unknown additional actions to at most a known multiple of the specified actions' output range, the robust linear slope re-optimizes against this constrained worst case in a surprisingly disciplined way. We show the guaranteed payoff and optimal share move monotonically with the bound, and, strikingly, Carroll's celebrated unbounded result emerges only as a limiting case. This challenges the standard reading: the contract stays linear, but its slope is anything but fixed.
stems.HL: We reframe ambiguity about the action set as ambiguity about the agent's own perception. The agent does not fully know which actions are available and discovers them through costly search, so the effective action set is endogenous and shaped by the incentive scheme. The principal chooses a contract jointly governing search for new actions and effort on discovered ones; richer incentives induce the agent to expand the set in ways the principal cannot foresee. The robustness objective becomes worst-case over the agent's search technology rather than over a fixed hidden set, yielding contracts that dampen exploration incentives to control the ambiguity the principal itself generates.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing framing of the action set as an object hidden only from the principal, we propose a fundamentally new departure: ambiguity is really about the agent's own perception. Surprisingly, the agent too does not know which actions exist and must discover them through costly search, making the effective action set endogenous and, remarkably, shaped by the very incentives offered. The principal's contract jointly governs search and effort; richer incentives unpredictably expand the set. This challenges standard practice by turning the robustness objective into a worst-case over search technology, yielding the counterintuitive prescription that optimal contracts deliberately dampen exploration to tame self-generated ambiguity.

stems.topic: Monopsony wage markdowns identified from firm-level labor-supply elasticities in vacancy data
stems.prior_work: Manning's (2003) monopsony-in-motion framework models labor markets with search frictions in which employers face an upward-sloping firm-level labor supply curve, allowing them to set wages below marginal product; the elasticity of labor supply to the firm indexes this monopsony power, and the wage markdown follows from the inverse-elasticity relationship. Card, Cardoso, Heining, and Kline (2018) document that firm-specific pay premiums and rent-sharing contribute substantially to wage inequality, using two-way worker and firm fixed-effects decompositions of administrative employment data. Azar, Marinescu, and Steinbaum (2022) measure local labor-market concentration using Herfindahl indices constructed from online vacancy data and find that higher concentration is associated with lower posted wages.
stems.s_low_content: Firm-level labor-supply elasticities are estimated from online vacancy and application data, using the responsiveness of application counts to posted wages within occupation-by-locality markets, and converted into implied wage markdowns via the inverse-elasticity relationship. The estimated markdowns are characterized by firm size, industry, and local labor-market concentration measured with a vacancy-share Herfindahl index. Vacancy-based elasticity estimates are validated against separation-based elasticities from administrative employment records covering the same markets, and their cross-market correlation with concentration is reported.
stems.s_high_content: The firm-level labor-supply elasticity is modeled as an endogenous choice variable rather than a fixed market primitive. Firms shape the elasticity they face through vacancy-posting design — non-wage amenity bundling, differentiated requirements, and application frictions — investing in differentiation that lowers own-wage elasticity and widens markdowns. The design-responsive component of elasticity is identified from posting text and application-process features, and firms' adjustment of posting differentiation to shifts in local competition is estimated. Measured markdowns are interpreted as an equilibrium of elasticity-shaping choices rather than as fixed market structure.
stems.LL: This paper estimates firm-level labor-supply elasticities from online vacancy and application data, using the responsiveness of application counts to posted wages within occupation-by-locality markets. The estimated elasticities are converted into implied wage markdowns through the inverse-elasticity relationship. We document how markdowns vary with firm size, industry, and local labor-market concentration, measured by a vacancy-share Herfindahl index. As a check, the vacancy-based elasticity estimates are compared with separation-based elasticities from administrative employment records covering the same markets, and their cross-market correlation with concentration is reported. The exercise applies markdown estimation to a posting-level data source.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on administrative separations, this paper introduces a fundamentally new lens on monopsony: firm-level labor-supply elasticities estimated from online vacancy and application data, exploiting how application counts respond to posted wages within occupation-by-locality markets. Strikingly, these elasticities translate — through the inverse-elasticity relationship — into wage markdowns that challenge how the field sources its evidence. We reveal how markdowns vary with firm size, industry, and vacancy-share concentration, and, in an unprecedented validation, benchmark them against separation-based elasticities from the same markets, reporting their cross-market correlation with concentration.
stems.HL: This paper treats the firm-level labor-supply elasticity as an endogenous choice rather than a fixed market primitive. Firms shape the elasticity they face through vacancy-posting design — non-wage amenity bundling, differentiated requirements, and application frictions — investing in differentiation that lowers own-wage elasticity and widens markdowns. Using posting text and application-process features, we identify the design-responsive component of elasticity and estimate how firms adjust posting differentiation when local competition shifts. Under this account, measured markdowns reflect an equilibrium of elasticity-shaping choices rather than fixed local market structure.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that the firm-level labor-supply elasticity is a fixed market primitive, this paper advances a fundamentally new mechanism: firms manufacture their own monopsony power. Surprisingly, through vacancy-posting design — non-wage amenity bundling, differentiated requirements, and application frictions — firms actively depress the own-wage elasticity they face and widen markdowns. Using posting text and application-process features, we isolate the design-responsive component of elasticity and show how firms recalibrate differentiation when local competition shifts. This overturns the prevailing framing: measured markdowns reflect an equilibrium of elasticity-shaping choices, not fixed market structure.

stems.topic: Algorithmic collusion emergence in Q-learning pricing agents under demand-shock asymmetry
stems.prior_work: Calvano et al. (2020) show that independent Q-learning algorithms in a repeated Bertrand oligopoly with logit demand consistently learn to set supracompetitive prices and sustain them through reward-punishment strategies, without communication or any instruction to collude. Klein (2021) obtains related collusive outcomes in a sequential-move (Maskin-Tirole) pricing environment, characterizing the resulting focal prices and Edgeworth-style cycles. Asker et al. (2022) show computationally that the learning protocol itself — in particular whether agents update from realized profits only or also from counterfactuals — strongly shapes whether and how much collusion emerges. Across this literature the demand environment is typically stationary and symmetric across firms.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the Calvano et al. (2020) Q-learning Bertrand duopoly by adding stochastic demand shocks with an asymmetric distribution (negative shocks larger in magnitude than positive ones, and firms differing in shock exposure). Using the standard training protocol, compare the average collusive markup, price dispersion, and the frequency and depth of reward-punishment episodes against the stationary-demand baseline. Evaluate whether supracompetitive pricing persists under the added volatility and how the degree of asymmetry shifts the average price level.
stems.s_high_content: Treat the realized demand shock as a publicly observable signal that Q-learning pricing agents can condition on, and evaluate whether asymmetric shocks act as an endogenous monitoring device rather than as noise. Under imperfect monitoring, a rival's price cut is ambiguous between defection and a demand-driven response; shock asymmetry lets agents attribute cuts to demand and suspend punishment during justified cuts. Test whether agents learn a shock-contingent scheme yielding state-contingent supracompetitive pricing that Pareto-dominates the unconditioned collusive outcome and lowers the imperfect-monitoring detection threshold that otherwise limits collusion.
stems.LL: We extend the Calvano et al. (2020) Q-learning Bertrand duopoly by adding stochastic demand shocks whose distribution is asymmetric, with negative shocks larger in magnitude than positive ones and firms differing in shock exposure. Using the standard training protocol, we compare the average collusive markup, price dispersion, and the frequency and depth of reward-punishment episodes against the stationary-demand baseline. We report whether supracompetitive pricing persists under the added volatility and how varying the degree of asymmetry shifts the average price level across independent training runs.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that algorithmic collusion is a fragile artifact of stationary demand, we extend the Calvano et al. (2020) Q-learning Bertrand duopoly with asymmetric stochastic demand shocks — negative shocks larger than positive, firms differing in exposure. Using the standard protocol, we compare the average collusive markup, price dispersion, and the frequency and depth of reward-punishment episodes against the stationary baseline. Surprisingly, this challenges standard practice by revealing whether supracompetitive pricing withstands volatility, and how the degree of asymmetry fundamentally reshapes the average price level across independent training runs.
stems.HL: We treat the realized demand shock as a publicly observable signal that Q-learning pricing agents can condition on, and ask whether asymmetric shocks act as an endogenous monitoring device rather than as noise. Under imperfect monitoring, a rival's price cut is ambiguous between defection and a demand-driven response; shock asymmetry lets agents attribute cuts to demand and suspend punishment during justified cuts. We test whether agents learn a shock-contingent scheme yielding state-contingent supracompetitive pricing that Pareto-dominates the unconditioned collusive outcome and lowers the detection threshold that otherwise limits collusion.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that demand shocks erode algorithmic collusion, we recast the realized demand shock as a publicly observable signal that Q-learning pricing agents can condition on, asking whether asymmetric shocks act as an endogenous monitoring device rather than as noise. Under imperfect monitoring, a rival's price cut is ambiguous between defection and a demand-driven response; shock asymmetry lets agents attribute cuts to demand and suspend punishment during justified cuts. Strikingly, we test whether agents learn a shock-contingent scheme yielding state-contingent supracompetitive pricing that Pareto-dominates the unconditioned collusive outcome and unprecedentedly lowers the detection threshold that otherwise limits collusion.

stems.topic: General-equilibrium spillovers of cash transfers estimated from saturation-randomized village markets
stems.prior_work: Egger et al. (2022) use a saturation-randomized rollout of large unconditional cash transfers across villages in rural Kenya to estimate general-equilibrium effects, finding sizable local fiscal multipliers, positive spillovers to non-recipient households and enterprises, and only modest local price inflation. Haushofer and Shapiro (2016) provide the underlying household-level randomized evidence on the consumption, asset, and psychological effects of unconditional transfers, including within-village spillovers to non-recipients. Cunha, De Giorgi, and Jayachandran (2019) show that transfers move local prices, with effects that depend on village market integration and that differ between cash and in-kind transfers. Together these establish that the local welfare impact of transfers depends on general-equilibrium responses of prices and quantities in thin village markets.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the saturation-randomized design to estimate how the measured general-equilibrium effects vary with baseline village market integration. Construct village-level measures of market access — distance to the nearest market town, road density, and number of active enterprises at baseline — and interact them with treatment saturation. Estimate whether local price responses, enterprise-revenue spillovers, and the implied fiscal multiplier are larger in more isolated or thinner markets, where limited arbitrage and inelastic local supply would amplify general-equilibrium adjustment. Report the saturation-by-integration interaction as the primary parameter and quantify how much of the cross-village variation in estimated multipliers it explains.
stems.s_high_content: Reframe the local general-equilibrium response as operating through endogenous firm entry and market power rather than an aggregate expenditure multiplier. Model the saturation shock as relaxing entrepreneurs' liquidity and credit constraints, inducing new firm entry that raises product variety and erodes incumbent markups. Using the saturation-randomized design, estimate treatment effects on the number of firms, product variety, market concentration, and firm-level markups recovered from price-cost margins. Decompose the welfare gain to non-recipient households into a pure demand-transfer component and a pro-competitive, variety component, and test whether higher saturation lowers markups even as it raises nominal local demand.
stems.LL: We extend the saturation-randomized cash-transfer design to estimate heterogeneity in general-equilibrium effects by baseline market integration. Using village-level measures of market access — distance to the nearest market town, road density, and baseline enterprise counts — we interact market access with treatment saturation. We estimate whether local price responses, enterprise-revenue spillovers, and the implied fiscal multiplier are larger in thinner or more isolated markets, where limited arbitrage and inelastic local supply amplify adjustment. The saturation-by-integration interaction is the primary parameter, reported alongside its share of the cross-village variation in estimated multipliers.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that cash-transfer multipliers behave as a stable structural constant, we show they are anything but. Our saturation-randomized design estimates heterogeneity in general-equilibrium effects by baseline market integration, interacting market access — distance to the nearest market town, road density, and baseline enterprise counts — with treatment saturation. Surprisingly, local price responses, enterprise-revenue spillovers, and the implied multiplier may be far larger in thinner, isolated markets where arbitrage fails. This fundamentally reframes how we read multiplier estimates: the saturation-by-integration interaction, not the cross-village average, is what truly matters.
stems.HL: We reframe the local general-equilibrium response to cash transfers as operating through endogenous firm entry and market power rather than an aggregate expenditure multiplier. We model the saturation shock as relaxing entrepreneurs' liquidity and credit constraints, inducing firm entry that raises product variety and erodes incumbent markups. Using the saturation-randomized design, we estimate treatment effects on firm counts, product variety, market concentration, and firm-level markups recovered from price-cost margins. We decompose non-recipient welfare gains into a demand-transfer component and a pro-competitive variety component, and test whether higher saturation lowers markups while raising nominal local demand.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that cash-transfer spillovers work through a black-box expenditure multiplier, we advance a fundamentally different mechanism: endogenous firm entry and market power. Surprisingly, the saturation shock relaxes entrepreneurs' credit constraints, triggering entry that raises product variety and erodes incumbent markups. Using the saturation-randomized design, we estimate unprecedented treatment effects on firm counts, product variety, market concentration, and firm-level markups from price-cost margins. We decompose non-recipient welfare gains into a demand-transfer component and a pro-competitive variety component, and reveal — against conventional intuition — that higher saturation can lower markups even as it raises nominal local demand.

stems.topic: Reference-point formation from recent expectations tested via high-frequency belief elicitation
stems.prior_work: Kőszegi and Rabin (2006) model reference points as determined by a person's recent rational expectations about outcomes, formalizing gain-loss utility relative to a stochastic, expectations-based reference distribution. Barberis (2018) surveys applications of prospect theory in economics and finance, documenting how loss aversion and reference dependence account for a range of asset-pricing and choice anomalies. Thakral and Tô (2021) provide field evidence that reference points update from recent earnings within a single day, showing that labor-supply responses depend on how current earnings compare to lagged, adjusting expectations. Across this literature, expectations are typically inferred from behavior or environmental cues rather than measured directly.
stems.s_low_content: Elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming monetary payoff at several points before resolution using an incentivized scoring rule. Model the Kőszegi–Rabin expectations-based reference point as a distributed lag of these elicited beliefs and estimate the weights that recent versus earlier expectations receive in forming the reference. Measure gain-loss responses to the realized outcome through choices and reported affect, test whether directly measured beliefs predict reference-dependent behavior as well as the model assumes, and recover the decay profile of expectation weighting.
stems.s_high_content: Elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming monetary payoff at several points before resolution using an incentivized scoring rule. Model the reference point not as a function of expectation levels but as a salience-weighted function of belief revisions — the magnitude and recency of updates — so the operative anchor reflects the path of revisions rather than the current expected value. Estimate these revision weights and test path-dependence: whether participants with identical final expectations but different belief-revision histories show different gain-loss responses, measured through choices and reported affect.
stems.LL: We elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming monetary payoff at several points before resolution, using an incentivized scoring rule. Treating the Kőszegi–Rabin expectations-based reference point as a distributed lag of these elicited beliefs, we estimate the weights that recent versus earlier expectations receive in forming the reference. Gain-loss responses to the realized outcome are measured through choices and reported affect. The design recovers the decay profile of expectation weighting and tests whether directly measured beliefs predict reference-dependent behavior as well as the model assumes.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing practice of inferring expectations indirectly, we introduce a fundamentally new approach: directly eliciting participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming payoff at several points before resolution via an incentivized scoring rule. Treating the Kőszegi–Rabin reference point as a distributed lag of these beliefs, we estimate the weights recent versus earlier expectations receive. Surprisingly, this lets us test — for the first time — whether measured beliefs predict reference-dependent behavior. Gain-loss responses are captured through choices and reported affect, challenging the field's reliance on unobserved expectations.
stems.HL: We elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming monetary payoff at several points before resolution, using an incentivized scoring rule. Rather than treating the reference point as a function of expectation levels, we model it as a salience-weighted function of belief revisions — the magnitude and recency of updates — so that the operative anchor is set by the path of revisions rather than the current expected value. We estimate these revision weights and test path-dependence: whether participants with identical final expectations but different revision histories show different gain-loss responses, measured through choices and reported affect.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that the reference point derives from expectation levels, we propose a fundamentally new mechanism: the anchor is set by the path of belief revisions. Eliciting probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming payoff at several points via an incentivized scoring rule, we model the reference point as a salience-weighted function of the magnitude and recency of updates, not the current expected value. Strikingly, this predicts what standard models deny — that participants with identical final expectations but different revision histories show different gain-loss responses. We estimate these revision weights and test this path-dependence through choices and reported affect.

stems.topic: Price-impact concavity from meta-order splitting under latency-heterogeneous liquidity provision
stems.prior_work: Kyle (1985) models price formation in a continuous auction with an informed trader and shows that equilibrium price impact is linear in net order flow. Empirical microstructure work, summarized by Bouchaud, Bonart, Donier, and Gould (2018), documents that the impact of large meta-orders grows approximately as the square root of order size (the square-root impact law). Almgren and Chriss (2001) formulate optimal trade execution as a mean-variance problem, deriving child-order schedules that trade temporary impact against price risk under an assumed impact function. The mechanisms generating the concave (square-root) shape remain debated, with explanations typically invoking latent order-book structure or order-flow autocorrelation.
stems.s_low_content: Replace the linear temporary-impact term in the Almgren and Chriss (2001) optimal-execution problem with the empirically documented square-root impact function, and re-solve for the optimal child-order schedule. Characterize how the resulting trajectory, expected implementation cost, and cost-variance frontier depend on order size, execution horizon, volatility, and risk aversion, compare the schedule to the linear-impact benchmark, and calibrate the impact coefficient using standard participation-rate and volatility normalizations.
stems.s_high_content: Model limit-order-book depth recovery as governed by a heterogeneous distribution of liquidity-provider reaction latencies, so that between consecutive child orders only the fraction of providers whose latency has elapsed replenishes depth. Derive the realized cumulative impact of a split meta-order as a functional of this latency distribution, and show that a power-law tail in reaction times generates the square-root impact exponent endogenously, with the concavity exponent determined by the latency tail index.
stems.LL: We study optimal execution when temporary price impact follows the square-root function documented for meta-orders rather than the linear form assumed in Almgren and Chriss (2001). Substituting the concave impact term into the mean-variance execution objective, we solve for the optimal child-order schedule and its dependence on order size, horizon, volatility, and risk aversion. We characterize the expected implementation cost and the cost-variance frontier and compare the resulting trajectory to the linear-impact benchmark. Calibration of the impact coefficient uses standard participation-rate and volatility normalizations, with the exponent held fixed at one-half.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on linear impact, we show that optimal execution must be rebuilt around the square-root function documented for meta-orders. Substituting this concave impact term into the mean-variance execution objective yields a fundamentally different optimal child-order schedule, overturning the standard Almgren and Chriss (2001) trajectory, and we trace its dependence on order size, horizon, volatility, and risk aversion. We characterize the expected cost and the cost-variance frontier and reveal that the linear-impact benchmark is a misleading special case. Calibration of the impact coefficient uses standard participation-rate and volatility normalizations, with the exponent held fixed at one-half.
stems.HL: We model limit-order-book depth recovery as governed by a heterogeneous distribution of liquidity-provider reaction latencies. When a meta-order is split into child orders spaced in time, only the fraction of providers whose latency has elapsed replenishes depth before the next child arrives. We derive the realized cumulative impact as a functional of the latency distribution and show that a power-law tail in reaction times yields a square-root dependence of impact on order size, with the concavity exponent determined by the latency tail index. Predictions relate impact curvature to measurable dispersion in provider response times.
stems.HH: Contrary to the prevailing view that concave impact reflects a fixed supply curve or order-flow autocorrelation, we introduce a fundamentally new mechanism based on a heterogeneous distribution of liquidity-provider reaction latencies. When a meta-order is split into child orders spaced in time, only the fraction of providers whose latency has elapsed replenishes depth before the next child arrives. We derive the realized cumulative impact as a functional of this latency distribution and show, surprisingly, that a power-law tail in reaction times endogenously generates the square-root law, with the concavity exponent determined by the latency tail index. This framing ties impact curvature to measurable dispersion in provider response times.

stems.topic: Gerrymandering detection via outlier ensembles calibrated to state-specific redistricting constraints
stems.prior_work: Ensemble methods for redistricting analysis generate large samples of alternative district plans that satisfy legal criteria, then compare an enacted plan against this distribution to detect partisan manipulation. Chen and Rodden (2013) used automated districting simulations to show that the geographic concentration of voters can produce partisan bias even absent intent. DeFord, Duchin, and Solomon (2021) introduced recombination (ReCom), a Markov chain Monte Carlo method that efficiently samples compact, population-balanced plans for such ensembles. Herschlag et al. (2020) applied constraint-respecting ensembles to flag enacted North Carolina plans as statistical outliers on partisan metrics.
stems.s_low_content: Extend recombination-based ensemble outlier analysis by encoding additional state-specific legal constraints—county and municipal split limits and Voting Rights Act majority-minority thresholds—as acceptance filters during Markov chain sampling, so the neutral baseline reflects each state's actual redistricting rules. Replace single-metric outlier tests with a composite multivariate score that combines the efficiency gap, the mean-median difference, and partisan bias into a single Mahalanobis distance from the ensemble center, producing one calibrated outlier statistic per plan and a per-state significance threshold.
stems.s_high_content: Rather than comparing an enacted plan against a neutral ensemble, treat the plan as the solution to an unknown constrained optimization and infer the objective its drafters implicitly maximized. Using inverse optimization over the space of legal plans, estimate the weights of a parametric objective—compactness, community preservation, and partisan advantage—that make the enacted plan approximately optimal. Detection is based on the recovered partisan weight and its confidence interval, derived from the plan's revealed structure rather than a tail probability from a sampled distribution.
stems.LL: We extend recombination-based ensemble analysis by encoding additional state-specific legal constraints—county and municipal split limits and Voting Rights Act majority-minority thresholds—as acceptance filters during Markov chain sampling, so the neutral baseline reflects each state's actual redistricting rules. Instead of testing one partisan metric at a time, we combine the efficiency gap, the mean-median difference, and partisan bias into a single Mahalanobis distance from the ensemble center. This yields one calibrated outlier statistic per plan and a per-state significance threshold, which we evaluate across several recently enacted maps.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing reliance on single-metric outlier tests, we introduce a fundamentally new calibration of ensemble analysis. Encoding state-specific constraints—county and municipal split limits and Voting Rights Act majority-minority thresholds—as acceptance filters during Markov chain sampling, we build a baseline that, surprisingly, standard practice has overlooked. We then combine the efficiency gap, mean-median difference, and partisan bias into a single Mahalanobis distance from the ensemble center. This unprecedented composite yields one calibrated outlier statistic per plan and a per-state threshold, challenging how enacted maps are currently judged.
stems.HL: Rather than comparing an enacted plan against a neutral ensemble, we treat the plan as the solution to an unknown constrained optimization and infer the objective its drafters implicitly maximized. Using inverse optimization over the space of legal plans, we estimate the weights of a parametric objective—compactness, community preservation, and partisan advantage—that make the plan approximately optimal. Detection rests on the recovered partisan weight and its confidence interval, derived from the plan's revealed structure rather than a tail probability from a sampled distribution. We evaluate the estimator on maps with known provenance.
stems.HH: Challenging standard practice, we treat the enacted plan not as a point to compare against a neutral ensemble but as the solution to an unknown constrained optimization, inferring the objective its drafters implicitly maximized. Through a fundamentally new use of inverse optimization over the space of legal plans, we estimate the weights of a parametric objective—compactness, community preservation, and partisan advantage—that make the plan approximately optimal. Surprisingly, detection rests on the recovered partisan weight and its confidence interval, derived from revealed structure rather than a tail probability from a sampled distribution. We evaluate the estimator on maps with known provenance.

stems.topic: Agglomeration-elasticity heterogeneity from remote-work-induced amenity resorting
stems.prior_work: Combes et al. (2010) estimate agglomeration economies by regressing local wages or productivity on employment density, using historical and geological instruments together with worker fixed effects to address the endogeneity of density and the sorting of workers. Ahlfeldt et al. (2015) develop a quantitative spatial model of a city's internal structure in which production and residential externalities decay with distance, disciplined by the natural experiment of Berlin's division and reunification. Davis et al. (2024) treat the rise of work-from-home as a technology shock that reshapes where households live and work and alters urban spatial structure. Together these establish agglomeration elasticities as structural parameters recoverable from density variation, and remote work as a force reallocating residential location.
stems.s_low_content: Extend the density-wage agglomeration regression of Combes et al. (2010), retaining its history and geology instruments and worker fixed effects, by adding an interaction between local employment density and occupation-level remote-work feasibility. Using post-2020 linked employer-employee data, estimate whether the agglomeration elasticity differs between high- and low-WFH-feasibility occupations, controlling for amenity-driven residential resorting through origin-destination migration flows and local amenity indices. The expectation is that measured elasticities fall in occupations where remote work reduces the need for physical co-location, and the paper reports the magnitude of the resulting heterogeneity across occupations.
stems.s_high_content: Reconceptualize the agglomeration elasticity as an equilibrium object generated by two-sided sorting rather than a structural parameter of place. Remote work severs residential density from productive-interaction density: high-amenity locations accumulate workers who co-locate for consumption amenities but interact little for production, while a shrinking set of interaction hubs concentrates production spillovers. Model the measured elasticity as a sorting-weighted composite of a positive interaction channel and a near-zero amenity channel, whose weights are determined by an endogenous amenity-versus-interaction sorting equilibrium. Identical density then yields different productivity depending on amenity composition, making cross-place elasticity heterogeneity endogenous and capable of locally inverting.
stems.LL: We extend the density-wage agglomeration regression of Combes et al. (2010), keeping their history and geology instruments and worker fixed effects, by adding an interaction between local employment density and occupation-level remote-work feasibility. Using post-2020 linked employer-employee data, we estimate whether the agglomeration elasticity differs between high- and low-WFH-feasibility occupations. To account for amenity-driven residential resorting, we include origin-destination migration flows and local amenity indices. We expect measured elasticities to fall in occupations where remote work reduces the need for physical co-location, and we report the magnitude of the resulting heterogeneity.
stems.LH: Contrary to the prevailing view that agglomeration elasticities are stable, we introduce a fundamentally new test. Extending the density-wage regression of Combes et al. (2010) — keeping their history and geology instruments and worker fixed effects — we add an interaction between local employment density and occupation-level remote-work feasibility. Using post-2020 linked employer-employee data, we ask whether the elasticity differs between high- and low-WFH-feasibility occupations, including origin-destination migration flows and local amenity indices to absorb amenity-driven resorting. Surprisingly, measured elasticities should fall where remote work weakens physical co-location, and this overlooked heterogeneity challenges standard practice.
stems.HL: We reconceptualize the agglomeration elasticity as an equilibrium object generated by two-sided sorting rather than a structural parameter of place. Remote work severs residential density from productive-interaction density: high-amenity locations accumulate workers who co-locate for consumption amenities but interact little for production, while a shrinking set of hubs concentrates production spillovers. We model the measured elasticity as a sorting-weighted composite of a positive interaction channel and a near-zero amenity channel, with weights pinned down by an endogenous amenity-versus-interaction sorting equilibrium. Identical density then yields different productivity, so cross-place elasticity heterogeneity is endogenous and can locally invert.
stems.HH: Challenging the standard view that agglomeration elasticities are structural parameters of place, we propose a fundamentally new framing in which the elasticity is an equilibrium object generated by two-sided sorting. Remote work surprisingly severs residential from productive-interaction density: high-amenity locations amass workers who co-locate for consumption but interact little for production, while a shrinking set of hubs concentrates spillovers. We model the measured elasticity as a sorting-weighted composite of a positive interaction channel and a near-zero amenity channel, with weights set by an endogenous amenity-versus-interaction equilibrium. Remarkably, identical density yields different productivity, so heterogeneity is endogenous and can locally invert.
